### cantweight — 2014-04-23T04:03:59+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/labs.10627/post-123111>

These are my January labs. Drawn on CD8. Trying to figure out what I need to test at my next draw.

I am having horrendous issues with weight gain, skin dryness and acne, severe edema that ends in a day of constant urination with urine smelling strongly of ammonia. Something is wrong...docs are shaking their heads...like they just don't know.

Hoping you all can give some words of wisdom.

I really appreciate it.

### nicld — 2014-04-23T16:39:57+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/labs.10627/post-123188>

One thing that caught my eye was your pregnenalone is in the gutter (so was mine). I am on cream now and it is getting better.

If you can swing it, I would start working with Dr. Tim if you are not already. The only issue with that might be is he in no longer doing single consults but the package year long deal. I think he said that if you were already a client you can still do the single ones (which I need to do soon) but new patients will be required to do the year package.

### cantweight — 2014-04-23T17:12:42+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/labs.10627/post-123196>

That could definitely explain some of the cognitive issues I am having.....as well as inflammatory responses. I did a consult with Tim but after paying for it didnt have the funds for the 700 bucks worth of tests...so i kind of dropped that ball.

Might give the cream a go...thanks so much for the input. Really struggling to put my finger on stuff right now.

### cantweight — 2014-04-24T16:10:46+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/labs.10627/post-123310>

@[nicld](https://forum.jackkruse.com/members/6689/) I took your advice and ran out and got some pregnenolone yesterday and I shit you not an hour after taking it I was dancing around and more clearheaded than I have been in months. Did not expect that! I also forced my ass into the tub to CT...it had been a while and added a new liver/GI detox supplement....I don't know which it was but I felt so much better by yesterday afternoon and my face is about 80% clear....drastic reduction in inflammation. I know oral preg can cause a rise in T but mine has a little wiggle room and I actually like a little T boost....gives me edge and my hubby likes my T boosted too

@[JanSz](https://forum.jackkruse.com/members/933/) and @[Shijin13](https://forum.jackkruse.com/members/969/) any words of wisdom? I am just not putting some pieces together. My field tests excellent, I stay at home so no work environment issues, I ground for 3-10 hours a week, I could use more sunlight for sure but that is a seasonal thing. My sleep is very good snoring and apnea under control with oral appliance, 3-4.5 litres of RO water daily. I have lightened my diet...breakfast was skyr, spinach, pineapple, and green banana smoothie and espresso with HWC. Added raw beef and seafood. I have lessened my fats and cooked proteins. I either feel good or feel horrible and the see sawing back and forth is exhausting. I am back to living life where I dont make plans to do things because i dont know how I will feel and I hate that.

I am considering adding metformin, modafinil, maybe even procrit to get my c4a down.

I have PCOS, hashi's, and am morbidly obese. My biggest concerns are rapid weight gain in the last year (40lbs), cognitive decline, edema, ammonia urine.

### Shijin13 — 2014-04-24T17:45:08+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/labs.10627/post-123319>

Christi

your comment the other day about not being able to even consider CO, and seeing the High levels of IgA and IgM for candida - have me thinking your gut microbia might be driving that aversion?

HbA1C is high.... at 5.6 and Insulin 21.7 are high need to bring them down. this coupled with your edema, and ammonia - possible dehydration, issues w/hormones, diabetes/metabolic syndrome or kidney issues.

Free T4 looks low, you're not making enough to support hormone cascade... and linked with elevated TSH; free T3 is low. I feel better when mine is above 3.5 and your FreeT3:rT3 ratio is 34. so that's good. I think you need more Armour. Have things changed with increased dose of Armour? ACTH - looks "normal" and indicates pituitary is working - though the question how much is actually working...

Pg Is low - but you knew that. coupled with your low pregnenolone and low thyroid = tanked hormone panel. Low pregnenolone = disconnected vitamin A cycle. which also impacts hormone cascade... which is verified by your vitamin A serum. Mag RBC is ok. Ideal is above 6. but if your dehydrated you're going to have issues with getting Mag RBC up while still being dehydrated (which you are given your Bun:Creatine ratio being 15)

SHBG isn't bad - but its above 30. so your Estrogen to Pg ratio is off... but then you know that!

let's see what @[JanSz](https://forum.jackkruse.com/members/933/) says.

### cantweight — 2014-04-24T18:28:53+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/labs.10627/post-123324>

The candida numbers really surprised me...I have had zero symptoms which i used to have a terrible time with especially in the mouth. So definitely still a stressor, not sure why the sudden flare. My gut has taken a nosedive. Currently trying to support that.

Hoping the metformin will help to address the pre-diabetic/Blood glucose issue

Thyroid is a tricky one. Doc wants me back up at 180mg but I cant take it due to heart palps. So I am taking 90 AM and 45 PM and will restest next week.

Two months of oral pg after many months of creams and no response both rx and otc...will see where that has gotten me. Have also added lots of DIM and Ca D Glucarate.

BUN/Creat. was 13 in Oct and 15 in Jan....water consumption increased. Feeling badly in that time, redox low, ca efflux high, muscle fatigue and burning severe....mag driven out? water driven out? Trying to turn that ship around...not proving easy.

Thanks so much for the input it is greatly appreciated!!!

### Rylee — 2014-04-24T23:43:53+0300

The skin and acne issues could definitely be related to low vitamin A - I agree with JanSz to eat more A - liver is a good way to get it. I think your thyroid issues are behind the weight gain, and probably high estrogen/low Pg is behind the cognitive issues. Have you thought about supplementing progesterone? (Sorry if you already are - I'm new and don't know everyone's story yet!)

### JanSz — 2014-04-26T21:31:56+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/labs.10627/post-123536>

> [Rylee said:](/goto/post?id=123358)
>
> The skin and acne issues could definitely be related to low vitamin A - I agree with JanSz to eat more A - liver is a good way to get it. Tanya has a thread going today with some liver recipes that sound totally good! I think your thyroid issues are behind the weight gain, and probably high estrogen/low Pg is behind the cognitive issues. Have you thought about supplementing progesterone? (Sorry if you already are - I'm new and don't know everyone's story yet!)

In my view when pregnenolone and progesterone are low and supplementation is contemplated,
it make sense (to me) to supplement with pregnenolone first.
With luck one can achieve number of goals with just one type of pill.

I figured that for my self and it works for me already for about three years.
I am man.
From pov of progestrone man=women in the first half of women's cycle.
[http://upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/c/c8/Hormones\_estradiol,\_progesterone,\_LH\_and\_FSH\_during\_menstrual\_cycle.svg](http://upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/c/c8/Hormones_estradiol%2C_progesterone%2C_LH_and_FSH_during_menstrual_cycle.svg)
In second half, specially around days 18-24 women need lots of progesterone,
so if need arise those are the days when on top of pregnenolone some progesterone may be need.

///

### JanSz — 2014-04-26T23:52:10+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/labs.10627/post-123545>

> [cantweight said:](/goto/post?id=123544)
>
> I have added the pregnenolone and am already seeing improvenment. I thank you so much for your time and input!

In my experience not all pregnenolones are equal. Either when in form of pills or transdermal.
Commonly doctors like more transdermal kind.
In my case I found the hard way that the only type that works for me is:

Pregnenolone Micronized Lipid Matrix 150mg tabs, from Nutricology.

I am using currently two of those tabs per day, one at wakeup the other by noon.
But initially for many months I had to use 6 tabs (900mg/day)

Above still left me wanting on pregnenolone,
but my progesterone went high
and
my cortisol AM went up to top range

So really my pregnenolone dose is decided by my cortisol(AM)

This may not be your case. It all depends of status of enzymes facilitating conversion process.
You must do blood tests to monitor your status.

Initially I would not use any supplemental progesterone, that may cloud analysis of your progress.
But it is good idea to top off on DHEAs.

///

### Martin — 2014-04-27T00:53:05+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/labs.10627/post-123548>

Bunch of tests! Cool!

Vit. D is toast-ish. I had to switch to water-based D cuz of VDR snp. Really helped!

I noticed your C4a was up... 5244 (0-650) Something's revved up.

Dr Shoemaker writes:
**C4a
Normal Range**: **0-2830 ng/ml

C4a has become the inflammatory marker of greatest significance looking at innate immune responses in those with exposure to [Water Damaged Buildings](http://www.survivingmold.com/diagnosis/what-is-a-water-damaged-building) (WDB).

The complement system is a group of proteins that move freely through your bloodstream. The proteins work with your immune system and play a role in the development of inflammation.

Each complement activates inflammatory responses, with spillover of effect from the innate immune response to acquired immune response and hematologic parameters.

These short-lived products are re-manufactured rapidly, such that an initial rise of plasma levels is seen within 12 hours of exposure to biotoxins, and sustained elevation is seen until definitive therapy is initiated.
**

I just finally switched to the D-mulsion....VDR snps in the whole family. Hoping that might help us all, so good to hear it worked for you!

And yes....the dreaded C4a. You are spot on. My poor youngest was at almost 10,000. I have moved out of emf house into mold house it seems. So I am faced with some big decisions and I have struggled with them for months now. I can tear apart the house in mold remediation, but I will not have enough money to rebuild. Or I can clear it out as best I can, invest in good air purifiers, hit the house with a couple coats of killz and see if our c4a falls. Of course I'll also have to work on finding some detox that is effective as we are a house full of poor methylators on top of it. I just saw that Hope posted about CSM...something to look into for sure. This is not very fun Martin!!!

### Martin — 2014-04-27T22:14:12+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/labs.10627/post-123595>

A lot to consider for you and your family. 10,000! Yikes-almighty! Pardon me if you've covered this, but have you done a HERMI? Have you read Shoemaker's references on remediation? ### Josh (Paleo Osteo) — 2014-10-09T12:02:02+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/joshs-journal.10628/post-143299>

i thought even though i am symptom free i should write some context re: my life story here, as i would like to be able to reflect back if anything pops up in the future, and welcome any comments etc

i was born in 1980, an olnly child. my parents were dad age 30 and mother aged 35
complicated delivery, my mother's coccyx was anteverted, meaning with each contraction i was essentially sustaining cranial trauma as i was forced into the bony structure
the obgyn had to break the sacrococcygeal joint to get me out. i was born looking like i'd gone 5 rounds with tyson (but had ears)
i suffered convulsions throughout my infancy at least in the first year, but all developmental patterns and reflexes happened at the right times
i believe my parents used phenergan when my convulsions were bad
all childhood i was essentially raised on a whole foods diet devoid of added sugars etc, although my dad is italian so pasta and bread were a staple too.
an early memory from kindergarten was one of those "bring a plate" days where everyone wanted pink cakes and shit
my mother was a terrible baker and loathed the idea, but was relieved when i told her i wanted to take cheese and cured meats, pate and olives
obviously i wasnt the most popular kid at the kindergarten, but a secret teachers favourite...

early memories were always being around adults (as an only child) and always reading my favourite book which was an 80's concise atlas of the Earth.
I really think this is what started my feelings that it was all of modern life's "luxuries" that would be humankinds undoing, as i remember often saying things like this when seeing the news with my parents

mum used to feed me wild alaskan red salmon from a can almost every day for lunch, which might explain the DHA in the labs above
i was always told in primary school i was very perceptive and bright

throughout my younger years i was prone to mucus in the chest if i ever caught a cold, and was routinely prescribed antibiotics

at high school age i was awkward socially, but played elite level ice hockey in australia
acne abounded from about age 13-14, and i remember mum taking me to a dermatologist, Cynthia Weinstein (google her, she is now in jail!!!!)
she put me on Roaccutane () which i took for a year i think, with horrible side effects such as extremely dry lips etc, this also added to the social anxiety, but looking back it was probably the gut/AB interactions

my mother died when i was 16, just at the time i was feeling like i was coming into myself as a person, becoming more confident as my ice hockey was successful at this age making the state sides regularly
this rocked me a fair bit as mum was an inspiration (clients at the clinic still tell me today they remember her teaching their kids etc)
however although it was tough i had a good support network by now, and i was the rock while my father suffered, my time to lose it would come later...

at 17 the pressure was building because school over here ramps up the importance of how you do in year 11 and 12
at this time i was also invited to the australian natuonal youth team for ice hockey, but also was discovering a social life and mucked around with booze and occasionally weed etc
through hockey i met my career inspiration, Ian HOlmes who was a TCM practitioner and very amazing coach
he inspired me to look into Osteopathy after talking at a camp about wholism

i ended up doing pretty well at school depsite being with the wrong crowd, although was very emotionally ragged at this age, as mums death caught up with me, and i had a girlfriend who was more focused on her school than me which was hard at the time

when i was accepted into osteopathy i was againa bit socially awkward, although sure who i was. the problem was the other types in the osteo course (lots of private school jocks etc) this gave me a few body image issues as i was quite skinny/gangly compared to them, and many classes we were all in our underwear!)
Nevertheless during uni i started a music career and released many records on international labels between this time and the first few years into practice...

through the years of working and gaining clinical experience with my perceptions fully tuned, i began to realise what i had always believed since reading the atlas as a kid was becoming apparent, and i found the ancestral health community, and subsequently Jack's material 10 years on, and here i am.
nowdays i am 6'2" 85kg, lean, fit and have found my career blossoming through meeting likeminded individuals and fostering relationships
it is like everything has fallen into place and i have "come home" so to speak

.

[Jack Kruse](https://www.facebook.com/jack.kruse?fref=ufi): I almost hesitate to go here......because it is a future blog topic.......but here it goes. The pineal sits in the posterior third ventricle. If you read my quantum sleep blog you'll get a bird's eye view of what it does. It is a laser. It absorbs magnetic flux energies t make polarized light. This alters a vortex within CSF. Our ependymal cells sense the direction of the vortex to signal. Ependymal cells are within a cavity called the third ventricle. Why is this important? Ventricles are filled with CSF that is 99% an ultrafiltrate of blood plasma. The choroid plexus structures CSF with iodine. The third ventricle has a special size and shape. If you been following my OSF 3-5 blogs you'll see I speak about protein size and shape in terms of thermodynamics. Cavities do the same thing to sound and fluid waves. For light the waves are called photons. For cavity waves they are called phonons. Phonons are waves moving through solid matter either solid or liquid. Here we are talking liquid water. Different objects make distinctive sounds because of the way they vibrate. The "sound" of the vibration depends upon the shape of the object and the material from which it is made. For musicians, they covet Stradivarius violins from the last Maunder minimum because the sounds are pure because the wood from that era was more dense due to the colder temps for those 80 years. In the brain the story is the same. The surrounding walls of the third ventricle has a specific resonance. This is lost in many neuro-degenerative diseases. The configuration of any cavity of the instrument help determine its particular sound or phonon. that phonons transmits specific energy and information to your hypothalamus. The anterior roof of the third ventricle is where the most important parts of your autonomic nervous system is. Oh noes!!!  We've got some leading indicators of stress.

That said, the longer term indicators like Vit D are fine. Looks to be a result of the stress of the sudden change of seasons down South. (10hr 25min sunshine today)

Main question that I'm asking myself is if what mainly brought this on is a change to levels of non-native EMF in your environment, or just a "more natural" stressor of declining magnetic field and declining sunshine during the autumn transition.

I assume that you keep your own nn-EMF environment controlled, but those can still rise if your neighbours decide to turn on central heating (amidst other changes). This should show up on a trimeter measurement, so a quick test is probably a good idea.

\----

Subjectively, what is the feeling? A sudden shitty feeling in the last few days/weeks? Any sleep disruptions? Level of happiness/satisfaction? (indicator of dopamine) etc ...

\----

I have personally stayed away from Coffee for more than a month. The benefits from coffee can be gotten elsewhere IMO. (I still have the habit of green tea + nicotine gum though )

\----

Will wait for more updates

### Josh (Paleo Osteo) — 2015-05-05T11:04:41+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/joshs-journal.10628/post-163341>

i think stress too
tax bills, sleep disturbances from being in two environments a week
to be honest the cousin's house that i stay at in bendigo has all this shit in it that is always on, lamps on timers etc
it is better for quietness and darkness in my room there, but really not much chop for emf i suspect
however doesnt appear to be any Ca effluxing happening on labs, vit D has remained high

it may be a seasonal change thing especially in the context of the late daylight saving shift, i thought the light cycle had already well and truly swung about 4 weekd before the clock change was to occur...

slightly waning levels of satisfaction, nothing that i would consider unhappy
i am worried for jaimes health and her iphone addiction, it is frustrating that she knows EMF is her issue at work but her downtime consists of gaming on her phone and instagram scrollin...i think that drives the ol cortisol up

coffee must go, i went off it for a month and felt pretty good, in all honesty i think i use it in bendigo at work because sometimes it can seem a drag that everyone is falling apart and it helps me maintain some drive, but one shouldnt rely on exogenous substances for that...

these labs were the first of a new lot i wanted to get to check for adrenal fatigue onset, as i think whilst i am really only working 3.5 days a week i am dealing with hectic issues and patients who are unwilling to change. being a slight perfectionist with regard to following up i believe i am taking too much on, giving too much, and not seeing enough results, not through a lack in my own work but through a loss in general drive from my clientele to change what is hurting them

as you know my next door neighbour is close and she has a lot of solar flashing lights that come on at night, i also suspect a cordless phone and some overt energy use which would be feeding out her smart meter. might have to look into teh shieldingz
i have shielded our own, we have no wifi, changed globes to yellow/red and limit the rest

will re-test environment when i get home. i suspect the commute and bendigo in general is partly to blame

### yewwei.tan — 2015-05-06T05:03:04+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/joshs-journal.10628/post-163421>

> [Jill1 said:](/goto/post?id=163419)
>
> I found a company that sells water infused with H+ gas. I thought I read something about the benefits of H+ on here?
> .
>
> research on H+ [http://www.molecularhydrogeninstitute.com/](http://www.molecularhydrogeninstitute.com/)
>
> .

H2 gas infused water is always good. We're talking about getting both H+ and H- here.

You produce lots of H2 in the gut, on the order of 12-14L a day. Infusion isn't going to get you past 2L in most cases. It's especially effective if the person has a bad gut flora, but if gut flora is functioning alright, it's not a super positive intervention.

Some calculations:

Molar volume of Hydrogen is 22.4L at STP
\=> 1L of hydrogen gas -> 0.04464mol of H2 gas
\=> 12L of hydrogen gas -> 0.5357mol H2

Maximum hydrogen dissolvability in water is 0.8mM (1.6ppm)
Most machines get you 6% of this level. ie: 0.048mM (0.096ppm) -- [http://www.molecularhydrogeninstitu...ow-to-get-the-benefits-of-molecular-hydrogen/](http://www.molecularhydrogeninstitute.com/core-information/how-to-get-the-benefits-of-molecular-hydrogen/)

If you drink 3L of water out of most machines, you get 0.144mol of H2, which is about 26% of 0.5357. Hence the claim of "20-30% boost" in H2 from H2-enriched water.

The potential for saturating water with a lot of hydrogen is good, but the tech isn't available yet.
​

If you could get H2-infused water cheaply, I'd say go for it. But the machines cost a ton of money. In the case of Josh, if he were to choose between a Hydrogen Infuser and a red LED device, I'd take the LED device.

\----

Being tolerant to exercise is a good thing. Kinda like where I'm at right now, whereby exercise of any form (strength, endurance, HIIT) makes my collagen better (and not worse like it did in the past)

....

> The potential for saturating water with a lot of hydrogen is good, but the tech isn't available yet.
> ​
>
>
> If you could get H2-infused water cheaply, I'd say go for it. But the machines cost a ton of money. In the case of Josh, if he were to choose between a Hydrogen Infuser and a red LED device, I'd take the LED device.
>
> \----
>
> Being tolerant to exercise is a good thing. Kinda like where I'm at right now, whereby exercise of any form (strength, endurance, HIIT) makes my collagen better (and not worse like it did in the past)
>
> ....

Hydrogen ions affect the electrical potential within cell membrane walls.

When you hydrate the cells they plump and become healthy and the body goes into an anabolic state - when the cells become dehydrated, the body goes into a catalytic state and eats its own muscles.

Our bodies store hydrogen in "hydrogen pools" in the organs with the greatest amount stored in the liver which is the body's chemical factory and our most important organ for protection

Hydrogen ions assist in maintaining the electrical balance that enables cell structures to communicate and function properly.

Damaged or diseased cells present an abnormally low TMP, about 80% lower than healthy cells.

Everyone is deficient in H-. A machine called the BTA or Biological Terrain Analyzer developed by a Dr. Morrell which tests blood, saliva and urine for H+, H- and minerals found 100% of people low in H-, especially as they got older. They were all overoxidized. The absence of electrons causes numerous diseases.

### Josh — 2015-05-28T16:35:37+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/joshs-journal.10628/post-165677>

[http://www.jbc.org/content/273/11/6163.full](http://www.jbc.org/content/273/11/6163.full)

> Cytochrome P450s are a superfamily of enzymes essential for the oxidative metabolism of different endogenous compounds and xenobiotics ([1](http://www.jbc.org/content/273/11/6163.full#ref-1)). For many members of the family, enzyme activity varies between individuals due to polymorphisms in the coding parts of the genes. Four cytochrome P450 enzymes are involved in the synthesis of cortisol in the adrenal cortex ([2](http://www.jbc.org/content/273/11/6163.full#ref-2)). Impaired production of cortisol due to reduced function of any of these enzymes causes increased secretion of adrenocorticotropic hormone (ACTH) from the pituitary and congenital adrenal hyperplasia (CAH)[1](http://www.jbc.org/content/273/11/6163.full#fn-4)([3](http://www.jbc.org/content/273/11/6163.full#ref-3)). Of particular interest is the cytochrome P450 steroid 21-hydroxylase (P450c21) since more than 90% of all cases of CAH are due to mutations in the gene encoding the enzyme. P450c21 participates in the synthesis of both glucocorticoids and mineralocorticoids. In addition to lack of cortisol and aldosterone, P450c21 deficiency leads to overproduction of adrenal androgens. The disorder has a wide spectrum of manifestations ([4](http://www.jbc.org/content/273/11/6163.full#ref-4)), ranging from a life-threatening neonatal condition in both sexes with ambiguous genitalia in girls, to inconspicuous symptoms of hyperandrogenism in adult women.

[http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK1171/](http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK1171/)

There seems to be "Classical" and the always ambiguous "non-classical" versions of CAH.

### Josh — 2015-05-28T16:44:22+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/joshs-journal.10628/post-165679>

> Congenital adrenal hyperplasias (CAH) are inherited defects of cortisol biosynthesis. More than 90% of CAH are caused by 21-hydroxylase deficiency (21-OHD), found in 1:10 000 to 1:15 000 live births. Females with ‘classical’ 21-OHD, being exposed to excess androgens prenatally, are born with virilized external genitalia. Potentially lethal adrenal insufficiency is characteristic of two-thirds to three-quarters of patients with the classical salt wasting (SW) form of 21-OHD. Non-SW 21-OHD may be diagnosed on genital ambiguity in affected females, and/or later on the occurrence of androgen excess in both sexes. Non-classical 21-OHD, detected in ≥1:100 of certain populations, may present as precocious pubarche in children or polycystic ovarian syndrome in young women. 21-OHD is caused by mutations in the _CYP21_ gene encoding the steroid 21-hydroxylase enzyme. More than 90% of these mutations result from intergenic recombination between _CYP21_ and the closely linked _CYP21P_pseudogene. The degree to which each mutation compromises enzymatic activity is strongly correlated with the clinical severity of the disorder. This close association between genotype and phenotype makes it possible to predict clinical outcome in affected subjects. The risk of SW and prenatal virilization can be estimated, and overtreatment can be avoided in mildly affected cases. Glucocorticoid and mineralocorticoid replacement therapies are the mainstays of treatment, but additional therapies are being developed. A first trimester prenatal diagnosis should be proposed in families in whom molecular studies have been performed previously. The state of heterozygotism can be predicted by hormonal testing and confirmed by molecular studies. Prenatal diagnosis by direct mutation detection in previously genotyped families permits prenatal treatment of affected females in order to avoid or minimize genital virilization. Neonatal screening by hormonal methods identifies affected children before SW crises develop, reducing mortality in this disorder.

> Congenital adrenal hyperplasia (CAH) results from inherited defects in one of the five enzymatic steps required for the biosynthesis of cortisol from cholesterol. These disorders are so named because the adrenal glands are hyperplastic at birth due to unrestrained ACTH stimulation already in fetal life. Indeed, chronic overstimulation of ACTH secretion due to low levels of cortisol results in hyperplasia of the adrenal cortex. In addition, in all forms of CAH, other than lipoid CAH, there is increased output of the steroid precursors above the block, as well as of their urinary metabolites.

[http://humupd.oxfordjournals.org/content/10/6/469.long](http://humupd.oxfordjournals.org/content/10/6/469.long)

[http://emedicine.medscape.com/article/919218-overview](http://emedicine.medscape.com/article/919218-overview)

### Josh — 2015-05-29T02:17:06+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/joshs-journal.10628/post-165736>

I think the reality is that CAH is misnamed based upon our current understanding. Some of the cases may be "genetic" in origin, and these express due to epigenetic conditions. In addition, there must be epigenetic environment and lifestyle manifestations of enzyme malfunction at multiple levels due to quantum factors that create similar "syndromes" at almost anytime in an indivdual's life. Obviously, there are certain periods of life where individuals are more susceptible based upon developmental and or decling function/aging issues. I am sure a wide range of traumas and persistent stressors could add up to create a "quantum shift" that dramatically alters function. I do not feel that current diagnostics and practice are well equipped to track this longitudinally. So the etiology seems less important than the axiomatic approach to remediation and repair. I imagine the genetic testing for the mutation is of some importance as it may indicate susceptibilities in one's children and grandchildren. This is not my area of historical expertise.

You played hockey?

> **_Renal injuries in children are second only to head injuries in frequency,_**[35](http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC1472993/#B35) and 10% of renal injuries occur in congenitally malformed kidneys.[35](http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC1472993/#B35) Blunt injuries occur in 60%–90% of patients, and only a renal contusion occurs in most cases.[36](http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC1472993/#B36) In the past, the American Medical Association recommended disqualification of athletes with renal disease or solitary kidneys from collision or contact sports.[37](http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC1472993/#B37),[38](http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC1472993/#B38) Recently, people with disabilities have won the right to participate in contact sports, citing the Federal Rehabilitation Act of 1973, and athletes who had previously been disqualified from participation in some sports are now able to compete.[37](http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC1472993/#B37)
>
> In 2001, the American Academy of Pediatrics (AAP) published an updated statement on medical conditions affecting sports participation.[39](http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC1472993/#B39) The AAP classifies sports by their degree of contact and collision. **_The AAP does not recommend participation in boxing and suggests only a limited amount of body checking for hockey players of 15 years and younger._** Individuals with one kidney need individual assessment for contact/collision and limited-contact sports. Protective equipment such as “flakjackets” may make limited-contact/ impact sports safe.[37](http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC1472993/#B37),[38](http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC1472993/#B38) Sports with high contact/collision potential are boxing, basketball, diving, field hockey, football (tackle), ice hockey, lacrosse, martial arts, rodeo, rugby, ski jumping, soccer, team handball, water polo, and wrestling. This does not mean that some of the limited-contact sports, such as downhill skiing and gymnastics, cannot be as dangerous as high-contact or collision sports.[39](http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC1472993/#B39)

[http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC1472993/](http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC1472993/)

### Josh — 2015-05-29T02:19:51+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/joshs-journal.10628/post-165737>

> Adrenal insufficiency can be divided into two types, a primary and a secondary form, depending on the length of disease and the primary hormones affected. Primary adrenal insufficiency results from a loss of both cortisol and aldosterone secretion due to the near or total destruction (Addison’s) of both adrenal glands. Most reported cases of primary adrenal insufficiency result from destruction of the adrenal cortex by the body’s own immune system and the process takes months to years. Often times, individuals are not aware they have the disease and may be misdiagnosed with another similar disorder \[[9](http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC3648788/#R9)\]. Secondary adrenal insufficiency can be traced to a lack of ACTH, which causes a drop in the adrenal glands’ production of cortisol but not aldosterone. **_This is most commonly found with overtraining in athletes. Symptoms are not usually apparent until over 90% of the adrenal cortex has been destroyed and very little adrenal capacity is left_**. This is due to the non-specific nature of symptoms and their slow progression. Symptoms are more times than not, often missed or ignored until physically stressful event or crisis, known as an Addisonian crisis, which is characterized by a sudden, penetrating pain in the lower back, abdomen, or legs, along with severe vomiting and diarrhea, followed by dehydration, low blood pressure and a loss of consciousness.

[http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC3648788/](http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC3648788/)

Ideal SHBG~20
Do not use any chemicals to lower it (Danazol, Stanozolol).

Second,
measure your underarm temperature, in the mid-day, when calm and rested.

Do thyroid work, FreeT3 should be =>3.5
optimal FreeT3= (3.5-4.2) pg/mL

Three;
Figure out your GH, Growth Hormone status.

Do that by checking
IGF-1
IGFBP3

Problem is that automatic tests (chemoluminescence) are often not only inaccurate,
but inaccurate to the point that it may show bad direction, high when it is low or vice versa.

Acceptable are (manual) RIA, radioimmunoassay.

LabCorp's division, Esoterix have those tests.
You have to ask for them by name and number (and hopefully they will do it for you).

....

### Josh (Paleo Osteo) — 2015-05-30T01:50:22+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/joshs-journal.10628/post-165871>

thanks guys

here is my current breakdown of the situation

born with epigenetic adrenal switch ON (trauma)
raised w essentially epi-palo diet but of course bread and pasta added
quite resilient child, very top of range intelligence at early school
age 12 started playing competetive ice hockey, some stress associated with performance around 14-19
age 16 mother died bowel cancer...more stress response, most markedly pronounced at age 18 during year 12 exams when more stress poured on top...v emotional

university year 1 at bendigo = adrenal fatigue
i used to come home from classes at midday and sleep til dinner time on couch
father thought i was on drugs
this lasted maybe 8 months, never diagnosed
looking back many of my classes were in a building with a cell tower on top of it!

next year - went to melbourne for university in osteopathy
felt good, but had some social anxiety associated. class members all different people to me

many stressors since, however moving from bendigo to the coast has made me feel great compared to how i did there, so i think environment is not such a big deal, house tested, all clear, very good

in summary, whilst brain resilience has stayed high it appears that the adrenal tone is becoming exhausted and cracks are beginning to form

because of the dysfunctions appearing as they are it seems appropriate to supplement the required hormone precursors 1) to decrease output stress on the adrenal glands, 2) to support downsytream hormone synthesis to maintain body metastability which will 3) allow the optimal living principles to begin to heal the pathways

unfortunately i have zero faith in any doctors in Australia to run this and DHEA, Pregnenolone etc are precription only

in USA i will consult with Dr Kruse hopefully and can organise some supps that end to bring home as the way i see this is that i really feel quite well considering the labs showing me i should be in a hole by now
i think giving myself a jump start to allow the adrenals some time to rest and regenerate will likely see my practises lift me again

thanks for all your input @[JanSz](https://forum.jackkruse.com/members/933/) and @[Josh](https://forum.jackkruse.com/members/8791/)

### yewwei.tan — 2015-05-30T09:46:10+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/joshs-journal.10628/post-165905>

You know I'm the Cynic around here .

I don't believe that Humans can live optimally anywhere on this planet, which now has vastly different solar spectrum and atmospheric stressors than what we are designed to work with as eutherian mammals.

While a cold and dark environment was what led to the dominance of the eutherian mammals, it was only because eutherian mammals and birds had lots of spare mitochondrial capacity to cope with the harsh environment. It is still a harsh environment, and demands as close to full efficiency of the mitochondria as possible. ie: There is little room for error.

The massive light, temperature, magnetic, and pressure swings at the extreme latitudes (which nowadays means anything outside the Cancer-Capricorn band) are IMO an environmental stressor that demands a human living in that environment to have much higher mitochondrial efficiency to cope. Again, very low margin for error.

\----

Regarding labs, on one hand, I like detailed testing to target specific pathways. On the other hand, it's still patching leaks as they arise.

Personally, I wish that you could have Ruben's LED bed right now

....

### Jill1 — 2015-05-31T06:02:01+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/joshs-journal.10628/post-166006>

.
Takes about 48 hours to get a permit from the Gov.**

Copied this from the Gov Website:**

You will need to obtain a permit before entering Australia. To apply for a permit you need to provide the Office of Chemical Safety and Environmental Health with a signed letter on official letterhead from your prescribing doctor, detailing:

-   Prescribing doctors name
-   Prescribing doctors address, telephone, fax and email contact details
-   Patient’s full name and date of birth
-   Name of medicine, including active drug substance
-   Strength of medicine
-   Dosage regime
-   A statement that the named patient has been prescribed the specified medicine to treat an ongoing medical condition.

You should also provide:

-   your planned arrival and departure dates in Australia and the country/s you are travelling from and returning to
-   your full contact details

Requests for permits can be sent to the Office of Chemical Safety and Environmental Health.
OCSEH contact details
Fax 02 6289 2500
Email [tmu@health.gov.au](mailto:tmu@health.gov.au)
.

### Shijin13 — 2015-06-01T22:42:40+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/joshs-journal.10628/post-166143>

@[Josh (Paleo Osteo)](https://forum.jackkruse.com/members/9815/)

I've been thinking about this... as your ASI is similar to what mine looked like several y ears ago... and I know I've been having morning cortisol issues of recent due to challenges. my work environment was my biggest driver...

But some of the things I used to mitigate that was adaptogens, one that I found that worked very well was [http://www.naturalhealthyconcepts.com/adaptocrine-k2-p-apex-energetics.html](http://www.naturalhealthyconcepts.com/adaptocrine-k2-p-apex-energetics.html)

I've gone back time and again to this post... which I know you're familiar with... [http://www.jackkruse.com/what-might-casey-anthony-and-oj-have-in-common/](http://www.jackkruse.com/what-might-casey-anthony-and-oj-have-in-common/)

I will say i was able to get my ASI back into a normal curve, at the top range for each point. But I also followed several of Jack's tips here [http://www.jackkruse.com/brain-gut-16-adrenal-fatigue-rx/](http://www.jackkruse.com/brain-gut-16-adrenal-fatigue-rx/)

so that might as well be worth revisiting. I've been going back to basics... and I think that's helping..... Like I said in our PM this years Day light Savings time (Spring forward) leveled me. where in preivous years... transitioning from summer to fall killed me. Which seems to be the case of what you've got going on- so I think you need to go back and read @[TheKid](https://forum.jackkruse.com/members/477/) 's post about seasonal decline in the fall... I'll see if I can find it... but that's an oldie and a goodie... and I think it has relevance here for you...

[http://forum.jackkruse.com/index.php?threads/seasonal-decline-in-health.8582/](http://forum.jackkruse.com/index.php?threads/seasonal-decline-in-health.8582/)

so your fall crash and adrenal tank might be related to transitioning from Cytochrome 1, to cytochrome 2. you can't quite get the NAD+ to move to the PPP. hence the rough transition... which in addition to the tanked adrenals from the stress of the commuting work environment = dragging ass...

@[yewwei.tan](https://forum.jackkruse.com/members/6824/) any additional thoughts on this?

other than what we've all be working for the past 4years?

### Josh (Paleo Osteo) — 2015-11-22T00:13:13+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/joshs-journal.10628/post-182488>

great weather here on the surf coast lately, between 25 and 35 rather consistently
last two weeks UV index between 7 and 9

still travelling between here and bendigo to the clinic, which i believe is still my lifes major stressor
making plans to sell our house up there which will take us close to debt free and allow me to work less...some people think i dont work enough as it is, but i am feeling work to be more and more of a "give" and an "energy sink"

good to see @[yewwei.tan](https://forum.jackkruse.com/members/6824/) last weekend, as ever, some great conversation around health "memes" and lots of unpacking mitochondrial dynamics, juman design etc

one thing to note regarding sleep:
i have never had trouble getting to sleep. perhaps the only time it is ever hard is if i go to bed before i am tired, or too early.
once there, usually nodded off within 5 minutes.

lately i have been getting into intense REM with extremely vivid and lucid dreams that are so clear that i can even remember things like making plans, forming friendships/relationships with strangers, forming trust, taking phone calls to orchestrate meetings etc
what i have noticed however is that amidst these dreams, tension mounting from the dream situation creates a huge surge in body temperature and i wake up and have to pee...it is as if the urgency from the dream situation manifests as nocturia...(last night it was missing three important phone calls, then finally taking the call but the voice was muddled russian, and in trying my hardest to slow the conversation down and take notes, i missed a train in siberia and was stranded in a small town)
often after getting up, i find my mind is 100% switched on and clear, but i am running over all the possibilitied of what is happening in my head and creating further agitation...there is also the fact that i never know what time it is when it happens (no power in room/no clock) though i suspect it is usually 2-3am not later...it can often take 30 mins to get back to sleep with diaphragmatic breathing exercises and non dual awareness exercises.

i guess this has happened on and off for a year or more, but seems more pronounced. i have tried spending 4 days in a row outside for 75% of the day either ocean swimming or sunning whilst grounded, no change to the sleep pattern...
the last time i slept a full night with no waking was i think after spending an entire day paddleboarding in the sun, mood was ecstatic afterwards and i experienced the "full body tired" i remember from when i was playing ice hockey, i had not had that in some time...

sometimes the waking to pee is in a haze and i am straight back asleep again, when it coincides with the temperature rise and vivid dreams it is harder to get back to sleep...i will clarify that the dreams are not really "nightmares" just very full on clear situations...

i suspect this is an inappropriate cortisol response as my AM ASI was, whilst normal curve, slightly on the low side, and i think the catalyst, whilst it was great for other stressors in my life, was moving out of our bendigo house and down here, i remember many sleepless nights as the time to move rolled around and i think it screwed my PVN~ :\\

### yewwei.tan — 2015-11-22T04:10:45+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/joshs-journal.10628/post-182490>

First thought is some sort of Serotonin imbalance. That then hints at some gut-mediated issues, since almost all serotonin is produced there.

This is a study in snails, but there is plausible reason to believe the same mechanisms are at work in us. "**Reinstatement of long-term memory following erasure of its behavioral and synaptic expression in Aplysia"** (Chen et. al., 2014) -- [http://elifesciences.org/content/early/2014/11/17/eLife.03896](http://elifesciences.org/content/early/2014/11/17/eLife.03896)

Basically some speculation that Serotonin can express neural connections that re-potentiate long-term memory. They leave a speculatory note that this has implications for PTSD; ie: having a traumatic memory re-activated again and again by serotonin excess.

Some will link the Serotonin and Estrogen systems together to explain Hot Flushes, "**The role of serotonin in hot flushes**" -- [http://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0378512200001511](http://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0378512200001511) . Again, this is plausible.

I haven't read this, though I should  "**Dreams, Hallucinogenic Drug States, and Schizophrenia: A Psychological and Biological Comparison**" (Fischman et. al., 1983) -- [http://schizophreniabulletin.oxfordjournals.org/content/9/1/73.short](http://schizophreniabulletin.oxfordjournals.org/content/9/1/73.short) . Claims that serotonin is a key regulator of such states, including dreams. Believable, especially since serotonin is so chemical similar to hardcore hallucinogens like DMT. (both contain the tryptamine structure and constituent indole ring. That ring is the likely functional structure)

Ray Peat is very much against serotonin, as well as dietary tryptophan excess (eg: too much muscle meats) -- [http://raypeat.com/articles/aging/tryptophan-serotonin-aging.shtml](http://raypeat.com/articles/aging/tryptophan-serotonin-aging.shtml) . Basically mentions all the symptoms you describe, like serum osmolarity dysfunction (waking up and going to pee).

The article is worth reading, but the practical recs are:

Gelatin, besides being a good source of glycine, also contains a large amount of proline, which has some antiexcitatory properties similar to glycine.

If a half-pound of steak is eaten, it would probably be reasonable to have about 20 grams of gelatin at approximately the same time. Even a higher ratio of gelatin to muscle meat might be preferable.

Carbon dioxide, high altitude, thyroid, progesterone, caffeine, aspirin, and decreased tryptophan consumption protect against excessive serotonin release. When sodium intake is restricted, there is a sharp increase in serotonin secretion. This accounts for some of the antiinflammatory and diuretic effects of increased sodium consumption--increasing sodium lowers both serotonin and adrenalin.

The polyunsaturated oils interact closely with serotonin and tryptophan, and the short and medium chain saturated fatty acids have antihistamine and antiserotonin actions. \[avoid PUFA and eat some Coconut oil\]​

Sounds like something to test out  (and drop the gluten)

Apparently the affects of the drug Ecstasy is Serotonin-mediated as well  "**The serotonin uptake inhibitor citalopram reduces acute cardiovascular and vegetative effects of 3, 4-methylenedioxymethamphetamine (‘Ecstasy’) in healthy volunteers**" (Matthias E. Liechti and Franz X. Vollenweider, 2000) -- [http://jop.sagepub.com/content/14/3/269.short](http://jop.sagepub.com/content/14/3/269.short)

haidut on the Peat forums claims that having lots of dreams is a sign of high serotonin, and recommends acute doses of Niacinamide (amide form of nicotinic acid / Vitamin B3 / niacin) when under stress -- [https://raypeatforum.com/forum/viewtopic.php?p=33531#p33531](https://raypeatforum.com/forum/viewtopic.php?p=33531#p33531)

At 2,000mg \[of niaciamide\] you should have a very good night sleep with no awakenings and no dreams. I am not sure where Peat mentioned that, but vivid dreams happen when the body is not in deep sleep and is usually caused by too much serotonin/melatonin. Niacinamide, for me, brings back the childhood quality of sleep - go to bed and wake up in the morning in the same position I fell asleep.​

Again, if funds allow, this is a nice experiment to do, and the results will be apparent after a single night (to decide whether to continue using this strategy). I would only do such doses of Niaciamide acutely (say at most once a week).

\----

As for "The Perfect Sleep", I have a biased intuition to say that "child-like sleep", whereby you sleep straight through the night with no waking and no dreams whilst feeling refreshed afterward, is a good symptom to shoot for. Whatever methodology that promotes that is probably good.

\----

Heheh, you know I can work hard, but also can spend half my time doing nothing at all . Need to take today and tomorrow as solid planned do-fuck-all time before getting back to the grind . Time to live the "Layzee Aussie" stereotype .

....

### yewwei.tan — 2015-11-22T06:16:39+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/joshs-journal.10628/post-182492>

> [Josh (Paleo Osteo) said:](/goto/post?id=182491)
>
> serotonin excess...hmmf...negative feedback control with the receptors/serotonin resistance?? one would expect to be bouncing off the walls with joy...this is not really me at present
> i agree that perfect sleep should be childlike. this is what i am craving lately.
>
> also recall ben lynch having some issues with niacin use because it has a depleting effect on methyl groups...i guess this is why acute dosing is important, and probably a time release form...looks like a hack for niacinamide + sauna
>
> i am waiting for someone to say environment, but i am pretty sure its quite good now (although the travel to the other town means 3 days/2 nights not so good...better sell the other house

I do not agree that Serotonin is a "happiness hormone", and even some of the mainstream share this view -- [http://io9.com/the-most-popular-antidepressants-are-based-on-a-theory-1686163236](http://io9.com/the-most-popular-antidepressants-are-based-on-a-theory-1686163236)

Basically, some drugs that happened to raise serotonin **seemed** to prevent certain symptoms of depression, and then "low serotonin" was attributed as the cause (probably to sell more drugs).

The article also talks about completely conflicting results, where some serotonin uptake enhancers also had the same effect as serotonin uptake inhibitors, as measured by symptoms of depression. Linking something fuzzy like "symptoms of depression" to "serotonin is good/bad" does not allow us to answer mechanistic effects of serotonin.

The article mentioned a quick note about how in rodents, high serotonin is often linked to depression. Heheh, probably true, since inescapable electrical shocks raised serotonin in rats -- [http://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0006899397013139](http://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0006899397013139) . Though "Rats that were exposed to comparable shock treatment, but that were given the opportunity to escape, did not show an increase in 5-HT. Rats that were restrained but not shocked also did not show an increase in 5-HT"

Another study confirming that "inescapable stress" is something that raises serotonin in rats -- [http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/7991722](http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/7991722)

But now we look at different stress stimuli, and different pre-treatment protocols, and we have people concluding "low serotonin is bad" -- [http://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/009130579290163A](http://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/009130579290163A)

-   Look at how after having been faced with "escapable stress" (ES) once, the rats continue to try and escape from an inescapable situation, and their serotonin levels are maintained.
-   The 2 drug protocols have the same effect, but this "natural" ES protocol had the same effect without exogenous substances to alter physiology.
-   The rats that were dumped straight into the inescapable stress scenario had low serotonin, and showed a learned helplessness state (did not try to escape)

What people try to make of such studies is debatable. An absolutely wrong conclusion is that "drugs increase serotonin and therefore cure learned helplessness".

-   (a) drugs weren't even needed; endogenous response maximisation does not correlate with exogenous regulation strategies
-   (b) I don't find the so-called "test for learned helplessness" to be sufficient proof (how about "confused as fuck rats put into a novel situation and see no reason to try and escape")

In any case, there is so much problem with the serotonin theory of depression / happiness, that that is not a good way to measure the effects of serotonin.

\----

Well, so what does serotonin do? As usual, I have no clue on the actual mechanism, so we are left with looking at observations of having active serotonin (which activates the 7 5-HT receptors)

Just a couple of examples:

\- "**Serotonin-induced stimulation of cortisol secretion from human adrenocortical tissue is mediated through activation of a serotonin4 receptor subtype.**" (Lefebvre et. al., 1992) -- [http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/1374544](http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/1374544)

Pretty straightforward, more serotonin activity leads to more corticosteroids, which all can be thought of as "energy use enhancers" to basically allow oxidation of any available fuel.

Another 2 similar studies (done by the same group):

\- "**Overexpression of the Serotonin 5-HT2B Receptor in Heart Leads to Abnormal Mitochondrial Function and Cardiac Hypertrophy**" (Nebigil et. al., 2003) -- [http://circ.ahajournals.org/content/107/25/3223.full](http://circ.ahajournals.org/content/107/25/3223.full)

Transgenic mouse heart displayed a specific reduction in the expression levels of the adenine nucleotide translocator associated to i**ncrease in the succinate dehydrogenase and cytochrome C oxidase mitochondrial activities.**​

\- "**Functional Consequence of Serotonin/5-HT2B Receptor Signaling in Heart. Role of Mitochondria in Transition Between Hypertrophy and Heart Failure?**" (Nebigil et. al., 2003)

Same finding basically ('COX' refers to Cyt C oxidase, and 'SDH' refers to Succinate Dehydrogenase):

Overexpression of 5-HT2BR in the mouse heart leads to abnormally high mitochondrial numbers as observed by transmission electron microscopic analysis and red ragged fiber staining. Moreover, mitochondrial enzyme activities such as COX and SDH are increased in TG mouse hearts.

Our work has shown that 5-HT via the 5-HT2BR acts as a survival factor, by inhibiting serum withdrawal-induced apoptosis as manifested by DNA fragmentation, nuclear chromatin condensation, and terminal deoxynucleotidyl transferase UTP nick end-labeling (TUNEL)

5-HT prevents cytoC release and caspase-9 and -3 activation after serum deprivation via cross talk between phosphatidyl-inositol-3 kinase (PI3K)/Akt and ERK1/2 signaling pathways. On activation of Akt by 5-HT, phosphorylation of IκB-α triggers its degradation to release nuclear factor (NF)-κB.

This activation of NF-κB inhibits serum deprivation-induced adenine nucleotide translocator (ANT) expression to maintain mitochondrial permeability

Parallel to this pathway, ERK activation by 5-HT inhibits elevation of Bax expression induced by serum deprivation. Defects in the survival pathway in the 5-HT2BR KO heart are not secondary to the cardiomyopathy, because defective cardiomyocyte survival can be reversed by transfection of 5-HT2BR cDNA and leukemia inhibiting factor (LIF) survival signaling is intact.

In addition, 5-HT did not induce hypertrophy of cultured cardiomyocytes (unpublished observation).

image: [http://circ.ahajournals.org/content/108/7/902.full](http://circ.ahajournals.org/content/108/7/902.full)

{ "lightbox\_close": "Close", "lightbox\_next": "Next", "lightbox\_previous": "Previous", "lightbox\_error": "The requested content cannot be loaded. Please try again later.", "lightbox\_start\_slideshow": "Start slideshow", "lightbox\_stop\_slideshow": "Stop slideshow", "lightbox\_full\_screen": "Full screen", "lightbox\_thumbnails": "Thumbnails", "lightbox\_download": "Download", "lightbox\_share": "Share", "lightbox\_zoom": "Zoom", "lightbox\_new\_window": "New window", "lightbox\_toggle\_sidebar": "Toggle sidebar" }

​

If you ask me, such results hint at **Serotonin being yet another "stress hormone", that is, a cellular signalling compound to tell cells to mobilise as much energy as possible in order to deal with an incoming stressor.** More mitochondria, more succinate use (fat oxidation), higher threshold autophagy signal so that mitochondria are forced to stick around longer, etc ... just more acute energy production capacity in general.

Like any such hormone, some is absolutely needed to deal with acute stress, and prolonged stress demands chronically elevated levels of such hormones, which chronically demand energy from the body, which eventually can't keep up. Then some people in the mainstream blame the lack of such hormones as the cause of a disease

I won't cite the studies here, but there are a couple of studies that show improvements to depression after supplementation of T3, which obviously is pro-metabolic, and can provide energy for the body in place of having to raise serotonin. The efficacy of such thyroid hormone treatment then depends on many other factors

In any case, we're back down to stress avoidance ....

....

# Thread: Modern domestic animal traits may have appeared far more recently than previously thought
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/modern-domestic-animal-traits-may-have-appeared-far-more-recently-than-previously-thought.10629/>
**Subforum:** The Cave
**Posts:** 1
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Gagnrad — 2014-04-23T07:50:34+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/modern-domestic-animal-traits-may-have-appeared-far-more-recently-than-previously-thought.10629/post-123139>

> … "Looking today at our pets and livestock including dogs, cows, sheep, and chickens, there's been an assumption that if a particular trait is found in a majority of breeds, that it must have made its first appearance at the dawn of domestication thousands of years ago," explained Dr Greger Larson from Durham University's Department of Archaeology. "That makes sense since it is reasonable to assume a correlation between ubiquity and time – the more common a trait is, the longer it's likely to have been kicking around.
>
> "Our research, however, suggests that even some of the most common traits, like yellow legs in chickens, were extremely rare as recently as 500 years ago, and that humans can drive selection rapidly, making it look like the trait has a deep history. In this way, domestic animals evolve more like fashion and culture than like wild animals. Just like taking a 'selfie' on your smart phone is both recent and universal, chickens across the world possess traits their recent ancestors didn't have." ...

[http://phys.org/wire-news/159607267...raits-may-have-appeared-far-more-recentl.html](http://phys.org/wire-news/159607267/modern-domestic-animal-traits-may-have-appeared-far-more-recentl.html)

# Thread: What are the conditions fueling modern human brain?
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-are-the-conditions-fueling-modern-human-brain.10630/>
**Subforum:** The New Monster Thread
**Posts:** 1
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Melanie Procter — 2014-04-23T12:10:21+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-are-the-conditions-fueling-modern-human-brain.10630/post-123157>

**In Brain Gut 4, Jack wrote the following are the conditions that built an optimum human brain. This is an incredible approach to thinking about how to go optimal. I do wonder what the conditions are that have been created to create such a great leap forward in our cognitive evolution. Considering so many people are devoid of the nutritional components below, what precursors exist to fuel our cognitive upgrade? Is there something in the air? Are we in a more engergetic part of the universe? How will our cognitive upgrade be explained hundreds of years from now?**

**What else do we need to build a human brain?**

1.  Iodine
2.  Iron
3.  A placenta that was capable of creating a massive fat furnace to fuel the perfect mix of 03 DHA to 06 EPA to make the neural framework.
4.  Lots of oxygen to complete the desaturation converting shorter chain to long chain PUFA’s: DHA requires 6 O2 molecules. This is why previous to the Cambrian explosion we have no ‘big brained’ animals in the fossil record. We had no oxygen to fuel a brain’s growth. When O2 showed up life exploded.
5.  Efficient mitochondria carry out the oxidative metabolism and lots of them in the brain.
6.  Phospholipids and the special chemistry of lipids in aqueous milieu (brain and spinal cord sit in a bag of CSF fluid)
7.  Cell membranes respond to diet, temperature and to pressure changes epigenetically. Since humans are homeothermic, our neural lipid epigenetic variables are diet and environment dependent. These features allow biochemistry to specifically affect the acyl groups on our lipid layers in the brain for optimal cell signaling. You might be a great biochemist or organic chemist but if you do not know neural biochemistry you might not be aware of just how much you do not know.
8.  Proteins receptors are inserted in these special lipids to signal cells chemically and electrically. This means lipids and proteins have to be multidimensional in configuration and must thermodynamically matched to the lipids in our membranes. DHA is that “**magic lipid**” that does all of this. You don’t find that information in fossils or in bones on the land.

# Thread: Anyone with a good beef liver recipe?
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/anyone-with-a-good-beef-liver-recipe.10632/>
**Subforum:** Epi-Paleo Recipes
**Posts:** 22
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Tanya — 2014-04-23T18:41:04+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/anyone-with-a-good-beef-liver-recipe.10632/post-123209>

Yesterday I finally bought grassed beef liver and now I am looking for a recipe to use it. Absolutely, 100% fully cooked!!!! Still not sure why I even bought it in the first place because I hate liver with passion. ....

Hope that this time it would be different somehow - I never thought that I would eat any seafood either, but now it is a different story....

### Andrea — 2014-04-23T18:48:25+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/anyone-with-a-good-beef-liver-recipe.10632/post-123211>

I like Heston Blumenthal's "Meat fruit": [http://cumbriafoodie.wordpress.com/2011/02/27/meat-fruit-making-a-heston-favourite-at-home/](http://cumbriafoodie.wordpress.com/2011/02/27/meat-fruit-making-a-heston-favourite-at-home/)
The recipe says chicken liver, but it works just as fine with any mammalian liver.

I suppose a more simple liver pâté could work too...

### Tanya — 2014-04-23T19:31:11+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/anyone-with-a-good-beef-liver-recipe.10632/post-123217>

Thanks, Rerun. It really looks good, but I don't think I am quite ready to incorporate all the ingredients listed there (eggs, vine, brandy), and without them, I am afraid it will not be the same....

### Andrea — 2014-04-23T20:50:33+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/anyone-with-a-good-beef-liver-recipe.10632/post-123222>

A more simple suggestion - if you're ok with dairy.

A plain, simple liver mousse/pâté
400g chicken/lamb/beef liver
butter
200 ml heavy cream
spices/herbs

Cut the liver in small pieces. Sear it in plenty of butter until it's done. Add salt & pepper and other herbs or spices (I used to add thyme and garlic when I did this).
Mix the liver in a mixer, together with the cream and some extra butter.
Pour the mixture into a sealable mold - it will be runny at this point, but will set in the fridge - and let set.

### raysblue — 2014-04-23T21:02:03+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/anyone-with-a-good-beef-liver-recipe.10632/post-123223>

This is a simple! From Nigel Slater: Warm some butter in a shallow pan. Lay 4 slices of liver in the pan. Fry for a couple of minutes on each side (toasty brown edges are fine , but inside should still be rose pink) . Remove to a warm plate and pour a small glass of malbec red wine into the pan. Bubble stir and scrape then reduce by half over a high heat. Stir in 4 tablespoons of course paleo apple and onion chutney. Slide the liver in and serve. Enjoy!

### Tanya — 2014-04-23T22:13:00+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/anyone-with-a-good-beef-liver-recipe.10632/post-123232>

> 400g chicken/lamb/beef liver
> butter
> 200 ml heavy cream
> spices/herbs
>
> Cut the liver in small pieces. Sear it in plenty of butter until it's done. Add salt & pepper and other herbs or spices (I used to add thyme and garlic when I did this).
> Mix the liver in a mixer, together with the cream and some extra butter.
> Pour the mixture into a sealable mold - it will be runny at this point, but will set in the fridge - and let set.

This is more doable, I think... If I substitute butter with ghee and heavy cream with coconut milk, I hope that it still might resemble the original version of it. Thanks again, Rerun

### nicld — 2014-04-24T03:58:01+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/anyone-with-a-good-beef-liver-recipe.10632/post-123263>

I make liver tacos. Take the liver and slice it thin, fry it in some oil and lots of chipotle and jalepenos. Makes it fairly tolerable and the DH LOVES it and he loves liver. I just use lettuce as the wraps.

I have also cooked it, chopped it up fairly fine and added it to my "earthy" soup. It's made with bone broth, cabbage, rutabaga, carrots and onions. Also add some ground beef to it.

Oh and my hats off to those who can eat it raw, tried it this weekend and not for me.

### Tanya — 2014-04-24T17:26:02+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/anyone-with-a-good-beef-liver-recipe.10632/post-123317>

> [nicld said:](/goto/post?id=123263)
>
> I make liver tacos. Take the liver and slice it thin, fry it in some oil and lots of chipotle and jalepenos. Makes it fairly tolerable and the DH LOVES it and he loves liver. I just use lettuce as the wraps.
>
> I have also cooked it, chopped it up fairly fine and added it to my "earthy" soup. It's made with bone broth, cabbage, rutabaga, carrots and onions. Also add some ground beef to it.
>
> Oh and my hats off to those who can eat it raw, tried it this weekend and not for me.

I like the soup idea - I can probably disguise just about anything in it...lol I still must avoid, however, chipotle and jalapeños - It must be frustrating to try to help someone with all these food limitations.... Thank you so much for the recipes, Nicole

### Marie — 2014-04-24T18:31:07+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/anyone-with-a-good-beef-liver-recipe.10632/post-123325>

Soak 1 lb of calf liver overnight in milk.

Saute covered on low heat:
4-6 cups (or more) of chopped white onions
1-2 TBS chopped garlic
A dash of salt
1 TBS cinnamon (Yes this sounds like a lot)

Rinse liver removing traces of milk
Chop the liver into 1 inch cubes

When the onions are glassy, add the liver cubes.
Cover and simmer on low heat for 5-7 mins, stirring occasionally.

The result should be "sweet" with a subtle liver flavor.

### Andrea — 2014-04-24T18:48:26+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/anyone-with-a-good-beef-liver-recipe.10632/post-123328>

> [Marie said:](/goto/post?id=123325)
>
> Soak 1 lb of calf liver overnight in milk.

I suppose the milk will remove some of the flavor. Good one!
Cinnamon with liver? Interesting! Will try that next week.

Tanya, in case you still would feel that you just can't eat the liver, there's one "last resort".
Run it through a food processor and blend it with minced meat (or run some meat through the food processor as well) - 50/50 ought to do. Then make hamburgers or meat balls of the mix, adding an egg if necessary to keep the mix together. You could add some onion and spices to the mince as well.

### Tanya — 2014-04-24T22:05:00+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/anyone-with-a-good-beef-liver-recipe.10632/post-123343>

Thank you for all the ideas, Rerun and Marie. At this point, I should be able to whip up something.....However, without using any dairy, nightshades or eggs, it will probably resemble more of a mad scientist project then a gourmet cooking

### Hemming — 2014-04-24T22:12:17+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/anyone-with-a-good-beef-liver-recipe.10632/post-123345>

Last weekend I made beef liver sous vide. It came out almost like a paté, you could have used it as a spread. I really liked but I'm sure some who like fried liver would like the consistency together with the intense liver taste.

### cantweight — 2014-04-24T22:27:18+0300

### Marie — 2014-04-25T01:14:20+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/anyone-with-a-good-beef-liver-recipe.10632/post-123368>

> [Rerun said:](/goto/post?id=123328)
>
> I suppose the milk will remove some of the flavor. Good one!
> Cinnamon with liver? Interesting! Will try that next week.

Yes, the milk soak seems to kill 80% of the offensive "liver" flavor. It does take quite a bit of rinsing to get rid of the milk, so be forewarned. The cinnamon gives it a Mediterranean spin.

This is the only way I've been able to eat liver.

### JMO — 2014-04-25T03:39:00+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/anyone-with-a-good-beef-liver-recipe.10632/post-123369>

Here's a good recipe for liver - [http://thehealthyfoodie.com/beef-liver-with-fig-bacon-and-caramelized-onion-compote/](http://thehealthyfoodie.com/beef-liver-with-fig-bacon-and-caramelized-onion-compote/). I use to gag when I first ate liver, now it's not bad. I use bubbies horeseradish in a little whipped cream (no sugar--just whip the cream) or sour cream if I cook just liver with onions--all you can taste is horseradish--which I love! I soak it in some lemon juice before cooking---don't cook too long or it gets rubbery.

Here's a tip for eating raw liver. I get some grassfed liver which comes frozen. I let it unthaw slightly until I can cut it in bite size pieces. Then I refreeze all the pieces then put them in a bowl in my freezer so I can take out one piece at a time. I let it slightly unthaw then I bite down on it a couple times and swallow followed by a big drink of water. Dont let it unthaw too much or you have that gross liver texture. Just a bite a day, by the end of the week I prob had an entire piece of raw liver.

### sooperb — 2014-04-28T21:16:21+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/anyone-with-a-good-beef-liver-recipe.10632/post-123744>

Liver works well with oranges. Slice the oranges and fry with onions, garlic etc. add the liver and a little oj and cook till it's as you like it. The original recipe calls for the orange to be dipped in sugar so either use a little or substitute using honey or similar or just leave out.

### Tanya — 2014-04-28T22:47:11+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/anyone-with-a-good-beef-liver-recipe.10632/post-123757>

> [sooperb said:](/goto/post?id=123744)
>
> Liver works well with oranges. Slice the oranges and fry with onions, garlic etc. add the liver and a little oj and cook till it's as you like it. The original recipe calls for the orange to be dipped in sugar so either use a little or substitute using honey or similar or just leave out.

Thanks Sue - I'll give it a try... I actually made a pate this morning - used lots of spices and garlic.... It came out pretty good, if I may say so myself. I will not hesitate to make it again...

# Thread: St. George's Day
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/st-georges-day.10633/>
**Subforum:** The Cave
**Posts:** 1
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Gagnrad — 2014-04-23T19:30:16+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/st-georges-day.10633/post-123216>

Here's something for St. George's Day.

St. George is the patron saint of England, and also, according to Wikipedia, of

> Georgia, Egypt, Bulgaria, Aragon, Catalonia, Romania, Ethiopia, Greece, India, Iraq, Lebanon, Lithuania, Palestine, Portugal, Serbia, Macedonia, Ukraine, Russia and Syria, as well as the cities of Genoa, Amersfoort, Beirut, Botoşani, Drobeta Turnu-Severin, Timişoara, Fakiha, Bteghrine, Cáceres, Ferrara, Freiburg im Breisgau, Kragujevac, Kumanovo, Ljubljana, Pérouges, Pomorie, Preston, Qormi, Rio de Janeiro, Lydda, Lviv, Barcelona, Moscow and Victoria, as well as of the Scout Movement and a wide range of professions, organizations and disease sufferers.

Wow! He's got his work cut out.

The day's not much celebrated over here, perhaps because English people tend to take the view that if you're enthusiastic about something it's proof that you're not really sincere. But … well … we all have dragons to kill, so maybe the day is worth celebrating.

Here's a icon of St. George for the Russians:

{ "lightbox\_close": "Close", "lightbox\_next": "Next", "lightbox\_previous": "Previous", "lightbox\_error": "The requested content cannot be loaded. Please try again later.", "lightbox\_start\_slideshow": "Start slideshow", "lightbox\_stop\_slideshow": "Stop slideshow", "lightbox\_full\_screen": "Full screen", "lightbox\_thumbnails": "Thumbnails", "lightbox\_download": "Download", "lightbox\_share": "Share", "lightbox\_zoom": "Zoom", "lightbox\_new\_window": "New window", "lightbox\_toggle\_sidebar": "Toggle sidebar" }

And how about some music, too? Here's William Blake's immortal poem _Jerusalem_ set to music by Sir Hubert Parry.

The subject of the poem is "Did Christ come to Glastonbury?" as the legend had it. The poem is also a kind of protest against the ugliness and inhumanity of early industrialisation in England -- "dark Satanic mills". (I believe Blake was thinking of an actual factory building in a part of London he knew.) Perhaps we might think of those currently building on Greenbelt land and applying to uproot Ancient Woodland or polluting the soil.

Here's the text:

> And did those feet in ancient time
> Walk upon England's mountains green?
> And was the holy Lamb of God
> On England's pleasant pastures seen?
>
> And did the Countenance Divine
> Shine forth upon our clouded hills?
> And was Jerusalem builded here
> Among these dark Satanic mills?
>
> Bring me my bow of burning gold:
> Bring me my arrows of desire:
> Bring me my spear: O clouds unfold!
> Bring me my chariot of fire.
>
> I will not cease from mental fight,
> Nor shall my sword sleep in my hand
> Till we have built Jerusalem
> In England's green and pleasant land.

[http://rpo.library.utoronto.ca/poems/milton-and-did-those-feet-ancient-time](http://rpo.library.utoronto.ca/poems/milton-and-did-those-feet-ancient-time)

# Thread: The real Mediterranean Diet

**Subforum:** The Cave
**Posts:** 6
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Gagnrad — 2014-04-23T20:50:20+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-real-mediterranean-diet.10634/post-123221>

The Caveman Doctor reports on what (the very slim and active) people in Southern Italy are _actually_ currently eating.

> The amount of bread was ... minimal compared to what I expected. ...
>
> Finally, pasta, which I figured would be flowing out of the faucets, was consumed in much smaller amounts than I ever expected. ...
>
> Often there was a menu page or two devoted to meat, including beef, lamb, chicken, and a rare pork dish, with another page or two of fish, and a page or two of appetizers, which consisted of carpaccio, tuna, prosciutto and bufala mozzarella (ham and buffalo mozzarella). ...
>
> ... the food that dominated the menus and plates of most local restaurants along the coast was the calamari and octopus...

[http://www.myhealthwire.com/news/diet-nutrition/758](http://www.myhealthwire.com/news/diet-nutrition/758)

### sooperb — 2014-04-26T21:37:44+0300

# Thread: Fermented Cod Liver Oil Sources
