# Thread: Wired DSL Modems and Accessories
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/wired-dsl-modems-and-accessories.10652/>
**Subforum:** The EMF Rx
**Posts:** 6
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Chrysanthemama — 2014-04-25T18:24:44+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/wired-dsl-modems-and-accessories.10652/post-123449>

Greetings Everyone!
I am looking for some advice that hopefully you all have.
I recently switched ISPs, and now I have DSL internet.
Of course the company only provided a wireless modem.

I was looking at this:
[http://www.amazon.com/gp/product/B0061308MA/ref=gno\_cart\_title\_1?ie=UTF8&psc=1&smid=A2L77EE7U53NWQ](http://www.amazon.com/gp/product/B0061308MA/ref=gno_cart_title_1?ie=UTF8&psc=1&smid=A2L77EE7U53NWQ)

And I'm wondering if others have used this system, and what I would need. I am assuming a wired DSL modem. A wired router, and then this?

Any advice is appreciated!

### RobH — 2014-04-26T03:04:32+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/wired-dsl-modems-and-accessories.10652/post-123471>

I had ethernet cable runs in my home done. It was easy due to crawl space (ugh, not sitting on earth) My only concern with that is are you creating more "dirty electricity) going to every outlet in your house? Don't know.

RobH

### Jonathin — 2014-04-26T05:29:30+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/wired-dsl-modems-and-accessories.10652/post-123482>

Ethernet cables have twisted conductors that limit radiation. If you use your house wiring with a powerline adapter network you don't have that protection plus your whole electrical power wiring has essentially dirty electricity. I would stick with a basic inexpensive router with cat 5e or 6 cable. Internet connect speeds are slow compared to computer and router capabilities so nothing fancy or expensive is necessary.

### ssj3 — 2014-04-26T12:11:08+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/wired-dsl-modems-and-accessories.10652/post-123494>

If I am understanding your question correctly... most routers have additional network ports so you can just run additional Ethernet cables as needed to different areas. You could also consider a router that has a button to enable WIFI when you need it. I use a Netgear N150 which has this feature and works well.

[http://www.amazon.com/NetGear-DGN10...\_sbs\_pc\_6?ie=UTF8&refRID=16X5ZYSM031H8J0C7TN9](http://www.amazon.com/NetGear-DGN1000-Wireless-4-Port-Protected/dp/B002WOXYHS/ref=pd_sim_sbs_pc_6?ie=UTF8&refRID=16X5ZYSM031H8J0C7TN9)

### Shijin13 — 2014-04-26T18:52:30+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/wired-dsl-modems-and-accessories.10652/post-123521>

We have a VPN Router (required for work stuff) and a basic modem that doesn't emit wifi. specifically went out and bought that. I'm currently using a Henge docking station for my old macbook Pro. When I get my next MBP I plan on using a docking station for that, and plug the current MBP with the HengeDock by the TV, and run ethernet to that system and hook it direct up to the TV so we can officially ditch cable - minus internet....

### JMO — 2014-04-27T04:46:59+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/wired-dsl-modems-and-accessories.10652/post-123552>

I have DSL and an actiontec wireless modem. There is not a hardwired switch to turn wireless off but you can do it thru the software by going to the modem ip address 192.168.0.1 and change the configuration to turn wireless off.

### JackFrake — 2014-04-25T19:26:22+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ppp-in-the-early-ocean.10653/post-123453>

'Metabolism may have started in our early oceans':

[http://phys.org/news/2014-04-metabolism-early-oceans-life.html](http://phys.org/news/2014-04-metabolism-early-oceans-life.html)

_The findings suggests that metabolism predates the origin of life and evolved through the chemical conditions that prevailed in the worlds earliest oceans.

"Our results show that reaction sequences that resemble two essential reaction cascades of metabolism, glycolysis and the pentose-phosphate pathways, could have occurred spontaneously in the earth's ancient oceans," says Dr. Markus Ralser at the Department of Biochemistry at the University of Cambridge and the National Institute for Medical Research, who led the study.
_

# Thread: african origin of modern mankind  and cold thermogenesis
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/african-origin-of-modern-mankind-and-cold-thermogenesis.10654/>
**Subforum:** The Cave
**Posts:** 7
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### callag — 2014-04-25T20:02:50+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/african-origin-of-modern-mankind-and-cold-thermogenesis.10654/post-123454>

Hi

sorry if this topic was already covered but can anyone explain how dr Kruse is reconciling cold thermogenesis and the fact that as a species we all descend by Africans so we all need and love hot ?

[http://www.reuters.com/article/2007/07/18/us-evolution-skulls-idUSL1885586220070718](http://www.reuters.com/article/2007/07/18/us-evolution-skulls-idUSL1885586220070718)

[http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Recent\_African\_origin\_of\_modern\_humans](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Recent_African_origin_of_modern_humans)

It looks like we should be doing saunas instead of cold bath

Many thanks

### NeilBB — 2014-04-25T21:07:55+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/african-origin-of-modern-mankind-and-cold-thermogenesis.10654/post-123459>

> [callag said:](/goto/post?id=123454)
>
> Hi
>
> sorry if this topic was already covered but can anyone explain how dr Kruse is reconciling cold thermogenesis and the fact that as a species we all descend by Africans so we all need and love hot ?
>
> [http://www.reuters.com/article/2007/07/18/us-evolution-skulls-idUSL1885586220070718](http://www.reuters.com/article/2007/07/18/us-evolution-skulls-idUSL1885586220070718)
>
> [http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Recent\_African\_origin\_of\_modern\_humans](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Recent_African_origin_of_modern_humans)
>
> It looks like we should be doing saunas instead of cold bath
>
> Many thanks

Complicated issue but briefly off top of my head, I'd say this...

There is an excess of artificial warmth in modern life due to clothes, heaters, and indoor living, and therefore an unnatural relative absence of intense cold exposure, especially in winter. Lack of a cold winter is a significant modern chronobiological mismatch for most and a major contributor to chronic disease including diabetes, obesity, autoimmune conditions. Therefore year round CT is likely beneficial for many to counteract this modern problem. Even if not in cold climate, the early humans in Africa also still experienced a much more normal native seasonal variation and were not subject to the specific inflammatory effects of modern life that we are trying to counteract now.

Cold has many pro-life effects. Read the CT series for more details. It is anti-inflammatory and can cause fat cell apoptosis among other things. Most fundamentally though, according to physics, cold is antientropic, which means essentially that it slows down random molecular motion, e.g., degeneration, inflammation, and aging. It can also enhance the speed of biological semiconduction as it slows randomness.

There is also a place for targeted heat, specifically infrared electromagnetic radiation, which can energize the body's water stores by separating its charges into positive and negative components, creating a biological battery. (See Pollack, 4th Phase of Water) To do this well though, you need to maintain optimal hydration status at all times, which is difficult today. When well hydrated, IR heat from sunlight, fire, or infrared saunas are also very beneficial. If dehydrated, like most today, heat may make you feel good but won't really help health or longevity much.

### NeilBB — 2014-04-25T21:51:35+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/african-origin-of-modern-mankind-and-cold-thermogenesis.10654/post-123460>

I would also add this...

Benefits of cold are not unique to humans, are common to all life, and especially eutherian mammals (hibernation). (See factor X story.) When you study evolution, look for these benefits after earth's extinction events and at the origin of life. Or in the polar oceans.

So, from an evolutionary perspective, it makes much more sense to examine the ecological factors present at the origin of man from a perspective of what is _unique_ to man, i. e., his greatly enhanced central nervous system. This leads to the uniqueness of DHA and the aquatic food chain in human brain development, which did come out of Africa! _This_ is what was most important advancement specifically of _human development_. Cold-adaptation for us, by contrast, is a dormant genetic program that we inherited from prehuman ancestors, that we are now able to reactivate to help us normalize the environmental mismatches of modern life.

### callag — 2014-04-25T22:59:24+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/african-origin-of-modern-mankind-and-cold-thermogenesis.10654/post-123465>

thanks it is starts making sense..

### NeilBB — 2014-04-25T23:48:17+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/african-origin-of-modern-mankind-and-cold-thermogenesis.10654/post-123468>

Also remember that in Africa at the dawn of humanity there was a particularly strong magnetic field present, partly due to the local intersection of three tectonic plates, but today we have a declining magnetic field on earth everywhere, and then consider that cold enhances the strength of diamagnetic and ferromagnetic materials. Which is probably another reason to keep the bedroom as cold as possible if you've got a Magnetico, I'd bet...

### Dali Dula — 2014-04-26T01:41:03+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/african-origin-of-modern-mankind-and-cold-thermogenesis.10654/post-123470>

Sweet synopsis, NeilBB.

### ssj3 — 2014-04-26T11:57:59+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/african-origin-of-modern-mankind-and-cold-thermogenesis.10654/post-123491>

# Thread: I know my husband loves me….
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/i-know-my-husband-loves-me%E2%80%A6.10656/>
**Subforum:** The EMF Rx
**Posts:** 15
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### SeaHorse — 2014-04-26T04:12:33+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/i-know-my-husband-loves-me%E2%80%A6.10656/post-123474>

He never blinked an eye as we went off this afternoon to look at used cars, me with my trusty tri-meter clutched in my hand. It was a very fun afternoon and by the end of the day the sale manager was getting me to test some of the newer vehicles to see what the differences were. These guys already knew my husband and I was just tickled to see how the whole question of emf and no "bells and whistles" was brought up. There was no eye rolling or condescension and one of the owners was actually saying that he didn't think all these electronics were good for people either. Now, I know they saw us as potential customers, but their interest went beyond the professional salesman behaviour. Anyway, it looks like I'll be trading in my Subaru for an older Honda…the readings were substantially lower and it had no satellite or bluetooth and computer stuff. YAY, maybe I'll enjoy driving again.

### cantweight — 2014-04-26T04:35:13+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/i-know-my-husband-loves-me%E2%80%A6.10656/post-123476>

Its an amazing feeling to be respected like that. The hoops I have made my hubby jump through with diet and lifestyle change....man....I dont know how he put up with it. The crazy is part is along he just wanted steak and veggies....should have followed his instincts instead of mine on that one. The poor man, I made him commit to veganism for a month and few years back....if he didn't divorce me over that we are golden!

He has bent over backwards to provide us with everything we could need to implement Jacks protocols simply because I believe in them. R/O faucet, cold tub and ice machine, sauna, magnetico, supplements, emf meters....you name it....monies we could have spent on so many other things we have gone without. Is a blessing indeed to have such a mate...although they are quite lucky themselves

For the record...10 yr old Suburbans test really well  and give the kids the opportunity to learn about cassette tape players!

### SeaHorse — 2014-04-26T04:58:44+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/i-know-my-husband-loves-me%E2%80%A6.10656/post-123478>

It is a great feeling to be in it together…I was just talking about HBOT with my husband tonight….and he asked me why HE wasn't doing it too! So far he's not jumped in the pond with me, but he's grounding and eating epi-paleo and enjoying the magnetico as much as I do…..

Thanks for the Suburban tip…have you see this site? [http://www.ahappyhabitat.com/cars.html](http://www.ahappyhabitat.com/cars.html)
Wish I still had my cassettes, most of them didn't make it in our last move.

### cantweight — 2014-04-26T17:14:58+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/i-know-my-husband-loves-me%E2%80%A6.10656/post-123513>

Its funny about the cars...I would love to know where a 2007 Honda Odyssey tests....I drove one for a year and then we sold it....I joked with DH that every time I started it it felt like it was sucking out my soul...lol.....hmmm emf maybe? Had ALL the bells and whistles!

I have a handful of cassettes, but no one really wants to listen to Michael Jackson or Bonnie Tyler! lol

### Shijin13 — 2014-04-26T18:46:14+0300

Downside is that after I'd swallowed a few and marvelled at how good (and expensive) they were I checked the ingredients. Doh, they also have coconut flower nectar in them. Here's a link to anyone lucky enough to live in the UK - [http://www.rawecstasy.co.uk/shop/activated-almonds-raw-chocolate/](http://www.rawecstasy.co.uk/shop/activated-almonds-raw-chocolate/)

# Thread: Magnetico Sleep Pads Effect on Measured Parameters
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/magnetico-sleep-pads-effect-on-measured-parameters.10662/>
**Subforum:** The EMF Rx
**Posts:** 7
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### persistence — 2014-04-26T22:34:46+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/magnetico-sleep-pads-effect-on-measured-parameters.10662/post-123541>

I'm intrigued by the Magnetico sleep pad and the page that summarizes some of the studies:

[https://magneticosleep.com/about-magnetism/scientific-validation/](https://magneticosleep.com/about-magnetism/scientific-validation/)

There is the dramatic photo at the top of red blood cell spacing, which they claim is attributable to loss of charge on blood cells. Has anyone done studies of this specific effect and quantified the result on any measurable parameter? For example, you could measure VO2 at peak exertion before and after using the pad. You could measure the amount of work performed at a specific level of cardiac exertion (e.g., 60% of max).

The study claims made on Magnetico's page are subjective and weak, and they don't really exploit the suggestion of that really dramatic photo at the top. The suggestion is that your red blood cells will be able to obtain more oxygen and contribute more energy. The photo is so dramatic that the effect should be measurable and quantifiable.

I'm really interested in seeing how good the science is backing up those photos. What's out there of note?

### persistence — 2014-04-26T22:55:22+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/magnetico-sleep-pads-effect-on-measured-parameters.10662/post-123542>

Something else I am confused about is what is the difference in value between grounding and being under a strong magnetic field?

What's strange is that the EMF blogs spend so much time telling you to avoid magnetic fields around your ears / head from use of cell phones and bluetooth headsets, yet the Magnetico pad is exposing your entire body to such a field.

# Thread: Persecution of the Bushmen
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/persecution-of-the-bushmen.10665/>
**Subforum:** The Cave
**Posts:** 1
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Gagnrad — 2014-04-27T13:50:01+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/persecution-of-the-bushmen.10665/post-123565>

This sorry tale of the attempted extermination of one of the last hunter-gatherer peoples continues.

> ... that haunting tragedy followed by this column since 1996: the ruthless drive of the Botswanan government to expel the last survivors of the original human inhabitants of southern Africa from the Central Kalahari Game Reserve (CKGR) – the area where the Bushmen’s right to live undisturbed in perpetuity was established by Britain in colonial days and guaranteed by Botswana’s constitution when it won independence in 1966.
>
> In 2006, ... most of the Bushmen had already been forced out of their ancestral lands to live in a degrading settlement they called “the place of death” ...
>
> What made the story even more disturbing was that **this attempt to wipe out their traditional way of life was defended by the European Union and by successive British governments**, eager to keep in with a country that, thanks to its **diamond reserves** (including those discovered in the CKGR), is, per capita, the richest in Africa.
>
> In 2008, Botswana’s new half-English president, Ian Khama, who likes to pose on the international stage as a “conservationist”, nevertheless made it a personal crusade to continue the **persecution of the Bushmen, denying them their right to access water or hunt for food**. Last summer, just before Mr Bennett was due to make another legal bid to allow the Bushmen access to water, he was barred from entering the country ...
>
> **Britain ... has not uttered a squeak of protest** against this crushing of the rights of \[a small\] and ... defenceless minority – rights that we ourselves, in more honourable days, wrote into the law of their land.

[http://www.telegraph.co.uk/news/wor...-turned-our-back-on-the-Kalahari-Bushmen.html](http://www.telegraph.co.uk/news/worldnews/africaandindianocean/botswana/10789457/Weve-turned-our-back-on-the-Kalahari-Bushmen.html)

See also:

[http://www.amazon.co.uk/The-Bushmen-Southern-Africa-Slaughter/dp/0701169060/](http://www.amazon.co.uk/The-Bushmen-Southern-Africa-Slaughter/dp/0701169060/)

# Thread: Craniofacial Development and other things
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/craniofacial-development-and-other-things.10667/>
**Subforum:** The Cave
**Posts:** 1
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Gagnrad — 2014-04-27T17:11:32+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/craniofacial-development-and-other-things.10667/post-123584>

New post from Sally Fallon at the WAPF:

> The narrowing of the face that occurs after a population has abandoned its native diet and embraced Western processed food continues to perplex the medical and dental establishments, which just can’t seem to make the connection between food and physical development. In Dr. Price’s day, scientists blamed lack of facial development on “race mixing,” a premise that Dr. Price soundly denounced. Today orthodontists blame crooked teeth on everything from genetics to thumb sucking to soft foods—that is, everything but the obvious decline in nutrient density in modern diets. ...

Ain't that the truth.

Well worth reading -- there's a round-up of a few pieces of news in this post, including:

> A growing number of farmers are abandoning genetically modified seeds, but it’s not because they are ideologically opposed to the industry. They are switching back to non- GMO crops because they are more productive and profitable (_Modern Farmer_, January 2014).
>
> … the GMO seeds are not working as well as they did originally. “Five years ago the \[GMO seeds\] worked,” said farmer Christ Huegerich, who along with his father planted GMO seeds. “I didn’t have corn rootworm because of the Bt gene, and I used less pesticide. Now, the worms are adjusting, and the weeds are resistant. Mother Nature adapts.”

This reminds me of the comment in _Jurassic Park_: "Life will find a way."

[http://www.westonaprice.org/caustic-commentary/caustic-commentary-spring-2014](http://www.westonaprice.org/caustic-commentary/caustic-commentary-spring-2014)

# Thread: WHAT IS THE EVOLUTIONARY MECHANISM?
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-is-the-evolutionary-mechanism.10669/>
**Subforum:** Educating Doctors
**Posts:** 4
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Jack Kruse — 2014-04-28T00:20:07+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-is-the-evolutionary-mechanism.10669/post-123608>

99.9999999999% will say natural selection and conditions of existence. I won't. In OSF 3 coming up next you will see the quantum mechanism.............it underpins everything.

And here is proof I might be onto something ...................

[http://www.newscientist.com/article/dn25471-spark-of-life-metabolism-appears-in-lab-without-cells.html?cmpid=RSS|NSNS|2012-GLOBAL|online-news&fb\_source=message#.U11zpSg0zcb](http://www.newscientist.com/article/dn25471-spark-of-life-metabolism-appears-in-lab-without-cells.html?cmpid=RSS%7CNSNS%7C2012-GLOBAL%7Conline-news&fb_source=message#.U11zpSg0zcb)

### Jack Kruse — 2014-04-28T00:36:29+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-is-the-evolutionary-mechanism.10669/post-123609>

-   19:42 25 April 2014 by [**Linda Geddes**](http://www.newscientist.com/search?rbauthors=Linda+Geddes)
-   For similar stories, visit the [**Evolution**](http://www.newscientist.com/topic/evolution) and [**Human Evolution**](http://www.newscientist.com/topic/human-evolution) Topic Guides

Metabolic processes that underpin life on Earth have arisen spontaneously outside of cells. The serendipitous finding that metabolism – the cascade of reactions in all cells that provides them with the raw materials they need to survive – can happen in such simple conditions provides fresh insights into how the first life formed. It also suggests that the complex processes needed for life may have surprisingly humble origins.

"People have said that these pathways look so complex they couldn't form by environmental chemistry alone," says [Markus Ralser](http://www.bioc.cam.ac.uk/people/uto/ralser) at the University of Cambridge who supervised the research.

But his findings suggest that many of these reactions could have occurred spontaneously in Earth's early oceans, catalysed by metal ions rather than the enzymes that drive them in cells today.

The origin of metabolism is a major gap in our understanding of the[emergence of life](http://www.newscientist.com/article/mg13217944.500-the-first-gene-on-earth.html). "If you look at many different organisms from around the world, this network of reactions always looks very similar, suggesting that it must have come into place very early on in evolution, but no one knew precisely when or how," says Ralser.

**Happy accident**
One theory is that [RNA was the first building block of life](http://www.newscientist.com/article/mg21128251.300-first-life-the-search-for-the-first-replicator.html) because it helps to produce the enzymes that could catalyse complex sequences of reactions. Another possibility is that metabolism came first; perhaps even generating the molecules needed to make RNA, and that cells later incorporated these processes – but there was little evidence to support this.

"This is the first experiment showing that it is possible to create metabolic networks in the absence of RNA," Ralser says.

Remarkably, the discovery was an accident, stumbled on during routine quality control testing of the medium used to culture cells at Ralser's laboratory. As a shortcut, one of his students decided to run unused media through a mass spectrometer, which spotted a signal for pyruvate – an end product of a metabolic pathway called glycolysis.

To test whether the same processes could have helped spark life on Earth, they approached colleagues in the Earth sciences department who had been working on reconstructing the chemistry of the Archean Ocean, which covered the planet almost 4 billion years ago. This was an oxygen-free world, predating photosynthesis, when the waters were rich in iron, as well as other metals and phosphate. All these substances could potentially facilitate chemical reactions like the ones seen in modern cells.

**Metabolic backbone**
Ralser's team took early ocean solutions and added substances known to be starting points for modern metabolic pathways, before heating the samples to between 50˚C and 70˚C – the sort of temperatures you might have found near a [hydrothermal vent](http://www.newscientist.com/article/mg20427306.200-was-our-oldest-ancestor-a-protonpowered-rock.html) { "lightbox\_close": "Close", "lightbox\_next": "Next", "lightbox\_previous": "Previous", "lightbox\_error": "The requested content cannot be loaded. Please try again later.", "lightbox\_start\_slideshow": "Start slideshow", "lightbox\_stop\_slideshow": "Stop slideshow", "lightbox\_full\_screen": "Full screen", "lightbox\_thumbnails": "Thumbnails", "lightbox\_download": "Download", "lightbox\_share": "Share", "lightbox\_zoom": "Zoom", "lightbox\_new\_window": "New window", "lightbox\_toggle\_sidebar": "Toggle sidebar" }

– for 5 hours. Ralser then analysed the solutions to see what molecules were present.

"In the beginning we had hoped to find one reaction or two maybe, but the results were amazing," says Ralser. "We could reconstruct two metabolic pathways almost entirely."

The pathways they detected were glycolysis and the pentose phosphate pathway, "reactions that form the core metabolic backbone of every living cell," Ralser adds. Together these pathways produce some of the most important materials in modern cells, including ATP – the molecule cells use to drive their machinery, the sugars that form DNA and RNA, and the molecules needed to make fats and proteins.

If these metabolic pathways were occurring in the early oceans, then the first cells could have enveloped them as they developed membranes.

In all, 29 metabolism-like chemical reactions were spotted, seemingly catalysed by iron and other metals that would have been found in early ocean sediments. The metabolic pathways aren't identical to modern ones; some of the chemicals made by intermediate steps weren't detected. However, "if you compare them side by side it is the same structure and many of the same molecules are formed," Ralser says. These pathways could have been refined and improved once enzymes evolved within cells.

**Reversible reaction**
Detecting the metabolite ribose 5-phosphate is particularly noteworthy, Ralser says. This is because it is a precursor to RNA, which encodes information, catalyses chemical reactions and most importantly of all, can replicate.

"I think this paper has really interesting connotations for the origins of life," says [Matthew Powner](http://www.ucl.ac.uk/chemistry/staff/academic_pages/matthew_powner) at University College London. It hints at how more complex enzymes could have evolved, he says, because substances that made these early processes more efficient would have been selected for.

There is one big problem, however. "For origins of life, it is important to understand where the source molecules come from," Powner says. No one has yet shown that such substances could form spontaneously in the early oceans.

A related issue is that the reactions observed so far only go in one direction; from complex sugars to simpler molecules like pyruvate. "Given the data, one might well conclude that any organics in the ocean would have been totally degraded, rather than forming the basis of modern metabolism," says[Jack Szostak](http://molbio.mgh.harvard.edu/szostakweb/), who studies the origin of life at Harvard. "I would conclude that metabolism had to evolve, within cells, one reaction and one catalyst at a time."

But Ralser disagrees. In his opinion, whether the reaction is catalysed by an enzyme or by a molecule in the Archean Ocean leads to the same result; "every chemical reaction is in principle reversible, whether an enzyme or a simple molecule is the catalyst," he says.

# Thread: Evanescent Light Waves A new twist in the properties of light
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/evanescent-light-waves-a-new-twist-in-the-properties-of-light.10672/>
**Subforum:** The Cave
**Posts:** 1
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Gracelind — 2014-04-28T11:07:19+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/evanescent-light-waves-a-new-twist-in-the-properties-of-light.10672/post-123633>

[http://phys.org/news/2014-04-properties.html#ajTabs](http://phys.org/news/2014-04-properties.html#ajTabs)

**A new twist in the properties of light**

Light has some well-established dynamical properties that have defined our understanding of electromagnetic radiation for over a century. Two of the most fundamental of these properties are that photons of light carry momentum in the direction of propagation, and a 'spin' about the propagation axis defined by the electromagnetic wave's circular polarization. These properties play critical roles in a range of everyday phenomena and experimental interactions between light and matter.

Konstantin Bliokh from the RIKEN Interdisciplinary Theoretical Science Research Group (iTHES) and Aleksandr Bekshaev and Franco Nori from the RIKEN Center for Emergent Matter Science have now made the remarkable discovery that a particular type of [light](http://phys.org/tags/light/) known as evanescent waves possesses unexpected dynamical properties that are in sharp contrast with previous knowledge about light and photons.

# Thread: Phases of matter
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/phases-of-matter.10673/>
**Subforum:** The Cave
**Posts:** 1
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Gagnrad — 2014-04-28T13:27:13+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/phases-of-matter.10673/post-123646>

> Melvyn Bragg and his guests discuss the science of matter and the states in which it can exist. Most people are familiar with the idea that a substance like water can exist in solid, liquid and gaseous forms. But as much as 99% of the \[visible\] matter in the universe is now believed to exist in a fourth state, plasma. Today scientists recognise a number of other exotic states or phases, such as glasses, gels and liquid crystals - many of them with useful properties that can be exploited.
>
> With:
> Andrea Sella
> Professor of Chemistry at University College London
>
> Athene Donald
> Professor of Experimental Physics at the University of Cambridge
>
> Justin Wark
> Professor of Physics and Fellow of Trinity College at the University of Oxford

[http://www.bbc.co.uk/programmes/b03zdbr4](http://www.bbc.co.uk/programmes/b03zdbr4)

> Thus, as we sleep on our coil-spring mattresses, we are in effect sleeping on an antenna that amplifies the intensity of the broadcast FM/TV radiation. Asleep on these antennas, our bodies are exposed to the amplified electromagnetic radiation for a third of our life spans. As we slumber on a metal coil-spring mattress, a wave of electromagnetic radiation envelops our bodies so that the maximum strength of the field develops 75 centimeters above the mattress in the middle of our bodies. When sleeping on the right side, the body’s left side will thereby be exposed to field strength about twice as strong as what the right side absorbs.

### yewwei.tan — 2014-04-29T11:30:50+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/grounding.10678/post-123841>

My guess is that yes, you should move.

Moving around means collagen deformation, and every time collagen is deformed, an electric charge is generated. It is these piezoelectric effects that generate your redox potential.

I would then think that movement atop the earth would increase the electric potential between your cells and the earth, and allow you to steal more electrons as a result.

When ancient man slept he made contact with the earth too, so it should work either way?

### Sue-UK — 2014-04-29T14:40:10+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/grounding.10678/post-123857>

My FIL being ill means I have very limited time, so I wanted more for my buck with being barefoot, and I’m surprised at how much better I feel with even a couple of minutes of intentional grounding whilst walking, that I just don’t get from standing in one spot for the same amount of time.

Our ancestors had access to electrons constantly, so although I think passively collecting electrons by standing still works, I need to be a bit more active about hydration, collecting electrons and reaping all the potential physical benefits of being barefoot , because I'm not living in the same environment, and spend very little time in direct contact with the earth.

So I am walking and also adding intention ….. I am now paying attention to the sensations through my feet as I move between drier grass bathed in sunlight, and wetter, muddier ground that is still in the shade… Sometimes I alternate that with following my breath, sometimes I stand still and just look and listen, or focus intention on my feet to hoover up electrons …. I’m finding it good stress management, the more stressed I get the more I need it.

I think Jack is referring to the same thing here, meaning that the potential gradient is setup in such a way that electrons will flow from the earth, through us, and right into the environment without being able to store them.
>
> I would imagine that this would be the case in a horrible non-native EMF environment regardless of how good your redox potential is.

Finally, I'm getting the picture of "lost to the environment"....... can now envision me as a electrical conduit with an electric streak rising from my head! Hell's bells, am now imagining everyone in cities with Zorro signs atop their heads..........

### Jude — 2014-05-01T03:19:04+0300

# Thread: Weston Price, Gerald Cox and Fluoridation
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/weston-price-gerald-cox-and-fluoridation.10679/>
**Subforum:** The Cave
**Posts:** 2
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Gagnrad — 2014-04-29T08:54:22+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/weston-price-gerald-cox-and-fluoridation.10679/post-123833>

The WAPF has been on a roll recently. This is the third interesting blog they've posted in just a few days.

Cox I hadn't heard of. Here's the whole sorry saga of fluoridation. Cox it seems, spoke up for sugar when employed by the sugar industry and for aluminium when employed by the Mellon Institute, which had connections with Alcoa Aluminum. He was also an enthusiast for fluoride, which was a waste product for Alcoa Aluminum. Cox ended his professional career working for Corn Products Refining Company, at which time he claimed that corn oil was a cure for just about everything.

Weston Price's genius aside, the important difference between the men seems to be that Weston Price served others and Gerald Cox served himself.

A final odd note:

> Cox’s professional papers have not been preserved in any archive. In a 1969 letter he says that he has to “get rid of the many papers” from his office.

[http://www.westonaprice.org/notes-from-yesteryear/nutrition-fluoridation-and-dental-health](http://www.westonaprice.org/notes-from-yesteryear/nutrition-fluoridation-and-dental-health)

Keep scrolling -- there's a list of references, and then the article restarts again.

### sooperb — 2014-04-30T20:29:20+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/weston-price-gerald-cox-and-fluoridation.10679/post-124112>

So many of those cause junkies about. Whoever pays gets their support! Bit like politicians, Tony Blair hasn't looked back since he left politics. How to make a fortune, ruin a country's economy and then everyone wants to hear how you did it and will pay handsomely for the privilege.

### kyrakitty — 2014-04-29T19:16:57+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/pork-rinds.10687/post-123908>

Looking for a quick response. I searched the forum but didn't find much. I have a client to see today who wants to know if pork rinds made by US Wellness meats. They are all about high quality meats, organic, pastured raised in the best possible conditions.

Rinds are fried in good pork lard. So are these safe to eat or do the high temps used to fry the rinds cause them to be basically poison to our system

This is what they say on their website about them

Pork Rinds - everybody's favorite fair food, and we have shelf stable pork rinds in stock. Sourced from our Missouri Pork and fried in our very own pork lard, these make a tasty treat that your whole family will love.

[http://www.grasslandbeef.com/Detail.bok?no=1261](http://www.grasslandbeef.com/Detail.bok?no=1261)
​

### Jack Kruse — 2014-04-29T19:55:10+0300

great sub for tortilla chips when guacamole and salsa are in the vicinity.

### BJK77 — 2014-04-29T20:06:10+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/new-orleans.10688/post-123916>

Looks like I may have an opportunity to go to New Orleans in mid May. I've been in such a bad place lately it would probably do me really good to get away. Only problem is that travel isn't very easy these days! I'm sure there will be an abundance of fresh seafood, so food isn't the problem, but lodging may be.

I need to be sure where I stay hasn't had major water damage and of course I want limited EMF, so hotels are out of the question. I'm going to look into renting a house.

Any suggestions on where to look? I know nothing about the area and would like to visit the closest beach while there. In fact, if it isn't too far, I'd rather stay at a beach and drive to NO when necessary.

### Jack Kruse — 2014-04-29T21:26:35+0300

# Thread: Bionaut's Journal
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/bionauts-journal.10690/>
**Subforum:** My Optimal Journal
**Posts:** 61
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### bionaut — 2014-04-30T11:31:34+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/bionauts-journal.10690/post-124020>

Hi all.
Just looking for a place to post my progress for my n=1. I am 15 days into a consistent ketotic diet and have gone from 230 to 223 lbs. My labs indicate that I need to stick to this for a while to repair my mitochondria. Currently I sleep on a magnetico and biomat and get an hr of sun and grounding. I work at night but am switching to days soon and am looking forward to getting leptin sensitive again. Thanks to Jeremy's blog I have picked up bouldering once a week in great falls va. If you live in nova you should get out there, it is a little oasis.

### nonchalant — 2014-04-30T15:54:49+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/bionauts-journal.10690/post-124051>

### bionaut — 2014-04-30T16:08:17+0300

> [cantweight said:](/goto/post?id=124040)
>
> Very cool. I am in NoVa...where is this?

This is north by the Potomac. Here is a link [http://www.nps.gov/grfa/index.htm](http://www.nps.gov/grfa/index.htm)

You have to hop a short wood fence to climb boulders. Nobody has ever stopped us. I think it is just to protect kids.

### cantweight — 2014-04-30T16:18:28+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/bionauts-journal.10690/post-124057>

Oh ok...so beautiful up there. My husband used to rock climb up that way...Carderock. Something I always wanted to do.

### bionaut — 2014-06-16T22:09:12+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/bionauts-journal.10690/post-131045>

For the last two years I found promise in CT and have used it to make me feel great. It never really fixed anything permanently and I think that is because I wasn't trying hard enough for a change. I didn't ever do a leptin reset because it was too inconvenient. Little did I know how much of a disservice I was doing. Moving to a night shift I literally broke my circadian biology. Over the last year I have gained about 15lbs, and then my sleep started going. I had never understood what it was like to lose the ability to sleep. There is almost a permanent haze that sits on your mind. My dependency on coffee has grown and carbohydrates were a requirement. Without consistent CT to encourage Keto I have been using hacks here and there to keep up my energy.
I knew that this was a temporary phase and have really spent the last year listening, learning and studying my ass off. I am ready for a change. Fathers day, I had a great day with my 5 month old twins, I started my leptin reset and practically every rx I know. I am back on days now, time to right the ship. 237lb 36%

[http://jackkruse.com/the-dopamine-rx-good-choices-or-bad/](http://jackkruse.com/the-dopamine-rx-good-choices-or-bad/)

### bionaut — 2014-06-22T17:13:17+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/bionauts-journal.10690/post-131758>

Thanks everyone Leptin reset is going well am down to 230.5. I dug my icevest out of the basement and have got a pretty awesome tan from the sun I have been getting. Being ketotic I have had to up my water to about 1.5 gallons a day. Didn't keep up with water intake over the weekend and had some wine and whiskey which caused my progress to slow. Going to just have to nix those for a while.
I didn't realize how much I was snacking on nights.. I had some major habits to break the first two days. Since then carb cravings are gone and I am feeling mentally sharper. This is huge for just one week of doing this, can't wait till next Sunday.

### bionaut — 2014-06-29T21:05:41+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/bionauts-journal.10690/post-132639>

Down to 226.5. I am pretty sure that I am being limited by my water intake. Most days I am doing fine but today I have a raging headache. Funnily enough I was consuming about a pound of beef in the mornings thinking I was around 50grams, boy was I off. I am pretty sure I needed that much anyways until I was able to kill the ridiculous cravings I was having. At this point my sleep has improved fantastically, I get up only 3 times a night to attend to the kids but I am actually to go right back to bed until sunrise.

### bionaut — 2014-07-06T15:49:55+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/bionauts-journal.10690/post-133289>

This week I introduced carbs into the leptin reset. I have been getting tons of sun and wanted to see what effect I would have. Weight is up to to 230.5, I have always fluctuated pretty wide and quickly on the scale and now I can see why. Just takes a little bit of carbs to stall progress. Am not really worried about it though, I can say this is the best I have felt in a year. One thing I have noticed I am getting leaner, this is good news for me. I haven't worked out since last summer. I am not sure if I am going to go for the body by science program or go with kettle bell training in the yard. Three more weeks till I get to pick that up. Luckily my wife has seen an improvement in me and has jumped on board, after about a week her mood is up and this is becoming a good habit for us.

### bionaut — 2014-07-14T05:51:13+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/bionauts-journal.10690/post-134266>

Been a very stressful week with loads of work. Scales moved against me to 233. Overall my sleep has improved a million times over and I get tired when I am supposed to as well as wake up at dawn. This weekend I got plenty of time outside and got to kick back in the sun and with CT. I felt an itch to do something so I picked up some kettle bells and for the first time in a year I did a short workout. Kettlebell swings to exhaustion, ironically I thought they were going to be very hard and I found that I was doing them better than I was a year ago. I was surprised at how much I was not winded. I do not want to overdo it so I will slowly be adding exercises over the next few months. Took some PQQ and ate a steak dinner right after.

### bionaut — 2014-07-27T18:05:22+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/bionauts-journal.10690/post-135720>

Feeling decent this week. I have added the mitochondrial RX and am steadily humming along. Fats can easily satiate me in a meal. I have been around some pretty bad foods this week, doughnuts, pizza. Had zero urge to eat them. After reading OSF1 again I feel like I have a better handle on the gut interaction and why I need to eat seasonally.

Have added a little bulletproof intermittent fasting into the routine, noticed my sleep get a little worse and I had back pains at the end of the week. I can only guess it is from excess cortisol and not enough IR at the right times. Time to back off of coffee. I am picking up neurostabilizer cream for any stressful days due to a homozygous mthfrC677t.

Had an interesting experience this week where I ate sushi out in the sunlight for about 30 minutes. About 30 minutes after I felt pretty amazing. That is something new that I have never had happen before. Over the last month I have gone from about 236 to 227.

Am pretty excited for next week, I am heading up to a lake in new york to disconnect for a week. It is time to catch up on some reading.

### bionaut — 2014-08-16T23:28:12+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/bionauts-journal.10690/post-138044>

Didn't get any sleep in the stars but I got a whole lot of tanning, time in a cold lake and grounding  . Being at the lake was awesome, exactly what I needed. Out there I read the last few blogs a few times and have seriously upped my DHA content and nixed coffee to days where I get extra sun or am not working (1-2 days a week). 2 out of 3 meals contain DHA. I have had loads of sardines, mussels, shrimp and salmon this week. I am 0 to low carb this week.

A few immediate differences with DHA -
Stress levels have tanked, there are a few rooms passing megawatts of electricity in my office, I would sense a cognitive haze hit me after about 90 minutes in there and major stress. I would normally hack this with ubiquinol, however I no longer feel fatigued like I used to in there.

I do not get nearly as thirsty. I am maintaining about a gallon a day of water intake but before this it was sometimes not enough.

Smaller wins, binge eating is nonexistent, libido is up, I feel mentally a little sharper and I feel like colors seem a little more vivid ( I should know, I painted a lot in my unversity)

I will continue to drown myself in seafood over the next week to continue this experiment.

At this point I have tried many of the hacks I have learned here. The big ones stick and I am finding ways to work them into my life and slowly improving. Last year I did a 23 and me test and found that I had a few SNP's that were probably contributing to me feeling chronically fatigued 2 years ago. I decided not to address them and stick to the basics first. At this point I am going to give a go at fixing them after going through heartfixer and yasko's work. At the top of the list is homozygous C677t and am going to try this [http://mthfr.net/mthfr-c677t-mutation-basic-protocol/2012/02/24/](http://mthfr.net/mthfr-c677t-mutation-basic-protocol/2012/02/24/) and then will pile on some mushrooms.

### bionaut — 2014-10-03T16:15:22+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/bionauts-journal.10690/post-142684>

I returned from vacation and it was really difficult to regain my work rhythm. I put on some weight and was back at 234. Got a lot of labs drawn 25 days ago, took some pics and I added CT into the mix daily continuing with the copious seafood. Shucktoberfest 1$ oyster month just started at my local fish shack.

So a few things to note, today I jumped on the scale and I am down to 219! starting to fit into old clothes and feeling much more energized. I used to have a bad case of athletes foot, had it for years, GONE. Also, I am feeling much more sensitive, acute awareness I would say.

Props to all of Josh's guru talk, I did some meditation with CT this weekend and it was splendid.

### bionaut — 2015-01-17T01:57:08+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/bionauts-journal.10690/post-153078>

> [Jack Kruse said:](/goto/post?id=152188)
>
> I love it..............tell us more about the move.

Few things to mention. I have been getting pwned on sleep for the last year. As any parent on here knows there are a lot of wake up moments. I will say that while I have been down here my stress has decreased tremendously. My previous workplace was very high emf, I was living in a home right behind a major highway into the capital and got zero sun, plus I worked some shift work for a few months out of the year. So this was not a good situation. I wish I could say the move was easy, but it wasn't, I am in a new area and there is a lot I have left back in VA. Thankfully Jack's protocols have been pretty good for maintaining myself over that time. I have been able to put the brakes on weight gain and recover from stress and chronic fatigue which plagued me for a while.

So we have just literally settled in the new place in FL. Bad news first... I am 1-2mi near 2 FM towers and 2 radio station towers, also the power is funky down here, I see a dimming of lights telling me we have dirty electricity. This had me worried for a bit, but I unpacked blackout curtains, magnetico. Then we bought a foam mattress, btw if you are sleeping next to someone, this dampens vibration soooo much. I had the best night of sleep I have had in a YEAR!

I am working remotely so I have to spend a lot of time on the comp, however I can get outside in the sun for a few hrs a day. I am continuing the clean water and have been drinking a lot of kelp broth heated in RO water. Short term things are on the up and up, a good start for a new year.

> [caroline said:](/goto/post?id=152198)
>
> Yew loved meeting you! can't wait to see your babies at the 1 year mark

> good luck with those oysters!

### bionaut — 2015-03-07T18:20:30+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/bionauts-journal.10690/post-157743>

Been doing good so far. Have been keeping an eye on weight loss and I am at a plateau but I feel great so I am not worried about it. For the last 30 days I have been doing CT around 11 and getting in the sun right after. With seafood 3 times a week. I also have been IF by just eating fat for breakfast and skipping lunch with a big dinner. Down about 4 lbs of fat and very stable mood and feeling good. I cheated and ate pretty poorly on one day and I got dehydrated and felt like shit the next day. We all know why. I can say that I did have a burning sensation on certain points of my skin where a fungus was previously on my feet. My guess is that is where I have defective mito.

Based on Ubiquination 6 I am flipping my IF to the evening and ramping up seafood intake daily in the morning leptin rx style. Canned sardines here I come! Also, now that CT is consistent I am adding in diving to the bottom of the pool for rings a few times. Yesterday was the first day doing this. I did a 10 minute set of kettlebell swings and 10 minute set of dives. I have absolutely zero soreness. It is pretty weird. I am able to maintain a fasted day only... if I drink enough water... and I mean A LOT. I was chuggin 128oz yesterday, today I needed a little less but it is really high. From what I have read this is typical when in ketosis but I never ramped my intake this high. I will see how this goes the next 30 days and afterwards I will be adding in small amount of carbs as april comes around.

### bionaut — 2015-03-08T14:11:26+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/bionauts-journal.10690/post-157830>

> [Jude said:](/goto/post?id=157799)
>
> Gorgeous kids bionaut.
>
> Have been doing a few dives to pool bottom as well!!! asked a question in UBI 5 thread about how long the effects of the dive might last??...do you know??

I would think the immediate effects would last as long as you could maintain the water density and that would depend on your redox. Under the water at high pressure it would be the greatest and as you come up it would dissipate. You should have a higher oxygen tension as long as you remained cooler afterwards.

As for long term effects my thinking is that if you have a bunch of balls submerged in fluid, buoyant at different locations, as you dove the pressure would drive them to the center and as you came up they would reverse but at a lesser pace. So over time there would be a net drifting of mitochondria to the center. This is just how I picture it in my mind, not too sure how accurate it is. So I think I am just going to try it for a while and see what happens.

### bionaut — 2015-10-17T20:26:23+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/bionauts-journal.10690/post-179744>

Since the cruise kruse I have been a lot more diligent with getting things right. Took a little time to build the habits but I am getting about 1-2hrs of morning light each day. Over the last 5 weeks I have lost about 12 lbs. I always seem to stall at 220 I do not think this is necessarily a result of the number but more of consistency. I am feeling very good this time around and so I do not expect things to get locked up. Been keeping up with nightly use of a cryohelmet and morning meditation.

I can say that my memory retention is going up, I picked up juggling about 60 days ago and now I can do it decently. Never been able to do it in my life. I study the movement of stock prices for a living so I figured being able to juggle might build an intuitiveness to understanding waveforms.

Paleo Osteo gave me a back adjustment on the trip and showed me where my body was out of alignment starting at my feet. Just working my back gave me a 10% temporary cognitive boost. I did not know that was a deficiency of mine. Since I got back I have been doing yoga about 4 times a week in the sun. Feet were flat as a board, not any more, there is a small arch building and my feet have gotten incredibly strong. It is changing the way I walk and stand. My father has a minor hunch and I think I was getting one too but it is being reversed with regular practice. Since we are talking about feet here, I can say that the fungus I have had on my nails is now in major retreat. Healthy nail is now growing out of my worst nail and my feet not longer have irritated skin. Also the rash on my femoral artery on my leg has been gone for quite some time. So far so good.

### caroline — 2015-10-20T06:58:34+0300

### bionaut — 2015-10-24T21:11:17+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/bionauts-journal.10690/post-180369>

Sure Caroline I will get you some baby pix.

Used Dminder to track my Vit D. My god my vit d creation is horrible. My wife can make around 10k units while I make 2k. Skin color makes a difference. Apparently I need a shitload, so I have been trying to organize my life so I am outside as much as possible.

Added a couple supps 5k vit d, 150mcg molybdenum, B12 methyl folate. I will cut the vitD at the end of November, there is a lot of sun here in fl. I take these as I get into the sun in the AM. Sleep has taken a huge leap. I now sleep like a log.

Been reading A People's History of the United States, pretty good reading so far. It's got me thinking a lot about our fundamental connection with nature. In the beginning of the book, the author talks about these native cultures approaching settlers with open arms before they were brutally subjugated. The organization of groups around community seems very alien to how things are done today. I find it funny that english settlers would defect to the indian tribes for a better life, but it rare for this to be the other way around.

If a thriving people can create a thriving society, then it is a function of entanglement and electron capture. It seems at some points in history when groups have gotten large enough, individuals have found ways to channel resources to small groups. Perhaps there is a certain population threshhold people need to be within to maintain a healthy community? Many, not all technological advances have been used to funnel resources to the top. It seems nowadays, our technological progress is accelerating but to what end?
If our society is fundamentally organized around activities and technology that disconnect us electron capture then a tipping point will be reached where cognitive decline outweighs cognitive improvement. Technology will be a crutch to maintain the system to the breaking point.

You could argue we are in that phase now but for me things would have to be significantly worse. From the study of cycles we are entering that point at the beginning of the maunder minimum. Studying financial cycles from princeton, you can see that the path we are heading in mirrors romes, there is an increase in taxation to funnel money into the government. Focus on big pharma, climate change and terrorism are ways to create systems to harvest money from the collective. We are about to have a serious financial tipping point which will spark war and conflict because this is what has happened historically to unite populace's that have an economic decline. In rome, when the taxes remained high the government and culture disintegrated because it wasn't worth it to people, they just left. OK, rant over! back to the pool and sun!

[http://www.economicconfidencemodels.com/](http://www.economicconfidencemodels.com/)

### bionaut — 2016-04-06T22:07:18+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/bionauts-journal.10690/post-192183>

I have been practicing wim hof's modified tummo technique. Wow, that breathing makes you feel good. Since losing weight has stalled for a bit and I have been a bit foggy I built a hack I plan on doing for 21 days straight.

I am taking a drop of mblue, nicotine and ice cold water that has been sitting on a magnet. Then I go outside with one foot on the ground and another in an icewater footbath. At this point I begin the tummo hyperventilation and holding of air breathing techniques eyes open. In between rotations and rub it on to cool all exposed surfaces. This is about 30 - 45 minutes of FL sun.

So far, I am getting dark... way darker than usual, like I am gonna start looking like a one sided oreo cookie in a few days. Some skin flushing, usually I do not get red out in the sun. Way thirstier than normal throughout the day and I am literally a furnace at night. Upon awakening I am alert and very focused and I have an elevated mood all day. So I take my weight with a grain of salt because I know it can fluxuate but in the last 3 days I have slammed down 8 lbs and about 2-3% bf. I think after a week I should see a more accurate sign of what it is actually at but so far these are good signs. I will let you guys know how it turns out.

### bionaut — 2016-04-25T22:59:58+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/bionauts-journal.10690/post-193474>

Inger, have not had those issues with MB but I use pharma grade. So far so good, funny enough my scale broke, I figure I am just going to pick up another one in a month or two. The weight doesn't matter much to me, I can tell things are happening.

Wim H breathing techniques have been moved to the morning, with a cold shower and then get morning sun (not possible with two little ones running around midday). Noon I have been getting about an hr of sun with Nicotine and MB daily. Dual Nback brain training with kettlebell swings3-4 times a week.

So in the last 19 days Dminder says Vit D has gone from 20 to 25. I can make about 4-5k a day in an hr at noon, made about 86k in the last 30 days. I did not have a blood test baseline, I suspect it is higher but I am just going to take its word for it for now.

Nback is steady, I have been ranging from 4 to 6 sequences which is a good enough, I hope to make it to 9 by the end of 20 sessions. I feel like my memory is improving, this is subjective Nback is an ok measurement of working memory. I am also finding spaced repetition memorization to be easier. I am definitely getting leaner, I noticed an uptick in leaness after I purchased some tan through trunks. Get sun on your junk ppl, and if you have dark skin this is a must I am getting about another 20% of sun for my time with these.... and I got the purple ones..

### bionaut — 2016-06-12T17:06:47+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/bionauts-journal.10690/post-196434>

Continuing to biohack the wim hof breathing techniques. I read the latest blog and have been doing this with AM sun hitting my face and the shower running in the background. A couple changes I have noticed, there is a point a few breathing cycles in where noise is severely dampened. It is as if noise cancelling headphones were put on, fidelity auditory and visual fidelity increases massively. I have noticed a blue strobe effect when I close my eyes. It is a pulsing that covers my entire visual field and contracts down to a dot and then repeats. Salivation increases. If I do this in a cold air environment I see neon green. I can focus a pressure onto the front of my forehead, I can also direct it to almost any part of my head, it feels like increased flow of fluid wherever I want it to go and there is a similar effect but different for other locations in the body besides the head. Only twice I have had a memory trigger, was something like washing spatulas in the sink lol.. but it was extremely vivid, to the point that it could be fooled into thinking I was there.

Vitamin D is continuing to climb, been out at the beach and I should be around 33 or more by now. Thinking about getting a blood test when they go on sale to get a baseline.

Been trying the Fasting Mimicking Diet, the protocol is essentially what jack talked about in the CT series with kids that ate 90% water and fats with a bunch of caratenoids added in. I added seaweed and have done this regiment twice for 5 days on and 20 days off and then another 5 days while getting copious sun. I seem to respond very very very well to fasting. Dropped 3% of fat gained 1.5 % of muscle. Down 12 lbs. It is much more effective for me than CT. Makes sense considering my haplotype. But I am going to continue to ice my shoulders to maximize any brown fat Ive got.

Since Hurricane Mathew came around, the sun has changed here in Orlando, I think all of the particulate got cleared out of the air because the sunlight is bright white. Way more vibrant than normal, not something I noticed last year at all. Wish I had a spectrometer to see how the light has changed. Been sungazing off axis at dusk and dawn with an icepack over my eyes at 5 min intervals. I can say that I have never ever had as good vision as I do right now. It is crystal clear and very very detailed.

Got a question for anyone who may know. Jack I am looking in your direction . My wife's heritage is polish, has had 3 kids, and is 30. She is not over weight and seems to be relatively healthy. She had lasik 4 years ago, so she is protecting her eyes with blue blockers often. # Thread: Naked and Afraid
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/naked-and-afraid.10694/>
**Subforum:** The Cave
**Posts:** 14
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### nicld — 2014-04-30T16:40:59+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/naked-and-afraid.10694/post-124062>

Has anybody else watched this show. When it first came out I thought it was such a stupid premise for a show but I got sucked in and now I find it very fascinating as to what the human body can take.

The premise of the show is that they take a man and a woman who already possess some survival skills and drop them into remote “warm” parts of the world. They get naked and have to survive for 21 days with just a machete and a fire starter. They are not “alone” there is a camera crew with them and medic nearby if they need it and I am sure there is SOME help from the crew but they suppose to stay as hands off as they can. Also sure that in the editing they leave a lot of stuff unseen.

So what I find most fascinating is how quickly the people drop weight and how sick they seem to get from not eating. I am so curious to find out what type of diet they are eating BEFORE they begin this 21 days. The contestants are pretty fit to begin with but the guys seem to lose way more weight than the women. Some men have lost 40 pounds in 21 days.

I wonder if they are eating a SAD diet or a more ketogenic diet before they go, sugar burner versus fat burner? I would imagine that a fat burner would do better in face of starvation for 21 days then a sugar burner.

Also show how crucial good water is. When they do not have a good water source or can boil it, many have become real real sick.

Next season starts in July on the Discovery Channel.

### sooperb — 2014-04-30T17:14:32+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/naked-and-afraid.10694/post-124066>

Sounds like a similar format to a British programme where they drop a random selection of men and women in the Australian outback (allegedly lol) and they have to participate in various tasks in order to secure food and privileges. There is much speculation about the bits that don't get shown and some of the tasks are either pretty grim like having to eat live wichity grubs or sit in a box full of cockroaches but it is also a show that highlights contestant's insecurities and brings out the worst in them. Those participating all tend to come out a lot lighter than when they went in. On the whole, your show sounds a bit more balanced and interesting. Are the contestants ordinary folk or celebrities as they tend to be in the Australian camp?

**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/naked-and-afraid.10694/post-124094>

They get naked and have to survive for 21 days with just a machete and a fire starter. They are not “alone” there is a camera crew with them and medic nearby if they need it and I am sure there is SOME help from the crew but they suppose to stay as hands off as they can. Also sure that in the editing they leave a lot of stuff unseen.

Is there a sign up page somewhere? This sounds fun  Thanks for the heads up, I may have to at least give this show a try

> [nicld said:](/goto/post?id=124062)
>
> So what I find most fascinating is how quickly the people drop weight and how sick they seem to get from not eating. I am so curious to find out what type of diet they are eating BEFORE they begin this 21 days. The contestants are pretty fit to begin with but the guys seem to lose way more weight than the women. Some men have lost 40 pounds in 21 days.
> I wonder if they are eating a SAD diet or a more ketogenic diet before they go, sugar burner versus fat burner? I would imagine that a fat burner would do better in face of starvation for 21 days then a sugar burner.
> Also show how crucial good water is. When they do not have a good water source or can boil it, many have become real real sick.
> Next season starts in July on the Discovery Channel.

@[nicld](https://forum.jackkruse.com/members/6689/): I'd agree with your thoughts here... similar to how the initial switch from a SAD->epi-paleo/primal diet tends to drop some serious water weight.. I'd imagine those with the most significant fluctuations are the ones with less healthy diet/lifestyles going in. I'd bet the closer to optimal the participant, the more stable their state will stay across the 21 days.

### Jack Kruse — 2014-04-30T19:21:22+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/naked-and-afraid.10694/post-124096>

you better watch it............we all may need those skills in our lifetime.

### NeilBB — 2014-04-30T19:39:29+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/naked-and-afraid.10694/post-124102>

> [Jack Kruse said:](/goto/post?id=124096)
>
> you better watch it............we all may need those skills in our lifetime.

Great show! I loved the episode where the guy went onto a tropical island as white as a ghost, looking like he had never heard of the damn sun, and then he got his butt sunburned so fast they had to chopper him out after he drank the infected water! But the other episode that seemed to give them the most trouble was in the Cajun Swamp!!! Snakes and gators and insects in the marshland!! Not far from where I grew up, LOL.

### Gagnrad — 2014-04-30T20:23:16+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/naked-and-afraid.10694/post-124111>

> [nicld said:](/goto/post?id=124062)
>
> Has anybody else watched this show. When it first came out I thought it was such a stupid premise for a show but I got sucked in and now I find it very fascinating as to what the human body can take.

First I've heard of it.

> The premise of the show is that they take a man and a woman who already possess some survival skills and drop them into remote “warm” parts of the world. They get naked and have to survive for 21 days with just a machete and a fire starter. They are not “alone” there is a camera crew with them and medic nearby if they need it and I am sure there is SOME help from the crew but they suppose to stay as hands off as they can. Also sure that in the editing they leave a lot of stuff unseen.

Yeah, how it tends to be (to all that).

I looked it up on Wikipedia (I know …).

There it's said that they are "survivalists" as you mentioned. Also there's this:

> Brian Lowry of _[Variety](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Variety_\(magazine\))_ found _Naked and Afraid_ to be "typical of the genre, but it's still kind of a risible kick, if only for how seriously the show takes itself."

Sure. This kind of thing is now a genre.

> Mike Hale of _[The New York Times](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/The_New_York_Times)_ expressed skepticism that the dangers implied by the situations faced by the couples in each episode were authentic, even by the standards of reality television, observing that the couple in the series premiere appeared to be cleaner and more shaved than he'd have expected.

If he's right, nothing surprising there.

[http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Naked\_and\_Afraid](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Naked_and_Afraid)

Apparently, it gets better ratings that any other survival TV show has. Wiki gives the figures -- around 2 million viewers per episode. It sounds a lot, but I guess the U.S. is a populous country. Still, the advertising slots -- which is, I suppose, what this is all about -- must be worth quite a bit. For comparison I checked Wiki's page on "Desperate Housewives" which I've heard is popular viewing over in the U.S. That gets anything up to 10 times as many viewers.

> I wonder if they are eating a SAD diet or a more ketogenic diet before they go, sugar burner versus fat burner? I would imagine that a fat burner would do better in face of starvation for 21 days then a sugar burner.

Dunno. I imagine sheer absence of food would be a pretty foolproof way to get into fat-burning, and indeed ketogenic, mode. But the first few days would be painful for someone on a high carbohydrate diet, sure.

> Also show how crucial good water is. When they do not have a good water source or can boil it, many have become real real sick.

Yes. There's a whole raft of questions raised by that thought.

Interestingly, the solution to the problem of clean water in circumstances of population growth in Europe was alcohol. The habit was taken to the U.S., which was awash in the stuff -- to the extent that it provoked prohibition. Drinking in Colonial America is caught very cleverly in Kenneth Roberts' very vivid novel _Northwest Passage_ -- and even in the (rollicking but really rather dreadful) [Spencer Tracey film](http://www.youtube.com/watch?v=Ns0X_OqcPgw) made from it. In the Far East tea was the solution. There the water is, of course, boiled, but the tea itself is also antiseptic. Some historians believe that the discovery and importation of tea into Europe is one of the things that made the Industrial Revolution possible. First, it allowed more people to live in closer proximity; secondly, you could drink it and still operate machinery safely. Try doing that when ale or cider is your staple drink.

### sooperb — 2014-05-04T20:21:13+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/naked-and-afraid.10694/post-125080>

Creepy crawly bitey things would be enough to put me off, I just really dislike them. Just as the warm weather is gearing up a bit here, so the horse flies start to emerge, they fly about in their dozens and land on any bare flesh within reach. One got me on my finger a couple of years ago, it swelled horribly and I had to have my wedding rings cut off! I get really paranoid about them, it makes walking the dog, which I've been doing in bare feet up till now, a very challenging experience. Repellent doesn't seem to affect them so you just have to cover up, no fun at all. Then there's the mosquitoes, fortunately not malarial but they still bite and it itches like mad. I sometimes wonder why we have such creatures!

I am completely on board with what Jack keeps repeating about the importance of your zip code.

> When your atmosphere is loaded with non native electromagnetic force it is pulling electrons from every part of your body you **lose your magnetic sense.**.........So this means if you lose the ability to sense magnetism; than you have only 2 of the 3 legs of stool working in your semiconductors. If you do not think this is a big deal realize right now that Mars has 2 out of 3 of the main variables for life. It is a **_dead red planet._**
>
> A magnetico has a massive benefit when you live in an extreme environment. Every person on the face of this planet lives on an extreme planet now; The Northeast corridor happens to be one of the worse areas geographically in our country...................Your zip code determines the rate of electron loss the best...............We did not evolve on a planet without a magnetic force...........our species just created one in the last 120 years.

Yesterday, however, when I was trying to explain this concept to my husband, his immediate response to me was asking me to give him some of those zip codes, so that he could look at them.

I went back to the blog hoping to quickly find and get back to him with that information.... but all that I could see were just some general guidelines like these:

> 3 key things for relocation.
> Excellent water supply
> Good forrest
> And low EMF
> 4th one would be easy access to decent electron dense foods.

or some discussions regarding the latitude.

Could someone actually list any of the zip codes that would encompass all of these attributes?

# Thread: Getting skinny after switching to raw meat
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/getting-skinny-after-switching-to-raw-meat.10699/>
**Subforum:** The Epi-Paleo Diet
**Posts:** 15
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Milton2 — 2014-04-30T20:38:34+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/getting-skinny-after-switching-to-raw-meat.10699/post-124115>

Hi All!
Since switching to eating raw meat and raw eggs I've noticed that I've started looking more skinny and weak. Lower muscle strenght has also been noticable. No other negative effects occur. On the contrary, I've never felt so vigorous (maybe except the occasional times when I eat to much carbs). What is the difference between raw and cooked paleo diet causes such a change? Any ideas? Any experience in this field?

Regards,
Milton

### JackFrake — 2014-04-30T22:00:09+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/getting-skinny-after-switching-to-raw-meat.10699/post-124124>

I was in the process of composing a question on the 'How do you do raw' thread that's relevant ("What have people noticed when they switch to a more 'raw' diet?)".

Can't say that I'm experiencing strength loss or weakness. But my appetite's significantly less than pre-raw. Hydration needs too.

Overall I'm noticing more changes than previous N=1s I've adopted.

Verdict's still out, but so far I'm enjoying the shorter prep time in the kitchen and more frequent trips to the market for fresh squid, scallops, oysters, and offal.

### Jack Kruse — 2014-04-30T22:06:39+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/getting-skinny-after-switching-to-raw-meat.10699/post-124126>

it works............because this is how we diversify the gut. This is where SIBO starts and begins and it gets worse the less electrons you got in the GI system.

### JackFrake — 2014-04-30T22:53:48+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/getting-skinny-after-switching-to-raw-meat.10699/post-124136>

Yes. I can definitely notice a change down there.

I suspect diversity is more and more important for girding our guts and immune systems against the coming storm of superbugs: [http://www.dailymail.co.uk/health/a...g-drugs-devastating-says-landmark-report.html](http://www.dailymail.co.uk/health/article-2616794/Antibiotic-resistance-needs-taken-seriously-AIDS-Implications-bacteria-evading-drugs-devastating-says-landmark-report.html)
