### Inger — 2014-05-01T08:24:13+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/getting-skinny-after-switching-to-raw-meat.10699/post-124209>

try some high / fermented heart? + eat everything else as the plain muscle meat
maybe yo could write here what you normally eat in a day? How much, and what?

I have not noticed weight loss or muscles disappear.. I am pretty strong.. and I eat raw animal foods mainly for many years.
But I eat a lot - and I drink much water too, even if raw makes me way less thirsty I still drink about 4 liters / day

### Milton2 — 2014-05-01T09:06:53+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/getting-skinny-after-switching-to-raw-meat.10699/post-124216>

Inger,
This is not muscle dissappear as you may understand it. It's just like the muscles are still there but not filled with anything. If I would now stuff myself with carbs tomorrow everyone arround would thing I'm on steroids (and you cannot build muscles in one day so it must be about the glycogen and water storage I think).

As for what I eat it is mainly raw wild meat (mostly the fatty parts) and raw eggs accompanied by some cooked meat (lard, ofal) and a little bit of seasonal veggies. I eat this in one large meal after work (about 6 p.m.). But if I divide it into two meals, breakfast and supper, the effect is the same. Raw food just makes me feel healthier (especially in terms of the gut reactions to what I eat), more vigorous and a little bit weaker. Maybe it's that cooked protein gets partialy denaturated and converted to sugar?

### Inger — 2014-05-01T12:28:35+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/getting-skinny-after-switching-to-raw-meat.10699/post-124226>

you eat no seafood and seaweeds Milton? those would be important too, I do mostly 2 out of 3 meals a seafood meal

I eat mostly 3 meals / day... and they are not too small... mostly around 250 grams of seafood or organs/meat / meal... can be less or more though, I never measure
but I am a woman, they might be different?
I also add fat...
IDK why I eat so much, I feel like it... maybe because my temp is so warm, 37,0 - 37,5 degree C always
maybe because I live in Finland... maybe because this house has mold too? Because I need electrons?
If I eat less I lose weight tho, I have tried. But right now my desire to be skinny is not that big so I gorge to my hearts desire lol
I had maybe 250 grams raw Baltic herring + raw onions for BAB with a BPC and lots of raw organic Hazelnuts + soaked raw seaweed water + some wild greens from the garden - lots of these [http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Aegopodium\_podagraria](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Aegopodium_podagraria) and some dandelion leaves too for liver function .....

IDK if cooked protein gets converted to sugar... hmmm.. At least the raw version makes me feel better too

I barely eat any muscle meat anymore. I just eat raw heart and liver...... they are cheaper too  and they makes me feel better

### Milton2 — 2014-05-01T13:33:34+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/getting-skinny-after-switching-to-raw-meat.10699/post-124245>

> [Inger said:](/goto/post?id=124226)

When I accomplished this I started switching to the raw ketogenic diet. I know that one day I'll include raw seafood but as for now it's just to damn disgusting for me to eat.

> [Inger said:](/goto/post?id=124226)

You're getting hungry so fast on raw keto? For me one meal (a large one!) a day feels optimal. 4-6 p.m. best.

### caroline — 2014-05-01T14:23:09+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/getting-skinny-after-switching-to-raw-meat.10699/post-124260>

### Inger — 2014-05-01T16:05:49+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/getting-skinny-after-switching-to-raw-meat.10699/post-124291>

yeah pic please

Milton I do not really get hungry to be honest  I just eat!

I just feel like eating... I can easily go longer but somehow it feels right to have 3 meals - I do sometimes have only 2 if late breakfast

I some days eat dinner at 3-4 PM and wake up next morning and never feel hunger, I still eat! I have great appetite it seems...
I feel almost the same before and after eating but eating do warms me up
Sometimes I get a little hungry

You need to make something delicious out of raw seafood for the beginning, or go to a restaurant and let them serve you something! I bet you are gonna like it

### JackFrake — 2014-05-01T16:48:25+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/getting-skinny-after-switching-to-raw-meat.10699/post-124307>

Raw oysters with horseradish and squeezed lemon are a good intro to raw seafood. You can just slurp them down.

I was indifferent about the taste at first. Now I'm addicted, and absolutely love them every morning. They helped wean me off daily vitamins/supplements too, which was a mental hurdle (even after reading/buying into Jack's recs these past few years). Nature's multivitamin.

Recently added raw squid, scallops, and shrimp. I don't seem to tolerate the shrimp as well. But feel great when I eat the other two.

Inger is also correct when she claims 'raw' eventually tastes much better than cooked. I'm now eating liver and heart raw, and searching out various tartare recipes.

My grill is sad.

### Inger — 2014-05-01T17:39:11+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/getting-skinny-after-switching-to-raw-meat.10699/post-124332>

yes - great stuff! I would take a fish head smoothie over supplements any day! From supps, I feel nothing... but the smoothie - or oysters.. my body says YAY - immediately! Go figure!

My stove is sad too  i practically never use it...

I cant get raw shrimp here... but prefrozen raw squid and scallops I can, and I do buy it once in a while, very good!

### Ken23 — 2014-06-07T20:15:44+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/getting-skinny-after-switching-to-raw-meat.10699/post-130102>

Inger- it seems as though raw is working really well for you. I'd love to start eating the way you've been describing but I just can't get myself to consume raw organ meat or fish ( other than oysters). Mental block

### Inger — 2014-06-08T09:30:30+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/getting-skinny-after-switching-to-raw-meat.10699/post-130156>

Mental blocks we can overcome, Ken  you just need to figure out a way to think... use your imagination

I have used:

Oh man, this is good! (because seriously it tastes good, only the MHS is not so good but then I hold my nose) You can also spice it up, use ACV or balsamic vinegar, raw onions, salt, spices....

Thinking about a very courageous friend of mine that was scared of nothing... and ate raw organs and meats he had hunted himself.... a special guy. If he could, sure I can too!

First time I had raw liver I closed my eyes and tasted it without seeing. I really wanted to judge the taste... and I figured it tasted a bit like chocolate - it was pretty good! I just decided to let go of all my feelings and thoughts about it I had so far. I can assure you I thought it was yucky too first... before I let go

In Russia it is common (or at least was) to eat raw liver thin sliced with sliced raw onion, like a carpaccio! I do that too, very good! Make sure you have good quality liver. Calves liver / grassfed beef or lamb liver are best. Game is great too! The raw packaged grainfed beef liver is not very good

### NeilBB — 2014-04-30T23:48:58+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/kruses-law.10700/post-124140>

Okay, I believe I have finally induced a general axiom of this site and of Jack's approach. I would state it thusly:

Any significant technological advancement that man can design or invent has already been incorporated into living systems by evolutionary biology in some way. You just have to look for it. In order for the biological and medical sciences to advance, its practitioners must realize that mother nature is not only smarter and faster than they are but also smarter and faster than our best physicists and engineers have ever been. And they are all painting on the same canvas.

The leading edge of our technology today is where mother nature and evolution were operating millennia ago. So if you want to get a clue of how we work, you'd better start looking there.

Boom.

### Jack Kruse — 2014-04-30T23:53:47+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/kruses-law.10700/post-124142>

True dat.

Docosahexaenoic acid (DHA) (all-cis-docosa-4,7,10,13,16,19-hexaenoic acid, C22:6ω3 or C22:6, n-3, DHA) is a major feature of marine lipids. It requires six oxygen atoms to insert its six double bonds, so it would not have been abundant before oxidative metabolism became plentiful. This means the best collector of the best subatomic particle allow evolution to be complex. DHA provided the membrane backbone for the emergence of new photoreceptors that converted photons into electricity, laying the foundation for the evolution of other signalling systems, the nervous system and the brain. It was the first topologic insultor naturally created by light oxygen old prokaryote membranes.

For the first 2.5 billion years of life there was ample opportunity for DNA modification. Yet there is no evidence of significant change in life forms during that time. It was not until about 600 million years ago, when the oxygen tension rose to a point where air-breathing life forms became thermodynamically possible, that a major change can be abruptly seen in the fossil record. The sudden appearance of the 32 phyla in the Cambrian fossil record was also associated with the appearance of intracellular detail not seen in previous life forms.

To date...................600 million yrs later not on other lipid has replaced this DHA molecule...............ask yourself why?

It is the perfect Maxwell Demon for the electron.

This is my Law.................If I ever become so lucky. Ten yrs ago I realized the final common pathway of life..............the collection and assimation of electrons is critical to healthspan and longevity thermodynamically.

### Jack Kruse — 2014-05-01T04:29:30+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/kruses-law.10700/post-124189>

Regarding cross-linking: Recall collagen is extensively cross-linked. Why is that important with respect to electrons?

**The transfer of electrons between proteins is an essential step in biological energy production. Two protein redox partners are often crosslinked in a lab to investigate the poorly understood mechanism by which they interact. To better understand the effect of crosslinking on electron transfer rates, researchers have constructed dimers of azurin by crosslinking the monomers. The measured electron exchange rates, combined with crystal structures of the dimers, demonstrate that the length of the crosslinker can have a dramatic effect on the structure of the dimer and the electron transfer rate. The electron transfer rate is a synonym for semiconductor current. The presence of ordered water molecules in the protein−protein interface may considerably influence the electronic coupling between redox centers.**
**
[http://www.nature.com/nsmb/journal/v9/n1/full/nsb736.html](http://www.nature.com/nsmb/journal/v9/n1/full/nsb736.html)**

### NeilBB — 2014-05-01T15:29:17+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/kruses-law.10700/post-124275>

It makes a ton of sense to look at technology to understand evolution...

Evolution and technology do similar things because fundamentally, according to physics, there is only one constructive thing to do. Harness, organize, and structure the flow of energy that is already moving through the universe, and in so doing decrease the entropy of some subsystem somewhere, which will increase it somewhere else according to the 2nd law of thermodynamics. Whether it’s a Maxwell Demon protein or a steam engine, that’s what innovation ultimately does. For example, DHA is the biotechnology that nature utilized to help us steal electrons away from the rest of the universe and then convert that power into our brainpower; and similarly the transistor is the engineering device that allows modern electronic gadgets to capture and precisely regulate the flow of electrons under various conditions to power the gadget to do amazing things.

Ultimately whether physical, chemical, biological, or technological, whether electronic or thermodynamic, that is essentially all that there really is to do in order to alter the cosmological path of the universe. Redirect usable energy here. Dump excess entropy there. The rest is just details.

And mother nature had about a 13 billion year head start on the physicists at MIT!

BOOM

Modern technology is greatly complicating the thermodynamic equation that sustains our life! I am looking forward to hearing the details!

Intact ecosystems seem to be necessary to maintain the juggling act, as one form of life's waste product can serve as another form's fuel. The ultimate cosmic energy recycling system.

### yewwei.tan — 2014-05-02T02:21:55+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/kruses-law.10700/post-124458>

**Random Thoughts
**
My job as a software dev involves breaking things apart. Most technological advancement eventually progresses toward **modularity over efficiency**.

We want things to be modular so that we can replace and evolve components as demands on the technology change. Example: you can technically have a "20 year old car" that has had every component in it replaced at some point. A mechanic can replace the radiator in an engine any time, so long as they find an appropriate replacement that fits the interface to the engine -- there are few dependencies between a car radiator and the engine, just a simple contract about how each component should work.

Nature is all about efficiency, and found us the most energy efficient solutions to a lot of our problems. It did so through the combination of enough time, random chance, and emergent phenomena based on the contingent factors in the environment. The classic example are eyes -- nature found that photons (and by extension, electrons) are by far the means of transmitting information. Eyes hence evolved independently many times on this planet, and if you could mirror this planet in another galaxy, eyes would evolve there too, because it was the most efficient design. (same with DHA in the brain)

Designing for efficiency puts you at a fundamental deficit when it comes to evolvability and robustness. The tradeoff that happens with efficiency is tight coupling between implementation details and environment (something that bites us software developers time and time again). You can't replace a lung like you replace a car engine radiator because you need to worry about much much more than just the connection between the lung and the bronchus. To use manufacturing jargon, human "cycle times" are way too long (sexual reproduction of another generation), and put us fundamentally at risk with regards to the crazy pace of human design.

I wish it were the case that we could make the human body modular, and only replace the minimum amount necessary to restore optimal function, while honouring the rest of the contracts between all inputs from other organ systems and environments. That won't ever happen until certain contingent discoveries are made. ie: We depend on the work of a few visionaries.

Or put differently, what would happen if Einstein didn't discover E=mc2 or the photoelectric effect? Surely someone would have discovered it eventually (it was the only answer), but when? Nature is old, and can wait -- what if an Einstein only came around 500 years from now? We'd have technological progress delayed by 500 years ... which is a blink of an eye from the perspective of nature .... (And note that events have happened many times through history that delayed our progress by large timescales with respect to human lifetimes -- eg: fall of the Rome, Genghis Khan wrecking the Muslim world, Cortez in the Americas, maybe the younger dryas impact, etc ...)

I'll believe Kurzweil's visions on AI when I see the contingent technologies that make it possible, and when I see the people who adopt the right philosophical mindset surface within the community and start innovating in public. I personally am skeptical regarding exponential growth (and don't forget that no matter how much progress we can make, all it takes is one crucial event to cause an entire system to come crashing down completely). Until then, we're stuck with the body, so let's change the environment.

### yewwei.tan — 2014-05-02T02:25:45+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/kruses-law.10700/post-124459>

And BTW, when I heard "Kruse's Law" I immediately thought about Greenspun's 10th rule (about my first programming language, Common Lisp) -- [https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Greenspun's\_tenth\_rule](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Greenspun's_tenth_rule)

Any sufficiently complicated [C](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/C_\(programming_language\)) or [Fortran](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Fortran) program contains an [ad hoc](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ad_hoc), informally-specified, [bug-ridden](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Computer_bug), slow implementation of half of [Common Lisp](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Common_Lisp).
​

And then I immediately thought:

Any sufficiently complicated human technology contains an ad hoc, informally-specified, bug-ridden, slow implementation of a tiny fraction of nature.​

'Nature is all about efficiency, and found us the most energy efficient solutions to a lot of our problems. It did so through the combination of enough time, random chance, and emergent phenomena based on the contingent factors in the environment. The classic example are eyes -- nature found that photons (and by extension, electrons) are by far the means of transmitting information. Eyes hence evolved independently many times on this planet, and if you could mirror this planet in another galaxy, eyes would evolve there too, because it was the most efficient design. (same with DHA in the brain)"

Eyes and the brain........both one and yet not. They both sense the world from inside out. The most sensitive cells to light are oriented away from the photons.......yet they are lightening fast reactors to its photons. One has to ask why?

The extraordinary conservation and irreplaceable nature of DHA in neuronal signaling and its high concentration in the photoreceptors of the retina can be explained if it functions as an electron tunnelling device providing quantized signals. The retina has more DHA in it than any other part of the brain. Quantum mechanical treatment explains the absolute precision of the membrane depolarization in phototransduction. This precision is essential to human visual acuity and synaptic signaling. Quantum mechanics also happens to explain why the photoreceptors are oriented in a counter-intuitively manner away from incoming light. This is true in the retina with the most light sensitive cells deepest in the retina and the neocortex below the skull in what some think is total darkness..........but is not.

### Jack Kruse — 2014-08-23T18:40:02+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/kruses-law.10700/post-138713>

December 4, 2013

Source:
Karolinska Institutet

Summary:
New research shows that omega-3 fatty acids in dietary supplements can cross the blood brain barrier in people with Alzheimer’s disease, affecting known markers for both the disease itself and inflammation. The findings strengthen the evidence that omega-3 may benefit certain forms of this seriously debilitating disease.
**Omega 3** fatty acids are essential for human nutrition. Our bodies cannot produce these fatty acids, so we must consume them through our diet. Integral to the proper functioning of cell membranes, omega-3 fatty acids help to regulate hormone and cell receptor functions. According to the Harvard School of Public Health, these protective and supportive properties of Omega-3 benefit the cardiovascular and nervous systems.Beneficial for:

• Anti-Aging ( **promotes the production of Telomerase**)

• Heart Health

• High Blood Pressure

• High Cholesterol

• Diabetes Management

• Periodontal Disease

• Improved Mental Alertness

• Improved Stamina & Endurance

• Improved Immune Function

• Prevention of LDL

\* Fights inflammation

\* Mental Stress

-   A derivative of docosahexaenoic acid (DHA), a main ingredient of fish-oil supplements, can alleviate and prevent neuropathic pain caused by sensory system injuries, researchers from Duke University Medical Center reported in the _Annals of Neurology_.
    The authors say that the DHA derivative – a compound called neuroprotectin D1=protectin D1 (NPD1=PD1) – may provide doctors and patients who are concerned about pain medication addictions and opioids with a more natural and healthier alternative.
    NPD1=PD1 is a bioactive lipid which cells produce in response to external stimuli. It is present in human white blood cells and plays a role in resolving brain and abdominal[inflammation](http://www.medicalnewstoday.com/articles/248423.php).
    Head researcher, Ru-Rong Ji, professor of anesthesiology and neurobiology at Duke University Medical Center, said:
    “These compounds are derived from omega-3 fatty acids found in fish oil, but are 1,000 times more potent than their precursors in reducing inflammation.”
    In an animal experiment, the scientists used lab mice models of nerve injuries to simulate the pain symptoms commonly linked to nerve trauma caused by surgery. The mice were treated with chemically-synthesized NPD1=PD1, which was administered either locally or by injection, to determine whether the compound might relieve these symptoms.
    **Not only did NPD1=PD1 relieve pain symptoms, but it also reduced nerve inflammation following the injuries**. The compound reduces pain by inhibiting the productions of chemokines and cytokines, small signaling molecules that attract inflammatory macrophages to the nerve cells.
    NPD1=PD1 protected nerve cells from further damage by preventing the production of chemokines and cytokines. The lipid compound also reduced neuron firing, resulting in less pain for the injured mice.
    Ji said “Chronic pain resulting from major medical procedures such as amputation, chest and breast surgery is a serious problem.”
    Current neuropathic pain medications, including gabapentin and several opioids, can lead to dependency and sensory nerve cell destruction.
    Below is a list of the **main advantages of NPD1=PD1**:

    -   Only low doses are needed to relieve neuropathic pain
    -   It does not appear to lead to physical dependence
    -   No enhanced tolerance towards the compound was observed in the mice
    -   It reduces nerve inflammation following injuries
    -   currently dosages given via transdermal patches allow the use of natural DHA for immediate delivery to the bloodstream

    Ji said “We hope to test this compound in clinical trials.” He suggested that in apreliminary trial, DHA could be administered via diet and transdermal patches. “DHA is very inexpensive, and can be converted to NPD1 by an aspirin-triggered pathway.” for those who can take "baby"aspirin.White willow bark as a supplement will also trigger the pathway.
    Health Flex suggest that to achieve rapid pain relief that you double your [omega-3/COQ10 patch](http://www.tennishealth.biz/apps/webstore/products/show/3902165) to twice a week instead of once a week along with a baby aspirin or white willow bark extract. It is safe non toxic and cheaper than drugs!
    The team said their ultimate objective is to develop a safer approach to controlling chronic pain.

    [Omega oils protect the heart from mental stress.](http://www.medicalnewstoday.com/articles/260964.php) A study study published in the_American Journal of Physiology - Regulatory, Integrative, and Comparative Physiology_revealed that people who took fish oil supplements for over a month experienced less mental [stress](http://www.medicalnewstoday.com/articles/145855.php) in measurements of cardiovascular health than those who did not.

    -   **Benefits for the fetus** - [omega-3 consumption boosts fetal cognitive and motor development](http://www.medicalnewstoday.com/releases/103555.php) - scientists from L'Université Laval Laval found that omega-3 consumption by the mother during her last three months of pregnancy improved her baby's sensory, cognitive and motor development.
    -   **Protecting from vision loss** - adequate dietary [consumption of DHA protects people from age-related vision loss](http://www.medicalnewstoday.com/articles/246371.php), Canadian researchers reported in the journal _Investigative Ophthalmology & Visual Science_.
    -   **Heart benefits found** - a 2011 study, on the other hand, carried out by researchers at Michigan Technological University, found that [fish oil consumption can improve blood flow](http://www.medicalnewstoday.com/releases/216346.php) by reducing triglyceride levels, as well as slowing down the growth rate of atherosclerotic plaques.

        [Fish oils help patients with stents in their arteries](http://www.medicalnewstoday.com/releases/226676.php). People with stents in their heart who took two blood-thinning drugs as well as omega-3 fatty acids were found to have a lower risk of [heart attacks](http://www.medicalnewstoday.com/articles/151444.php) compared to those not on fish oils.

        **Are low Japanese heart disease rates linked to high fish oil consumption?**
        Researchers from the University of Pittsburgh Graduate School of Public Health set out to determine why the incidence of [heart disease](http://www.medicalnewstoday.com/articles/237191.php) in Japan is much lower than in the USA, Canada, Western Europe and Australasia.

        They reported in the _Journal of the American College of Cardiology_ in April 2008 that omega-3-rich fish consumption in Japan is much higher than in other developed nations. The authors believe that the [greater consumption of fish oils in Japan is a main contributor to its relatively lower heart disease rates](http://www.medicalnewstoday.com/releases/116383.php).

        The scientists explained that the difference cannot be explained by genetic factors. Third and fourth generation Japanese-Americans have either the same or higher rates of hardening of the arteries ([atherosclerosis](http://www.medicalnewstoday.com/articles/247837.php)) than the rest of the US population. Atherosclerosis is a major risk factor for heart disease.

    **Journal Reference**:
    1.  Yvonne Freund Levi, Inger Vedin, Tommy Cederholm, Hans Basun, Gerd Faxén Irving, Maria Eriksdotter, Erik Hjorth, Marianne Schultzberg, Bengt Vessby, Lars-Olof Wahlund, Norman Salem, Jan Palmblad. **Transfer of omega-3 fatty acids across the blood-brain barrier after dietary supplementation with a docosahexaenoic acid (DHA)-rich omega-3 fatty acid preparation in patients with Alzheimer's disease: the OmegAD study**._Journal of Internal Medicine_, 2013; DOI: [10.1111/joim.12166](http://dx.doi.org/10.1111/joim.12166)

### Jack Kruse — 2015-07-25T00:57:41+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/kruses-law.10700/post-172912>

> [Jack Kruse said:](/goto/post?id=124143)
>
> ^^^^consider that mitochondria's change programs work how to signal? one electron loss = superoxide, 2 electrons loss = H2O2, 3 electron loss = the Fenton reactions or hydroxyl free radical...........oonly controlled in what system? Cell mediated immunity. Ironically the last of the two complex evolved systems in primates..........that and neurogenesis due to MHC1. After OSF 3 you will see my biologic play ground for the last ten years in my mind...........I am a quantum molecular dynamist who understands how biology really innovates.

Peroxidases like H2O2 are electron acceptors.............in redox reactions.

How do redox reactions in proteins and light link?

Porphyrin rings in chlorophyll and hemoglobin.

Photosynthesis froms the basis of the food chains on Earth.

Did you know plants us both chlorophyll and hemoglobin?

Both are heme proteins.

Do you know that cytochrome c the major signaling molecule in mitochondria is also a heme protein?

Cytochrome c is absolutely essential for life; organisms that lack it cannot live. It has been shown that the human cytochrome c protein works in yeast (a eukaryotic unicellular organism) that has had its own native cytochrome c gene deleted, even though yeast cytochrome c differs from human cytochrome c over 40% of the protein. Cytochrome c is a heme related protein. It is related to porphyrins which absorb UV light. In fact, the cytochrome c genes from tuna (fish), pigeon (bird), horse (mammal), Drosophila fly (insect), and rat (mammal) all function in yeast that lack their own native yeast cytochrome c .Only about a third of the 100 amino acids in cytochrome c are necessary to specify its function. Most of the amino acids in cytochrome c are hypervariable. Importantly, Hubert Yockey has done a careful study in which he calculated that there are a minimum of 2.3 x 1093 possible functional cytochrome c protein sequences, based on these genetic mutational analyses. For perspective, the number 1093 is about one billion times larger than the number of atoms in the visible universe. Thus, functional cytochrome c sequences are virtually unlimited in number, and there is no a priori reason for two different species to have the same, or even mildly similar, cytochrome c protein sequences.
In terms of a scientific statistical analysis, the "null hypothesis" is that the identity of non-essential amino acids in the cytochrome c proteins from human and chimpanzee should be random with respect to one another. However, from the theory of common descent and our standard phylogenetic tree we know that humans and chimpanzees are quite closely related. We therefore predict, in spite of the odds, that human and chimpanzee cytochrome c sequences should be much more similar than, say, human and yeast cytochrome c - simply due to inheritance. Humans and chimpanzees have the exact same cytochrome c protein sequence. The "null hypothesis" given above is false. In the absence of common descent, the chance of this occurrence is conservatively less than 10-93 (1 out of 1093). Thus, the high degree of similarity in these proteins is a spectacular corroboration of the theory of common descent. Furthermore, human and chimpanzee cytochrome c proteins differ by ~10 amino acids from all other mammals. The chance of this occurring in the absence of a hereditary mechanism is less than 10-29. The yeast Candida krusei is one of the most distantly related eukaryotic organisms from humans. Candida has 51 amino acid differences from the human sequence. A conservative estimate of this probability is less than 10-25. One possible, yet unlikely, objection is that the slight differences in functional performance between the various cytochromes could be responsible for this sequence similarity. This objection is unlikely because of the incredibly high number of nearly equivalent sequences that would be phenotypically indistinguishable for any required level of performance. Additionally, nearly similar sequences do not necessarily give nearly similar levels of performance.

Nonetheless, for the sake of argument, let us assume that a cytochrome c that transports electrons faster is required in organisms with active metabolisms or with high rates of muscle contraction. If this were true, we might expect to observe a pattern of sequence similarity that correlates with similarity of environment or with physiological requirement. However, this is not observed. For example, bat cytochrome c is much more similar to human cytochrome c than to hummingbird cytochrome c; porpoise cytochrome c is much more similar to human cytochrome c than to shark cytochrome c. As stated earlier in prediction 1.3, the phylogenetic tree constructed from the cytochrome c data exactly recapitulates the relationships of major taxa as determined by the completely independent morphological data (McLaughlin and Dayhoff 1973). These facts only further support the idea that cytochrome c sequences are independent of phenotypic function (other than the obvious requirement for a functional cytochrome c that transports electrons).

Is there a point to all this? Yep..........

### yewwei.tan — 2015-07-25T05:48:56+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/kruses-law.10700/post-172922>

Well, to tie the above back to Ubiquitination, I'd highly suggest that people read Nick Lane's latest book 'The Vital Question'. In it, he describes precisely how it is cytochrome c function that determines the decision of a cell to kill itself.

Dysfunction or loss of cytochrome c => signal to nucleus for apoptosis => more cell turnover => expensive on the body => disease if kept up chronically.

In general, to make UV light useful, you are going to need some form of Nitrogen-containing ring (like Porphyrin). One direct example is Phenothiazin, which is pictured below ([https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Phenothiazine](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Phenothiazine)). Heme Porphyrin is pictured below Phenothiazin.

Here is a direct study of UV irradiation (close tho 380nm band), in which production of Phenothiazine in a more basic environment (pH 7 -8) allowed for stability of the cytochrome c protein, while a lack of Phenothiazine in an acidic pH led to destruction of the protein -- [http://journals.plos.org/plosone/article?id=10.1371/journal.pone.0076857](http://journals.plos.org/plosone/article?id=10.1371/journal.pone.0076857)

I am not sure how to apply this clinically. Will be interesting to see where the Quantlet biohack goes

### Jack Kruse — 2015-07-25T06:28:03+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/kruses-law.10700/post-172925>

Eukaryotic cells all release ELF UV light, and in Roeland Van Wijk, book Life Sculpting Light, you begin to see how O2 consumption and cellular light release seem linked in a very interesting way. Hemoglobin (_made of porphyrins_) carries O2 but it appears that free radical release and O2 utilization are the key to light generation in cells. This makes looking at RBC's contents critical (_porphyrins_). They are loaded with hemoglobin, have no mitochondria, and are loaded with catalase and deal with H2O2. H2O2 breaks down into H2O and O2 in most places but not in arteries or in veins where **hemoglobin is present.** Many heme-binding proteins have diverse functions. Some known functions include electron-transferring cytochromes, intracellular peroxidases and lignin-degrading extracellular peroxidases. Peroxidases are heme-containing enzymes that use **hydrogen peroxide** as the electron acceptor to catalyze a number of oxidative reactions. Did you know the thyroid has a peroxidase that is usually altered in Hashimoto's? Most people with Hashimoto's have low Vitamin D, low UV exposures, excessive blue light exposures and low tissue DHA content.

What protein links T3 to the brain in humans?

### Jack Kruse — 2015-07-25T06:29:26+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/kruses-law.10700/post-172926>

More light is released from cells in pseudohypoxic states. This also explains why fluorescent light has an adverse effect on human tissues exposed to peroxides. RBC’s are loaded with an enzyme catalase. Catalase is present in the peroxisomes of nearly all aerobic cells, serves to protect the cell from the toxic effects of hydrogen peroxide by catalyzing its decomposition into molecular oxygen and water **without** the production of free radicals.

Interesting huh?

I have Hashimoto's (diagnosed 2004). Context: VDR mutation, computer programmer since 1985, lived on a farm eating home grown land animals but only a handful of seafood meals per year for 13 years prior.

All that has turned around now (sun, grounding, no artificial lights in home, limit computer work and use blue blockers, seafood for breakfast and dinner (raw or cooked on very low heat) for about a year.

I have antibodies less than 40 now, but if I stop my desiccated thyroid medication (120mg) they shoot back up to a very high level.

I'm hoping with more time to reverse the Hashi's for good. The extreme fatigue from lack of functional thyroid tissue has already lessened by at least 50%. I realize I'm somewhat alone in this thyroid / hashi bio-hack but keeping my eyes and ears open here on the forums.

Thanks for all the nuggets thus far.

# Thread: Confused, unsure but determined
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/confused-unsure-but-determined.10705/>
**Subforum:** Meet and Greet
**Posts:** 16
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Goldi — 2014-05-01T11:42:50+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/confused-unsure-but-determined.10705/post-124224>

Hi ... I'm not totally new to the site, but new to posting. Never used face book, twitter or a chat room - so that's all new. Overwhelmed by the amount of info. Stunned by the acronyms. .. have already made mistakes sending stuff to the wrong place but what the heck..

Was told about Whole 30/Paleo/Leptin thing - watched a video on utube and started that afternoon. Coming up 3 weeks. My shape has changed as I can tell by my clothes. I've stopped weighing and never considered measuring myself. However, the more I read the less sure I am of doing this correctly. Things seem to have stalled and not sure if this is normal - particularly as the re-set is not about weight loss. However, if I'm not doing something correctly I'd like to be aware and change it.

I'm usually a quick study but this has me really really unsure of what I'm doing. It's not the embracing of fat .. I love fat! It's not giving up sugar, carbs and dairy all at once, I've had no problem with that. It's hearing that honey is ok, coconut water/milk/paste ok, eating "lots" of fruit and "heaps" of vegetables while keeping most of the calories by fat, then protein, and .. 1 cup of veggies .. this doesn't equate to "heaps" of veggies to me.

I'm 67. Was skinny till 5 years of age, (remember hating my skinny arms) then when my tonsils were removed and I obeyed the directive to "eat or you'll die" I started to slowly put on weight. My Mum put me on my first diet at 12 (Mayo Clinic) and during my teens I starved for 4 days eating 2 hard boiled eggs 2 tomatoes and a plate of spinach (lots of black coffee and cigarettes) to stay an Australian size 12. In my 20's I started to think there's something wrong with me, I'm not normal. By 30's I believed something was 'broken' in the way my body handles food. From 30 to 67 one long round of denial and deprivation followed by return to 'healthy' eating and slow relentless return of the same 30lbs.

Healthy eating has meant at various times, no dairy ... omg what about my bones? Very low carbs, omg I have no energy. No meat, omg what about my whole body. Sugar never been a problem, no sweet tooth. Just don't bring out cheese or nuts. It has NOT been about lack of will power.

I had reached a point where I completely accepted myself but was still flummoxed about what it was that was the 'reason'.

I don't seem to have any overt major medical issues similar to many participants here although there must be lots of inflammation going on. I have mild arthritis in my fingers. I used to have a lot of bloating. In the 3 weeks I have lost the bloating, much less gas, can wear my engagement ring again, but the miracle of miracles is .. I'm not hungry. Appetite decreased enormously.. I'd guess by 50%. Night cravings and snacking has gone, I eat 3 meals a day.

For the first 10 days I had no fruit and very very little veggies. No veggies at all at breakfast. I cannot do 50g protein at breakfast but I start cooking my bacon on rising. I started to have the occasional (2/week) coconut water and I added a small fruit serve every second/third day. Last 4 days have added more salad veggies. I'm sourcing the cleanest meat/fish/produce I can find. Not that easy here in Australia. Use bacon fat and coconut oil for cooking and C.O. in my coffee, and the occasional spoonful straight into the mouth, yum!

Darkened the bedroom. Taken out all electrical except tv bolted to wall now turned off at power point. Have to find out what these blue glasses are!

Most of all .. I cannot express the relief of feeling like someone has found 'the reason' I've been looking for for 35 years. Dr Kruse if it was up to me you'd have the Nobel Prize! The ramifications of this are unlimited.

Now I've introduced myself stay tuned for the questions!

### Gagnrad — 2014-05-01T12:23:58+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/confused-unsure-but-determined.10705/post-124225>

Hi. The way the time difference works I expect there are only a handful of people, mostly from Europe and Australasia, here at the moment.

I agree that the acronyms can be confusing. If you'd like to start a thread on one of the boards on acronyms, mentioning any in particular that you'd like clarified, I expect people would be glad to help. I had been thinking of starting one myself in case it would be useful.

I also agree that claims about what one should eat, even _within_ the wider Ancestral Health Movement, are conflicting and even bewildering. I think this partly because the evidence is not always clear, so that people must make assumptions. The movement also exists at the confluence of several different streams. There's a stream flowing from Loren Cordain's work on the Paleo Diet (important, for example, to the Whole30 people whom you mention); then there's the Low Carb people; and then there are streams coming out of wholefood movements and so forth. All these areas overlap somewhat, but there are a lot of different interpretations and ways of thinking in there and there are divergences, too.

I think you should turn a deaf ear to people who speak of "lots of fruit". It's OK to eat some in season, but most modern fruit is highly overbred and it's just not there all year -- not for much of it in Northern Europe, for example. Highly refined "industrial" foods -- sugar, and white flour, (and, actually equally importantly, vegetable oils) -- are worse, though. And perhaps everyone can at least agree on that.

One of the best arguments I've heard for a fairly low-carb, natural wholefoods diet comes in this interview with Dr. Tim Noakes. I know Dr. Kruse approves of Dr. Noakes: he's mentioned him before. Since it's explained so well here, it may be worth a listen:

[http://vinnietortorich.com/2014/04/angriest-trainer-256-dr-timothy-noakes/](http://vinnietortorich.com/2014/04/angriest-trainer-256-dr-timothy-noakes/)

It seems to me that a small amount of honey or coconut water is going to be OK. The low-carb people would probably avoid them totally, but that's perhaps a bit reductionist. AFAIK, there are hundreds of compounds in raw honey, so it perhaps shouldn't just be seen as a source of carbohydrate, and teaspoon or two here or there doesn't amount to much, anyway.

### Inger — 2014-05-01T13:11:06+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/confused-unsure-but-determined.10705/post-124234>

Welcome Goldi.. nice to have you here - and ask away

I like to keep it simple - pretty much ketogenic year round... but I live up North... with an eating disorder background
I do so well on 3 meals a day with mainly raw seafood and some raw heart and liver and seaweeds and wild berries and wild greens and nuts, very little veggies, some organic cucumber and fermented cabbage and dirty carrots from the garden

I always think... natural is the way to go

### Jack Kruse — 2014-05-01T15:15:13+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/confused-unsure-but-determined.10705/post-124267>

If you are from Australia you have a good break coming. I did a webinar with Cyndi O'meara recently and it is due to go live on May 6th on her site changing habits. In this webinar we go back to the basics with leptin but what is new is how electrons tie directly into this situation. It was very easy to understand one of the better one's I have done for newbies. Cyndi told me she has lots of newbies and she wanted to get the leptin story tied to electrons clear and easy.

### SueZ — 2014-05-01T17:15:27+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/confused-unsure-but-determined.10705/post-124321>

Hi Goldi. I identify with your confusion at being new to this and with not being used to chat rooms, facebook sharing etc. Although it is difficult to share publicly, I think that it is worth it since we learn from each other's experiences. I am eating lots of nonstarchy vegetables (6-8 cups a day) at lunch and dinner at this time. I need to avoid all sugars though due to fungal infections. The sugars make them worse. We are each different, but there may be a common ground between people that is helped by sharing. Thank you for sharing your story.

### Goldi — 2014-05-02T01:05:11+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/confused-unsure-but-determined.10705/post-124440>

Wow !!! Thanks to all of you for your replies .. it's not difficult for me to share personally but here in Australia there's very strict rules (written and unwritten) about therapists (I'm a psychotherapist) putting themselves 'out there'. Things have softened a bit recently but hard to shake those deeply ingrained ethical positions from many years standing. Same to be said for my self talk about food/eating /weight etc. Which has been interesting to observe in my 3 weeks re-set. I felt pretty much done with it but here's a new take on eating .. It's natural for our clients to be curious about a therapists personal life but they're better served with it being all about them. If I choose to share it's carefully considered and specific to that client. That all said .. I throw caution to the wind as this is - as mentioned - manna from heaven for me. I have the same words from the same song often in my mind when I think of this work .. wonder of wonders, miracle of miracles .. here is the answer I've been searching for for 35+ years! Why would I not do it? Why would I not follow guidelines? Why would I ever want to deviate if I know the result?

If I'm not reading too much into Dr Kruses' comments thinking and mind set is crucial to whatever we do.

So, answers; TEDx was astounding thanks Caroline. A day each year filled with the hope of the current generation (and some of us from other gens) sharing, helping, caring to create a better world for all. It was this sharing that determined me to change my thoughts about 'public exposure' and post here.

Gagnrad, Your comments were quite reassuring and thank you for the link, which I will absolutely check out.

There _is_ a thread .. well a page that has the acronyms listed but I couldn't get it to open to the explanations .. this was the same day I'd spent 3 hours (yes.. 3) trying to take a selfie where I didn't look like a reject of Walking Dead and upload it. I now know you can't upload files from a camera that's been emailed .. so I was out of time to fiddle around on the acronyms. I've not been able to find my way back to it. I was thinking a printable page would be fab. You could have it right by you and add to it as necessary.

BTW is there a way one can 'bookmark' those links/pages you find you'd return to time and time again. Like a favorites folder? I've done this by having two pages open and bookmarking on the page I'm not reading in regular bookmarks. I still find it a bit confusing to remember what was what. Need better titles .. maybe. Hope this isn't boringly elementary to those who use computers regularly..

I so appreciate all these comments as, although I take full responsibility for my journey through all this - guidelines are so very helpful. Keep it simple is, for me, totally the way to go. But what constitutes simple !

Will check out Cindi O'Meara thoroughly Dr Kruse.. looks very specific and focused. Thank you - thank you at _many_ levels of gratitude!

I used to weigh myself every day. My body is usually immediately reactive to whatever I'm eating/doing so it was a guideline - actually now that I think of it my only 'empirical'/hardcopy guideline. My constant refrain about my weight was, "Nothing makes sense or adds up in the context of current healthy eating guidelines and the responses of my body". Now I'm not weighing (although there is a site that recommends it) I'm floating in this new space of checking out by look (omg do I have body dysmophia) and trusting over time that what I'm doing is working internally to reset my 'broken' bits. Please don't read _I'm_ broken ... it's some body mechanism that's broken. Quite a leap of faith but my gut instinct just knows this is right. I think what needs to be nurtured is trust in my body's innate wisdom regarding food when this mechanism is repaired and how it gets repaired. I trust my body wisdom implicitly in virtually all other areas.

For now .. I'm not worrying about calories because I'm on the reset and will not think of adding anything until I have the indications of reset. Ok .. now you'll really think I'm nuts, but last night just as I lay down to sleep I had this sensation in my head of something changing . Something new emerging. I thought "It's like a Super Nova in my head". I don't know if that's the correct term but it felt like a gentle but significant explosion of 'new' being born in my head. Very interesting, not at all scary but deeply felt.

I need to find a 'fat' link to read but apart from a lot of coconut oil I am using the bacon fat from breakfast to cook in and I'm not to worry about saturated fat like this, correct? I use olive oil on any hot veggies or salad. Still at this stage there needs to be lots of these oils.. correct?

Thanks for the welcome guys ..

### caroline — 2014-05-02T01:15:42+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/confused-unsure-but-determined.10705/post-124445>

Goldie - can you transfer this to the journal section? Or just start a journal..... I will help when I get home ....

Or don't worry ..... See you for coffee soon!

### fitness@home — 2014-05-02T02:34:45+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/confused-unsure-but-determined.10705/post-124460>

Goldi, welcome! You have my respect for the work you do as a psychotherapist. Important for many people to have someone to talk it through with at times in their life.

There are many helpful people here. Just keep asking questions and learning

### Gagnrad — 2014-05-02T10:39:24+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/confused-unsure-but-determined.10705/post-124526>

> [Goldi said:](/goto/post?id=124440)

> I need to find a 'fat' link to read ...

[http://www.westonaprice.org/know-your-fats](http://www.westonaprice.org/know-your-fats)

Keep scrolling and you'll see a list of articles at the bottom of that page. Mary Enig, who wrote most of them, is an expert on lipid biochemistry. The one to start with would be number 20.

### Gagnrad — 2014-05-02T10:42:40+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/confused-unsure-but-determined.10705/post-124527>

> [Goldi said:](/goto/post?id=124440)
>
> BTW is there a way one can 'bookmark' those links/pages you find you'd return to time and time again. Like a favorites folder? I've done this by having two pages open and bookmarking on the page I'm not reading in regular bookmarks. I still find it a bit confusing to remember what was what. Need better titles .. maybe. Hope this isn't boringly elementary to those who use computers regularly.

Yes, you could make a subfolder to hold everything related to "health" or "diet" or "paleo" or whatever. Which operating system are you using and which browser? For example, are you using Microsoft Windows, and is the browser Internet Explorer?

EDIT

I am forgetful. I should add: is your computer regularly backed up to an external hard-disk or anything of the sort? If it's not, you may also like to keep a list of bookmarks online in case, after making a collection of links you want to keep, your computer dies and you lose them.

One solution would be to also save links to an online bookmark service. Here's one:

[https://delicious.com/](https://delicious.com/)

There are some abbreviations that are specific to this site. For example, people will post BAB which stands for "big arse breakfast". You won't find that in any technical documents online.

# Thread: Sleep CT -- your experience...? (or NO COVERS)
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/sleep-ct-your-experience-or-no-covers.10710/>
**Subforum:** Cold Thermogenesis
**Posts:** 66
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### RobH — 2014-05-01T18:05:30+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/sleep-ct-your-experience-or-no-covers.10710/post-124341>

I can happily sit in a 44F tub for an hour. I can wear an ice vest all day. I can jump in a 55F lake and swim around comfortably.... so, I seem to be reasonably cold adapted (haven't checked to see if body temp rises when thinking about CT yet though...)

However... I can't seem to "get" sleeping without covers, even if not that cold.

I try many times to start sleeping without covers but usually half wake up sometime (I don't check clock) and pull them on. Vaguely remember "feeling cold." I also feel vulnerable or something like that.

I know that melatonin is supposed to work better in the cold however it seems harder for me to fall asleep without them. Maybe it's all psychological not physiological (where is the blurry dividing line there?)

I guess I could remove the option entirely but it would be tough to get my wife to do without them.

Just wondered what people's experience / success has been giving up the covers at night?

RobH

### NeilBB — 2014-05-01T18:10:12+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/sleep-ct-your-experience-or-no-covers.10710/post-124343>

I keep the bedroom very cold all year round. Between 60-65 degrees typically. Always sleep naked, but usually do use a sheet on top.

There is something to the vulnerability of it, also for some reason I like the weight or pressure of something over me as I sleep. I keep the house cool always but use the same sheet and light down blanket all year round.

Sleep is precious to me...Im not screwing around with it.

Plus...I still have little kids that think nothing of walking in my bedroom door...a cold naked mommy and daddy sighting is not how any of us want to start our day

### Inger — 2014-05-01T20:42:26+0300

### sooperb — 2014-05-01T21:47:58+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/sleep-ct-your-experience-or-no-covers.10710/post-124416>

I sleep naked, always have and many nights go to bed quite hot, lay outside the covers and think I will try to go to sleep without them. I never manage it, I always seem to become just a bit too cold. I also recognise the vulnerability of sleeping without a cover of some kind, naked under one I do easily, naked without one, really difficult. ### Sue-UK — 2014-05-02T13:09:05+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/sleep-ct-your-experience-or-no-covers.10710/post-124542>

I wonder whether our ancestors would have used covering of some sort whilst they slept to hide from predators for example ones who see say in the infra-red frequency, or any that could detect a fresh “leak” in the natural electromagnetic field of a newly dug pit. Lying on the bare earth or rock covered in a foliage canopy or animal skins could have produced a safer cocoon whilst they slept, and held in and circulated the earth energies rather than leaking them to the environment?  I think predators don’t always seek out the most vulnerable based on visual cues, I reckon they may detect the easiest prey by the one leaking the most energy to the environment. The unhealthiest I have been, the more I have needed to cover up at night. It just made me feel safer.

I’ve got the bedroom colder (about 55f) and the covers lighter, and I’m snuggling under, I woke this morning and I was sooooooo snuggled under that my first thought was that I was self-inducing hypoxia ….. Weird.

I kept thinking about the aborigines of Tierra del Fuego last night. They sleep in a cold, damp depression on the ground with a tiny clump of grass or something to break up the high winds.

> To look at the Wigwam; any little depression in the soil is chosen, over this a few rotten trunks of trees are placed & to windward some tufts of grass. Here 5 or 6 human beings, naked & uncovered from the wind, rain & snow in this tempestuous climate sleep on the wet ground, coiled up like animals. In the morning they rise to pick shell fish at low water; & the women winter & summer dive to collect sea eggs; such miserable food is eked out by tasteless berries & Fungi. Jerking out little fish out of the Beds of Kelp

[http://forum.jackkruse.com/index.php?threads/yaghan-natives-of-south-america.4313/](http://forum.jackkruse.com/index.php?threads/yaghan-natives-of-south-america.4313/)

On the grass/ ground, light cover under (it became wet and damp by morning) and over. Stargazed until I fell asleep. It was a bit chilly out with a cool breeze.

I have and sleep on a magnetico but it didn't compare. Well perhaps it did but it was much more powerful on the ground. Right away went into deep vivid dreams. The kind where your almost awake at times but you can't move your body. And you can't wake up from your dream.... Your frozen.

Woke up exelerated. I'll be doing this more often!

### sjoshua — 2014-06-19T01:21:50+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/sleep-ct-your-experience-or-no-covers.10710/post-131314>

My intuition is that this discussion is answering the initial question in quite the round-about way!

What if the 'comfort' and 'warmth' felt from sheets and a blanket is due to us NOT touching the ground?

Think about it like this: Our friend the Leptin Receptor tallies our electrons sometime around midnight, and then depending on the tally count, determines whether we are lacking or not. If we are at an optimal/sufficient level, then the leptin receptor is going to give the "all is good" signal to the body, which will in turn burn off any extra fat stores that won't be necessary since energy balance is OK. All of these extra fats are being turned into heat, thus the mitigated need for a blanket/covering. Additionally, due to our electron charge, our body will naturally buffer, or repel, the cold.

In nature, that count would be quite accurate. The contact with the earth throughout the entire sleeping period would work to preserve this balance through grounding.

Now, what happens today? Instead of sleeping outside, we decided to sleep indoors. Then we decided to sleep on beds, elevated further from contact with the ground. We lost our connection to Earth for maintaining the balance. Then we took it further... we ran 50-60Hz AC currents all over the place, then plugged a bunch of electronics into them. At this point most people's bodys could still manage some energy balance. Then, we started playing with semiconductors... now he have EMF in our pockets and home in the name of staying "connected" to life. Ha, we asked and the quantum dance produced... our new form of "connected" is stealing energy from us instead of giving it back.

This means in a "modern" home, our friend the Leptin Receptor takes count around midnight if your cells still have a gauge of circadian "time"... and let's say the "all is good" signal is still achieved. Well, leptin receptor was not factoring in the steady stream of electron losses we are subjected to in our modern environments, so part way through the night while your body is still relying on the "all is good" signal and burning excess fat it didn't think it needed, your body will end up at some confusing epiphany of sorts. Coupled with the electrons we were losing to our environments, and the fact we weren't re-attaining any from contact with the earth, our ability to repel cold is diminished.

Even on the "healthier" end of the scale this makes sense. i.e. to those like myself that find a sheet sufficient 99% of the time, the sheet is still amplifying our body's ability to buffer cold, just a lesser extent than a blanket. Sure, we have ample charge to issue the "all is good" command, and our bodies will be burning excess fat stores for heat (which, may help mitigate a blanket's need), but the electron losses will still diminish our ability to buffer the cold sufficiently to ditch the sheet.

Something that Jack said during the June Q&A is that fat oxidation basically generates lots of protons. To stay warm in the cold, your body then needs to be able to use uncoupling proteins to "get rid off" these protons and generate heat. Now the question is, what is required to mobilise the fatty acids in the first place, and then to actually burn them off as heat, and how is that stifled by sleeping indoors on a bed not in contact with the earth?

This is one of those times where I wish I knew all my redox chemistry well. Are there any reactions required to burn fat as heat that are dependent on a consistent electron supply? (which you would get from the ground, but not on your bed)

Well, looks like I need to buy a sheepskin and sleep in the garden for a change. Report back once I get back to Melbourne's close to freezing mornings.

### sjoshua — 2014-06-24T18:35:55+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/sleep-ct-your-experience-or-no-covers.10710/post-132040>

Now the question is, what is required to mobilise the fatty acids in the first place, and then to actually burn them off as heat, and how is that stifled by sleeping indoors on a bed not in contact with the earth?

My take: The fatty acids are mobilized for two reasons: 1 - when they aren't needed (since energy balance is already present; this is waste heat) or 2 - when they are needed (since your body determines your core tempurature is too low; this is purposeful heat). Trigger one of these reasons, and you are mobilized!

EDIT: missed the second part of your question. Reason #1 is going to happen if it needs to happen whether you are indoors in a bed or in contact with the earth. But, understand that reason #1 is only happening to those with an efficient body and a proper redox - aka people that can sleep in a bed, or in a NYC skyscraper, or in an X-ray machine turned on (thic would be contra-indacated ) and either way their system was heathly so they were going to waste some heat.

Reason #2 is the reason people choose to sleep indoors in a bed with blankets and sheets. Due to NOT being healthy, and NOT having an efficient system with a proper energy balance, their systems cannot "afford" to heat them. They rely on the blankets and sheets, and climate controlled environment for their warmth instead. What they think is 'helping' is like putting a bandaid on a broken bone. It might make you feel better for a moment, but its not helping the problem or treating the cause.

> [yewwei.tan said:](/goto/post?id=131390)
>
> This is one of those times where I wish I knew all my redox chemistry well. Are there any reactions required to burn fat as heat that are dependent on a consistent electron supply? (which you would get from the ground, but not on your bed)?

My take: Eukaryotes operate on electrons. So, everything you do including burning fat as heat would require electrons. Having a constant supply to restock said electrons is just making your job easier.

This makes sense!

Also explains why conventional dieting approaches are really bad for the system in general. You're getting a simultaneous decrease in food electrons, with a constant or increased need for body heat generation.

​

I now think it's most likely that "burning body fat" while on a calorie deficit isn't to satisfy some absolute "calories in, calories out" balance. Instead, as sjoshua mentioned, burning body fat is required for heat generation in order to build the EZ of intracellular water -- particularly the mitochondrial water micelles. It may also be that the EZ needs to become larger during times of "caloric deficit" because of the lack of food electrons, and this is the actual driver for body fat mobilisation when you eat less.

Unfortunately for most modern humans (including myself in my past dieting attempts), you need to be able to uncouple the normal proton gradient in order for safe heat generation to take place. A healthy cell which has the correct shape, size, and protein composition can do this.

If uncoupling doesn't work because the cell is unhealthy (energy inefficient due to swelling, bad protein folding, etc ...), heat generation is reduced, while ROSes are simultaneously made. This is a guess, but the cell may be naturally regulating ROS generation, and "refuses to burn more substrate" due to potential high oxidative burden, which explains why people feel so subjectively cold on a diet, and their hormone panels go south.

Then of course the electron demand for the body hasn't been reduced, so in order to meet that demand, the body has to do something. I think this may have something to do with cortisol, but I'm waiting for OSF#7 to shed more light on that topic.

Sidenote: The 'How Do Mitochondria Work' thread was a good catalyst for this line of thought, and I need to research more -- [http://forum.jackkruse.com/index.php?threads/how-do-your-mitochondria-work.11080/page-3#post-131362](http://forum.jackkruse.com/index.php?threads/how-do-your-mitochondria-work.11080/page-3#post-131362)​

\====

Practically speaking, the only way I'm ever going to get to and easily maintain sub-10% body fat levels is to find a way to constantly supply electrons to my system while simultaneously triggering a need for heat generation. Sleeping outside in the cold sounds like the trick.

Now I'm fired up to see if sleeping outside on the ground is going to get me shredded by springtime . Ordered my sheepskin and eager to start this experiment once I'm back in Melbourne.

### sjoshua — 2014-06-26T17:44:13+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/sleep-ct-your-experience-or-no-covers.10710/post-132341>

@[yewwei.tan](https://forum.jackkruse.com/members/6824/) I look forward to hearing how this experiment goes  I'm sleeping alone this week, and there is a high probablity I sleep outside in my backyard (and let the dogs join me) tonight. This drought hasn't left us much grass, but when you lay on the ground all the time, it feels quite nice.

But I missed an important piece, that dawned (ha... pun!) on me overnight related to this.

Light. If your bedroom is not 100% dark and sealed from the outside, then as soon as light starts creeping in, your skin is going to wake you up and tell you to grab that blanket/sheet to 'turn the lights back off' until you are ready to wake up. This is just us not following proper circadian rhythms again

### yewwei.tan — 2014-07-07T05:53:40+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/sleep-ct-your-experience-or-no-covers.10710/post-133340>

> [sjoshua said:](/goto/post?id=132341)
>
> @[yewwei.tan](https://forum.jackkruse.com/members/6824/) I look forward to hearing how this experiment goes  I'm sleeping alone this week, and there is a high probablity I sleep outside in my backyard (and let the dogs join me) tonight. This drought hasn't left us much grass, but when you lay on the ground all the time, it feels quite nice.
>
> But I missed an important piece, that dawned (ha... pun!) on me overnight related to this.
>
> Light. If your bedroom is not 100% dark and sealed from the outside, then as soon as light starts creeping in, your skin is going to wake you up and tell you to grab that blanket/sheet to 'turn the lights back off' until you are ready to wake up. This is just us not following proper circadian rhythms again

Quick preliminary update: I just got back to Melbourne on Saturday, and have tried to sleep outside for the last 2 nights.

First night I wore a jacket and shorts, so that my feet and calves were in constant contact with the ground. Only lasted 1.5hrs  Definitely not easy getting used to this feeling.

Last night I wore a t-shirt and shorts, but brought a blanket with me. Same as the previous night, my lower body was in contact with the ground. This time I was in the garage due to fear of rain. Slept from 10pm until I got up to go to the toilet, and then tried to get back to sleep, but couldn't due to the concrete floor being a little too uncomfortable. Went back to my bed to sleep after that, realised it was already 4:30am, and caught another 2.5hrs of sleep. At least I got 6.5hrs of grounding

So with a blanket, at an ambient temp of supposedly 4C / 39F, I wasn't feeling cold enough to prevent sleep onset. Sleep was as good as usual.

Tonight it's not likely going to rain, so I'll try the garden again, still using a blanket for the time being. At the very least, I'll be getting 8+hrs connected to the earth, which can't be a bad thing.

Anyway, taking baby steps. And hopefully I'll be able to sleep (almost) naked in the garden soon.

\====

**Sidenote on Grounding
**
On my trip to Cairns, I basically spent 12 days of being grounded and exposed to the Sun for at least 2 hours while on the beach. I ate more than usual (+500-700kcal a day), moved around a little more (+300-500kcal in daily expenditure), and my weight dropped from 81.5kg with a 81cm waist, to 78.4kg today with a 78cm waist. I've been tracking calories and activity expenditure for 7 years now, and am pretty sure those numbers are within striking range of accuracy.

There goes the calories-in-calories-out theory out the window  I ate more and lost 3kg / 6.6lbs in bodyweight and 3cm / 1.5inches off my waist, and I wasn't fat to begin with (here's a picture of my starting body composition at 81.5kg -- [http://tanyewwei.com/static/pics/20140524-p.png](http://tanyewwei.com/static/pics/20140524-p.png))

Getting grounded during sleep CT is likely a crucial factor in successfully pulling it off.

I could sleep on ice packs, but DH cannot yet. The first night, after an hour or so, I was miserable. I had to spring out of bed and rip off the grounding sheet. My ears still ring when the a/c is on, but not with such insistence.

### kathylu — 2014-07-09T05:28:50+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/sleep-ct-your-experience-or-no-covers.10710/post-133641>

I have actually noticed that my body temp regulation is much better since I consume more DHA, get better sleep and am at least somewhat cold adapted. I am so grateful that I don't have to deal with painfully cold hands and feet anymore. I sleep naked in the summer with just a sheet for a cover (that I often throw off) and just wear a cotton t shirt in the winter while using a light cotton comforter. So much nicer than having to pile multiple layers of blankets and down on the bed while wearing thermal underwear and two pairs of socks...lol. I'm also warm 98% of the time...while others shiver and complain. So nice!

### Inger — 2014-07-09T08:22:43+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/sleep-ct-your-experience-or-no-covers.10710/post-133662>

I should be packing... and not here... but I just have to tell...

Since it rained and rained here I moved back into my bedroom because i feared my magnetico would rot in the tent. So it has been warm and sunny lately, and in my bedroom it has been warm too... no AC here. and i have no curtains at all in front of the window, just keep it wide open, all light comes in. And it is light until 11 PM.....
anyways - despite the light and the warm - I have been sleeping so well! I get so tired even if sun is still up... around 10 PM.. and then I wake up around 6-7 PM. One day I even slept from 10 PM to 8 PM!
And i checked the temp in my bedroom one morning and it was 19 degree C....

I love it! I do not even do any real CT, only swimming a bit in the river a couple of times / day, just shortly. And taking some super short cold showers and facedunks in the morning.

But I am in the sun many hours a day. And always barefoot! Maybe that is why I do so well... and I eat ketogenic... practically no carbs, just some wild berries and onions etc. Around 2 pounds of seafood and meat / day. Raw.
But I have to drink so much! I am thirsty for more than 4 liter - oh man I just drink and drink... it is almost funny..lol If I have to do something little heavier work I have to drink even more. It is like my body have found back to its real thirst...

I am just so surprised I can sleep so well.... i would not have thought that! With all the stress with packing and moving too! Way cool

But I do not take one single supplement.... and I barely eat chocolate either... and have only 1 cup of black cold cardamom coffee between breakfast and lunch. Maybe that helps.
I bet it is the time nude in the sun that helps too. I am very careful to always fit that into my day!

# Thread: Help gaining weight
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/help-gaining-weight.10711/>
**Subforum:** The New Monster Thread
**Posts:** 7
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### BJK77 — 2014-05-01T18:07:32+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/help-gaining-weight.10711/post-124342>

I need some suggestions on how to gain weight. I understand why I'm losing - electron steal syndrome. I'm losing energy at a very rapid pace. I got that. I'm doing everything I can to gain them back: earthing every day, getting sun whenever possible, eating raw oysters, etc, etc. What else can I do in the meantime? I'm 5'6" and I'm down to 98 lbs. It's getting quite scary. It feels like the more I eat the more I lose right now. It doesn't help that I'm so limited in my diet.

My knee-jerk reaction is to pump up the carbs, but I know that's not the answer. I'm just getting quite scared and desperate. The last time I saw my doctor I was at 104 and he said if I lost any more he would put me in the hospital. Obviously that is the last place I need or want to go!

This is what I'm currently eating:

**Breakfast**
8 oz ground beef
40 gm raw beef fat
seaweed, little zucchini sometimes
2 oz beef liver

**Lunch**
10-12 oysters
avocado

**Dinner**
Scallops or Shrimp

There really are no seasonal carbs in my area right now that I can eat. I can't increase my fat much beyond this - even the extra 40 gm of beef fat in the morning is causing me some issues. I don't digest fat well at all even with taking ox bile. My body creates and passes gallstones like crazy with the more fat I eat. Jack has said to eat even more fat to help the stones, but in me the exact opposite happens.

I do deal with histamine reactions and I have issues with high oxalate foods, so this really limits me.

Any suggestions at all? I NEED to put some weight on soon!

### cantweight — 2014-05-01T20:01:28+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/help-gaining-weight.10711/post-124376>

The only advice I can think of is to try something you have not before. If you keep eating as you are and this is the result it continues to have you might need to mix it up. Your N=1 is unique and it is imperative your body start hanging onto some weight.

Can you get raw milk? Raw Cheese? What about nuts are there any you can tolerate? Would resistant starch help in your case....cooked cooled rice?

### Jonathin — 2014-05-01T20:38:58+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/help-gaining-weight.10711/post-124391>

I am a "skinny" person. I have never been concerned about putting on weight. After almost 2 yrs Epi-paleo and a few trials I have "gained" weight. Since I don't own a scale the only way I know that is that pants that were loose are now not loose around the waist. I have not gained much subcutaneous fat determined by pinching. I feel that my abdomen has gotten larger due to hydration and modest protein intake. I do a few very modest body-weight exercises when I think of it to stimulate the collagen system.
Consider that a quart of water = 2 lbs. Consider that EZ water has a higher density. I wouldn't be surprised that 5 pounds could be gained by proper binding of water to proteins/collagen. How much good water do you drink? Do you magnetize it? Jack's take on getting electrons and plugging electron loss seems to be the key for health dysfunctions and weight normalization.

### Jude — 2014-05-02T07:02:08+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/help-gaining-weight.10711/post-124514>

Like the way you keep on going...all the best!

### sooperb — 2014-05-02T13:15:53+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/help-gaining-weight.10711/post-124543>

Do you not drink coffee, BPC is an easy way to get extra fat. Maybe think back to what you were eating when you weren't losing weight, was there anything there that isn't too off the menu that you could experiment with? I can't remember what is in your journal but why did you start the reset, I presume you've investigated any possible health issues? It can be so difficult getting to the bottom of the why can't it?

### Shijin13 — 2014-05-02T14:57:19+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/help-gaining-weight.10711/post-124546>

BJ

take a look at Hemming's journal. he's working his way back to a normal weight from an ED and over exercising.... he's been doing a really good job trying to capture how do gain weight properly

also.... when we talk about weight gain - you want to gain muscles not fat... how about adding more raw foods in (as you can) as well as starting to do some BW exercises to build muscles... as well as maybe doing some movnat exercises...

### Jack Kruse — 2014-05-02T16:25:23+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/help-gaining-weight.10711/post-124568>

When your body reacts it is usually a mitochondrial problem......what occurs there? Electrons go in and protons come out. The electrons from food you can control.......but what comes out is 100% tied to your environment. What controls what comes out? The ideal functioning of the cytochrome proteins and water around the mitochondria.

What can change things in mitochondria? Two change programs......autophagy and apoptosis. Both require optimal sleep. If sleep is off change is not possible. If change is not possible it must force you to look at your environment WHY.
