# Thread: Amesbury oldest town
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/amesbury-oldest-town.10713/>
**Subforum:** The Cave
**Posts:** 1
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Gagnrad — 2014-05-01T20:03:38+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/amesbury-oldest-town.10713/post-124377>

Investigation shows that Amesbury in Wiltshire, England has been occupied continuously since 8820 BC, making it the oldest town in Britain.

[http://www.bbc.co.uk/news/uk-england-wiltshire-27238503](http://www.bbc.co.uk/news/uk-england-wiltshire-27238503)

The significance of this is that people were not waiting for the Neolithic Revolution to take place before "staying put" as the archaeologist who's interviewed nicely and idiomatically puts it:

> "It provides evidence for people staying put, clearing land, building, and presumably worshipping, monuments.
>
> "The area was clearly a hub point for people to come to from many miles away, and in many ways was a forerunner for what later went on at Stonehenge itself. ...

I was reminded of a book by the distinguished South African scholar David Lewis-Williams in which he argues (among a myriad of other interesting things) that farming may actually have come about _as a result_ of ritual activity, something that was originally suggested a long time ago. For example, if you want to sacrifice wild cattle, you first have to corral them. (That of course, turns the Marxist view of "Historical Materialism", popular in archaeology at one time, on its head, since in that view technological and economic change happens first and people's ideas follow along afterwards -- "base/superstructure".)

[http://www.amazon.co.uk/Inside-Neolithic-Mind-Consciousness-Cosmos/dp/0500288275/](http://www.amazon.co.uk/Inside-Neolithic-Mind-Consciousness-Cosmos/dp/0500288275/)

# Thread: Magnetic polarity?
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/magnetic-polarity.10714/>
**Subforum:** Educating Doctors
**Posts:** 1
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Jack Kruse — 2014-05-01T20:30:15+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/magnetic-polarity.10714/post-124387>

Becker found a magnetic polarity during wakefulness and anesthesia?

Why?

DHA is why.

The pi electrons are the only lipid that has the ability to reverse polarity.

The nuclear Overhauser effect explains this quantum phenomena. It can be seen in NMR spectra when the magnetic polarity is reversed in neural tissue. The intrinsic angular momentum of polarizable elementary particles (electrons) will align with a given direction in a magnetic field. If a region of a molecule has an electron distribution which is polarizable (DHA has that because of its pi electron clouds) that will respond to polarity reversal and is detectable by subtracting the two spectra. It can be seen from NOE spectra of DHA that the polar head group (as expected), methylene group (unexpected), double bond (expected) and terminal methyl (unexpected) responded to reversed polarity. This is why you can wake up and go to sleep and it is also why sleep is associated with autophagy and repair. DHA is the key to your reversal. It is not a paleo solution..........this is the evolutionary Rx for humans.

# Thread: WHY IS DHA THE KEY PIECE TO INFLAMMATION IN HUMANS?
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/why-is-dha-the-key-piece-to-inflammation-in-humans.10715/>
**Subforum:** Educating Doctors
**Posts:** 11
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Jack Kruse — 2014-05-01T20:48:47+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/why-is-dha-the-key-piece-to-inflammation-in-humans.10715/post-124394>

Every single study you find links dietary marine intake correlating with decreases in inflammation.

Why?

It collects electrons is the short answer. Where ever you problem is with inflammation realize you need DHA.

Consider the cell mediated immune system. Its most critical cell that activates both arms of immunity need the macrophage to work.

We've known for a long time that DHA tames inflammation, now, we learn exactly how DHA works: via new substances called maresins. These chemicals are breakdown chaperone proteins from DHA that change Macrophages from inflammatory to anti inflammatory actors in the immune system. This means that macrophages change their function with the addition or subtraction of electrons in maresins.
DHA does this by switching them from M1 to M2 phenotypes in this immune cell line. This cell is very commonly found in the human GI tract. If you got a gut problem........you need the Epi paleo Rx. You need oysters. If you do not like them........then enjoy your illness.

This is a critical spot in autoimmune activation. We encounter inflammation almost daily in our species, but our body has ways of turning it off naturally. DHA is that switch in CMI in macrophages. This is an important step toward understanding exactly this happens. You're likely to be hearing a lot more about maresins when medicine realizes that DHA is an electron story.

In the July 2013 issue of _[The FASEB Journal](http://www.fasebj.org/)_, researchers found that macrophages use DHA to produce the chaperone like "maresins," which serve as the "switch" that turns inflammation off and switches on resolution in cell mediated reactions.

CITES:

Jesmond Dalli, Min Zhu, Nikita A. Vlasenko, Bin Deng, Jesper Z. Haeggström, Nicos A. Petasis, and Charles N. Serhan. The novel 13S,14S-epoxy-maresin is converted by human macrophages to maresin 1 (MaR1), inhibits leukotriene A4 hydrolase (LTA4H), and shifts macrophage phenotype. _FASEB J_ July 2013 27:2573-2583; doi:10.1096/fj.13-227728 ; [http://www.fasebj.org/content/27/7/2573.abstract](http://www.fasebj.org/content/27/7/2573.abstract)

### Jack Kruse — 2014-05-01T21:14:17+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/why-is-dha-the-key-piece-to-inflammation-in-humans.10715/post-124403>

Resolvins and neuroprotectins and maresins can be produced by intra- and transcellular metabolism of DHA. These second messengers, commonly called docosanoids, are potent endogenous anti-inflammatory and proresolving chemical mediators and have major effects in animals that have large brains........They are critical in leptin resistant states in the brain.

### Jack Kruse — 2014-05-01T22:24:23+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/why-is-dha-the-key-piece-to-inflammation-in-humans.10715/post-124419>

Studies have shown that EPA and DHA activate PPARs, which in turn suppress nuclear factor-kappab (NF-kb), the main transcription system that activates inflammation.
One of the principal cell-signaling pathways affected by both EPA and DHA is the peroxisome proliferator-activated receptors, or PPARs. When activated, this pathway inhibits proinflammatory cytokine production, thus greatly reducing inflammation, both systemically and in the brain. ( has a levee in the QUILT)

Thus, DHA, which is more potent than EPA in activating PPARs, may have important implications for reducing inflammation in the brain. A high intake of DHA and EPA also reduces inflammation by displacing arachidonic acid (a proinflammatory precursor) and cholesterol from the cell membrane, reducing the starting material used to make inflammatory mediators. Furthermore, DHA inhibits inflammatory eicosanoid production induced by the release of arachidonic acid from cell membranes.

### Jack Kruse — 2014-05-01T22:28:37+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/why-is-dha-the-key-piece-to-inflammation-in-humans.10715/post-124420>

Deficiencies in DHA have been shown to lower the level of dopamine and the dopamine receptor D2 in the frontal lobe, which can result in problems with attention and learning....This is why the Epi Paleo Rx limits anxiety.

### Jack Kruse — 2014-05-01T22:29:45+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/why-is-dha-the-key-piece-to-inflammation-in-humans.10715/post-124421>

One important effect of DHA-deficient diets was seen in a study using the reward chemical dopamine, which found that long-term omega-3 deficiency raised dopamine levels in a region of the brain closely linked to addiction behavior. This, in essence, significantly increases susceptibility to addictive behaviors, whether to foods, sex, or illicit drugs.

Zimmer L, Vancassel S, Cantagrel S, et al. The dopamine mesocorticolimbic pathway is affected by deficiency in omega-3 polyunsaturated fatty acids.
Am J Clin Nutr. 2002 Apr;75(4):662-7.

### Jack Kruse — 2014-05-02T19:00:53+0300

### NeilBB — 2014-05-02T19:09:45+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/oysters-vs-scallops.10716/post-124649>

I can tell you this Tanya. The part of the scallop that is generally eaten is just the adductor muscle. It corresponds to just the little chewy knob portion on the oyster. The whole scallop is huge, much larger than an oyster, and the rest of the scallop's body is almost always discarded when they clean it, unless you are getting them in the huge shells. So eating a scallop the way we normally eat it is more like eating a "steak from the sea," (its pretty much all muscle meat), than it is eating the whole animal like in the case of the oyster (sans shell). The way we eat mussels is closer to oysters, except that we rarely eat mussels raw.

### JackFrake — 2014-05-02T21:14:14+0300

### sooperb — 2014-05-04T15:53:41+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/oysters-vs-scallops.10716/post-124977>

Okay, so from Oysters:
The good: This food is a good source of Magnesium and Phosphorus, and a very good source of Protein, Vitamin D, Vitamin B12, Iron, Zinc, Copper, Manganese and Selenium.

The bad: This food is high in Sodium, and very high in Cholesterol.

From Scallops:
The good: This food is very low in Saturated Fat. It is also a good source of Vitamin B12, Calcium, Iron, Magnesium, Potassium, Zinc and Copper, and a very good source of Protein, Phosphorus and Selenium.

The bad: This food is high in Cholesterol.

And mussels:
The good: This food is a good source of Vitamin C, Thiamin, Riboflavin, Folate, Phosphorus and Zinc, and a very good source of Protein, Vitamin B12, Iron, Manganese and Selenium.

The bad: This food is high in Cholesterol.

In terms of protein it would actually be scallops, 83% v 44% in oysters. It seems each of those three has a lot to offer individually but actually, a mixture of the three would give the best overall nutritional intake, discounting the cholesterol of course as I think most would agree it isn't an issue.

The 'fatal conceit' of thinking we can centrally plan systems that **naturally evolved**.

Eating _x > y_ b/c it has % of protein _x_, fat _y_, and mgs _z_ will not be the deciding factor of whether you get closer to optimal.

If you're environment sucks, eat as many dense sources of the foods outlined in the epi-paleo Rx. Your bailing water from a leaky boat, and only more hands and buckets (electrons) will keep you afloat long enough to improve the bigger issues.

I've noticed the most improvement (better energy, sleep, digestion, etc) since starting my mornings with raw oysters, treating them as my 'daily multi.'

I then prep my BAB, rotating various foods from the epi-paleo Rx every morning. No idea how many calories, fat, protein, carbs, etc I'm getting. I just know that if I give my body the resources it needs to carry out its countless array of complex processes, it will sort it out better than I can hope to measure.

### Jack Kruse — 2014-05-04T17:12:19+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/oysters-vs-scallops.10716/post-125011>

Iodine is one of 7 nonmetals exist as diatomic molecules in their standard states. Iodine however has some interesting emergent properties. In crystalline form, is semi-lustrous, and a semiconductor in the direction of its layers, both of these attributes being consistent with incipient metallic character. It is also a great insulator. This makes it idea to work in neural synapses to protect DHA and to augment electron tunneling across the synapse. Because iodine has a higher atomic number than the bad halogens, it can intercalate between DHA in synapses to semiconduct. This is how thyroid hormones work as well. Iodine also forms strong hydrogen binding networks which means it works well with water. Iodine most important affect with DHA is its strong electron affinity.......it helps move electrons from the pi electron cloud between DHA molecules at synapses.

> So many only give 50% - if even that much and then they complain it doesn't work.
>
>
> As my DH always used to say .... You gotta be all in ....or stay home....

I would say, it has a price but not the one of money.

Many times you must give up everything, all the things in you mind you thought was right, all the things you where feed with and let go. This is a big one today, when do most do that? When they know they only have a limited time left to live. A trauma...

I live with a simple rule, i love everything about death, i talk about it, i read about it, i feel it. This makes me worship life even more, i know that i could die tomorrow but do not cling on to that, i live my bucket list 24/7/365.

### prAna303 — 2014-05-04T21:25:47+0300

I have always gone the other way... When it gets to much i cut down on them, i think it is entropy dump... I stop showing up when it gets to much fake, when the small details get wiped out because the big effects that become standards demand more noice.

Perhaps this is why i never could stand the normal parts of a relationship, by today standards... It gets to noice and to much must do.

When i am with a girl, it must be sublime... I can stroke a shoulder for hours if it suits my demands, i can worship all the small parts of her but when it gets to do this and do that i must go on. I must dump that chaos because i can not stand more than a few highlights in my life at the same time.

So i tend to keep my friends simple and few but change them quite often IF they do not give me a lift anymore.

> Sooperb, just like I am, is adopting to this new lifestyle at her own pace. The fastest wasy to fix my health, would be to transform myself to an island somewhere and live like Robinson Crusoe - but would anyone want to do that just to restore their health? There are so many different things to consider and keep in balance while making all these changes. So, lighten up, children and start playing nicely.....

Tanya let me be clear........I am not up in arms on anyone. I merely pointing out that each persons trajectory to optimal is a **function of one thing**..........how good they are at positioning themselves to collect electrons, assimilate them, and then use them. When I see a person write on here, I am seeing how they think...........and trust me, I pay deep attention to this. It tells me a lot. When I see poor thinking, I know its because dopamine has left the building because of Electron Steal syndrome. The Key point: **When you stop chasing the wrong things you finally give the right things a chance to catch you**.........What our perceptions are really is not what reality truly is. Most people are not aware of this queerness of life because it is part of quantum design. Our brain creates a reconstruction what we think it is, based upon the number and uses of electrons it currently has at its disposal..............and there in lies the key. The more electrons one has the sharper their focus of reality becomes. Moreover, **_there is not a lot of things to consider_**.........the job of a human brain is to collect assimilate and use electrons........it is very simple concept to grasp. what is harder to get is why this is the case.......and that is what my blog is doing slowly. So when you advocate another way to think about what we should do........I will step in and say what must be said.
If you can’t control what you think, you will not be able to control what you do. Your thoughts control your feelings, and your feelings control your actions. All of this is controlled by electrons in your brain and none of you seem to know it. I do. Being honest may not get you a lot of friends but it gets you the right friends……......the right friends are called starfish.
In today's world there is many experts that focus on one aspect of biology and they try to make it fit the current plight of the patient. I rejected that 10 years ago. When you use nature's universal laws as your only backstop, what is optimal no longer is subject to your truths, beliefs, wants, needs, RCT's, or experts. Why? They are the universal laws to which all things are subject. When you use modern medicine beliefs for every expert, there is an equal and opposite expert. This does nothing but confuse patients. So I really disagree with you and sooperb on many fundamental levels. I am teaching you about the most fundamental aspects of matter and how they determine your trajectory and why you do things that keep you standing still. And it is important you understand why I do.

### cinnamon — 2014-05-05T03:36:05+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/oysters-vs-scallops.10716/post-125196>

> [yewwei.tan said:](/goto/post?id=125185)
>
> ...Still, I can't get to the markets every day to grab raw oysters (since they're not open every day), and I don't have space here for a fish tank to store live oysters . ...

You can keep fresh oysters in the refrigerator if they have not yet been shucked.

Here are some storage notes from the [Prince Edward Island tourism site](http://www.tourismpei.com/pei-oysters) (home of the Malpeque oyster):

**Storing**
Store in cold environment. Live oysters should be refrigerated (flat side of the shell up) for up to 3 weeks at a temperature of 5 degrees Celcius (40 degree F). Oysters prefer airy packaging; do not store in closed containers or plastic bags. Do not wash shells until ready to use. Shucked oysters can be refrigerated up to 1 week or frozen 2 to 3 months.​

### Lahelada — 2014-05-05T03:39:37+0300

Round one goes to the oyster. It wonT open . .. But I have a back up plan . Apparently ehem you lay them on their back with the flat side up they open albeit within a day and you lose the juice which I am absolutely heartbroken about...

### Tanya — 2014-05-05T03:39:49+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/oysters-vs-scallops.10716/post-125199>

> [cinnamon said:](/goto/post?id=125196)
>
> You can keep fresh oysters in the refrigerator if they have not yet been shucked.
>
> Here are some storage notes from the [Prince Edward Island tourism site](http://www.tourismpei.com/pei-oysters) (home of the Malpeque oyster):
>
> **Storing**
> Store in cold environment. Live oysters should be refrigerated (flat side of the shell up) for up to 3 weeks at a temperature of 5 degrees Celcius (40 degree F). Oysters prefer airy packaging; do not store in closed containers or plastic bags. Do not wash shells until ready to use. Shucked oysters can be refrigerated up to 1 week or frozen 2 to 3 months.​

That makes it so much easier for me - I usually use them within 48 hours of getting them....Thank you for that information, cinnamon

### prAna303 — 2014-05-05T09:03:43+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/oysters-vs-scallops.10716/post-125244>

> [Tanya said:](/goto/post?id=125153)
>
> Since eating raw oysters is not an option for me, having them lightly steamed for now is better than none a t all. Perhaps, when I am ready, I could try it again and the definition of insanity will not apply here lol
>
> I am dismayed how easily people can go up in arms against someone who is not in step with how and what they have done to achieve their own progress. Sharing your experience is nice and helpful, but pounding on your chest is best left for a succes story forum, in my opinion. Also, not everyone, for various and unfortunate reasons, is a starfish material...
> Sooperb, just like I am, is adopting to this new lifestyle at her own pace. The fastest wasy to fix my health, would be to transform myself to an island somewhere and live like Robinson Crusoe - but would anyone want to do that just to restore their health? There are so many different things to consider and keep in balance while making all these changes. So, lighten up, children and start playing nicely.....

Okey, to chime in...

> I am dismayed how easily people can go up in arms against someone who is not in step with how and what they have done to achieve their own progress.

This makes me wonder, where could i read that? Is it because, depending on the one who reads the words and put them into their own perspective? Many have come and gone in this place because some of the people here demands the best of us and that is not the same as hunting down the broken ones... Some people will say the hard words and some the soft ones but it is with a purpose of lifting. The world of "thumb up" and "likes" is not going to do it... Perhaps it is just me, i do not read those words between the lines, i read stories about true pain and true success and instead of feeling sorry or just fake supporting i try to push others.

> Sharing your experience is nice and helpful, but pounding on your chest is best left for a succes story forum, in my opinion. Also, not everyone, for various and unfortunate reasons, is a starfish material...

Is this wrong? It could really give someone a different perspective by reading about success... How many pound their chest without any reason? It is not about foods or stuff like that any longer, it is about being alive and understanding what we all are parts of, the milky way and beyond. And starfish, why do you doom others here? Why is not everyone starfish material in your way of thinking, i must ask you... Starfish mode is for everything living, you, dogs, birds, rain and everything that once again is placed upon the milky way.

> The fastest wasy to fix my health, would be to transform myself to an island somewhere and live like Robinson Crusoe - but would anyone want to do that just to restore their health? There are so many different things to consider and keep in balance while making all these changes.

Yes some of us would do that... Some even have done that to be still alive and yes i myself do it every single day... Sorry but in times like these, the simple things become extreme. What is your life worth without health? If you can not be there for others in a powerful state is that any good?

Balance... In Sweden we have words like "lagom" and "jante". They mean just that but in a very special way, you should not try to be anything higher than the person next to you, this is what balance is for me. I want energy balance, transfers BUT in other areas this means, dead ends... Balance means lukewarm and that is a state where nothing has to change, i had it good yesterday and boy i wish i can have it today also, but it is not...

Remember, small changes can make tons of effects in yourself and the ones round you. Try to start demand more of yourself and others and stuff happen.

And one last line... Some of us, i included do make comments. All with different perspective and mindsets but what i really do not like that much here, right now, and that is I... I really do not like the function of "like" on this forum, just like the whole world looks like today it gets going like a virus. Almost any post will be liked and without any words, it is so easy to support but without putting your meaning into words, as a reply it gets quite washed out.

I want words, minds put thru words, i want to see what you all bring to the table, everyone can and must talk so we can discuss the big questions not the food issues.

### ssj3 — 2014-05-05T11:14:06+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/oysters-vs-scallops.10716/post-125246>

> Your Rx is usually found in what you do not like.........

Still don't think I can bring myself to eat pumpkin soup and frozen peas... it took 25 years to start eating potatoes if they are well burnt or dowsed in vinegar.

On serious note... should oysters be eaten all year round, including during spawning season? Since they do not taste as great and spawning is induced when water temps increase nutrient availability they may even be better during this time. However, at this time it is peak season for other crustaceans like cockles (vongole).

### Sue-UK — 2014-05-05T13:35:53+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/oysters-vs-scallops.10716/post-125262>

Soo, I find it easier to think that it's not it being an oyster, but a great source of electrons. The worse your environment, or the more you leak them, the more you need to gather electrons. When your environment is OK, or you have good ongoing electron status, the less important food becomes as an electron source. (Light, magnetism, water). As we move around our environment (for example a trip to a city with a lot of nn EMF) electron dense foods may become more important again until we find our balance.

My personal experience is that I had to get to a point where I had collected and was holding enough electrons to change the way I think, and did something so outside my comfort zone that my understanding shifted ... ..With me it was raw lamb heart, I chopped up supplement sized pieces and dropped them down my throat, gagging. I got the theory about not chewing, the gut bacteria etc, dithered for a bit and then just did it ..... If it had been easy I don't think I would have understood, and its not easy describing the mental shift .....

Jack says thoughts can change your DNA, but I find it easier to think that thoughts can change my electron status and that my electron status can change my thoughts. (I was thinking about E=MC 2 and MC 2 =E) So I had to work the equation backwards first, collect enough electrons from wherever I could to change my thoughts sufficiently enough to now be able to begin to work the equation forwards .... I'm gonna need to order those oysters

I agree with you Peter about the "like" function….it's a kind of ersatz communication, half-mast instead of fully developed. But it is a way for people to feel like they're connected in some form…you just have to question the value of the form.

Good points all around and I think I'll drop my "like" habit and see what happens.

As for support….it's tricky, because you don't really know where someone is in their process. If you are teetering on the edge of capacity a little support can be a huge help….if you are STUCK then support is often a reinforcer of this state…..and the same story comes forth over and over. I agree that change, however small, is the key. As long as you keep moving and changing, experimenting and challenging your beliefs….then magic starts to happen and you may end up on an island recovering your health. As you said: "The simple things become extreme."

### JanSz — 2014-05-05T17:17:22+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/oysters-vs-scallops.10716/post-125283>

Is there information available on "high" oysters or "high" seafood?
Would eating it be beneficial?
I mean "high" in the context of "high" meat.
Inger eats high meat and benefits greatly from it.
IIRC she keeps it in refrigerator for months but air it from time to time,
but there are also low tech methods.

To open couple dozens of oysters is a hard work, I assume that oysters left on kitchen table for few days would open on their own.

\-------
High meat is a meat left to ferment on its own, typically at room temperature, for weeks and sometimes months. The end product is teaming with stable bacterial cultures and typically has strong taste and smell. Many indigenous peoples use high meat for healing and to improve mood.
[http://wiki.answers.com/Q/What\_is\_high\_meat](http://wiki.answers.com/Q/What_is_high_meat)

...

### Tanya — 2014-05-05T17:28:27+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/oysters-vs-scallops.10716/post-125286>

> [SeaHorse said:](/goto/post?id=125266)
>
> I agree with you Peter about the "like" function….it's a kind of ersatz communication, half-mast instead of fully developed. But it is a way for people to feel like they're connected in some form…you just have to question the value of the form.
>
> Good points all around and I think I'll drop my "like" habit and see what happens.
>
> As for support….it's tricky, because you don't really know where someone is in their process. If you are teetering on the edge of capacity a little support can be a huge help….if you are STUCK then support is often a reinforcer of this state…..and the same story comes forth over and over. I agree that change, however small, is the key. As long as you keep moving and changing, experimenting and challenging your beliefs….then magic starts to happen and you may end up on an island recovering your health. As you said: "The simple things become extreme."

Obviously, I am still using the "like" button", because I agree with what you said, SeaHorse.

I am always so grateful for all the support and encouragement that people are giving each other here. There were a few times that I also shared a few tips when I thought they might be of help...."Pushing" someone, on the other hand, without knowing their circumstances and before they are ready, does not fit that category, in my opinion.

Last year while I was in the middle of fighting my RA, I lost two very close to me people - my sister and my best friend. My sister died suddenly from a heart attack, but it took almost 6 month of huge suffering before my friend was gone. It all started for her with some pain around her knees. All the tests that she underwent did not disclose the source for that pain which was getting worse very quickly. Still not seeing any swelling, I kept pushing her to exercise more, thinking that it might help.... It did not. Only 3 mod. before she finally died, I learned that she had Lou Gehrig's disease that she did not want anyone to know about. I am still crying every time when I think how impatient I must have been with her when she was refusing to follow my suggestions at that time....

If what Peter said in his "chime-in" made him feel good about himself, I am happy for him.

### RobH — 2014-05-05T17:40:47+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/oysters-vs-scallops.10716/post-125288>

> [caroline said:](/goto/post?id=125084)
>
> We were paying around $3.50 a piece here in Florida. ....you gotta do what you gotta do....

Ouch. Was out of town for my birthday dinner and we went to a nice little place advertising steak and seafood. Only fried oysters on the menu. None of us were happy with that option!

The proprietor stopped by to chat and tell us about the specials. As an afterthought as he started to walk away, I asked "do you happen to serve raw oysters!"

"Indeed we do and today is Friday so they are only 50 cents. I have been doing 50 cent oysters on Wednesdays and Fridays for years."

One of the best dinners I've ever had. 4 dozen nice little kumamoto oysters.

What a great birthday present that was.

Usually I pay about $1.20 for them at the local seafood market. my thought is that the more I spend on oysters (still not enough) the less of everything else I eat....

RobH

### JanSz — 2014-05-05T17:42:01+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/oysters-vs-scallops.10716/post-125289>

There are variety of oysters.
Are they all of same health benefits when eaten?

In my American supermarket dozen of live oysters cost $6.50 (up $1 recently)
They are about the same size as I can get in restaurant.
2-3 dozens at one sitting is about right for me.

Nearby me, 3 miles, in Asian supermarket, (Kam Man Supermarket) I can get a one pound jar with pre-shucked oysters, $8.99, I can eat it usually in two sittings. They are large, to swallow it without chewing I have to cut them in quarters.
It takes three dozens of live oysters from American supermarket to fill up that jar, they are labeled (Medium Oysters).
\------------

[http://hmart.com/](http://hmart.com/)

[http://www.kammanfoods.com/en/index.asp](http://www.kammanfoods.com/en/index.asp)

\------------

I have two types of oyster knifes.
Neither one works on its own.
Some oysters are specially difficult.

I am assuming that I lack technique.
The usual description of the process is:

Insert a paring or oyster knife between the shells, near the hinge.
Twist the knife so that the oyster's muscles are detached.

Sounds simple, but it is not.
What I am missing?

/

> In my American supermarket dozen of live oysters cost $6.50 (up $1 recently)
> They are about the same size as I can get in restaurant.
> 2-3 dozens at one sitting is about right for me.
>
> Nearby me, 3 miles, in Asian supermarket, (Kam Man Supermarket) I can get a one pound jar with pre-shucked oysters, $8.99, I can eat it usually in two sittings. They are large, to swallow it without chewing I have to cut them in quarters.
> It takes three dozens of live oysters from American supermarket to fill up that jar, they are labeled (Medium Oysters).
> \------------
>
>
> [http://hmart.com/](http://hmart.com/)
>
> [http://www.kammanfoods.com/en/index.asp](http://www.kammanfoods.com/en/index.asp)
>
> \------------
>
> I have two types of oyster knifes.
> Neither one works on its own.
> Some oysters are specially difficult.
>
> I am assuming that I lack technique.
> The usual description of the process is:
>
> Insert a paring or oyster knife between the shells, near the hinge.
> Twist the knife so that the oyster's muscles are detached.
>
> Sounds simple, but it is not.
> What I am missing?
>
>
> /

I can not open them myself either, but once they are lightly steamed they open up.

### Jack Kruse — 2014-05-05T17:55:00+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/oysters-vs-scallops.10716/post-125296>

no oyster has beaten yet. It is a point of pride.

### RobH — 2014-05-05T17:57:33+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/oysters-vs-scallops.10716/post-125298>

> [Lahelada said:](/goto/post?id=125157)
>
> I went out and bought one oyster. I will open it tonight. If I dont do tell me where they stamp the exiry date on that thing.... It came with two barnacles attched. The dog is ready and lwilling. Me? I dont think lI will ever be ready so now is asgood a time as any

You can do it!

I want to encourage you -- it an acquired taste and you won't love the first one, or the second. But keep trying. get relatively small ones, tilt your head back and let them slide on down. Don't chew them, just swallow.

I grew up inland. Hated eating the awful "filet of sole" my mom cooked. Never liked seafood. Thought there could be nothing more disgusting than an oyster. Probably emblematic of my problem as I really didn't even started eating salmon until age 30.

It has been an acquired taste for sure and I started out grilling them and dousing with butter. I actually find a I prefer the raw ones now, and usually the smaller ones (the large ones sold around here as "BBQ Oysters" aren't as tasty and are in fact better grilled. I finally learned to shuck them.

We are eating much more shellfish than ever -- oysters, clams, mussels. It is really good stuff. I never feel hungry after oysters (never feel much hunger much anymore anyway). I finally go out of my way to eat seafood as opposed to avoiding it in my previous life...

I know I need to eat more and more of them. I'd rather spend my food budget dollars there than on much of anything else. They cost more up front but I eat way less than I used to in terms of volume and overall cost.

Am building a "shell midden" in my back yard under willow tree so that archaeologists someday will know a shellfish eater lived here... 8^)

RobH

### RobH — 2014-05-05T18:07:21+0300

> [Jack Kruse said:](/goto/post?id=125290)
>
> It has by me.....I find anyone over 260 seem to fair best.

You realize (I know you do!) that currently for a conventional doctor to see that number and not push STATINs would likely be considered malpractice. ARRRGGGHH.

Just got mine back. 190 total (no statin!). Long way to go to optimal. No wonder I'm not thinking so great.

RobH

### Paul S — 2014-05-05T18:09:06+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/oysters-vs-scallops.10716/post-125301>

> [Sue-UK said:](/goto/post?id=125262)
>
> Soo, I find it easier to think that it's not it being an oyster, but a great source of electrons. The worse your environment, or the more you leak them, the more you need to gather electrons. When your environment is OK, or you have good ongoing electron status, the less important food becomes as an electron source. (Light, magnetism, water). As we move around our environment (for example a trip to a city with a lot of nn EMF) electron dense foods may become more important again until we find our balance.
>
> My personal experience is that I had to get to a point where I had collected and was holding enough electrons to change the way I think, and did something so outside my comfort zone that my understanding shifted ... ..With me it was raw lamb heart, I chopped up supplement sized pieces and dropped them down my throat, gagging. I got the theory about not chewing, the gut bacteria etc, dithered for a bit and then just did it ..... If it had been easy I don't think I would have understood, and its not easy describing the mental shift .....
>
> Jack says thoughts can change your DNA, but I find it easier to think that thoughts can change my electron status and that my electron status can change my thoughts. (I was thinking about E=MC 2 and MC 2 =E) So I had to work the equation backwards first, collect enough electrons from wherever I could to change my thoughts sufficiently enough to now be able to begin to work the equation forwards .... I'm gonna need to order those oysters

Sue - how has your experience with raw meat been going (aside from how you described your first attempts at it above)?

**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/oysters-vs-scallops.10716/post-125320>

> [Jack Kruse said:](/goto/post?id=125290)
>
> It has by me.....I find anyone over 260 seem to fair best.

My experience has been that every time my cholosteral gets measured, it is usually over 260 for total, the first words out my doctor's mouth are "here is your statin prescription." My response is usually, "Really!??" How absurd. Then the doc looks at my other numbers as says "Keep doing whatever you are doing." How long is the tyranny of the numbers going to last, when will it end? And even my insurance company penalizes me for having high cholesterol--they base their pricing decisions on antiquated and wrong science/medicine. Ridiculous, but you can't seem to beat the system. I have never and will never take statins, and I am proud of my cholesterol numbers because they are what they are--naturally.

Sue..I've been having raw heart almost every day for the past few weeks….I often will sauté some mixed shredded veg or cabbage till hot and wilted, then cut up the heart in very thin small sections…dump the heart on top of the hot veggies, mix it up put on some garlic/dulse sprinkles and there you go….I wouldn't even know what to call it, but it actually taste lovely and I'm eating a lot of raw because of it.

### sooperb — 2014-05-06T00:41:03+0300

### Danco3636 — 2014-05-06T02:32:27+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/oysters-vs-scallops.10716/post-125376>

When I was bringing my nieces out for visits from Texas I would always take them down to Santa Cruz CA and my kids and I would introduce and share in fresh oysters. My kids now older love seafood. I am known as the crazy uncle. They will never forget it and has instilled the flavor in them at a young age. I have some great pictures of them slurping them down off the shell for the first time.

Now last week I had my 50th birthday party. Got to share with some good friends and my son along with his girlfriend BBQ'ed oysters, salmon and bacon, YUM! It was a lot of fun chowing them down and seeing everyone enjoying my cooking. My aunt was loving them..... The oysters we had both raw and BBQ'ed.
No Birthday cake at my party..... Not allowed. The bacon was the cake and it went fast.

Like a good wine, you build a liking and sharing of them.

Now if I can only get my wife to like them. She likes, salmon, sashimi, fish eggs and nato so that's a win!

This is a fun thread.....

### sooperb — 2014-05-06T10:32:27+0300

We can't get tinned oysters without smoke, scoured the net but can't find any. A company called Epicure used to do them but not any more. OH used to use them occasionally for a Cordon Bleu stuffing recipe for the Christmas bird. We don't even look in a Cordon Bleu cookbook these days.

Working my way through my raw steak and have some raw tiger prawns too. Not sure about them they don't taste of anything much. Well they do actually taste a bit like prawn. Maybe some garlic mayo would cheer them up except I don't have any. It's odd, we're really conditioned to chew aren't we and trying to swallow without doing so is quite a challenge. I've failed so far lol.

### Gagnrad — 2014-05-06T11:33:12+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/oysters-vs-scallops.10716/post-125440>

> [sooperb said:](/goto/post?id=125435)
>
> We can't get tinned oysters without smoke, scoured the net but can't find any. A company called Epicure used to do them but not any more. OH used to use them occasionally for a Cordon Bleu stuffing recipe for the Christmas bird. We don't even look in a Cordon Bleu cookbook these days.

Of course, oysters aren't in season for us in Britain now.

Interesting to read how some bands of hunter-gatherers would wander around between different sites to get what was -- down to the coast for the shellfish season, over to where the prickly pear grew when that was abundant, and so on. It must have given a rhythm to their lives.

On tiger prawns, you may want to consider this linked article -- huge environmental and social issues:

> This sudden glut in tiger, or warm-water prawns, is the result not of a natural harvest but of an explosion of intensive fish farming in ... from Thailand, Vietnam, Indonesia, Pakistan, and Bangladesh, but also from Latin America - from Ecuador, Honduras, Guatemala, and Mexico ...
>
> The price of providing an everyday luxury for consumers in industrialised countries has been a catalogue of damaging consequences in developing nations. Serious environmental degradation, disease, pollution, debt and dispossession, illegal land seizures, abuse of child labour and violence have afflicted the dozen or so countries entering the market. Western diners, meanwhile, are eating a food dependent on the heavy use of antibiotics and growth hormones. ...
>
> In Vietnam, more than 80% of original mangrove cover has been deforested in the last 50 years. ...
>
> But the environmental damage goes wider than that. Shrimp are carnivorous, and farming them intensively requires protein feeds of more than double the weight of the prawn produced. Inevitably, the fishmeal and fish oil required to raise the shrimp further deplete wild stocks of fish.
>
> Salination and chemical pollution of drinking water and agricultural land also frequently result from prawn farming. The farms pump out their waste water into the canals, rivers and nearby seawaters, contaminating them with pesticides, antibiotics and disinfectants. In Sri Lanka, 74% of fishermen in shrimp farming areas no longer have ready access to drinking water.
>
> Evidence from several countries suggests that the farming is unsustainable not just in the long term but after a few years. Disease quickly sets in and productivity declines rapidly.
> Dr Duong Van Ni, a Vietnamese hydrologist at Cantho University, who has studied the social impact of prawn farming, is gloomy about the immediate future. "Shrimp farming will be Vietnam's final choice, because it is so damaging to the environment and so polluting to the soil, trees, and water, that it will be the last form of agriculture. After it, you can do nothing." ...

[http://www.theguardian.com/news/2003/jun/19/food.fishing](http://www.theguardian.com/news/2003/jun/19/food.fishing)

### nonchalant — 2014-05-07T16:30:00+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/oysters-vs-scallops.10716/post-125633>

I wanted to try oysters for breakfast, so I bought a jar yesterday from the grocery store. Pacific oysters, extra small. This morning I see that yes, **some** of the oysters are small...

I sit outside, watching the sunrise though it is somewhat obscured, and start swallowing the smaller ones. It is a bit more difficult than I'm used to, however, and I balk on the third one. Perhaps it is the taste that is different than the gulf oysters I normally slurp? I put the rest back into the jar to attempt at another time. And to think I actually spent more on them than I do for a dozen at The Fish Shack!

Nonchalant, I have tried canned oysters and jarred oysters. Horrible compared to the real, raw, fresh oysters you get at the local pub. Texture, taste, and smell are nearly revolting to me. I only eat the fresh ones now. The oysters that come in a jar or a can would be a last resort--from hell. Not enough horseradish and tobasco sauce on the planet to make them go down easy.

Randy

### Gagnrad — 2014-05-07T21:03:06+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/oysters-vs-scallops.10716/post-125674>

> [HoneyChild said:](/goto/post?id=125668)
>
> I couldnn't find a suitable knife for opening my oysters so I put one in the oven whilst I was reheating something for my Dad and it worked out very well. It was just a small oyster and managed to swallow it whole

Well, that's authentically Palaeolithic of you.

You can't, of course, open an oyster with a flint blade (you need a steel blade for that). If you did manage to get it in at the hinge, the blade would fracture when you twisted. So how did prehistoric man open oysters? -- and, as far as I can tell, bivalves in general? -- he put them on the fire and steamed them open. (Effectively, similar to what you'd do for _moules marinière_, except you'd use a saucepan). Hence the large middens of scorched shells that turn up at prehistoric sites.

Here's one description -- which, as it happens, has been posted on these boards before:

[http://www.pasthorizonspr.com/index.php/archives/01/2014/opening-oysters-shells-the-mesolithic-way](http://www.pasthorizonspr.com/index.php/archives/01/2014/opening-oysters-shells-the-mesolithic-way)

### yewwei.tan — 2014-05-08T12:33:22+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/oysters-vs-scallops.10716/post-125812>

Haha, there's so many Prestons around the world thanks to the Brits.  This Preston I'm referring to is about 10km North and slightly East of the Melbourne (Victoria) CBD.

Mother's Day is also the 2nd Sunday of May here in Australia. Gotta get my seafood before they all run out. I'm sure Yianni at my favourite 'Melbourne Seafood and Oyster Spot' will have some oysters waiting for me -- [https://www.facebook.com/SeafoodandOysterSpot](https://www.facebook.com/SeafoodandOysterSpot)

### Gagnrad — 2014-05-08T12:38:49+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/oysters-vs-scallops.10716/post-125814>

> [caroline said:](/goto/post?id=125809)
>
> never heard of it .... is that what Yew said?

Yeah, he said he went to a grocer's in Preston. It may be a district of another town I guess. That's often the case over here, although everyone always thinks of Preston, Lancashire, of course.

On the other thing I'd mentioned ... have you noticed how behind the traditional seasons and festivals and holidays there's the agricultural year? And behind that the seasons and the movement of the earth as it orbits the sun. That's essentially why they are when they are. Or that was so.

Now, of course, our lives tend to be shaped by the needs of industry. (It's probably no accident that the Reformation preceded the Industrial Revolution. Even after that I understand that people were likely to down tools whenever some particular saint's day came round.) In England people now want to make all the school terms the same length and dispense with the long summer holiday. That was there, because the children used to help with the harvest. I think it a shame if it does happen -- nice for the children to enjoy a long break in the hot weather.

Anyway, the other thing that occurred to me was how curious it must be for you in Australia to have the year marked by a rhythm that belongs to the Northern hemisphere and so is upside-down for you. But perhaps people don't, in practice, notice.

### Sue-UK — 2014-05-08T13:52:17+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/oysters-vs-scallops.10716/post-125824>

> [Gagnrad said:](/goto/post?id=125674)
>
> Well, that's authentically Palaeolithic of you.
>
> You can't, of course, open an oyster with a flint blade (you need a steel blade for that). If you did manage to get it in at the hinge, the blade would fracture when you twisted. So how did prehistoric man open oysters? -- and, as far as I can tell, bivalves in general? -- he put them on the fire and steamed them open. (Effectively, similar to what you'd do for _moules marinière_, except you'd use a saucepan). Hence the large middens of scorched shells that turn up at prehistoric sites.
>
> Here's one description -- which, as it happens, has been posted on these boards before:
>
> [http://www.pasthorizonspr.com/index.php/archives/01/2014/opening-oysters-shells-the-mesolithic-way](http://www.pasthorizonspr.com/index.php/archives/01/2014/opening-oysters-shells-the-mesolithic-way)

I wonder how long you'd have to scorch the outside or at what temperature to open the shell while leaving the inside technically raw...? Thinking of the "proton push" in Pollacks book ....His example is pyramid construction, but the principle is the same ... ?

### Gagnrad — 2014-05-08T14:11:29+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/oysters-vs-scallops.10716/post-125830>

> [Sue-UK said:](/goto/post?id=125824)
>
> I wonder how long you'd have to scorch the outside or at what temperature to open the shell while leaving the inside technically raw...?

If you want to eat them raw, why not use an oyster knife? If you have difficulty getting it in there, put the oyster on a board and whack the haft of the knife with a rolling pin. Shockingly inelegant use of blunt force, but it works.

Or you could hie yourself down the shore and gather some limpets, which wouldn't require opening.

### Gagnrad — 2014-05-08T15:36:08+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/oysters-vs-scallops.10716/post-125847>

> [Sue-UK said:](/goto/post?id=125844)
>
> I was musing that maybe the ancients were cleverly using radiant heat, the EZ and proton power to open them up, and there is probably a knack to doing that whilst still leaving the inside raw. Can't experiment at the moment though as I've only got frozen oyster meat, but if I get any fresh in shells, I might borrow a blowtorch and experiment.

Well, yes, they were using heat to open them, Sue, but I doubt they were trying to "leav\[e\] the inside raw". It's not clear to me whether they intended to cook them, though: it may have merely been a case of trying to get the damn things open.

However, archaeologists at the Cueva Bajondillo site near Malaga -- I wonder if @[Albert83BCN](https://forum.jackkruse.com/members/5984/) knows it -- seem to think that Neanderthalers were "cooking" shellfish. This site is significant, because it had been thought by some authorities that only modern humans exploited shellfish and that there was a close link there to our apparently superior intelligence. But, in fact:

> "The use of shellfish resources by Neanderthals in southern Spain started some 150,000 years ago," the paper concluded. "It was almost contemporaneous to Pinnacle Point (in South Africa) when shellfishing is first documented in archaic modern humans."

[http://www.telegraph.co.uk/news/wor...food-far-earlier-than-previously-thought.html](http://www.telegraph.co.uk/news/worldnews/europe/spain/8765346/Neanderthal-man-lived-on-seafood-far-earlier-than-previously-thought.html)

Neanderthal Man never ceases to surprise, though:

[http://www.nhm.ac.uk/about-us/news/...rthal-diet-like-early-modern-humans21201.html](http://www.nhm.ac.uk/about-us/news/2008/september/neanderthal-diet-like-early-modern-humans21201.html)

### Albert83BCN — 2014-05-08T16:18:57+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/oysters-vs-scallops.10716/post-125857>

I didn't know about it @[Gagnrad](https://forum.jackkruse.com/members/9746/), I suck big time when it comes to history. I've just done a quick search on the topic and if researchers got it right, it really seems like they could be eating cooked shelfish more than 150.000 years ago. Probably the first ones applying the Epi-Paleo RX? LOL

From a Spanish article I could find a research paper on this exact subject. On that paper, though, I could not see any statement implying that they were cooking them, or not:
_["Earliest Known Use of Marine Respurces by Neanderthals"](http://www.plosone.org/article/info%3Adoi%2F10.1371%2Fjournal.pone.0024026)

"It has been recently claimed that the expansion of the diet to incorporate foods of marine origin constitutes a key adaptation of modern humans (_Homo sapiens_) and a reflection of a major shift taking place in the cognitive capabilities of the species [\[24\]](http://www.plosone.org/article/info%3Adoi%2F10.1371%2Fjournal.pone.0024026#pone.0024026-Klein1)–[\[29\]](http://www.plosone.org/article/info%3Adoi%2F10.1371%2Fjournal.pone.0024026#pone.0024026-Marean3). In addition, this coastal adaptation, exemplified by marine mollusk exploitation, has been postulated as crucial to a potential coastal route of modern humans out of Africa to Asia via the Red Sea after ~120 ka[\[30\]](http://www.plosone.org/article/info%3Adoi%2F10.1371%2Fjournal.pone.0024026#pone.0024026-Walter1)–[\[31\]](http://www.plosone.org/article/info%3Adoi%2F10.1371%2Fjournal.pone.0024026#pone.0024026-Bruggemann1). However, the earliest evidence reveals that coastal resource exploitation was already present 164 ka in Pinnacle Point, South Africa [\[25\]](http://www.plosone.org/article/info%3Adoi%2F10.1371%2Fjournal.pone.0024026#pone.0024026-Marean1), [\[29\]](http://www.plosone.org/article/info%3Adoi%2F10.1371%2Fjournal.pone.0024026#pone.0024026-Marean3), [\[32\]](http://www.plosone.org/article/info%3Adoi%2F10.1371%2Fjournal.pone.0024026#pone.0024026-Jerardino1), long before these migrations took place, and the claims that intensive shellfish collecting is a trait of modern humans disregard those studies documenting Neanderthal use of shellfish during MIS 3 [\[1\]](http://www.plosone.org/article/info%3Adoi%2F10.1371%2Fjournal.pone.0024026#pone.0024026-Colonese1).

...

The data presented provide compelling evidence that shellfish harvesting was part of the Neanderthal trophic niche at essentially the same time when Modern Humans were also exploiting the coast in South Africa [\[25\]](http://www.plosone.org/article/info%3Adoi%2F10.1371%2Fjournal.pone.0024026#pone.0024026-Marean1)–[\[29\]](http://www.plosone.org/article/info%3Adoi%2F10.1371%2Fjournal.pone.0024026#pone.0024026-Marean3), [\[32\]](http://www.plosone.org/article/info%3Adoi%2F10.1371%2Fjournal.pone.0024026#pone.0024026-Jerardino1). Although such behavioural convergence may strike some as remarkable it is probably far from extraordinary, given the use of stones for breaking open oysters, crabs and other coastal fauna by certain primates in Asia, first documented by Carpenter in 1887 and “rediscovered” in the aftermath of the 2004 tsunami[\[33\]](http://www.plosone.org/article/info%3Adoi%2F10.1371%2Fjournal.pone.0024026#pone.0024026-Carpenter1)–[\[34\]](http://www.plosone.org/article/info%3Adoi%2F10.1371%2Fjournal.pone.0024026#pone.0024026-Malaivijitnond1).

...

From such a perspective, and although undisputable evidence is presently lacking, it might well be that early hominids started exploiting marine mollusks along the Mediterranean shores as early as the Middle Pleistocene. Reflecting on it, as well as on data such as the 800 ka-old stone tools from the island of Flores [\[35\]](http://www.plosone.org/article/info%3Adoi%2F10.1371%2Fjournal.pone.0024026#pone.0024026-Brumm1)–[\[36\]](http://www.plosone.org/article/info%3Adoi%2F10.1371%2Fjournal.pone.0024026#pone.0024026-Finlayson1), the contraction of Neanderthal populations [\[37\]](http://www.plosone.org/article/info%3Adoi%2F10.1371%2Fjournal.pone.0024026#pone.0024026-Finlayson2)after this first documentation of their adaptation to the coast runs counter to some of the expectations of the proposed model of territorial expansion for modern humans out of Africa.

The coincidence of dates at Bajondillo Cave with those from Pinnacle Point in South Africa, suggest that shellfish gathering reveals yet another case where Neanderthals and Modern Humans might have been following parallel behavioural trajectories, with different evolutionary outcomes. For that reason, and also because shellfish gathering appears to be totally disconnected from the symbolic sphere, those data reinforce our suspicion that the coastal adaptation, however important it might have been at the local level of specific populations, may be yet another overrated phenomenon in the list of behaviors long considered to represent modernity [\[24\]](http://www.plosone.org/article/info%3Adoi%2F10.1371%2Fjournal.pone.0024026#pone.0024026-Klein1), [\[30\]](http://www.plosone.org/article/info%3Adoi%2F10.1371%2Fjournal.pone.0024026#pone.0024026-Walter1)–[\[31\]](http://www.plosone.org/article/info%3Adoi%2F10.1371%2Fjournal.pone.0024026#pone.0024026-Bruggemann1), [\[38\]](http://www.plosone.org/article/info%3Adoi%2F10.1371%2Fjournal.pone.0024026#pone.0024026-Brearty1)."

_

### Jack Kruse — 2014-05-08T16:31:08+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/oysters-vs-scallops.10716/post-125864>

Several different perspectives suggests that some Neanderthals actually suffered iodinedeficiency disorders probably caused by inland environment or by a genetic difference of their thyroid compared to the thyroid of moder. H. sapiens (Dobson, 1998).

I think their lack of iodine and the fact they had different iodine receptors is big. Why? What I wrote in CT6. Thyroid hormones are sensitive to temperature swings depending upon how much DHA is also present in cell membranes. I think we had a lot more than them. When temps dropped they were screwed and we thrived.
As the climate got colder, Neanderthal populations probably migrated inland and/or higher up in the mountains distant from coastal habitats rich in thyroid hormone and an iodine-rich diet, such as shorelines near hunting grounds. Some of them, especially those living in iodine-deficient inland and mountainous regions, could have suffered from endemic cretinism due to iodine deficiency. On the other hand, hyperthyroidism could be another possibility since severe chronic hyperthyroidism has been shown to produce cretinoid offspring in rats (Waterman, 1958).

From modern genome examination of Neanderthals we have found something interesting.........they may have had a turnover rate for thyroid hormone similar to that of carnivorous modern dogs and cats (about 12–13 h) compared to about 7 days for modern humans (Kaptein et al., 1994). This is personally what I think is afflicting modern humans who do not eat a seafood laden diet and why hypothyroidism is explosive everywhere. If I am right what we are seeing today in modern humans happened to Neanderthals. The worse your thyroid is the less you can handle cold and the quicker you develop a pregnenlone steal syndrome because you can not capture electrons nor assimilate them well. A faster turnover rate for thyroid hormone implies that Neanderthals possessed a distinctly different thyroid circadian hormone rhythm, a criterion for distinguishing a discrete species.

We know that they had 125 grams more brain tissue than us........and since them, homo species has been losing brain mass. This implies when we move away from this primordial homo diet (channeling BG 5) the shit hits the fan. In my opinion, this is the key metric in electron collection.

These higher recommended daily allowance levels of iodine seem necessary for optimal function of a number of body systems, including **choroid plexus and cerebrospinal fluid**, eye, gastric mucosa, thymus, and salivary glands (Miller, 2006; Venturi and Venturi, 2007). This link to iodine in the CSF is critical in transfer electrons from CSF water via iodine to DHA to build complexity and creativity in the neocortex. When the Neanderthals lost this their species was cooked because we kept our ability to create because we did not go inland and in the cold while also losing our seafood diet.........they did. Today modern humans are doing the same thing all over again.

Having watched that I then googled, found this page, whilst the writer makes some valid points he makes so many assumptions that really he's no better than Aajonus (almost an anus lol): [http://www.30bananasaday.com/forum/topics/aajonus-vonderplanitz-is-a?xg\_source=activity&id=2684079:Topic:715550&page=2#comments](http://www.30bananasaday.com/forum/topics/aajonus-vonderplanitz-is-a?xg_source=activity&id=2684079%3ATopic%3A715550&page=2#comments)

I went to his seminar abt 5 years ago and have his book The recipe for Living wo Disease. The recipes use all raw. Smoothies with raw milk or cream, raw eggs, raw honey (for enzymes), berries. Also a lot of raw meat recipes. He also talks abt high meat(haven't tried). It was good for me as it made me start thinking abt getting more raw into my diet even if it was just raw eggs and raw milk at the time. Raw liver if I cut it in bite size pieces and freeze then have a mostly frozen piece everyday-no texture issues then.

Going raw with it is because I want to embrace the discomfort ... The mental shift from the raw heart and liver shows me there is more mental shifting up for grabs, for free , if I go more uncomfortable ....... I also want to see how my body shape and collagen changes if I am rebuilding with "raw materials" instead of cooked. No lemon juice or ACV because if I like it with that, and don't like it without, over time that will limit me, so leaving that til I'm past the discomfort stage..... Deep breath, 12 oysters await ....

### SeaHorse — 2014-05-09T15:20:35+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/oysters-vs-scallops.10716/post-126016>

> [Sue-UK said:](/goto/post?id=125999)

I also want to see how my body shape and collagen changes if I am rebuilding with "raw materials" instead of cooked. No lemon juice or ACV because if I like it with that, and don't like it without, over time that will limit me, so leaving that til I'm past the discomfort stage..... Deep breath, 12 oysters await ....

I love oysters and have since I was very young….oysters were part of our New Year's dinner which my parents cooked with a few neighbouring families… Each family made a course of the meal and we all wondered from house to house to eat in stages. The starter was always oysters and it they were prepared in a number of ways. I for one think the lemon juice is an excellent way to introduce the oyster as a friend not a foe. As you have more and more happy experiences then the subtleties of the different kinds of oysters and the changes in saltiness, brininess and texture become interesting.

What about lemon and four or five different herbs sprinkled on top….choose the one you like of that selection…you are still expanding your palate and comfort zone but it might be self-reinforcing instead of just forcing….

I had a "soup" yesterday of 2 dozen very small raw oysters lemon juice and chopped cilantro in a bowl….couldn't have been happier.. hope your collagen and your pleasure get a boost!

### Sue-UK — 2014-05-10T16:44:31+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/oysters-vs-scallops.10716/post-126190>

Made my whitebait pâté, just blitzed an onion and the fish in the blender, used a tablespoon of raw cream to get right consistency, piled it into individual pots, ate one as it was, and the rest I've poured melted butter on top and put into the fridge to set.

My more unusual recipe  I decided when I woke up this morning .... I asked DH if he remembered tapioca pudding. frogspawn he said, my family called it fishes eyes in glue ..... After the pate I took 2 frozen oysters, rinsed off the glaze and sliced into tiny pieces, took some raw cream, warmed it up just enough.... Chucked in the oyster pieces, quick stir, a big sprinkle of cinnamon and pudding was ready.  Oyster pieces small enough to go down without a fight or any chewing, just the taste of the cream and lots of cinnamon .....

### Lahelada — 2014-05-10T17:12:10+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/oysters-vs-scallops.10716/post-126192>

### NeilBB — 2014-05-10T19:27:09+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/oysters-vs-scallops.10716/post-126205>

I'm fortunate enough to have a local place about a mile from my house that has happy-hour on fresh gulf oysters 3-6PM Mon-Fri. Only 75cents/oyster, either raw or chargrilled in garlic butter. I usually eat quite a few of both kinds and take full advantage of that deal. The waitress is always a bit surprised when I just keep ordering more plates of oysters and never get anything else. Well, occasionally I get some steamed royal red gulf shrimp too but they are way overpriced. And their wine is pretty cheap too, half-price bottles on Wednesdays. But everything else on the menu is overpriced crap and a waste of time. You just gotta know what you're looking for.

### NeilBB — 2014-05-10T19:29:20+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/oysters-vs-scallops.10716/post-126208>

Shucking oysters also gets a lot easier once you have done it a couple hundred times or so. Well worth it. Need a good oyster knife and a towel. And practice. That's really it.

### Jack Kruse — 2014-05-12T04:36:49+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/oysters-vs-scallops.10716/post-126375>

Josh, no guts, no glory. Some may not have been born with guts, but we don't have to die without developing them. Curiosity usually bring guts out of hiding at times, maybe even get them moving. But curiosity usually evaporates for most people. For me.......curiosity is my minds fertilizer. Curiosity has its own reason for existing. You have to find out what that reason is for you. Remember no question is silly if you can not provide an answer for it.

> pretty big difference ....

A/C, there are tons of books out there that at least attempt to bring the science to the non-scientist. I think Jack does a great job. He starts out slow, takes you deep, then brings it all back home. And he does it in a variety of ways and from multiple directions. You just have to skip or skim over the deep parts. I haven't done any physics equations in ages. I am much more interested in the concepts, and it doesn't get much easier than this. I like the quote attributed to Albert Einstien, **Everything Should Be Made as Simple as Possible, But Not Simpler.** My own quote, and not original, is, "it isn't Rocket Surgery." Simple doesn't mean easy. Simple just means it's basic stuff, the application of which may not be at all very easy. If the learning were to be easy, what is the value? In fact, another good book I am reading is "Make it Stick: the Science of Successful Learning". The authors talk extensively about how we actually learn. A/C, the fact that you find some of the material or concepts difficult means that you may, in the end, gain the most value from all of this!! Don't ever count yourself out or believe that you can't "get it." Just enjoy the journey. I am.

Not a lecture, just a pep talk.

Randy

Funny thing Randy .....Dr. K. has been giving me the same pep talk for quite awhile! I just don't like to skim and skip .
It is pretty amazing - you and Jack are saying the exact same things!

### Gagnrad — 2014-05-12T11:04:45+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/oysters-vs-scallops.10716/post-126420>

> [caroline said:](/goto/post?id=126370)
>
> I know that Dr. K. keeps telling me I don't have to speak Chinese to eat Chinese food ..... but you guys keep leaving me in the dust .... and I hate it! ....just saying.....

That one's changed, Audrey.

Last time you mentioned this you said he had told you that you didn't have to speak Chinese to _enjoy_ Chinese food. That's a different thing.

I have to agree with you that you wouldn't have to speak Chinese to eat Chinese food. I'm tempted to say that all you'd need would be teeth. But, since at any rate much Chinese food can be eaten by people who've lost theirs maybe "a mouth" would do.

But who ever thought any different? And what's the relevance of this? Why the harping on language in relation to Chinese food?

And wouldn't it be useful to be able to speak Chinese if you were, say, in China trying to order food and no-one there spoke English? You can certainly get screw-ups in Chinese restaurants caused by linguistic confusion. I've seen them. I saw one that led to quite a nasty scene in London when I was visiting the British Museum last month. Suppose that there you are in Beijing trying to order food and you're coeliac and can't ask the waiter which dishes are free from wheat? Or suppose you had a life-threatening allergy to peanuts and could not ask if those were in a dish? Next thing you'd be in anaphylactic shock.

But I can't spend much time here. PM me if you like.

### Clayton — 2014-05-02T01:25:38+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/path-to-optimal.10717/post-124447>

Ok so I think its a safe bet for me to say I have a low redox potential and electron steal syndrome. I am a student and my school environment is full of nn-emf, its in the same building as a subway station on top of all the wifi etc. I'm pretty emf sensitive, so I can tell this. Have been using a faraday cage at home for quite some time but a recent phone consult with Michael Neuert revealed some issues to iron out in the sleeping location.

Context: I have been somewhat chronically ill for at least 10 years or so, have brain fog, memory issues, often poor mood. I have no trouble sleeping but can tell that something "isn't right" with my sleeping location and am not waking as rested as I want to be. I have attached my 23 and me results as well as the labs I managed to convince my doctor to run, not ideal but some info here.

Doing regular CT (15 min/day), red light therapy, magnetico 10 gauss, faraday cage over bed, Epi Paleo, grounding, RO water, optimizing gut flora, started 5mg daily amlodipine this week etc. I see my neutrophils are low, I will obviously monitor this. Testosterone is high, im hoping this is a good thing? Im a naturopathic student so I can get decent prices on salivary hormone panels which I will run here shortly. Unfortunately all my labs will come mostly out of pocket at the moment until I can find an open minded GP here in Canada. Any light you could shed on this situation is definitely welcomed! ### bionaut — 2014-05-10T05:58:52+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/path-to-optimal.10717/post-126152>

Best to be wired in for internet. Having a higher testosterone is a good thing but from your labs I can't tell much about your hormone cascade or thyroid. What can tell you is that you want to pay attention to your methylation cycle and also the quality of your water so that you can deal with environmental stressors.

If you are going out of pocket start by taking a look at vitamin D (sunlight), HS CRP (CT), bun/creatinine ratio (hydration/emf) and then go from there. You can try pasting some screenshorts of your labs, the text version is pretty rough to read.

The link below has some easy to digest info on methylation.
[http://www.autismnti.com/images/Website-\_Yasko\_Education.pdf](http://www.autismnti.com/images/Website-_Yasko_Education.pdf)

### Clayton — 2014-05-10T07:16:06+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/path-to-optimal.10717/post-126153>

Thanks bionaut. I am moving into a guest house and definitely wasn't going to install wi-fi, but was wondering what type of internet to ask my provider for. As far as methylation goes I read yasko's stuff extensively a year ago. Her protocols are anything but practical and involve a ton of supplementation depending on one's SNPs. Its the biggest thing in alternative medicine right now and I think people are focussing too much on it (not to say its not important).

I looked at my genomics and what it would have taken to follow yasko's protocol and threw my hands in the air. My COMT +/+ SNPs indicate that I can't handle excess methyl groups yet my MTHFR 1298 says I require more methyl groups. Her protocol seems like a shitty rabbit hole to crawl down and i've been down too many of those. I think its more of an EMF issue.

To address my SNPs I purchased a wave genetic program from Peter Gariaev last year. I had these programs muscle tested with Klinghardt's ART and they apparently put me in a "deep healing state".

I've read a lot of the Yasko stuff and agree that it's kind of crazy making. What worked was going through the process that Tim Jackson outlined but not adding in all the supplements. It all went very well and this is from somebody who has never been able to take b vitamins because of overstimulation and I have a ton of snps.

And as far as how I'm feeling now….it has certainly made a huge difference in my energy which of course is all about being able to capture the electrons….I've got a long way to go…but every electron counts.

There is a path through the madness.

### Clayton — 2014-05-14T19:11:41+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/path-to-optimal.10717/post-126909>

I have had improvements..i like to be all in or not bother doing it at all. I posted earlier this week wondering if the oysters I was eating were filled with heavy metals. Im not so sure that is the what was causing me brain fog-type symptoms. Over the past 2 months I have been eating seafood at least 3-4 times per week and never felt any issues I thought would be related to heavy metals. Just in the last week I have been having 5-6 raw oysters per day and I haven't had this much raw food in such a short period of time so I kinda wonder if these symptoms are due to a rapid gut flora change and possibly die off of candida (which can harbour heavy metals btw) or other microbes. For now in the next ten days or so im gonna take it a bit slower with the raw oysters, maybe every second day or so and let my body adapt and see what happens. Im just a little sketched out by this west coast seafood and should probably see if I can find oysters from the east coast. But ya, intending to load up on the DHA, I could tell my body/brain loved the oysters but a few hours later was having weird brain fog and waking up with it

Traveling in a metal tube (microwave) at 35km/h underground surrounded by thousands of people surfing the web, face booking,tweeting encased by concrete on a planet whose [combined speed](http://www.thelivingmoon.com/41pegasus/02files/Speed_of_Earth.html) of motion from its wobble, spin and orbit is 70,402.43mph **MUST have** profound effects on our biology through known effects and unknown spintronic quantum effects.

### av8r — 2014-05-02T13:06:09+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/london-underground-will-have-free-wifi-to-all-stations-by-2015.10718/post-124541>

Consumer ovens usually use **2.45 [gigahertz](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Gigahertz) (GHz)**

[http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Microwave\_oven](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Microwave_oven)

Wi-Fi, also spelled Wifi or WiFi, is a technology that allows an electronic device to exchange [data](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Wi-Fi) or connect to the internet [wirelessly](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Wireless_network) **using 2.4 GHz [UHF](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/UHF)** and 5 GHz [SHF](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/SHF) radio waves.

[http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Wi-Fi](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Wi-Fi)

Banging my head against the wall saying WTF!

Mankind's thoughts and wants do. We sacrifice our health in order to make money. Then we sacrifices our money to recuperate our health. The result being that we do not live in the present or the future, we live as if we are never going to die, and then we die slowly with disease never really having lived.

I am amazed that people put money over health constantly. Read Marino's book.........the military has sacrificed life for defense. It is unreal what has been done in 120 when you look at the 3 legged stool.

# Thread: Blue Zones
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/blue-zones.10720/>
**Subforum:** Factor X
**Posts:** 7
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Josh (Paleo Osteo) — 2014-05-02T11:33:09+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/blue-zones.10720/post-124531>

[http://www.bluezones.com/about-blue-zones/explorations/](http://www.bluezones.com/about-blue-zones/explorations/)

look at the geographical locales
high seismic activity, rift zones, seafood diets etc

they blew it with the okinawans, as most do...
its all down to pork, time in the water etc etc as we know, and geographical location...

smh @ how wrong they get it in the explanations

### Shijin13 — 2014-05-02T15:07:29+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/blue-zones.10720/post-124548>

> [Josh (Paleo Osteo) said:](/goto/post?id=124531)
>
> [http://www.bluezones.com/about-blue-zones/explorations/](http://www.bluezones.com/about-blue-zones/explorations/)
>
> look at the geographical locales
> high seismic activity, rift zones, seafood diets etc
>
> they blew it with the okinawans, as most do...
> its all down to pork, time in the water etc etc as we know, and geographical location...
>
> smh @ how wrong they get it in the explanations

LOL! Having lived in Oki. It always frustrates me to see how wrong they get it. The older generation (the pre-WWII group, and the post WWII group) cling heartly to the old ways. The younger generation, has been influenced by Mainland Japan. The arise of fast food is the result of the US Military (comforts of home)

But bento shops are found on every corner and in every connivence store. Rice is a side dish, not the primary source of food. long slow cooking is used, as is plenty of raw foods. meals are small and shared among the family. Awamore is consumed regularly (I do miss awamore) Deserts are limited to Onigiri (riced wrapped in seaweed) stuffed w/Ume (plum). Onigiri can also be a quick meal when stuffed w/raw fish!

While pork was offered on many a menu - all the Okinawans I ate with usually preferred fish, but they would eat it for special occasions... I ate sashimi at least 5x/week. There were "secret" restaurants - that Americans we're allowed to go to unless invited by an Okinawan - they served traditional foods, prepared in the old ways. that was some of the best food I've ever eaten...
