### Jack Kruse — 2014-05-09T18:16:53+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/on-the-thermodynamics-of-comfort.10786/post-126043>

You are all just truly beginning to learn how mass equivalence is your true identity. None of you see how it exerts its reality yet.

Let me give you a preview.......based upon all the recent webinars you just learned about water and collagen.

Long ago I told you about **supercooled reverse osmosis water** you learned about in the [EMF Rx](http://www.jackkruse.com/march-2013-webinar/). I told you it is ideal for super conduction. Nobody asked why? _**When supercooled water is heated, it does not expand as expected.**_ **Instead it shrinks. When something shrinks what two thing does this mean to mass equivalence? Mass decreases.** What did I tell you a decreasing mass means? It means there is more electrons in water. What is so special about electrons? They add energy and information without the constraints of mass. So this means cold water...........has more electrons and allows for higher energy transfers.
**
You may now see why people like Robb Wolf are fundamentally clueless about cold thermogenesis.
**
When cold water is placed under pressure (think gravity where masses increase), both the melting point and the maximum density point of water _shift_ to even _lower temperatures_ than would be expected. When chemists examine ordinary liquids, however, higher pressures promote freezing to higher temperatures. **Remember Mr Wolf is one of those former biochemists.**
A physicist sees a thermodynamic solution in this set of circumstance.........all tied to mass equivalence. Ya know the law of the universe. This is why water breaks symmetry in the universe. It also belies why a cell is organized the way it is. It is organized to control mass. This is why a cell molecules contain 98% water by shear number.
**
Water is best described as an electric dipole in which its positive and negative charges are separated. Oxygen contains its negative charge and the two hydrogens have its positive charge. Dipoles can stack together in dipole interactions with alternating positive and negative poles next to one another. They also can interact electrostaticaly with other charged ions and other dipoles that are dissolved in water.
**
Water is most famous for forming hydrogen bonds with other water molecules and with other ions dissolved in it. A hydrogen bond consists of a hydrogen shared between two electronegative atoms like oxygen or sulfur. the compound that donates the hydrogen to the chemical reaction is the hydrogen donor, and the acceptor atoms is the hydrogen acceptor. Water is unique because it can be both an acceptor and a donor of hydrogen. In fact it can donate two hydrogen’s. This makes the water molecule take on the tetrahedral structure in its frozen form linked in a crystalline hexagonal array in crystal ice.

The dipole nature and propensity for hydrogen bonding is why water has an unusually high dielectric constant of -78 at room temperature. This makes it the **most polar solvent** in all of chemistry and biology! This fact alone should have gotten biochemists attention that intracellular water is really critical but it has not. Now consider water is always found next to collagen anywhere in a cell. Still think this is a coincidence?

Why is this a big deal? In QED and semiconduction, anything with this high a dielectric constant becomes easily polarized by an electric field. This is why the quantum magic can happen with water. The dielectric constant is also known as a relative static permittivity ability. This is a measure of the extent of which it concentrates electrostatic lines of flux relative to a vacuum. This is very important in semiconduction science for coherent flow of current. The hydrogen bonds serve to align the dipoles, and at the same time, pulling away positive and negative charges within the molecule, and this acts to enhance the molecular polarization in liquid water.

Why do you need to be uncomfortable?

Because everything I just posted here above I have told you before many times but you are not seeing it work in you...............still. Everything in this story is organized around e=Mc2. It is an electron photons story which have little to no mass...................

Your reality is about this basic fundamental fact.

### Gagnrad — 2014-05-10T08:51:56+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/on-the-thermodynamics-of-comfort.10786/post-126160>

> [SeaHorse said:](/goto/post?id=126027)
>
> Ok…I understand all of this except your term "true identity." What do you mean by suppressing your true identity. Doesn't the engagement and discomfort uncover your true identity??

I wasn't familiar with these terms, Seahorse.

I googled them. It seems that Jack has taken them from something called "positive psychology". Better than "negative psychology" I guess. Here's a representative example:

> Opening the mind to what has previously been feared and avoided reveals the capacity to bear and truly embrace discomfort ...
>
> To open your mind to the silence that is the source of your mind is to open to your true self.

[http://positivepsychologymelbourne....logy\_Resources/post/the-truth-of-who-you-are/](http://positivepsychologymelbourne....logy_Resources/post/the-truth-of-who-you-are/)
This is from "Gangaji" -- real name John Smith probably. The book it comes from is called _The Diamond in Your Pocket_ and it really does exist -- I found it on Amazon.

I'm not sure I would call this "psychology". I think what Mr. Gagaji is offering us is a kind of theosophy. It seems rather a gallimaufry to me. He throws a bunch of different terms in from different religions and asserts that they mean the same thing, which they don't. None them, AFAICT, means quite what it would in its original context. Take "resurrection", for example, which he's taken from Christianity. It doesn't mean what he thinks it means. Where is Christ anyway? He had of course said he was "the life of the world" but he doesn't seem to make it into Gagji's system.

Anyway, Jack is using this language but re-purposing it. I doubt one can do that.

You've put your finger on one problem. It's this conception of a "true identity". If anyone thinks about this concept I think he will see that with it comes in value. Jack says he's talking about science, but, of course, science knows nothing of value. But Jack's smuggled in value with this term. When I say "smuggled" I don't mean that it's a deliberate act, just that it comes in with this term without his noticing.

Jack can tell Neil how things are, always supposing he can work it out, till the cows come home, but he can't tell him what he ought to do about it. You cannot derive an "ought" from an "is". That's the problem.

### NeilBB — 2014-05-10T14:43:58+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/on-the-thermodynamics-of-comfort.10786/post-126168>

> [Gagnrad said:](/goto/post?id=126160)
>
> Jack can tell Neil how things are, always supposing he can work it out, till the cows come home, but he can't tell him what he ought to do about it. You cannot derive an "ought" from an "is". That's the problem.

Many here will not agree with this and I will likely get stoned for bringing this up, but Objectivism did address the philosophical is-ought problem. Here is the official summary for those interested:

[http://aynrandlexicon.com/lexicon/is-ought\_dichotomy.html](http://aynrandlexicon.com/lexicon/is-ought_dichotomy.html)

### Shijin13 — 2014-05-15T00:23:28+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/on-the-thermodynamics-of-comfort.10786/post-126972>

So I'm finally surfacing... and getting through this whole thread.... love every word....

But when I think about Neil's original question - about the thermodynamics of comfort or discomfort, with respect to the journey I'm on. I have to say I have neither.

and it took me today to figure it out. And Jack's been telling us this for almost 4yrs now! I just now got it.

Nutrition isn't the key. Its the movement of the electrons which DHA supports which is. That's what [Jack](https://www.facebook.com/jack.kruse)'s been trying to teach us. If you're HPA is off - you can't count electrons. If you can't count electrons, you can't move the electrons to use them as energy properly. Its a thermodynamic equation...the big picture is actually smaller than we think. its not about the food. its not about the supplements. its about the electron spin, and the direction of it at the quantum level. Its the action of the Earth's magnetic field upon us, its the action of the sun's radiation upon us, its the action of water within us. Its the interaction btwn them across the fractals of nature's design.

In the end we're all quantum beings. The problem is we don't think like quantum beings or treat illness from a quantum perspective. (well those of us on the forum are learning)

electrons have no comfort or discomfort. they are either present or they're not. Kinda like Schrodinger's cat. the abundance or lack of the electrons alter my perception of comfort. but in reality all I am is energy - or the lack of it if I'm in a diseased state.

Same for everyone on this board, in your family, your co-workers, your friends. everyone. to include every single living thing. This is what Jack has been driving at.
everything we do is about electron transfer. and when thats effed up - you get disease, you get dying reefs, you get species extinction.... I think what challenges most of us, is that we don't get that electron transfer for each of us is specific to us. No one else has our specific electron "spin" Ask Randy about his spin vs his brother's spin. And what scares us about that electron "spin" is the fact that we know what's coming and we want to fix it so it doesn't come. so we focus on the things that we think will fix us (Food, CT,) or the things that hold us back (snps/toxins/carbs) trying to emulate Inger and Peter and N=1. When infact we need to focus on our Electron's n=1. Christi nailed it earlier today in her comments in the [Tick thread](http://forum.jackkruse.com/index.php?threads/tick-bite-prevention.10815/page-2#post-126962). NAILED IT!

Electrons are who we are. How how our brain uses them equates directly to our health and how we perceive our world. Electrons are our thoughts. Thoughts change your DNA. Electrons change your DNA. we transmit electrons through touch. You exchange electrons with those you love. When you do that you not only change their DNA but they change yours. and that my friends is the quantum entanglement Jack's been speaking of. We're all electrons. and electrons move entirely on the principles of the 2nd law of thermodynamics. Electrons are our resonance, our aura. Electrons are who we are, because all we are is stardust.

### Jack Kruse — 2014-05-15T01:08:46+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/on-the-thermodynamics-of-comfort.10786/post-126984>

Consider the difference between electrons and photons. So far on the site I have been using both terms interchangeably. Does biology do that, however? Has Lady evolution discovered a way to separate the two to her advantage. This is why DHA is special. The electron is a particle which has a certain tiny mass and negative electric charge. A particular electron exists at a specific position in space, travelling in a certain direction at a certain speed\*. If you accelerate an electron (eg if it passes close to a proton), it causes a disturbance in the electromagnetic field (think about what happens in a mitochondria now), which will propagate away from the disturbed electron at the speed of light. We call this propagating packet of energy a photon. Electrons and photons both respond to the electromagnetic force because they carry a charge, but they differ in: mass: electron is 9×10−31 kg; photon is zero (what did I say about massless energy? The brain is organized all around photons.......not electrons.)

Now I want to share some magic with you................Where is CSF? in a large space around the brain. The electrons from it have the ability to absorb photons from our skin photocells........and it is transferred to our CSF to alter the electron density and power that can be delivered to our neocortex. Now think about the volume of the CSF space............then think about where that flow of water is directed? Toward the white matter.......What is in the white matter? Lots of protons and lots of microtubules...........all of them have a diameter of 15nm. So water is columnated from a big space to a small space............What effect does this have to a water molecule and to an electron that is forced down this path?

See EE 6 and see OSF 3 for the answer..............

And the result is the June webinar for 2014.

### Jack Kruse — 2014-05-15T01:19:27+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/on-the-thermodynamics-of-comfort.10786/post-126987>

How can photons have energy and momentum, but no mass?
Classically (according to Newton) kinetic energy is given by and the momentum is given by , where m is the mass and v is the velocity. But if you plug in the mass and velocity for light you get E=1/2mv2 . But that’s no good mathematically. If light didn’t carry energy, it wouldn’t be able to heat stuff up and we know it does.

The difficulty comes from the fact that Newton’s laws paint an incomplete (and ultimately incorrect) picture. When Einstein's relativity came along it was revealed that there’s a fundamental difference in the physics of the massive and the massless. I have been pounding that into your brains for sometime now whether you know it or not. Relativity makes the (experimentally backed) assumptions that: #1) it doesn’t matter whether, or how fast, you’re moving (all physical laws stay the same) and #2) the speed of light is invariant (always the same to everyone).

Any object with mass travels slower than light and so may as well be stationary (#1). (Big deal in the brain's quantum computer)

Anything with zero mass always travels at the speed of light. (human brain) But since the speed-of-light is always the speed-of-light to everyone (#2) there’s no way for these objects to ever be stationary (unlike massive stuff). Vive la différence des lois! It’s not important here, but things (like light) that travel at the speed of light never experience the passage of time. Isn’t that awesome? This means the closer we get to turning electrons into photons the longer life span and health span becomes.

So what is the name of the game if you are a human.......collect electrons and turn them into to photons in the brain which then delivers that energy all over your power grid. any break in the system causes us to lose this critical relationship.......when it is lost.......time gets its arrow of direction. This is why the cookie crumbles and eggs can not be put back together again.

The critical point is: light and ordinary matter are very different, and the laws that govern them are just as different. This is why EMF 2 is a big deal...............

In 1905 Einstein managed to write a law that works whenever: . The same year (almost unbelievable) he figured out that light is both a particle and a wave and that the energy of a photon isn’t governed by it’s mass or it’s velocity (like matter is), but instead is governed entirely by f, it’s frequency: E=hf, where h is Planck’s constant.

For light m=0, so E=Pc (energy and momentum are proportional). Notice that you can never have zero momentum, since something with zero mass and zero energy isn’t something, it’s nothing. This is just another way of using words to say that light can never be stationary anywhere.

Also! Say you have an object with mass m, that isn’t moving (P=0). Then you get: E=mc2 ! THIS IS THE LAW of the UNIVERSE. Not subject to beliefs theories or RCT's. This is the rules that govern my ideas now.

### Jack Kruse — 2014-05-15T03:02:37+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/on-the-thermodynamics-of-comfort.10786/post-127009>

P450scc is associated with the [inner mitochondrial membrane](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Inner_mitochondrial_membrane), facing the interior (matrix). Adrenodoxin and adrenodoxin reductase are soluble [peripheral membrane proteins](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Peripheral_membrane_proteins) located inside the mitochondrial matrix that appear to associate with each other primarily through electrostatic interactions.

### Shijin13 — 2014-05-15T03:07:18+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/on-the-thermodynamics-of-comfort.10786/post-127010>

> [Jack Kruse said:](/goto/post?id=127009)
>
> P450scc is associated with the [inner mitochondrial membrane](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Inner_mitochondrial_membrane), facing the interior (matrix). Adrenodoxin and adrenodoxin reductase are soluble [peripheral membrane proteins](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Peripheral_membrane_proteins) located inside the mitochondrial matrix that appear to associate with each other primarily through electrostatic interactions.
>
> Now if memory serves me right Gretchen you mentioned adrenodoxin before

AND THIS IS WHY I HAVE PCOS... BOOM! P450!

from wikipedia
**NADPH:adrenodoxin oxidoreductase, mitochondrial** also known as **adrenodoxin reductase** is an [enzyme](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Enzyme) that in humans is encoded by the _FDXR_ [gene](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Gene), also known as _ADXR_. It catalyzes the following reaction:

NADPH + 2 oxidized adrenodoxin —→ 2 reduced adrenodoxin + NADP+ + H+
In both bovine and human genomes there is only a single copy of this gene.[\[1\]](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Adrenodoxin_reductase#cite_note-pmid3691502-1)

ADXR gene is expressed in all tissues that have mitochondrial P450s. The highest levels of the enzyme is found in the adrenal cortex, granulosa cells of the ovary and leydig cells of the testis that specialize in [steroid](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Steroid) hormone synthesis.[\[2\]](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Adrenodoxin_reductase#cite_note-pmid3011431-2)In addition the enzyme is also expressed in the liver, the kidney and the placenta.

Adrenodoxin reductase is a mitochondrial [flavoprotein](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Flavoprotein) as it carries a [FAD](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/FAD) type coenzyme. The enzyme functions as the first electron transfer protein of mitochondrial P450 systems such as [P450scc](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/P450scc). The FAD coenzyme receives two electrons from NADPH and transfers them one at a time to the electron transfer protein [adrenodoxin](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Adrenodoxin).[\[3\]](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Adrenodoxin_reductase#cite_note-pmid6475-3) Adrenodoxin functions as a mobile shuttle that transfers electrons between ADXR and mitochondrial P450s.[\[4\]](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Adrenodoxin_reductase#cite_note-pmid6766943-4)

Adrenodoxin reductase has been also called a [ferredoxin-NADP+ reductase](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ferredoxin-NADP%2B_reductase). But, determination of the sequence and structure of the enzyme revealed that it is completely different from ferredoxin reductase.[\[5\]](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Adrenodoxin_reductase#cite_note-pmid2924777-5)[\[6\]](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Adrenodoxin_reductase#cite_note-pmid10369776-6)

### Jack Kruse — 2014-05-15T03:07:54+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/on-the-thermodynamics-of-comfort.10786/post-127011>

Gretchen said, "so Redox potential... lets discuss Aldersterone and its relationship to K+ and Na+

From: [http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Aldosterone](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Aldosterone)
The [corticosteroids](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Corticosteroids) are synthesized from [cholesterol](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Cholesterol) within the [zona glomerulosa](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Zona_glomerulosa) of [adrenal cortex](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Adrenal_cortex). Most [steroidogenic](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Steroidogenic) reactions are catalysed by enzymes of the [cytochrome](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Cytochrome) P450 family. They are located within the [mitochondria](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Mitochondria) and require [adrenodoxin](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Adrenodoxin) as a cofactor (except [21-hydroxylase](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/21-hydroxylase) and [17α-hydroxylase](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/17%CE%B1-hydroxylase)).

Aldosterone and [corticosterone](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Corticosterone) share the first part of their biosynthetic pathways. The last parts are mediated either by the [aldosterone synthase](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Aldosterone_synthase) (for aldosterone) or by the [11β-hydroxylase](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/11%CE%B2-hydroxylase) (for[corticosterone](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Corticosterone)). These enzymes are nearly identical (they share 11β-hydroxylation and [18-hydroxylation](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/18-hydroxylation) functions), but aldosterone synthase is also able to perform a 18-[oxidation](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Oxidation). Moreover, aldosterone synthase is found within the zona glomerulosa at the outer edge of the [adrenal cortex](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Adrenal_cortex); 11β-hydroxylase is found in the [zona fasciculata](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Zona_fasciculata) and[reticularis](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Reticularis).

Aldosterone is the primary of several endogenous members of the class of [mineralocorticoids](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Mineralocorticoid) in humans. [Deoxycorticosterone](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Deoxycorticosterone) is another important member of this class. Aldosterone tends to promote Na+ and water retention, and lower plasma K+ concentration by the following mechanisms:

1.  Acting on the nuclear [mineralocorticoid receptors](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Mineralocorticoid_receptor) (MR) within the principal cells of the [distal tubule](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Distal_tubule) and the [collecting duct](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Collecting_duct) of the kidney nephron, it upregulates and activates the[basolateral](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Basolateral) [Na+/K+ pumps](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Na%2B/K%2B-ATPase), which pumps three sodium ions out of the cell, into the interstitial fluid and two potassium ions into the cell from the interstitial fluid. This creates a concentration gradient which results in reabsorption of sodium (Na+) ions and water (which follows sodium) into the blood, and secreting potassium (K+) ions into the urine (lumen of collecting duct).
2.  Aldosterone upregulates epithelial sodium channels ([ENaCs](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/ENaC)), increasing apical membrane permeability for Na+.
3.  Cl− is reabsorbed in conjunction with sodium cations to maintain the system's electrochemical balance.
4.  Aldosterone stimulates the secretion of K+ into the tubular lumen.[\[6\]](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Aldosterone#cite_note-pmid10760062-6)
5.  Aldosterone stimulates Na+ and water reabsorption from the gut, salivary and sweat glands in exchange for K+.
6.  Aldosterone stimulates secretion of H+ in exchange for Na+ in the [intercalated cells](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Intercalated_cells) of the cortical collecting tubules, regulating plasma [bicarbonate](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Bicarbonate) (HCO3−) levels and its acid/base balance.[\[7\]](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Aldosterone#cite_note-7)

Aldosterone is responsible for the reabsorption of about 2% of filtered sodium in the kidneys, which is nearly equal to the entire sodium content in human blood under normal[glomerular filtration rates](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Glomerular_filtration_rate).[\[8\]](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Aldosterone#cite_note-8)

Aldosterone, probably acting through mineralocorticoid receptors, may positively influence neurogenesis in the [dentate gyrus](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dentate_gyrus).[\[9\]](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Aldosterone#cite_note-9)

I think there's something here that can help us determine redox potential based upon what aldersterone is doing...."

### Jack Kruse — 2014-05-15T03:12:31+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/on-the-thermodynamics-of-comfort.10786/post-127015>

P450scc [catalyzes](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Catalyzes) the conversion of cholesterol to pregnenolone in three monooxygenase reactions. These involve 2 [hydroxylations](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Hydroxylation) of the cholesterol side-chain, which generate, first, 22R-hydroxycholesterol and then 20alpha,22R-dihydroxycholesterol. The final step cleaves the bond between carbons 20 and 22, resulting in the production of pregnenolone and [isocaproic acid](http://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Isocaproic_acid&action=edit&redlink=1).

Each monooxygenase step requires 2 [electrons](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Electron)

Gretchen...........2 electrons? Where did they go?

Into the DC air.

That is why pregnenolone is low.

### Jack Kruse — 2014-05-15T03:19:17+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/on-the-thermodynamics-of-comfort.10786/post-127022>

Pregnenolone steal syndrome = Electrons steal syndrome. The involvement of three proteins in cholesterol side-chain cleavage reaction raises the question of whether the three proteins function as a [ternary complex](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ternary_complex) as reductase:adrenodoxin450\. Both spectroscopic studies of adrenodoxin binding to P450scc and kinetic studies in the presence of varying concentrations of adrenodoxin reductase demonstrated that the reductase competes with P450scc for binding to adrenodoxin. These results demonstrated that the formation of a functional ternary complex is not possible. Once again you see how electrons affect protein bending. This is the quantum mechanism I just wrote about in OSF 3. From studies, it was concluded that the binding sites of adrenodoxin to its reductase and to P450 are overlapping and, as a consequence, adrenodoxin functions as a mobile electron shuttle between reductase and P450. These conclusions have been confirmed by 3 D structural analysis of adrenodoxin and P450 complex.

BOOM

OSF 3 wins.

### Jack Kruse — 2014-05-15T03:23:59+0300

Now let us talk in depth what I have avoided..........because you did not know the quantum mechanism:

The process of electron transfer from NADPH (from the PPP) to P450scc is not tightly coupled anywhere in cells; that is, during electron transfer from adrenodoxin reductase via adrenodoxin to P450scc, a certain portion of the electrons leak outside of the electron chain transport (more so when protons are present like they are in LR states) and react with O2, generating superoxide radicals.

Superoxide is generated when electrons are missing and this increases mitochondrial mass. Steroidogenic cells include a diverse array of antioxidant systems to cope with the radicals generated by the steroidogenic enzymes but all of them are inactivated by loss of electrons. The result is more protons.

### Josh — 2014-05-16T04:26:58+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/on-the-thermodynamics-of-comfort.10786/post-127180>

> [yewwei.tan said:](/goto/post?id=127053)
>
> Where are insects getting their DHA from? What insects are the highest in DHA? (maybe aquatic insects?)
>
> Finally, you often here that many species of insects are going extinct. Which types of insects are those? How do they derive they energy (inferred by figuring out what kinds of energy sources are being drained from their environment)?
>
> </end Random Afternoon Thoughts>

[http://www.fao.org/docrep/018/i3253e/i3253e00.htm](http://www.fao.org/docrep/018/i3253e/i3253e00.htm)

> Many Thai edible insects have been found to contain three omega-
> 3 (n-3) PUFAs, namely alpha-linolenic acid (ALA, 18:3n-3), eicosapentaenoic acid (EPA, 20:5n-3)
> and docosahexaenoic acid (DHA, 22:6n-3), plus two omega-6 (n-6) PUFAs, namely dihomolinolenic
> acid (DGLA, 20:3n-6) and arachidonic acid (ARA, 20:4n-6). DGLA was found in all insects
> studied \[8\].**_The highest concentration of total fatty acids was in the giant water bug (473 mg/100g), while EPA
> and DHA contents were found in all of Thai aquicolous insects, ranging from 149 to 569 mg/100g
> and 57 to 264 mg/100g, respectively. This composition is much higher than that found in sand
> whiting, mackerel, blue fish, sea bream, sea bass and marbled spinefoot \[37\]._** For Thai terricolous
> insects, EPA was only found in dung beetles (301 mg/100g), while DHA was only found in June
> 6 beetles (26 mg/100g) and Weaver ants (12 mg/100g).

[http://www.iufost.org/iufostftp/Revd Sirithon Chapter 16.pdf](http://www.iufost.org/iufostftp/Revd%20Sirithon%20Chapter%2016.pdf)

"Many Thai edible insects have been found to contain three omega-
3 (n-3) PUFAs, namely alpha-linolenic acid (ALA, 18:3n-3), eicosapentaenoic acid (EPA, 20:5n-3)
and docosahexaenoic acid (DHA, 22:6n-3), plus two omega-6 (n-6) PUFAs, namely dihomolinolenic
acid (DGLA, 20:3n-6) and arachidonic acid (ARA, 20:4n-6). DGLA was found in all insects
studied \[8\].**_The highest concentration of total fatty acids was in the giant water bug (473 mg/100g), while EPA
and DHA contents were found in all of Thai aquicolous insects, ranging from 149 to 569 mg/100g
and 57 to 264 mg/100g, respectively. This composition is much higher than that found in sand
whiting, mackerel, blue fish, sea bream, sea bass and marbled spinefoot \[37\]._** For Thai terricolous
insects, EPA was only found in dung beetles (301 mg/100g), while DHA was only found in June
6 beetles (26 mg/100g) and Weaver ants (12 mg/100g)."

### Jack Kruse — 2014-06-16T14:43:01+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/on-the-thermodynamics-of-comfort.10786/post-131012>

Your world view has to be structured in princiiple on some specific doctrine which shapes how you function and view humanity. Embracing QED is akin to wearing steel-toed boots in a ballet-slipper world. Understanding that life is bigger than your set of challenges will help you overcome obstacles that will come your way from time to time. It's impossible to increase the scope of our worldview when all that we are focused on is ourselves. When 'MY GOALS' become 'OUR GOALS', success follows. Whatever a person's ultimate concern may be, it will have an enormous influence on everything else the person does or believes.

### NeilBB — 2014-07-15T18:15:44+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/on-the-thermodynamics-of-comfort.10786/post-134471>

> [NeilBB said:](/goto/post?id=125856)
>
> Jack is always talking about "getting comfortable with your discomfort." I've been thinking about this from a thermodynamic perspective...
>
> For a living organism, the idea of “chronically seeking comfort” is extremely problematic because comfort is nothing but a state of psychological ease or equilibrium, which for a human means physical and mental relaxation, which if practiced chronically means the loss of the ability to adapt, stagnation, and then illness, atrophy, and eventually permanent equilibrium, better known as death.
>
> By contrast, embracing discomfort as a policy means remaining in an "athletic mental posture," ready to respond to any current or future challenge, known or unknown. It means placing the laws of the universe and the requirements of life ahead of one’s habits, wants, and desires. It is the policy of psychological adaptability and metastability. The psychological analog to the living state. One's acceptance of the responsibility implied by the laws of thermodynamics. Humans must aggressively avoid equilibrium in every way and seek metastability instead.
>
> Gretchen, why is the most depraved individual the one without a purpose? Because he values comfort more than productiveness. He increases rather than decreases both his own entropy and also the entropy of humanity. Productiveness really boils down to adaptability and metastability. Life requires movement and action. Physically and mentally. Literally and figuratively. Comfort is like a vacation. OK in small doses, but not as a lifetime aspiration.
>
> In medicine, "comfort" is really the tool of hospice, applicable to the dying, not the living. Yet our current medical system emphasizes satisfaction surveys over the hard reality of valid therapy, and pills over lifestyle adaptation. The entire modern world, in fact, happens to be built around the idea of maximizing the "comfort and convenience of the masses," regardless of consequences. It is basically the organizing principle of modern economic consumerism... Think about it... And then just look around to see the consequences…
>
> The "dream" that we have all been sold by the modern marketing machine is actually a nightmare which has blunted us to what it really means to have the responsibility of living. That responsibility is not about comfort, it is about metastability.

The foremost reason that happiness is so hard to achieve is that the universe was not designed with the comfort of human beings in mind.

\-Mihaly Csikszentmihaly

### NeilBB — 2015-02-13T00:05:53+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/on-the-thermodynamics-of-comfort.10786/post-155496>

> [CTforlife said:](/goto/post?id=155492)
>
> Then who is the universe for?

It is a philosophically invalid question. The universe simply _is_. "Purpose" is an attribute that can only exist _within_ the universe.

To speculate a purpose or beneficiary of the entire universe (which is by definition the totality of all that exists) is to apply an abstraction which can only apply to particular entities, instead to the universe as a whole. This is quite simply an epistemological error.

Or to put it even more simply, for the "universe qua universe" to have any purpose at all, there would have to be something/someone outside said universe to refer to. And if there were, then the "universe" would no longer be the "universe." The idea that there may by "multiple parallel universes" also leads to same conclusion ultimately if you carry out the reasoning fully. You have to start somewhere...

> Why have we been evolving and creating complexity?

Why? Because thermodynamic and quantum flux are built in to the fabric of this "eternal universe." (If you want to call that a purpose or even build a religion around it, go ahead.) Thermodynamics, and random movement of matter and energy eventually organized by physical processes into more and more complex and efficient structures, eventually leading to "life," then to "thought," then to beings that are able to "ponder the purpose of their own existence."

> Why are thermodynamic and quantum flux built in to the fabric of the universe?

_That_ is a question for _physics_ not philosophy to answer, if it can be even answered. So far, I do not think it has been satisfactorily answered at all, but there are theories and speculations...

Look... I'll put it yet another way...If there were anything, anything-at-all, that "created" our universe of matter and energy, it would have to be non-material and non-energy, otherwise it would be in the universe too. So, it would be so unrecognizable and so unfathomable to all of us, as to be not worth your time in pondering right now. The universe is. It is what it is. _Existence exists—and the act of grasping that statement implies two corollary axioms: that something exists which one perceives and that one exists possessing consciousness, consciousness being the faculty of perceiving that which exists. -Rand_

This is NOT to say that it is impossible that there could be some kind of alien life out there somewhere _in our universe_ that may have some kind of influence over earth and humanity. But that is a completely different question than the one you asked. That would be a scientific, rather than philosophical question. I personally do believe that it is possible, but the burden ultimately lies on us to provide valid credible evidence to support that assertion...otherwise it will just remain arbitrary speculation...

I just find it incredibly weird how quantum gravity or emergent gravity or where gravity is not a fundamental force and is something derived from other fundamental forces very very very odd. ### NeilBB — 2015-02-13T21:27:45+0200

We can never have whole picture. Biggest mistake humans make is putting themselves in some mystical godlike position of assuming we can, now or ever, "know it all." Well we can't. We are products of a universe of matter and energy and we have certain specific abilities. There are things we can do and things we can't do. There are things we can know and things we can't know. That is the nature of identity. To be is to be something...

There are limits to our knowledge and it has to be gained by a certain process. And once you understand that, I mean really understand it, then _what you do know_ has a chance to expand and flourish into certainty. Only then can you escape from speculation into serious knowledge. Few people bother...

### yewwei.tan — 2015-02-13T23:59:02+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/on-the-thermodynamics-of-comfort.10786/post-155557>

> [CTforlife said:](/goto/post?id=155544)
>
> I view it differently. I don't think we have the whole picture quite yet. There's so many anomalies it's ridiculous.
> You're definitely right for yourself, and that's what matters. Everything is vibrating. Everything.
>
> I just find it incredibly weird how quantum gravity or emergent gravity or where gravity is not a fundamental force and is something derived from other fundamental forces very very very odd. There seems to be a gap in knowledge somewhere along the line. A skew of "mainstream science" away from what they don't know. You can't see something with blinders and especially if you don't know where to look.
>
> I wonder about c^2 , crystals, pyramids, light, and 432 other things.

I'll say that as a software developer this doesn't feel too surprising . We basically "play god" and create our own little mini software worlds all the time, and seeing how those worlds grow via the natural constraints and demands placed on them is probably yet another fractal representation (applicable to the universe as a whole) of how entropy increases over time.

Just like any legacy software system, we can only add more components. Taking out components means that we break the system for everything that depends on those older components.

Want better visual perception? Add more spectral sensitivity to eyes. Want a fast way to transfer information across massive distances? Hey, we've got electromagnetism already, why not arrange particles so that they can be distorted by the EM force to create gravity

New components introduce emergent phenomena that create new demands on the system, which lead to more emergent innovation. The keyword there is "emergent", and the only way to figure out how something new will behave is to actually create it. In that sense, there is fundamental uncertainty.

What we can reason with certainty about is constraints. This is "negative knowledge".

eg: We can't transmit photons faster than the speed we humans denote as Celeritas, which also implies transmission limits to all the components that depend on photon transmission. We can therefore reason about how things break.

eg: We can't build Artificial Intelligence using silicon transistors because General Intelligence is about interpretation of incoming signals (not about predictive / computative capacity).

The best information gatherer is water, so any "Artificial Intelligence" will have to use water (back to the point @[NeilBB](https://forum.jackkruse.com/members/6879/) made before about everything man-made having been already designed by nature). "Best" here is defined by how much information we can pack per unit matter of the material (not mass!), and nothing will beat water given the constraints of the Universe.

Again, we can say that Artificial Intelligence is not possible given existing technologies. Will new technologies be invented? No clue. It took more than a billion years for DHA to be invented (appeared 600 million years ago), under specific constraints, a specific set of resources, and a specific demand for more energy transduction capabilities. We cannot claim as Humans that something like AI is around the corner .... until we see the contingent innovations that make it possible (like control over water crystals to gather information, or maybe the invention of an even smaller crystalline compound that has all the behaviours of water and more).​

\----

The Thunderbolts Project posted a video yesterday about asking 'What if' questions.

I do this on a daily basis as part of being a software developer, and you can see that sort of thinking in some of my posts.

Notable:

-   Obsessed with efficiency -- [http://forum.jackkruse.com/index.ph...elcome-comments-here.12199/page-7#post-148113](http://forum.jackkruse.com/index.php?threads/tensegrity-9-is-live-welcome-comments-here.12199/page-7#post-148113)
-   If I were god, why would I create a signalling molecule like Superoxide? -- [http://forum.jackkruse.com/index.php?threads/reverse-electron-flow.12744/#post-154292](http://forum.jackkruse.com/index.php?threads/reverse-electron-flow.12744/#post-154292)

EDIT: wrong dates for DHA creation

### NeilBB — 2015-03-08T22:58:38+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/on-the-thermodynamics-of-comfort.10786/post-157916>

> [NeilBB said:](/goto/post?id=125856)
>
> Jack is always talking about "getting comfortable with your discomfort." I've been thinking about this from a thermodynamic perspective...
>
> For a living organism, the idea of “chronically seeking comfort” is extremely problematic because comfort is nothing but a state of psychological ease or equilibrium, which for a human means physical and mental relaxation, which if practiced chronically means the loss of the ability to adapt, stagnation, and then illness, atrophy, and eventually permanent equilibrium, better known as death.
>
> By contrast, embracing discomfort as a policy means remaining in an "athletic mental posture," ready to respond to any current or future challenge, known or unknown. It means placing the laws of the universe and the requirements of life ahead of one’s habits, wants, and desires. It is the policy of psychological adaptability and metastability. The psychological analog to the living state. One's acceptance of the responsibility implied by the laws of thermodynamics. Humans must aggressively avoid equilibrium in every way and seek metastability instead.
>
> Gretchen, why is the most depraved individual the one without a purpose? Because he values comfort more than productiveness. He increases rather than decreases both his own entropy and also the entropy of humanity. Productiveness really boils down to adaptability and metastability. Life requires movement and action. Physically and mentally. Literally and figuratively. Comfort is like a vacation. OK in small doses, but not as a lifetime aspiration.
>
> In medicine, "comfort" is really the tool of hospice, applicable to the dying, not the living. Yet our current medical system emphasizes satisfaction surveys over the hard reality of valid therapy, and pills over lifestyle adaptation. The entire modern world, in fact, happens to be built around the idea of maximizing the "comfort and convenience of the masses," regardless of consequences. It is basically the organizing principle of modern economic consumerism... Think about it... And then just look around to see the consequences…
>
> The "dream" that we have all been sold by the modern marketing machine is actually a nightmare which has blunted us to what it really means to have the responsibility of living. That responsibility is not about comfort, it is about metastability.

One has a fundamental choice when dealing with man-man-made frustrations and irrational dogmatists. You can be honest and allow yourself to feel angry about it, or you can let it beat you down and accept the feelings of guilt they will surely throw at you for who you are or want to be. Guilt is the favorite weapon of all dogmatists, and they are experts at wielding it. Accepting their imposed guilt is a mental cop-out and a form of elevating a need for "social comfort" over personal integrity.

Personally, I haven't felt a shred of unearned guilt in 20 years. (I had to learn how to be that way, and I did.) But I still feel plenty of anger and lots of frustration, regularly. But that hasn't aged me a bit. In my opinion, that's the most important anti-aging strategy that there is. _Never feel guilty if you haven't done anything wrong. Carrying chronic feelings of guilt is the fastest ticket to chronic disease and death that I know of._

(If one feels guilt over a genuine transgression, that's different. That's "earned guilt" and needs to be remedied in some way. It's important to know the difference, so as not to be an asshole.)

Contrary to popular belief, anger alone won't age you. But guilt will. Anger can be very productive. Guilt is always self-destructive. Anger is outwardly-directed and solution-oriented. Guilt is inner self-condemnation and universally paralyzing. Feeling honest anger is a form of embracing the "thermodynamics of discomfort" in my opinion and is often a necessary catalyst to difficult action. Battles must be picked strategically, of course, but anger itself is not a bad thing. It is simply a passionate and honest acknowledgement of some external threat or injustice.

I think many of us are benevolent people and want to avoid conflict, but in today's world, to systematically avoid conflict will eventually lead to the acceptance of unearned guilt. Book it. This is the deepest reason why no matter how uncomfortable it makes us, we must all learn to embrace a certain amount of respectful conflict, in order to "return the entropy" back to the dogmatists that so deserve it.

I choose to always fully acknowledge, feel, and embrace my anger so that I may have a chance to use it to achieve and to grow. I will always choose anger over guilt.

### shah78 — 2015-03-08T23:49:14+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/on-the-thermodynamics-of-comfort.10786/post-157923>

Bradshaw(not a personal favorite) did make a wonderful distinction between shame and guilt. Quilt comes from a "genuine transgression" as you mentioned. Shame is where all the problems come from. Superb answer Neil!

### Aaron K — 2025-03-17T13:47:02+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/on-the-thermodynamics-of-comfort.10786/post-348755>

> [SeaHorse said:](/goto/post?id=125916)
>
> I've been thinking about these ideas around discomfort and sleep and then started wondering about trauma. Animals who have access to their natural environments and can express their species behaviours often have lives full of trauma…prey animals particularly. But their nervous systems allow for the "excess energy" to disperse after the flight, fight, freeze period is over. They know what to do and they do not stay traumatized…it would lead to extinction. Domestic animals are another story.
>
> We western humans are educated out of knowing how to dispel and overcome trauma naturally...

Yes, ducks flap their wings vigorously after a conflict, but most people have forgotten that type of release and instead stuff difficult feelings down inside rather than feeling them. When hard feelings come up, we can feel them instead of suppressing, and they can be released. The way out is through. Physical action can also help to dissipate that tension after it is created.

Here's a good podcast on releasing trauma and unblocking energy flow.
Michael Singer - Learning to Unblock Your Energy Flow

{ "lightbox\_close": "Close", "lightbox\_next": "Next", "lightbox\_previous": "Previous", "lightbox\_error": "The requested content cannot be loaded. Please try again later.", "lightbox\_start\_slideshow": "Start slideshow", "lightbox\_stop\_slideshow": "Stop slideshow", "lightbox\_full\_screen": "Full screen", "lightbox\_thumbnails": "Thumbnails", "lightbox\_download": "Download", "lightbox\_share": "Share", "lightbox\_zoom": "Zoom", "lightbox\_new\_window": "New window", "lightbox\_toggle\_sidebar": "Toggle sidebar" }

Biophotons are ultra-weak light emissions produced by living organisms, thought to arise from metabolic processes like oxidative reactions in mitochondria. Centralized scientists and AI bots hypothesized them to play a role in cellular communication, potentially influencing gene expression or signaling pathways. FIAF, also known as angiopoietin-like protein 4 (ANGPTL4), is a protein produced by tissues like fat and the gut, and it’s a key player in lipid metabolism and microbiome construction. Neither understand how UPEs control FIAF. If the UPE is coherent then FIAF inhibits lipoprotein lipase, affecting how fats are stored or broken down, and its expression ramps up during fasting. The microbiome, meanwhile, is the community of gut microbes that can shift in response to diet, fasting, or host metabolism, influencing energy balance and health. If the UPEs in mitochondria is not coherent, the UPE changes and mental illness becomes more probable. You cannot burn fat so it changes neural signaling.

Centralized scientists and technocrats who build AI systems will say no direct study says “biophotons control FIAF to affect the microbiome,” but we can hypothesize based on related mechanisms. This is patently false. Why?

FIAF has known absorption and emission spectra to light frequencies. Because of that, a study is superfluous because each chemical in the world has an optical fingerprint. Just because a scientist has not published the work is immaterial to this BASIC biophysical fact in spectroscopy. This was what I brought Ray Peat over ten years ago, and he was impotent enough to answer my critiques of his work. This podcast covers these details.

### Clayton — 2014-05-09T02:02:53+0300

The most recent comment is interesting:

["Russ](http://www.wired.com/2014/05/one-of-the-6-biggest-ecological-disasters-in-the-last-4-5-billion-years-is-happening-right-now/?mbid=social_fb#) • [a day ago](http://www.wired.com/2014/05/one-of-the-6-biggest-ecological-disasters-in-the-last-4-5-billion-years-is-happening-right-now/?mbid=social_fb#comment-1375113246)
Whoa. Deeply insightful and intellectual: "things are changing"… When in the history of the universe have things \_not\_ changed? What arrogance is it that some people possess to think that a particular instant in time is sacrosanct and must be how-it-should-always-be? Given that since the dawn of life on this planet, 98% of all species have gone extinct, one could conclude that extinction is the norm."

Do we miss dinosaurs or any of the other organisms that were once but aren't now? Some of those form our fuel, fern type trees for example. It's an ongoing cycle what we lose now may become useful in the future. Species die out all the time from natural selection and man's interference of course. Climate change? Climate has changed many times, there is evidence of ice ages, if we are heading for another, there's isn't much we can do. You can't stop evolution unless you blow up the planet. That could be facilitated by a meteor strike any time, we're not going to be able to do anything to avoid it, just as there wasn't a choice the last time it happened.

# Thread: Sustainable Medicine...the 64 Thousand Dollar plus a few Zeros Question...
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/sustainable-medicine-the-64-thousand-dollar-plus-a-few-zeros-question.10794/>
**Subforum:** Educating Doctors
**Posts:** 66
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Josh — 2014-05-09T23:46:08+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/sustainable-medicine-the-64-thousand-dollar-plus-a-few-zeros-question.10794/post-126099>

At one point during the AMMG Conference in Orlando, Dr. Kruse turned to us and said "this model is not sustainable..." I do not recall him elaborating on it much....could be my lack of sticky proteins....sticky AQP4 proteins....still it stood out and hung in the air. I have been pondering and experiencing this for years. Functional medicine in one form or another has gotten me back on my feet several times to a point. However, it has always been expensive and the returns begin to diminish and plateau. The absence of a solid unifying conceptual model of function seems to hamper every form of healthcare and breeds what Jack calls "woo woo" and others call "magical thinking", I do so wish I could "dream it right". I am sure this keeps more than one doctor/practitioner up at night.....In many ways it dovetails with the "comfort" issue we have been thrashing about on for a few days. I know that Dr. Kruse has this factored into his "future of medicine" somewhere, perhaps now on the eve of the imminent OSF3 release that is purported to "explain all", we could dig in....

**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/sustainable-medicine-the-64-thousand-dollar-plus-a-few-zeros-question.10794/post-126110>

I am going to need help digging I fear......

### NeilBB — 2014-05-10T05:25:35+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/sustainable-medicine-the-64-thousand-dollar-plus-a-few-zeros-question.10794/post-126148>

> [Josh said:](/goto/post?id=126099)
>
> The absence of a solid unifying conceptual model of function seems to hamper every form of healthcare and breeds what Jack calls "woo woo" and others call "magical thinking", I do so wish I could "dream it right".

Man Josh, that's a big topic with a lot of chapters. Here's my 2 cents. Becker hits on a lot of organized medcine's developmental flaws in the intro to _The Body Electric_, including its overconfidence in pharmacology after the success of penicillin and the materialist backlash against the exaggerations and the scams of the 19th century vitalists. These factors helped to shape the materialist and pharmacological biases present today in organized medicine.

Alternative medicine tries to revive non-western medical ideas of considering broader "energy medicine" approaches, some of which clearly work, but often without any real scientific grounding. So it's very hard to know what is real and what is "woo."

Then, of course, economic, political, and military factors have complicated medical discovery and delivery greatly in the last few decades. This has largely corrupted the science of medicine and created a largely biased and dogmatic knowledge base. But that's a whole separate discussion in itself.

And then massive subpecialization within medicine and science has actually advanced many areas greatly but also fragmented us and moved us overall much farther away from any unifying theory.

Functional medicine then comes along and attempts to reintegrate the body into a whole. A step in the right direction for sure, but integration of the body is really all about energetics, and they too have no clue how "energy medicine" really works. Because they don't understand anything at all about energy, which falls squarely within the domain of physics.

The fear of physics and even biochemistry is very pervasive in medicine, which creates a major "functional gap" between experts in all these disciplines. Very few people are able to speak all the different languages necessary to unify all of this science. It's like the damn tower of babel (man I'm going biblical tonight for some reason).

So Jack being able to bridge these enormous gaps to the extent that he has thusfar is no small feat. He always says he's just "shining the flashlight," but I'd say what he's really doing is building bridges and breaking down language barriers between all the multiple fragmented islands of science that developed in isolation from each other. That's a pretty big deal.

### Josh — 2014-05-10T05:43:16+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/sustainable-medicine-the-64-thousand-dollar-plus-a-few-zeros-question.10794/post-126149>

I am waiting for Dr. Kruse to frame the discussion by clarifying what he meant when he said "this is not sustainable.." I have too many ideas that are not in focus yet. I will see what is here in the morning....of course he may be taking a break. For me sustainable can be everything from the eukaryote vs. prokaryote evolution and survivability point that was made elsewhere to whether a country can sustain its healthcare system economically. I see health as a human right that has been lost sight of in the extremes of the modern world. It amazes me that human rights are so often defined by extremes of abuse rather than balance and sustainability.

### ssj3 — 2014-05-10T11:52:02+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/sustainable-medicine-the-64-thousand-dollar-plus-a-few-zeros-question.10794/post-126165>

> this is not sustainable...

Maybe Jack has already told us:

From the highest level humans are destroying the three fundamental elements needed to provide energy to support life: Magnetism, water and light. Human intervention and technology is altering our primal planetary systems, which is already leading to the extinction of other species needed to support a diverse eco-system.

On the next level down our lifestyle and food choices in an altered environment are leading to de-evolution through epigenetic factors.

Then we have serious issues with the way modern care medicine is in tune to support/treat this.

All scientific theories, regardless of area of science involved, are partial models of an underlying territory which may harbor additional unknown complexity. The central measure of a theory is how useful it is in describing phenomena and making predictions. In physics as well as in biology there may be no ultimate truths or final theories. All we have is partial models of what is going on related to an underlying reality that may or may not be completely knowable. Throughout history and probably going back early into our prehistory, we have created models to explain things. Such models have included ones like: “The earthquake occurred because the Gods were angry,” “Matter consists of tiny particles with little hooks that hold them together,“ “The plague happened because of the prevalence of sin,” “Aging is primarily a result of progressive telomere shortening associated with cell division,“ and “Aging is primarily attributable to loss of global REDOX potential in cells.” Some models explain things well, and some are poor at doing this. Some models address only special aspects of an underlying reality and predict certain things, and other models address other aspects of the same fundamental reality and explain and predict other things. These models are intellectual structures put together mainly using natural language and the language of mathematics. There is also ample use of specialized languages like Feynman Diagrams in physics. In biology, our models may depend heavily on the use of natural language and the language of chemistry. There are countless pathway diagrams encountered in biology research publications. In general, biological organisms comprise vast networks of interacting loops and pathways with positive and negative feedback characteristics. Many of the things JanSz post in pictures is a perfect example. Important process loops are highly interconnected.

Here you need to understand The Bénard-Rayleigh convection cells and how they work. This is where physics couples to chemistry and biology.

Hmmm, so now to learn about these Benard-Rayleigh Cells????

**Rayleigh–Bénard convection** is a type of [natural convection](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Natural_convection), occurring in a plane horizontal layer of fluid heated from below, in which the fluid develops a regular pattern of [convection cells](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Convection_cells) known as **Bénard cells**. Rayleigh–Bénard convection is one of the most commonly studied convection phenomena because of its analytical and experimental accessibility.[\[1\]](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Rayleigh%E2%80%93B%C3%A9nard_convection#cite_note-getling-1) The convection patterns are the most carefully examined example of self-organizing[nonlinear systems](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Nonlinear_system).[\[1\]](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Rayleigh%E2%80%93B%C3%A9nard_convection#cite_note-getling-1) [\[2\]](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Rayleigh%E2%80%93B%C3%A9nard_convection#cite_note-koschmieder-2)

[Buoyancy](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Buoyancy), and hence [gravity](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Gravity), is responsible for the appearance of convection cells. The initial movement is the upwelling of lesser density fluid from the heated bottom layer.[\[3\]](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Rayleigh%E2%80%93B%C3%A9nard_convection#cite_note-3) This upwelling spontaneously organizes into a regular pattern of cells.

**Physical processes\[[edit](http://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Rayleigh%E2%80%93B%C3%A9nard_convection&action=edit&section=1)\]**
The features of Rayleigh–Bénard convection can be obtained by a simple experiment first conducted by [Henri Bénard](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Henri_B%C3%A9nard), a French physicist, in 1900.

**Development of convection\[[edit](http://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Rayleigh%E2%80%93B%C3%A9nard_convection&action=edit&section=2)\]**
[
](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/File:ConvectionCells.svg)
Convection cells in a gravity field
The experimental set-up uses a layer of liquid, e.g. water, between two parallel planes. The height of the layer is small compared to the horizontal dimension. At first, the temperature of the bottom plane is the same as the top plane. The liquid will then tend towards an [equilibrium](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Thermodynamic_equilibrium), where its temperature is the same as its surroundings. (Once there, the liquid is perfectly uniform: to an observer it would appear the same from any position. This equilibrium is also [asymptotically stable](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Asymptotic_stability): after a local, temporary perturbation of the outside temperature, it will go back to its uniform state, in line with the [second law of thermodynamics](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Second_law_of_thermodynamics)).

Then, the temperature of the bottom plane is increased slightly yielding a flow of thermal energy conducted through the liquid. The system will begin to have a structure of [thermal conductivity](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Thermal_conductivity): the temperature, and the density and pressure with it, will vary linearly between the bottom and top plane. A uniform linear gradient of temperature will be established. (This system may be modelled by [statistical mechanics](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Statistical_mechanics)).

Once conduction is established, the microscopic random movement _spontaneously_ becomes ordered on a macroscopic level, forming Benard convection cells, with a characteristic correlation length.

**Convection features\[[edit](http://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Rayleigh%E2%80%93B%C3%A9nard_convection&action=edit&section=3)\]**
[
](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/File:RayleighBernardConvection.png)
Simulation of Rayleigh–Bénard convection in 3D.
The rotation of the cells is stable and will alternate from clock-wise to counter-clockwise horizontally; this is an example of [spontaneous symmetry breaking](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Spontaneous_symmetry_breaking). Bénard cells are [metastable](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Metastability). This means that a small perturbation will not be able to change the rotation of the cells, but a larger one could affect the rotation; they exhibit a form of [hysteresis](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Hysteresis).

Moreover, the deterministic law at the microscopic level produces a non-deterministic arrangement of the cells: if the experiment is repeated, a particular position in the experiment will be in a clockwise cell in some cases, and a counter-clockwise cell in others. Microscopic perturbations of the [initial conditions](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Initial_conditions) are enough to produce a non-deterministic macroscopic effect. That is, in principle, there is no way to calculate the macroscopic effect of a microscopic perturbation. This inability to predict long-range conditions and sensitivity to initial-conditions are characteristics of[chaotic](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Complex_system#Chaotic_systems) or [complex](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Complex_systems#Complexity_and_chaos_theory) systems (i.e., the [butterfly effect](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Butterfly_effect)).

**_If the temperature of the bottom plane was to be further increased, the structure would become more complex in space and time; the [turbulent flow](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Turbulent_flow) would become [chaotic](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Chaos_theory)._**

Convective Bénard cells tend to approximate regular right hexagonal prisms, particularly in the absence of turbulence,[\[4\]](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Rayleigh%E2%80%93B%C3%A9nard_convection#cite_note-4)[\[5\]](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Rayleigh%E2%80%93B%C3%A9nard_convection#cite_note-5)[\[6\]](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Rayleigh%E2%80%93B%C3%A9nard_convection#cite_note-6) although certain experimental conditions can result in the formation of regular right square prisms[\[7\]](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Rayleigh%E2%80%93B%C3%A9nard_convection#cite_note-7) or spirals.[\[8\]](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Rayleigh%E2%80%93B%C3%A9nard_convection#cite_note-8)

The convective Bénard cells are not unique and will usually appear only in the surface tension driven convection. In general the solutions to the Rayleigh and Pearson[\[9\]](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Rayleigh%E2%80%93B%C3%A9nard_convection#cite_note-pearson-9)analysis (linear theory) assuming an infinite horizontal layer gives rise to degeneracy meaning that many patterns may be obtained by the system. Assuming uniform temperature at the top and bottom plates, when a realistic system is used (a layer with horizontal boundaries) the shape of the boundaries will mandate the pattern. More often than not the convection will appear as rolls or a superposition of them.

### Josh — 2014-05-10T17:49:04+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/sustainable-medicine-the-64-thousand-dollar-plus-a-few-zeros-question.10794/post-126194>

**The Rayleigh–Bénard Instability\[[edit](http://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Rayleigh%E2%80%93B%C3%A9nard_convection&action=edit&section=4)\]**
Since there is a density gradient between the top and the bottom plate, gravity acts trying to pull the cooler, denser liquid from the top to the bottom. This gravitational force is opposed by the viscous damping force in the fluid. The balance of these two forces is expressed by a non-dimensional parameter called the [Rayleigh number](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Rayleigh_number). The Rayleigh Number is defined as:

{ "lightbox\_close": "Close", "lightbox\_next": "Next", "lightbox\_previous": "Previous", "lightbox\_error": "The requested content cannot be loaded. Please try again later.", "lightbox\_start\_slideshow": "Start slideshow", "lightbox\_stop\_slideshow": "Stop slideshow", "lightbox\_full\_screen": "Full screen", "lightbox\_thumbnails": "Thumbnails", "lightbox\_download": "Download", "lightbox\_share": "Share", "lightbox\_zoom": "Zoom", "lightbox\_new\_window": "New window", "lightbox\_toggle\_sidebar": "Toggle sidebar" }

where

-   _T_u is the Temperature of the top plate
-   _T_b is the Temperature of the bottom plate
-   _L_ is the height of the container.
-   _g_ is the [acceleration due to gravity](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Gravitational_acceleration).
-   _ν_ is the [kinematic viscosity](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Kinematic_viscosity).
-   _α_ is the [Thermal diffusivity](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Thermal_diffusivity)
-   _β_ is the [Thermal expansion coefficient](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Coefficient_of_thermal_expansion)

As the Rayleigh number increases, the gravitational forces become more dominant. At a critical Rayleigh number of 1708,[\[2\]](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Rayleigh%E2%80%93B%C3%A9nard_convection#cite_note-koschmieder-2) the instability sets in, and convection cells appear.

The critical Rayleigh number can be obtained analytically for a number of different boundary conditions by doing a perturbation analysis on the linearized equations in the stable state.[\[10\]](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Rayleigh%E2%80%93B%C3%A9nard_convection#cite_note-10) The simplest case is that of two free boundaries, which Lord Rayleigh solved in 1916.[\[11\]](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Rayleigh%E2%80%93B%C3%A9nard_convection#cite_note-11) and obtained _R_c = 27⁄4 π4 ≈ 657.51.[\[12\]](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Rayleigh%E2%80%93B%C3%A9nard_convection#cite_note-12) In the case of a rigid boundary at the bottom, and a free boundary at the top (which is the situation in a kettle without a lid), the critical Rayleigh number comes out as _R_c = 1,100.65.[\[13\]](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Rayleigh%E2%80%93B%C3%A9nard_convection#cite_note-13)

**Effects of surface tension\[[edit](http://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Rayleigh%E2%80%93B%C3%A9nard_convection&action=edit&section=5)\]**
Main article: [Marangoni effect](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Marangoni_effect)
In case of a free liquid surface in contact with air, buoyancy and [surface tension](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Surface_tension) effects will also play a role in how the convection patterns develop. Liquids flow from places of lower surface tension to places of higher surface tension. This is called the [Marangoni effect](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Marangoni_effect). When applying heat from below, the temperature at the top layer will show temperature fluctuations. With increasing temperature, surface tension decreases. Thus a lateral flow of liquid at the surface will take place, from warmer areas to cooler areas. In order to preserve a horizontal (or nearly horizontal) liquid surface, cooler surface liquid will descend. This down-welling of cooler liquid contributes to the driving force of the convection cells. The specific case of temperature gradient-driven surface tension variations is known as thermo-capillary convection, or Bénard–Marangoni convection.

**History and nomenclature\[[edit](http://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Rayleigh%E2%80%93B%C3%A9nard_convection&action=edit&section=6)\]**
Rayleigh–Bénard convection, whose effects are due solely to a temperature gradient, was first successfully analyzed by [Lord Rayleigh](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Lord_Rayleigh); Rayleigh assumed boundary conditions in which the vertical velocity component and temperature disturbance vanish at the top and bottom boundaries (perfect thermal conduction). Those assumptions resulted in the analysis losing any connection with [Henri Bénard's](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Henri_B%C3%A9nard) experiment. This resulted in discrepancies between theoretical and experimental results until Pearson reworked the problem based on surface tension.[\[9\]](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Rayleigh%E2%80%93B%C3%A9nard_convection#cite_note-pearson-9) This is what was originally observed by Bénard. Nonetheless in modern usage "Rayleigh–Bénard convection" refers to the effects due to temperature, whereas "Bénard–Marangoni convection" refers specifically to the effects of surface tension.[\[1\]](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Rayleigh%E2%80%93B%C3%A9nard_convection#cite_note-getling-1) Davis and Koschmieder have suggested that the convection should be rightfully called the "Pearson–Bénard convection".[\[2\]](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Rayleigh%E2%80%93B%C3%A9nard_convection#cite_note-koschmieder-2)

Rayleigh–Bénard convection is also sometimes known as "Bénard–Rayleigh convection", "Bénard convection", or "Rayleigh convection".

[http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Rayleigh–Bénard\_convection](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Rayleigh%E2%80%93B%C3%A9nard_convection)
