### Jack Kruse — 2014-05-10T21:27:59+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/sustainable-medicine-the-64-thousand-dollar-plus-a-few-zeros-question.10794/post-126213>

In non-equilibrium structures just as we find in a metastable state, there is a net cycling of molecules around the system, from the hot end to the cold end and back again, the situation being more visually dramatic in the second case. What happens when chemical reactions are involved? Does chemical cycling also take place as the result of energy flow? When you understand this you begin to see why biochemistry is really thermoplastic because of the effect of water on hydrophilic proteins.

At equilibrium, i.e., without energy flow, forward and backward reactions are equal. This is the principle of microscopic reversibility or detailed balance, which is considered of great importance in chemistry.

Morowitz theorem comes out of these ideas..........I have mentioned his work in the blog at least once already.........The key to his work was how energy flow in metastable solid state physics. Things are coupled.

**In steady state systems, the flow of energy through the system from a source to a sink will lead to at least one cycle in the system.** Now think about what I told you about the function of the human brain.......it is where energy flows begin with us because of how DHA captures electrons and converts them to photons. The formation of steady state cycles has very important thermo-dynamic consequence that, despite large fluxes of materials and chemical transformations in the system, the net change in entropy of the system is zero, because entropy is a state function, a return to the same state will always entail no net entropy change. Of course, the compensatory change in entropy of the surroundings could be greater than zero, **_but entropy does not accumulate in the system_**, provided that the cycles are perfect in the sense that exactly the same state is reproduced, which is definitely not the case in real life. But the steady state approximation leads to some quite remarkable consequences for a cell.

### Josh — 2014-05-10T21:58:45+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/sustainable-medicine-the-64-thousand-dollar-plus-a-few-zeros-question.10794/post-126214>

Morowitz:

"
Morowitz received a B.S. in [physics](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Physics) and [philosophy](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Philosophy) in 1947, an M.S. in physics in 1950, and a Ph.D. in biophysics in 1951, all from [Yale University](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Yale_University). Morowitz was a professor in the department of [molecular biophysics](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Molecular_biophysics) and [biochemistry](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Biochemistry) at Yale from 1955 to 1987, also serving as the Master of [Pierson College](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Pierson_College) from 1981 to 1986. He is currently on the faculty at [George Mason University](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/George_Mason_University), which he joined in 1988 as Clarence Robinson Professor of [biology](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Biology) and [natural philosophy](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Natural_philosophy). He served as the founding director of the [Krasnow Institute for Advanced Study](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Krasnow_Institute_for_Advanced_Study) at George Mason from 1993 to 1998.[\[1\]](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Harold_J._Morowitz#cite_note-autogenerated1-1) Morowitz has been closely associated with the [Santa Fe Institute](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Santa_Fe_Institute)since 1987, where he is Chairman [Emeritus](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Emeritus) of the Science Board. He also served as the founding editor of the journal _[Complexity](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Complexity_\(journal\))_.[\[6\]](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Harold_J._Morowitz#cite_note-Cajal-6)[\[7\]](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Harold_J._Morowitz#cite_note-7) In the 1990s he contributed a monthly column on science and society to _[Hospital Practice](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Hospital_Practice)_.[\[6\]](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Harold_J._Morowitz#cite_note-Cajal-6)

Morowitz is a longtime consultant for [NASA](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/NASA), and served on the committees that planned the [quarantine](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Quarantine) procedures for [Apollo 11](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Apollo_11) and the biology experiments the [Viking probe](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Viking_program) carried to the surface of [Mars](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Mars). He was a member of the science advisory committee for [Biosphere 2](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Biosphere_2) in [Oracle, Arizona](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Oracle,_Arizona), which, at 3.14 acres, is the largest enclosed [ecosystem](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ecosystem) ever built.[\[8\]](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Harold_J._Morowitz#cite_note-8)

Some leading biophysicists have suggested that Morowitz may have discovered a "fourth [law of thermodynamics](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Law_of_thermodynamics)" when, in 1968, he found that, "in [steady state](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Steady_state)systems, the flow of [energy](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Energy) through the system from a source to a sink will lead to at least one cycle in the system."[\[9\]](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Harold_J._Morowitz#cite_note-9) Eric D. Schneider, for example, says, "Morowitz's cycling theorem is the best candidate for a fourth law of thermodynamics."[\[10\]](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Harold_J._Morowitz#cite_note-10)"

**The origin of life\[[edit](http://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Harold_J._Morowitz&action=edit&section=2&editintro=Template:BLP_editintro)\]**
Morowitz's book _Energy Flow in Biology_ laid out his central thesis that "the energy that flows through a system acts to organize that system,"[\[11\]](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Harold_J._Morowitz#cite_note-Morowitz1968-11) an insight later quoted on the inside front cover of _The Last [Whole Earth Catalog](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Whole_Earth_Catalog)_. He has long been a vigorous proponent of the view that life on earth emerged deterministically from the laws of chemistry and physics,[\[12\]](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Harold_J._Morowitz#cite_note-Morowitz2004-12) and so believes it highly probable that life exists widely in the universe.[\[4\]](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Harold_J._Morowitz#cite_note-autogenerated2-4)[\[13\]](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Harold_J._Morowitz#cite_note-13)

In 1983, he testified at "[McLean v. Arkansas](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/McLean_v._Arkansas)" (nicknamed "[Scopes II](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Scopes_Trial)") that [creationism](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Creationism) has no scientific basis and so should not be taught as science in public schools.[\[14\]](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Harold_J._Morowitz#cite_note-14)

[http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Harold\_J.\_Morowitz](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Harold_J._Morowitz)

[http://provost.gmu.edu/robinson/wp-...a-Santa-Fe-Institute-Bulletin-2012-vol-26.pdf](http://provost.gmu.edu/robinson/wp-content/uploads/2012/02/Origin-of-Life-Theory-by-krista-Zala-Santa-Fe-Institute-Bulletin-2012-vol-26.pdf)

> A major conundrum in origin of life theory
> was that the ci tric acid cycle relies on a
> steady source of complex organic molecules,
> which makes a fairly profound assumption
> that they run in rampant supply. (Plants,
> 16 Santa Fe Institute Bulletin 2012
> in contrast, rely on the even more complex
> Calvin cycle to harness light energy and use
> it to build organic compounds from inor
> ganic matter.) Where could these complex
> biomolecules have arisen?
> It took a voyage to the deep to boost
> origin of life theory. In 1977, Jack Corliss, r
> Richard von Herzen, and Robert Ballard
> led the deep-water submersible Alvin to
> the oceanic trenches off the Galapagos,
> where they discovered life at the seemingly
> hostile hydrothermal vents. On examin
> ing the organisms thriving amid the scald
> ing temperatures, immense pressures, and
> sulfur-spewing chimneys, they found some
> of the deepest-branching organisms on the
> tree of life. These included bacteria that ran
> the citric acid cycle-but in reverse.
> The revelation \[hat the cycle could run \[he
> other way "was like watching a fracture form
> that opens everything to sense," says Smith.
> After decades of struggling with the chicken
> or-egg paradox of complex and simple
> molecules that each required the other for its
> existence, the solution came from the light
> less biosphere: The mechanism originally
> arose to build small molecules into bigger
> ones. What we call the reverse cycle was the
> original direction, and the only way it ran
> for the first billion years, he says.
> Morowitz's recent work has shown that
> some small molecules can, in fact, speed
> metabolic reactions. He has also discovered
> how metals ranging through the transition
> elements of the Periodic Table from titanium
> to zinc, especially iron, cobalt, and nickel,
> can grab on to small organic molecules and
> nudge the cluster into an arrangement that
> catalyzes other biochemical reactions. Armed
> with such intriguing insights into the citric
> acid cycle development, Morowitz is now
> focusing on the stage where the compound
> citrate splits, adding a side path to form
> fatry acids, to determine why the cycle is
> structured that way. "If it is the only solu
> tion, or the best one, to metabolism, we
> have to learn why it is so," he says.
> Miraculous or Inevitable?
> Whereas Morowitz looks at the biochemical
> reactions from the top down, Smith is ap
> proaching the problem from the bottom up.
> A statistical physicist, Smith came to SFI in
> 2001 to pursue his interest in the processes
> by which order stems from dynamic systems.
> As he grew to know the SFI community,
> members recommended he meet Harold.
> "They remarked that when the \[\\'10 of you
> talk, you sound a lot alike, even though you
> talk abo ut differen t things," he recalls.
> Smith turns conventional origin of life
> theory upside down. From a dynamic sys
> terns perspective, life may have been forced
> into existence as an outlet for the mounting
> free energy from the sun and geosphere.
> Rather than being miraculous, life could be
> inevitable. It could even be construed as a
> natural consequence of Earth's thermody
> namic order, where the myriad and diverse
> high-energy systems on early, lifeless Earth
> "may have forced life into existence as a
> means to alleviate the buildup of free energy
> stresses," as he and Morowirz explained in a
> 2006 paper.

### Josh — 2014-05-10T21:59:49+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/sustainable-medicine-the-64-thousand-dollar-plus-a-few-zeros-question.10794/post-126215>

sus·tain·a·ble
səˈstānəbəl/
_adjective_
adjective: **sustainable**

1.  **1**.

    able to be maintained at a certain rate or level.
    "sustainable fusion reactions"

    -
        conserving an ecological balance by avoiding depletion of natural resources.
        "our fundamental commitment to sustainable development"

2.  **2**.
    able to be upheld or defended.
    "sustainable definitions of good educational practice"

"In [ecology](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ecology), **sustainability** is how biological systems endure and remain [diverse](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Biodiversity) and productive. Long-lived and healthy [wetlands](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Wetlands)and [forests](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Forests) are examples of sustainable biological systems. In more general terms, sustainability refers to the endurance of systems and processes. The [organizing principle](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Organizing_principle) for sustainability, is [sustainable development](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Sustainable_development), which includes the four interconnected domains, ecology, economics, politics and culture.[\[1\]](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Sustainability#cite_note-1) [Sustainability science](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Sustainability_science) is the study of the concepts of sustainable development and environmental science.

Healthy ecosystems and [environments](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Natural_environment) are necessary to the survival of humans and other organisms. Ways of reducing negative human impact are [environmentally-friendly chemical engineering](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Chemical_engineering), [environmental resources management](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Environmental_resources_management) and[environmental protection](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Environmental_protection). Information is gained from [green chemistry](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Green_chemistry), [earth science](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Earth_science), [environmental science](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Environmental_science) and [conservation biology](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Conservation_biology). [Ecological economics](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ecological_economics) studies the fields of academic research that aim to address human economies and natural ecosystems.

Moving towards sustainability is also a social challenge that entails [international](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/International_law) and national [law](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Law), [urban planning](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Urban_planning) and [transport](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Transport), local and individual [lifestyles](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Lifestyle_\(sociology\)) and [ethical consumerism](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ethical_consumerism). Ways of living more sustainably can take many forms from reorganising living conditions (e.g., [ecovillages](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ecovillages), [eco-municipalities](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Eco-municipalities) and [sustainable cities](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Sustainable_cities)), reappraising economic sectors ([permaculture](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Permaculture),[green building](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Green_building), [sustainable agriculture](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Sustainable_agriculture)), or work practices ([sustainable architecture](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Sustainable_architecture)), using science to develop new technologies ([green technologies](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Green_technologies), [renewable energy](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Renewable_energy) and sustainable [fission](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Generation_IV_reactor) and [fusion power](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Fusion_power)), to adjustments in individual [lifestyles](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Lifestyle_\(sociology\)) that conserve natural resources.

Despite the increased popularity of the use of the term "sustainability", the possibility that human societies will achieve environmental sustainability has been, and continues to be, questioned—in light of [environmental degradation](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Environmental_degradation), [climate change](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Climate_change), [overconsumption](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Overconsumption), and societies' pursuit of indefinite [economic growth](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Economic_growth) in a [closed system](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Closed_system).[\[2\]](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Sustainability#cite_note-2)[\[3\]](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Sustainability#cite_note-3)

The word sustainability is derived from the Latin _sustinere_ (_tenere_, to hold; _sus_, up). _Sustain_ can mean “maintain", "support", or "endure”.[\[4\]](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Sustainability#cite_note-4)[\[5\]](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Sustainability#cite_note-5) Since the 1980s_sustainability_ has been used more in the sense of human sustainability on planet Earth and this has resulted in the most widely quoted definition of sustainability as a part of the concept _[sustainable development](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Sustainable_development)_, that of the [Brundtland Commission](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Brundtland_Commission) of the [United Nations](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/United_Nations) on March 20, 1987: “sustainable development is development that meets the needs of the present without compromising the ability of future generations to meet their own needs.”[\[6\]](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Sustainability#cite_note-6)[\[7\]](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Sustainability#cite_note-7)"

[http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Sustainability](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Sustainability)

> In non-equilibrium structures just as we find in a metastable state, there is a net cycling of molecules around the system, from the hot end to the cold end and back again, the situation being more visually dramatic in the second case. What happens when chemical reactions are involved? Does chemical cycling also take place as the result of energy flow? When you understand this you begin to see why biochemistry is really thermoplastic because of the effect of water on hydrophilic proteins.
>
> At equilibrium, i.e., without energy flow, forward and backward reactions are equal. This is the principle of microscopic reversibility or detailed balance, which is considered of great importance in chemistry.
>
> Morowitz theorem comes out of these ideas..........I have mentioned his work in the blog at least once already.........The key to his work was how energy flow in metastable solid state physics. Things are coupled.
>
>
> **In steady state systems, the flow of energy through the system from a source to a sink will lead to at least one cycle in the system.** Now think about what I told you about the function of the human brain.......it is where energy flows begin with us because of how DHA captures electrons and converts them to photons. The formation of steady state cycles has very important thermo-dynamic consequence that, despite large fluxes of materials and chemical transformations in the system, the net change in entropy of the system is zero, because entropy is a state function, a return to the same state will always entail no net entropy change. Of course, the compensatory change in entropy of the surroundings could be greater than zero, **_but entropy does not accumulate in the system_**, provided that the cycles are perfect in the sense that exactly the same state is reproduced, which is definitely not the case in real life. But the steady state approximation leads to some quite remarkable consequences for a cell.

Morowitz emphasizes that it is the balanced _flow_ of energy through the system, not just the _absorption_ of energy that is key to this coupling organization of matter. Without both an energy source and sink, too much energy would accumulate and this would be unstable and no longer fuel this cyclic process. He points out, for example, that the earth does not absorb net energy from the sun because it radiates heat from its dark side to dissipate the photonic absorption from the bright side to remain in constant equilibrium. If it were unable to do that, it would heat up and the conditions to support life would **not** be present.

The same would seemingly have to be true of an ecosystem or an individual living organism. The ability to maintain a perfect equilibrium of flow of energy (source and sink) through an animal capable of both fat storage and heat dissipation would seem to be the responsibility of the leptin system, as you have emphasized.

I wonder if hyperthyroidism/electron excess that you recently alluded to in that podcast is analogous to the earth not being able radiate enough heat to cool off? The problem is more related to a problem of energy outflow than intake in these cases?

And is that what global warming is essentially? The earth becoming hyperthyroid?

>
> When you are designed to collect electrons all you need to do is focus on that side of the equation in all disease states.

Yes but "collecting electrons" is clearly an oversimplification you have been using right? (The idea that hyperthyroidism is a problem of electron excess seems to open up a whole new can of worms now. So what is electron excess? It must be a problem of _flow_. Electron energy not being able to be harnessed properly.) What we really have to do is maximize proper electron _flow_ across our semiconductors at all times. It's about the continuous electron flow and the _harnessing_ of the electron's power as it falls back to ground state from an excited state, maybe giving off a photon like in a Feynman QED diagram. Dissipating the electron mass as heat and harnessing the massless photon for some holographic magic. Right?

So you can keep adding electrons to your system but you can't really _collect_ electrons per se because you cant really _store_ them too much. Can you? You can store fat, sure. But you always emphasize that breakdown of fat provides more protons, ATP, and heat than it does electrons. So with electrons, you can replenish them constantly and try to prevent their leakage into the hungry modern atmosphere (which seems basically like an electrical short circuit BTW).

If the atmosphere were cold and less electrified, I bet we would be "leaking energy" as protons and heat and not so much as electrons to establish our thermodynamic equilibrium. And then we wouldn't ever be fat or diabetic. That's what happened in the past isn't it?

So today, you need to establish the protein hydration semiconductor framework in your body and then you have to stay plugged in to both source and sink (sun and ground) most of the time otherwise electron flow stagnates. And magnetism of course, works precisely because it excites and moves electrons. It induces a current. Electrons have paramagnetic resonance, and so a magnetic field _moves_ electrons. And the wrong AC magnetic field _wastes_ electrons and shorts the circuit. And a targeted AC magnetic field is also capable of enhancing regional electron flow if done correctly.

Electrons have mass which means they can speed up and slow down. So our job has to be to keep them moving? Photons can't stop moving because they are massless. They always travel at _c._

Aren't we really trying to keep electrons flowing then, so we can ultimately collect the photons? Let's talk about photons...

They always move at the speed of light and carry different energies depending on their specific "wavelength." Wavelength carries chronobiological information that can be decoded and utilized. So how long is the life of a biophoton in the body? How is the photon's massless energy harnessed? Where is it eventually dissipated back into the environment?

The Onsager reciprocity relation Neil is another key issue for you to read about.........in that shows how symmetrical coupling of processes can arise naturally in a system under energy flow. This is critical in our bodies........and 1945 was a long time ago so we need to examine his ideas..........microscopic reversibility is a very key point.

The principle of microscopic reversibility requires that every forward transition in energy flow is balanced in detail by its reverse transition. Now consider what this means to today's world or our condition of existence.

If the thermodynamics of a system is being irradiated by some non native electromagnetic force we have a situation a constant influx of electromagnetic radiation to the system (ionosphere), such that there is net flow (efflux/influx) of photons/electrons by the system. Eventually a steady state will be reached. A cell is designed to break this It is a dissipative system to return entropy back to the environment naturally by its design..........it is not every at equilibrium when alive by design. We miss this point, big time in biology because we think left to right and not right to left. At equilibrium there is a flow of heat out into the reservoir (redox sink) equal to the flux of electromagnetic energy into the system. Death is when we are at equilibrium (Ling). As we approach equilibrium (illness) there is always a net loss of energy from the redox reservoir. Onsager original idea he usurped from Einstein.

All these ideas I have woven into OSF3.

See Onsager (1945). See Denbigh (1951) for a very clear and accessible account on what he was knocking on.

I don’t know if this is related or not to the conversion of energy that takes place but it’s something I have been pondering for some time and that is rectifying a wave by semiconducting. In electronics to rectify a wave is to convert its kinetic (alternating current) component to a potential (direct current) component. An alternating current can’t be stored but a direct current can. Rectifying circuits are typically used in power supplies to transform the line voltage alternating current to a steady state direct voltage… in other words kinetic to potential, stopping a wave in its tracts and storing it to be used at a later time.

### NeilBB — 2014-05-12T16:46:01+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/sustainable-medicine-the-64-thousand-dollar-plus-a-few-zeros-question.10794/post-126451>

> [RobH said:](/goto/post?id=126309)
>
> That is such a more simple explanation than you offered way back when in the first CT blogs. I guess in retrospect you were describing all the salutary effects on the system, but not giving us the "why"
>
> Yet another light goes on in my slow brain...
>
> RobH

Rob, the method that Jack uses of teaching a complex integrated subject by repeatedly returning to an older concept, but now with a new context and much deeper understanding is actually necessary to build new complex integrated knowledge. This is an epistemological issue. It occurs because human learning is a sequential process. Objectivists actually have a name for it, the "spiral theory of knowledge."

> The **spiral theory \[of knowledge\]**is simply this idea: first we learn a given idea; then we leave it -- we move on to something else, we learn other subjects. Then we encounter the original idea again, but now with more knowledge, with a deeper context.\[Extemporaneous, unedited formulation. From Leonard Peikoff, "Understanding Objectivism" lecture series (1984), Lecture 3.\]
>
> It's basically that the first time you encounter something, you cannot learn _everything_ about it, because some things about it are _in relation to things you don't yet know_. The more you learn, the greater your _potential understanding_ is of topics you've already visited. So on revisiting them, you learn new things about them, gaining a deeper, more tightly integrated understanding of them, and of the things they relate to. It's because understanding _is integration_.

This idea is implicit in Jack's "quilt" method. It's also the reason why when you go back and read an old blogpost, you can see things in there now that you might swear weren't even there before. It's because your contextual knowledge has grown so much. You can now make new connections. Spiral theory in action...

# Thread: Amazing Research Using Ketones + High Carbs to Fight Cancer
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/amazing-research-using-ketones-high-carbs-to-fight-cancer.10795/>
**Subforum:** Mitochondrial Rx
**Posts:** 6
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### persistence — 2014-05-10T09:40:17+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/amazing-research-using-ketones-high-carbs-to-fight-cancer.10795/post-126162>

I happened on a cancer study today that is remarkable. The researchers put mice with metastatic cancer onto a 60% carb diet, which would be normally a quick death sentence. But the test groups were then fed in addition to the carbs a ketone ester that forced production of ketones in addition to the dietary glucose.

The presence of ketones dramatically improved the survival rates, even though these rats were eating carbs like crazy:

[http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/24615175](http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/24615175)

To me this has to be an incredibly important study finding. The reason is that they are getting the benefits of ketogenic diets on cancer growth without forcing people to eat a ketogenic diet. This has huge implications for human survival because all of those cancer patients who lack the discipline or knowledge to pursue a ketogenic diet would be able to continue on a "normal" US diet of high carbs, supplement with a ketone generator, and still get a remarkable improvement on survival rates.

The research for me inspires additional questions. What if the patient was eating ketogenic and used the ketone salts to generate even higher levels of ketones. Would this further improve survival rates?

This is the first study I have seen in a while that makes me think they are getting close to putting a big dent into cancer.

### Gracelind — 2014-05-11T21:49:22+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/amazing-research-using-ketones-high-carbs-to-fight-cancer.10795/post-126328>

Thanks for posting this study, persistence. My son and I were just talking about his supervisor whose ovarian cancer is recurring. Her docs are giving her a sad prognosis, telling her she likely won't survive 10 years. To me that seems optimistic in the extreme, based on my experience with my mother's OVCA and immersion in online OVCA communities for several years. That was the first forum I've belonged to where women left . . . because they died.

My son says she has not removed sugar from her diet and is still eating SAD. She has year-old twins and is still early 30s. She has stuck to conventional medicine, only recently looking into acupuncture. My mother was the same, listening only to the oncologist and not the chemo nurses with their hands-on experience with the patients.

As we've all noted in our lives and many times in this forum, you can't help someone who doesn't want to listen and act. My son is reluctant to impose and it's true that when someone gets sick, the advice starts pouring in from all sides. So much of it usually garbage and just fending off the well-meaning misinformed can be exhausting.

If further study proves the value of ketones in human cancers it could make such a difference for patients. Maybe they will live long enough to learn about the Kruse insights and change their own lives.

### HoneyChild — 2014-05-10T12:39:40+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/radio-5-live-could-send-robins-on-the-wrong-flightpath.10796/post-126167>

**Electromagnetic noise from AM broadcast signal could be disrupting the migratory flight paths of birds, say scientists**

The popularity of stations such as Radio 5 Live could be causing migratory birds such as the robin to lose their way, scientists have suggested.

Researchers have found that robins' in-built compass is disturbed by electromagnetic "noise" emitted by AM radio transmitters.

Professor Henrik Mouritson, of the University of Oldenburg in Germany, said: “Every piece of equipment with a plug, when you plug it in, it’s going to make electromagnetic noise at some frequency range.

“If you could sense this it would probably be like some kind of rock concert going on all the time.”

Migratory birds can sense the Earth’s magnetic field and use it as an in-built compass to help them fly in the right direction.

It has long been suspected that electromagnetic noise made by humans could be interfering with their compass.

The team made the discovery after putting migrating birds in a cabin. Because birds are so eager to migrate, they will jump in the direction they want to fly.

However over three years, the team were surprised to find that none of the birds tried to fly in the correct direction.

But when they shielded the cabin against radio waves with aluminium, the birds began to jump in their usual migratory direction.

“The frequency that we found effects the birds is the radio frequency AM band, from about 50 kHz to about 5 mHz, and that means that it is not mobile phones and it’s not power line,” said Mouritson “It’s radio signals.”

“It may be a localised effect which only effects them when they are in towns and cities and as soon as they go out of town they are fine.”

The research was published in the journal Nature.

[http://www.telegraph.co.uk/journali...ould-send-robins-on-the-wrong-flightpath.html](http://www.telegraph.co.uk/journalists/sarah-knapton/10814764/Radio-5-Live-could-send-robins-on-the-wrong-flightpath.html)

### Lahelada — 2014-05-10T16:26:19+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/radio-5-live-could-send-robins-on-the-wrong-flightpath.10796/post-126186>

Interesting that he says that it is not power lines or cell phones. What does not make sense to me is that we have had AM radio for decades so why did they not get confused before. Has the AM frequency been in so much demand recently? Thanks for posting.

### sooperb — 2014-05-11T20:50:01+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/radio-5-live-could-send-robins-on-the-wrong-flightpath.10796/post-126319>

UK robins are not migratory so in the UK they wouldn't be affected (not effected!)

# Thread: Seed Crackers
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/seed-crackers.10798/>
**Subforum:** Epi-Paleo Recipes
**Posts:** 10
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### fitness@home — 2014-05-11T02:47:27+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/seed-crackers.10798/post-126236>

I miss crackers.

I can’t eat flax seeds due to sensitivity, so I had to drop the Mary’s Gone Crackers variety. Tweaked this recipe and I like it.

Basic Recipe:
1 C water
½ C chia seeds
½ C pumpkin seeds
½ C sesame seeds (didn't have any so dropped this ingredient and increased sunflower/pumpkin seeds)
½ C sunflower seeds
½ tsp sea salt (dissolved in the water)
Freshly ground pepper to taste
1 TBL seaweed sprinkles
¼ C chopped chives

Pre-soak the pumpkin, sesame, and sunflower seeds in spring water overnight. Use enough water to cover them. (I do not soak the chia seeds overnight because they would turn into a gelatinous glob) .
Drain the soaked seeds well.

\- Put 1C water in a blender or Magic Bullet and add chopped chives, sea salt, & seaweed sprinkles and blend well.
\- Combine ALL the seeds and the 1 C blended water in a bowl and allow to sit for 10 minutes to let the chia seeds become somewhat gelatinous.
\- Spread the batter onto a cookie sheet lined with parchment paper using a spoon. It should be less than ¼” thick.
\- Bake in preheated 350\* oven for 30 minutes and then slice into crackers using a pizza wheel.
\- Turn them over carefully to the other side and transfer back into oven for another 20-30 minutes.
\- Allow to cool completely before eating. Store in an air-tight container and it will keep up to a week.

I store portions in the freezer for later use.

Ideas for flavors: Mexican – chili powder, cumin, & cayenne pepper (Nacho chip replacement)
Greek seasonings – great with zucchini hummus
Savory – garlic clove, rosemary, thyme Italian – Italian herbs and a little Parmesan cheese
Slightly Sweet – ¼ raw honey dissolved in the water, cinnamon, nutmeg, ginger.

Will be experimenting ^^^

{ "lightbox\_close": "Close", "lightbox\_next": "Next", "lightbox\_previous": "Previous", "lightbox\_error": "The requested content cannot be loaded. Please try again later.", "lightbox\_start\_slideshow": "Start slideshow", "lightbox\_stop\_slideshow": "Stop slideshow", "lightbox\_full\_screen": "Full screen", "lightbox\_thumbnails": "Thumbnails", "lightbox\_download": "Download", "lightbox\_share": "Share", "lightbox\_zoom": "Zoom", "lightbox\_new\_window": "New window", "lightbox\_toggle\_sidebar": "Toggle sidebar" }

The content on here is pretty overwhelming thus far but keeps me coming back everyday! I've been absorbing as much as I can for the last week and my excitement is growing to try some of these concepts.

My health is good overall, but there are things I'd love to solve or improve upon. Hoping this is the beginning of a new phase for me. Talk to you all soon again.

Jason (I don't know why my picture is sideways, but I'll figure it out)

# Thread: Motherhood and body changes
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/motherhood-and-body-changes.10805/>
**Subforum:** The Cave
**Posts:** 19
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### fitness@home — 2014-05-11T20:16:50+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/motherhood-and-body-changes.10805/post-126314>

Happy Mother's Day to all the Moms here!

Didn't really know where to put this thread. For the women that have had children - we are well aware of the changes it leaves in/on our bodies.

It's been 27 years since my last daughter was born. Today after my shower I noted that my left hip is lower than my right by 1 to 1.5 inches. Thinking back, I always carried them on my left hip because I am right handed and it left that hand free to do other things.

Little did I know that I would carry it with me for this long!

### SeaHorse — 2014-05-11T21:11:39+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/motherhood-and-body-changes.10805/post-126322>

Happy Mother's Day …..today was the first Mother's Day in 22 years that neither of our girls was home. Spent the morning getting ready to hit the road with my husband and my horse later in the week…..seemed appropriate for this new phase of life and health in our family….we didn't have to be together to feel connected and we're all engaged separately in something we love to do. I don't think they make a Hallmark Card for that particular sentiment do they?

### fitness@home — 2014-05-11T21:32:41+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/motherhood-and-body-changes.10805/post-126327>

I think that is appropriate! Empty nest is a nice phase to get used to.

My three children live in three different states - Ohio, Colorado, and California. The last time we were all in the same location was at my yougest daughter's wedding last October. We do stay connected though. Seems like when one calls, I hear from the other two within 24 hours

Tried to call my Mom, but they are on a vacation in the Outer Banks, NC. My Dad's not real handy with their cell phone (only use it when traveling). Seeing them in June for their 55th wedding anniversary will have to suffice.

### Stella — 2014-05-11T22:59:58+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/motherhood-and-body-changes.10805/post-126336>

> [fitness@home said:](/goto/post?id=126314)
>
> Happy Mother's Day to all the Moms here!
>
> Didn't really know where to put this thread. For the women that have had children - we are well aware of the changes it leaves in/on our bodies.
>
> It's been 27 years since my last daughter was born. Today after my shower I noted that my left hip is lower than my right by 1 to 1.5 inches. Thinking back, I always carried them on my left hip because I am right handed and it left that hand free to do other things.
>
> Little did I know that I would carry it with me for this long!

Happy mothers day to all the mothers.

Dawn, I find it amusing that I too have a right hip that is lower than my left. So true that we carry these loving reminders on our bodies. Stretch marks etc. totally worth it.

My boys are still at home so I'm still enjoying them.

### caroline — 2014-05-12T01:33:33+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/motherhood-and-body-changes.10805/post-126353>

Happy Mother's Day to all the wonderful women here.....

It never occurred to me to look to see if one hip is lower than the other ......

### nicld — 2014-05-12T02:15:02+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/motherhood-and-body-changes.10805/post-126358>

Happy Mothers day to one and all as well (even if you are a mother of the 4 legged kind).

# Thread: Evolution and Thermodynamics
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/evolution-and-thermodynamics.10806/>
**Subforum:** Educating Doctors

══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### NeilBB — 2014-05-11T22:36:03+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/evolution-and-thermodynamics.10806/post-126330>

So, trying to think like Jack and look at bio-energy and evolution from an atomic physics (not molecular) perspective, energy seems to come in three flavors:

1\. High mass (protons) (prokaryotic)
2\. Low mass (electrons) (eukaryotic)
3\. No mass (photons) (advanced brains)

Evolutionarily it seems that the more advanced you are, the less massive particles you are capable of utilizing to power your primary life processes? This must provide you some major entropic benefit according to mass-equivalence. But advanced species seem to also retain the more primitive systems as well, which can be used when necessary, but at a higher energy cost. So is evolution really about working more efficiently within the laws of thermodynamics trying to create more organization with less entropy in a system? More energy flow through the system to structure cells and molecules with less overall entropic decay of the system?

The jump from prokaryotic to eukaryotic organisms was clearly about the advent of mitochondria which took advantage of the abundance of environmental oxygen to increase energy efficiency in a living system.

So is the jump from simple eukaryotic organisms to large brained animals a similar adaptation, but this time to take maximal advantage of the abundance of environmental _light_?!

### Josh — 2014-05-11T22:58:09+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/evolution-and-thermodynamics.10806/post-126335>

So I finished Kolbert's "The Sixth Extinction" and I was left wondering about what things look like at the microbiological level in the soil, water and within the bodies of larger organisms such as us? If they are responsible for sensing and adapting to the environments that are changing faster than the larger organisms can adapt, then they must be experiencing even more rapid rates of change and creep up the mountain, etc.. than the larger organisms. She only got into this a little with the plankton that handle calcium to form carbonates and the effect of ocean acidification on them. The image of the increasingly acidic ocean on the castle in the Mediterranean, must have its paralells in our gut microbiome and tissues.....more protons and less electrons favoring prokaryotes perhaps? Forget about advanced brains.....

### Jack Kruse — 2014-05-12T20:57:04+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/evolution-and-thermodynamics.10806/post-126500>

Water chemistry change is devastating to the southern hemisphere.......because all of their food chain is tied to it on land........they cant eat a lot of seafood because the sea is dying rapidly around them and none of them know it. This is why amphibians and the Great Barrier Reef are the canaries...........If your water can not carry as much energy as it needs........and you work your semiconduction on the lowest energy state possible........what do you think a small change in energy carrying ability in water would do to the overall system? It will destroy it fast. Hence, why Australia has the disease profile it now does. This is their core issue. The sea water is mixed globally so it has a much higher ability to carry energy than land based water cycle.......through ocean conduction currents and the Southern hemisphere corriolis effect is most powerful on the planet pulling cold water up from the South pole's continental shelf.........

### yewwei.tan — 2014-05-13T03:21:59+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/evolution-and-thermodynamics.10806/post-126554>

> [Jack Kruse said:](/goto/post?id=126500)
>
> Water chemistry change is devastating to the southern hemisphere.......because all of their food chain is tied to it on land........they cant eat a lot of seafood because the sea is dying rapidly around them and none of them know it. This is why amphibians and the Great Barrier Reef are the canaries...........If your water can not carry as much energy as it needs........and you work your semiconduction on the lowest energy state possible........what do you think a small change in energy carrying ability in water would do to the overall system? It will destroy it fast. Hence, why Australia has the disease profile it now does. This is their core issue. The sea water is mixed globally so it has a much higher ability to carry energy than land based water cycle.......through ocean conduction currents and the Southern hemisphere corriolis effect is most powerful on the planet pulling cold water up from the South pole's continental shelf.........

Dumping some random thoughts here ...

Using Australia as an example since that's the Island we're focused on. Also, let's assume no El Nino or La Nina events for now to focus the discussion.

I assume factors like ocean currents, winds, and seismic activity affect which regions are affected the most by the Donut Hole. If so, then disease statistics by Australian State should reveal which areas are affected the most, and then we can discern what geographical factors affect our health the most on this Island. Of course, my selfish goal will be to see which location (if any) in Australia is the most conducive to good health.

The west and south coast gets the brunt of the Antarctic currents. Major cities here are Perth, Adelaide, Melbourne, and all of Tasmania, which let's just say encompasses half of the Aussie population.

The other half of the population would be in the:

-   North -- Northern Territory, which gets the same weather patterns as Indonesia
-   North-East -- Queensland, which gets ocean currents from the south equatorial stream, as well as equatorial easterly winds
-   South-East -- New South Wales (hi caroline!), which share the same ocean currents as Queensland, but generally get the same weather systems as Victoria further down south due to its (NSW's) latitude; NSW is affected by the same westerly winds that affect basically the entire south half of Australia.

Note: the Great Barrier Reef (GBR) is in the North-East, basically from the north of Cairns, to the north of Brisbane (another big city with >1mil people). The water that ends up in the GBR from the south equatorial current comes partly from the northern hemisphere, and mostly from the southern hemisphere (from South America and Antarctic), which is probably why it is less electron dense. I'm also wondering what the effects on coral around other South Pacific like Fiji are at the moment.

I am not skilled enough to figure out which combination of those factors are best for human health, or if they even matter at all in the face of the Donut hole.

\----

Speaking of the disease spectrum, Autism seems to be more prevalent in the Southern states -- Victoria, Tasmania, and NSW. I have not yet looked at current data that is granular enough (most detailed data I found is from 2006-2007 at the latest). Many reports do note however that Autism rates that vary less across states than they are in the US.

Obesity rates (I assume higher means more electron steal) seem to be higher in areas that are further inland -- 2013 report [http://www.myhealthycommunities.gov...verweight\_and\_Obesity\_Report\_October\_2013.pdf](http://www.myhealthycommunities.gov.au/Content/publications/downloads/NHPA_HC_Report_Overweight_and_Obesity_Report_October_2013.pdf) . Maybe that has something to do with sun exposure and grounding.

\----

Personally, I guess I'll have to do some biohacks, and attempt to live in various parts of Australia for at least a year, and then try some places in the Northern hemisphere once I'm done. A trip to Cairns to catch the Sun and beach sounds awfully good right now ... apparently they don't fluoridate their water too.

### Josh — 2014-05-13T04:04:56+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/evolution-and-thermodynamics.10806/post-126567>

> [yewwei.tan said:](/goto/post?id=126555)
>
> Another random thought, I wonder how we can measure how electron dense an oyster from Coffin Bay (South Australia) is compared to one from the gulf.

Photon camera/sensor I believe they exist as they have measured this for things such as organic versus non-organic vegetables, eggs, etc.. Popp's work with the Biophoton meter...

[http://eko-eu.com/applications/measuring-photon-active-radiation-par](http://eko-eu.com/applications/measuring-photon-active-radiation-par)

[http://www.hamamatsu.com/us/en/technology/innovation/photoncounting/index.html](http://www.hamamatsu.com/us/en/technology/innovation/photoncounting/index.html)

"Since the onset of biophoton research, various
applications have been developed by Popp's group and
the other labs. Because biophoton emission reflects all
external influences and intemal changes of an organism
its measurement in principle can be used to determine
the state of the organism and for the detection and
assessment of all types of influences, even if their nature
is not known. Besides the use of biophoton
measurements for chemiluminescence detection, the
method can be used for the detection and damage
assessment of environmental contamination by all types
of solidi liquid, and gaseous chemicals, and
electromagnetic fields. **Another application that is already
used in practice is food quality assessment.** Some
medical applications are under developmentl but not yet
completely ready for use. For instance, it has been found
that cancer tissue displays completely different biophoton
emission characteristics that healthy tissue of the same
type. This could be used for developing a non-invasive
method of cancer diagnostics, and also could serve to
determine the optimal therapeutic treatment for a
particular patient. Biophoton measurements have also
widely been used to assess the effects and effectivity of
various therapeutic modalities, mainly in the field of
alternative and complementary medicine."

[http://www.rexresearch.com/biophotons/BiophotonsTheLightinOurCells6.pdf](http://www.rexresearch.com/biophotons/BiophotonsTheLightinOurCells6.pdf)

# Thread: CFS help
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/cfs-help.10807/>
**Subforum:** Biohacking 101
**Posts:** 40
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Sid6554 — 2014-05-12T00:07:36+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/cfs-help.10807/post-126344>

Hello Jack and firstly thank you so much for helping everyone that reaches your site with the RIGHT info!
Much more than can be said about my GP.

Anyway, as a sufferer of Chronic Fatigue Syndrome, I would appreciate if you list out the things we CFS sufferers need to do, just a para of bullet points.

Symptoms:
Insomnia, non-restorative sleep, debilitating fatigue, brain fog, thirst, muscle cramps, cold hands irritability, no concentration, no motivation, no pleasure response, unwanted weight loss, fullness, ibs, socially inept.
(No = v.v.little  )
For me, there was a temporary huge improvement in some symtoms using antibiotics - something you may or may not want to further research.
Since getting my illness I have poor attention span and so cannot navigate your site well, especially as there is no section for CFS as such, so on behalf of all the CFS sufferers, I would request a para of bullet points stating what we must do. Thank you very much

> An extreme example of this emergent phenomena is the function of chaperone proteins, which provide an environment favoring a specific folding. Methylation and acetylation programs are run by chaperone proteins. Therefore, we have the possibility that a single amino acid sequence, such as a peptide, dictated by a gene coding sequence, can fold into more than one protein and, therefore, perform more than one biological activity. This is precisely how virus’s and prions act in biology as well. They are capable of altering cell phenotype and function directly. It is also how autoimmune antibodies cause havoc in a cell. Molecular mimicry is a morphologic term for protein emergence after electronic induction by subatomic particles.

OSF3

Lewy Body transports the Prion or it is the Prion? Does the Lewy Body transport ASYN or is it just a product of ASYN misfolding?

> **Lewy bodies** are abnormal aggregates of [protein](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Protein) that develop inside [nerve cells](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Nerve_cell) in [Parkinson's disease](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Parkinson%27s_disease) (PD),[Lewy body dementia](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dementia_with_Lewy_bodies) and some other disorders. They are identified under the [microscope](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Microscope) when [histology](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Histology) is performed on the [brain](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Brain).
>
> Lewy bodies appear as spherical masses that displace other cell components. The two morphological types are classical (brain stem) Lewy bodies and cortical Lewy bodies. A classical Lewy body is an [eosinophilic](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Eosinophilic) [cytoplasmic](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Cytoplasm)inclusion consisting of a dense core surrounded by a halo of 10-nm-wide radiating fibrils, the primary structural component of which is [alpha-synuclein](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Alpha-synuclein). In contrast, a cortical Lewy body is less well defined and lacks the halo. Nonetheless, it is still made up of alpha-synuclein fibrils. Cortical Lewy bodies are a distinguishing feature of Dementia with Lewy bodies (DLB), but may occasionally be seen in ballooned neurons characteristic of [Pick's disease](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Pick%27s_disease) and [corticobasal degeneration](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Corticobasal_degeneration),[\[1\]](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Lewy_body#cite_note-1) as well as in patients with other [tauopathies](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Tauopathies).[\[2\]](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Lewy_body#cite_note-2) They are also seen in cases of [multiple system atrophy](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Multiple_system_atrophy), particularly the Parkinsonian variant.[\[3\]](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Lewy_body#cite_note-3)

[http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Lewy\_body](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Lewy_body)

ASYN misfolding causes Lewy Body buildup....

> The protein alpha-synuclein (ASYN) is thought to be involved in the development of dementia with Lewy bodies (DLB). Overexpression of ASYN has been linked to cellular toxicity and human disease, and in experimental models, chaperones such as heat shock proteins (HSPs) are protective against ASYN toxicity. We have assessed the abundance of mRNA for ASYN and chaperones and the abundance and solubility of the encoded proteins in temporal cortex from sporadic human DLB. We found a reduction of ASYN mRNA in DLB (44.9% of control). The abundance of the Triton-soluble fraction (bioavailable protein) was not altered, but there was an increase of the Triton-insoluble component (likely representing aggregates). We evaluated 3 chaperones: HSP70, HSP90, and HDJ1. HSP70 mRNA was increased in DLB, whereas the mRNAs for HSP90 and HDJ1 were unchanged. HSP70 accumulated in the Triton-soluble fraction, whereas HSP90 and HDJ1 proteins accumulated in the Triton-insoluble fraction. **_These observations suggest that sporadic DLB is not associated with overexpression of ASYN. Rather, the persistence of normal soluble ASYN protein levels, despite the reduction of its mRNA, suggests a primary defect in clearance of the protein. However, this reduced clearance cannot be attributed to a failure of chaperone expression, because their mRNA is unchanged or increased in the DLB brain._**

Sounds like an autophagy problem....

[http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/16319716](http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/16319716)

Lewy Body buildup and Parkinson's...linked with Autophagy issues...

> Changes in protein metabolism are key to disease onset and progression in many neurodegenerative diseases. As a prime example, in Parkinson’s disease, folding, post-translational modification and recycling of the synaptic protein α-synuclein are clearly altered, leading to a progressive accumulation of pathogenic protein species and the formation of intracellular inclusion bodies. Altered protein folding is one of the first steps of an increasingly understood cascade in which α-synuclein forms complex oligomers and finally distinct protein aggregates, termed Lewy bodies and Lewy neurites. In neurons, an elaborated network of chaperone and co-chaperone proteins is instrumental in mediating protein folding and re-folding. In addition to their direct influence on client proteins, chaperones interact with **_protein degradation pathways such as the ubiquitin-proteasome-system or autophagy in order to ensure the effective removal of irreversibly misfolded and potentially pathogenic proteins._** Because of the vital role of proper protein folding for protein homeostasis, a growing number of studies have evaluated the contribution of chaperone proteins to neurodegeneration. We herein review our current understanding of the involvement of chaperones, co-chaperones and chaperone-mediated autophagy in synucleinopathies with a focus on the Hsp90 and Hsp70 chaperone system. We discuss genetic and pathological studies in Parkinson’s disease as well as experimental studies in models of synucleinopathies that explore molecular chaperones and protein degradation pathways as a novel therapeutic target. To this end, we examine the capacity of chaperones to prevent or modulate neurodegeneration and summarize the current progress in models of Parkinson’s disease and related neurodegenerative disorders.

[http://www.actaneurocomms.org/content/1/1/79](http://www.actaneurocomms.org/content/1/1/79)

ASYN misfolding link to Lewy Bodies and "Prion-Like Diseases"...

> TP, long-term potentiation; CSF, cerebrospinal fluid; VPS, vacuolar protein sorting
>
> [Topof page](http://www.nature.com/cddis/journal/v3/n7/full/cddis201294a.html#top)
> **Facts**
>
> -   Alpha-synuclein is associated with both familial and idiopathic cases of Parkinson’s disease.
> -   The precise function of alpha-synuclein remains equivocal.
> -   Alpha-synuclein misfolds and forms protein aggregates in Parkinson’s disease and in various _in vitro_ and _in vivo_ models of synucleinopathies.
> -   Alpha-synuclein has been detected in human and mouse CSF and in the media of cultured cells.
>
> [Topof page](http://www.nature.com/cddis/journal/v3/n7/full/cddis201294a.html#top)
> **Open Questions**
>
> -   Is alpha-synuclein normally secreted from cells or is it released from dying/damaged cells? How is it secreted?
> -   What are the effects of secreted forms of alpha-synuclein?
> -   What is the toxic genus of alpha-synuclein?
> -   Is the spreading of alpha-synuclein pathology a prion-like phenomenon?

[http://www.nature.com/cddis/journal/v3/n7/full/cddis201294a.html](http://www.nature.com/cddis/journal/v3/n7/full/cddis201294a.html)

### Josh — 2014-05-17T19:50:53+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/cfs-help.10807/post-127368>

> Recently, it was hypothesized that PD, as well as other neurodegenerative disorders associated with protein misfolding and aggregation, might be a prion-like disease where pathological forms of aSyn spread throughout the brain. In particular, the spreading of aSyn pathology could contribute to the progression of neurodegeneration and clinical symptoms.[22](http://www.nature.com/cddis/journal/v3/n7/full/cddis201294a.html#bib22), [23](http://www.nature.com/cddis/journal/v3/n7/full/cddis201294a.html#bib23), [24](http://www.nature.com/cddis/journal/v3/n7/full/cddis201294a.html#bib24) Nevertheless, the molecular determinants underlying aSyn secretion, extracellular effects and transmission of pathology are still unclear.

{ "lightbox\_close": "Close", "lightbox\_next": "Next", "lightbox\_previous": "Previous", "lightbox\_error": "The requested content cannot be loaded. Please try again later.", "lightbox\_start\_slideshow": "Start slideshow", "lightbox\_stop\_slideshow": "Stop slideshow", "lightbox\_full\_screen": "Full screen", "lightbox\_thumbnails": "Thumbnails", "lightbox\_download": "Download", "lightbox\_share": "Share", "lightbox\_zoom": "Zoom", "lightbox\_new\_window": "New window", "lightbox\_toggle\_sidebar": "Toggle sidebar" }

> Proposed mechanisms of aSyn secretion. aSyn can be secreted both in its monomeric or aggregated forms by non-classical exocytic or endocytic pathways. aSyn can be directly integrated into secretory vesicles and subsequently released by exocytosis (**1**). Alternatively, aSyn can be translocated to early endosomes. From early endosomes, aSyn protein can either be released to the extracellular space through the recycling endosome (**2**) or incorporated to intraluminal vesicles of multivesicular bodies (MBV). MVB cargo including aSyn can be directed to degradation by fusion with lysosomes or to secretion by fusion with the plasma membrane and release of exosomal vesicles (**3**). TGN=trans-Golgi network

> The role of extracellular aSyn in neuroinflammation, neurotoxicity, and spread of pathology. aSyn can be actively secreted or released by dying neurons to the extracellular space. Extracellular aSyn can then activate surrounding astrocytes and microglia, eliciting glial pro-inflammatory activity. Upon activation microglia produce pro-inflammatory cytokines, nitric oxide, and reactive oxygen species, which may be toxic to neurons. aSyn can be directly transferred between neurons, leading to propagation of aSyn aggregation process and compromising the viability of the recipient neuron

[http://www.nature.com/cddis/journal/v3/n7/full/cddis201294a.html](http://www.nature.com/cddis/journal/v3/n7/full/cddis201294a.html)

### Josh — 2014-05-17T20:00:02+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/cfs-help.10807/post-127369>

A key here...things we have heard before...

> Additionally, a fraction of aSyn was shown to be secreted in association with exosomes by a calcium-dependent mechanism ([Figure 2](http://www.nature.com/cddis/journal/v3/n7/full/cddis201294a.html#fig2))[61](http://www.nature.com/cddis/journal/v3/n7/full/cddis201294a.html#bib61) and exosomal aSyn release was found to be increased upon lysosomal dysfunction.[62](http://www.nature.com/cddis/journal/v3/n7/full/cddis201294a.html#bib62) However, a very recent study reports no enrichment of aSyn in exosomal pellet of neuronal cells overexpressing aSyn as well as an absence of aSyn-positive signal in exosomal fraction derived from CSF of PD patients and control subjects.[63](http://www.nature.com/cddis/journal/v3/n7/full/cddis201294a.html#bib63) This study further shows that in neuronal cells aSyn is present in endosomal compartments ([Figure 2](http://www.nature.com/cddis/journal/v3/n7/full/cddis201294a.html#fig2)), and that aSyn secretion and lysosomal targeting is regulated by vacuolar protein sorting 4. Moreover, it was suggested that the recycling pathway regulated by Rab11 has functional relevance in aSyn secretion ([Figure 2](http://www.nature.com/cddis/journal/v3/n7/full/cddis201294a.html#fig2)).[63](http://www.nature.com/cddis/journal/v3/n7/full/cddis201294a.html#bib63)

1.  "**_Calcium-Dependent mechanism"=excitotoxicity due to EMF_**
2.  **_"lysosomal dysfunction"=changes in PH of lysosyme=functional change_**
3.  **_"protein sorting" and "recycling pathway"=autophagy_**

**_[http://www.nature.com/cddis/journal/v3/n7/full/cddis201294a.html](http://www.nature.com/cddis/journal/v3/n7/full/cddis201294a.html)_**

### Josh — 2014-05-17T20:03:26+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/cfs-help.10807/post-127370>

> In recent years, extracellular aSyn has received a lot of scientific attention due to its potential role in disease initiation and progression. **_Considering the progressive nature of neurodegenerative disorders as well as the defined, step-wise spreading of Lewy body pathology in PD,[22](http://www.nature.com/cddis/journal/v3/n7/full/cddis201294a.html#bib22) the idea of extracellular aSyn as a pathogenic ‘prion-like’ agent is extremely appealing. This scenario is strengthened by observations describing the presence of aSyn-positive Lewy-body-like inclusions in long-term mesencephalic transplants in PD patients.[23](http://www.nature.com/cddis/journal/v3/n7/full/cddis201294a.html#bib23), [64](http://www.nature.com/cddis/journal/v3/n7/full/cddis201294a.html#bib64) These findings suggest host-to-graft propagation of aSyn-associated pathology._** Subsequent _in vitro_ experiments showed inter-neuronal transmission of aSyn ([Figure 3](http://www.nature.com/cddis/journal/v3/n7/full/cddis201294a.html#fig3)).[58](http://www.nature.com/cddis/journal/v3/n7/full/cddis201294a.html#bib58), [65](http://www.nature.com/cddis/journal/v3/n7/full/cddis201294a.html#bib65) Moreover, the host-to-graft transmission of aSyn has also been observed _in vivo_ in aSyn transgenic mouse models.[24](http://www.nature.com/cddis/journal/v3/n7/full/cddis201294a.html#bib24), [65](http://www.nature.com/cddis/journal/v3/n7/full/cddis201294a.html#bib65) This transmission process seems to rely on endocytic uptake of aSyn by the recipient neurons.[24](http://www.nature.com/cddis/journal/v3/n7/full/cddis201294a.html#bib24), [66](http://www.nature.com/cddis/journal/v3/n7/full/cddis201294a.html#bib66)
> All of these findings indicate that aSyn can be both secreted and transmitted between neuronal cells. Moreover, there is evidence suggesting that aSyn secretion is stimulated by cellular stress conditions including lysosomal dysfunction.
> Although many studies showed secretion and cell-to-cell transmission of aSyn, this phenomenon might not, _per se_, be sufficient to explain the spread of aSyn Lewy-body-like pathology and progressive neurodegeneration observed in PD. There is additional evidence, however, that aSyn can act as a nucleation/seeding agent for aggregation and can affect the viability of the recipient neurons ([Figure 3](http://www.nature.com/cddis/journal/v3/n7/full/cddis201294a.html#fig3)).

**_[http://www.nature.com/cddis/journal/v3/n7/full/cddis201294a.html](http://www.nature.com/cddis/journal/v3/n7/full/cddis201294a.html)_**

### Josh — 2014-05-17T20:04:12+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/cfs-help.10807/post-127371>

Prions are....

"
A **prion** (  [](http://upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/9/96/Pronunciation_prion.ogg)[i](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/File:Pronunciation_prion.ogg)[/](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Help:IPA_for_English)[ˈpriːɒn](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Help:IPA_for_English#Key)[/](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Help:IPA_for_English)[\[1\]](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Prion#cite_note-1)) in the [Scrapie](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Scrapie) form (PrPSc) is an [infectious agent](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Infectious_agent) composed of [protein](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Protein) in a [misfolded](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Protein_folding#Incorrect_protein_folding_and_neurodegenerative_disease) form.[\[2\]](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Prion#cite_note-Sherris-2) This is the central idea of the Prion Hypothesis, which remains debated.[\[3\]](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Prion#cite_note-3) This would be in contrast to all other known infectious agents, like [viruses](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Virus), [bacteria](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Bacteria), [fungi](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Fungi) or [parasites](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Parasite)—which must contain [nucleic acids](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Nucleic_acid) (either [DNA](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/DNA), [RNA](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/RNA), or both). The word_prion_, coined in 1982 by [Stanley B. Prusiner](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Stanley_B._Prusiner), is derived from the words _protein_ and _infection_.[\[4\]](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Prion#cite_note-Nobel-4) Prions are responsible for the [transmissible spongiform encephalopathies](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Transmissible_spongiform_encephalopathy) in a variety of [mammals](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Mammal), including [bovine spongiform encephalopathy](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Bovine_spongiform_encephalopathy)(BSE, also known as "mad cow disease") in [cattle](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Cattle). In humans, prions cause [Creutzfeldt-Jakob Disease](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Creutzfeldt-Jakob_Disease) (CJD), variant Creutzfeldt-Jakob Disease (vCJD), [Gerstmann–Sträussler–Scheinker syndrome](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Gerstmann%E2%80%93Str%C3%A4ussler%E2%80%93Scheinker_syndrome), [Fatal Familial Insomnia](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Fatal_Familial_Insomnia) and [kuru](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Kuru_\(disease\)).[\[5\]](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Prion#cite_note-5) All known prion diseases affect the structure of the [brain](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Brain) or other [neural](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Neuron) tissue and all are currently untreatable and universally fatal.[\[6\]](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Prion#cite_note-6) In 2013, a study revealed that 1 in 2,000 people in the United Kingdom might harbour the infectious prion protein that causes vCJD.[\[7\]](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Prion#cite_note-7)

**_Prions are not considered living organisms but may propagate by transmitting a [misfolded protein](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Protein_folding#Incorrect_protein_folding_and_neurodegenerative_disease) state. If a prion enters a healthy organism, it induces existing, properly folded proteins to convert into the disease-associated, prion form; the prion acts as a template to guide the misfolding of more proteins into prion form._** These newly formed prions can then go on to convert more proteins themselves; this triggers a chain reaction that produces large amounts of the prion form.[\[8\]](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Prion#cite_note-8) All known prions induce the formation of an [amyloid](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Amyloid) fold, in which the protein polymerises into an aggregate consisting of tightly packed [beta sheets](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Beta_sheet). Amyloid aggregates are fibrils, growing at their ends, and replicating when breakage causes two growing ends to become four growing ends. The [incubation period](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Incubation_period) of prion diseases is determined by the [exponential growth](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Exponential_growth) rate associated with prion replication, which is a balance between the linear growth and the breakage of aggregates.[\[9\]](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Prion#cite_note-Masel_99-9) (Note that the propagation of the prion depends on the presence of normally folded protein in which the prion can induce misfolding; animals that do not express the normal form of the prion protein can neither develop nor transmit the disease.)

This altered structure is extremely stable and accumulates in infected tissue, causing tissue damage and cell death.[\[10\]](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Prion#cite_note-10) This structural stability means that prions are resistant to [denaturation](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Denaturation_\(biochemistry\)) by chemical and physical agents, making disposal and containment of these particles difficult. Prions come in different strains, each with a slightly different structure, and, most of the time, strains breed true. Prion replication is nevertheless subject to occasional [epimutation](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Epimutation) and then [natural selection](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Natural_selection) just like other forms of replication.[\[11\]](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Prion#cite_note-11)

All known mammalian prion diseases are caused by the so-called prion protein, [PrP](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/PRNP). The endogenous, properly folded form is denoted PrPC (for _**C**ommon_ or _**C**ellular_), whereas the disease-linked, misfolded form is denoted PrPSc (for [_**Sc**rapie_](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Scrapie), after one of the diseases first linked to prions and neurodegeneration.)[\[12\]](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Prion#cite_note-Krull-12)[\[13\]](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Prion#cite_note-13) The precise structure of the prion is not known, though they can be formed by combining PrPC, polyadenylic acid, and lipids in a [Protein Misfolding Cyclic Amplification](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Protein_Misfolding_Cyclic_Amplification) (PMCA) reaction.[\[14\]](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Prion#cite_note-minimal_prion-14)

Proteins showing prion-type behavior are also found in some [fungi](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Fungus), which has been useful in helping to understand mammalian prions. [Fungal prions](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Fungal_prion) do not appear to cause disease in their hosts.[\[15\]](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Prion#cite_note-15)"

**_Misfolded Proteins....dependent upon COE's to express and propogate...sounds like a magnetic moment issue whether the organism is susceptible????_**

[http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Prion](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Prion)

OF THE PROPHET, HAZRAT ALI.

EIGHTEEN CAMELS YOU WILL GIVE HALF – NINE CAMELS – TO THE OLDEST DISCIPLE. THE
SECOND SHALL HAVE A THIRD OF THE TOTAL, WHICH IS SIX CAMELS. THE LAST DISCIPLE
MAY HAVE ONE-NINTH, WHICH CAMEL – IS LEFT OVER TO BE RETURNED TO ME.
THIS WAS HOW THE DISCIPLES FOUND THE TEACHER FOR THEM."_**

I'd wager there would be a good possibility that after a day or two of said trial I'd prefer to fast the rest of the time instead of continuing to use soylent... This stuff might act like rocket fuel to my diesel engine

> [nonchalant said:](/goto/post?id=126722)
>
> That's one of the problems. He consulted the folks who think ATP synthase is all you need. Also, it appears now that just having the processed substance, like iodine, is very different from the substance as it occurs in nature.

Hmm... I wonder if they actually took such considerations in mind, if this would still be a viable endeavor. i.e. instead of processed iodine, use a derivative of seaweed naturally containing the iodine... At the end of the day any manufacturing/processing, packaging, shipping, etc. is only going to screw up the natural energy balance of the foods more and more.

What about making the "optimal" version... I'm envisioning a weekly delivery from the local marina of 7 dz oysters, a stop at the butchers for some pastured lard and some fresh veg from the neighborhood garden. Oh wait...

# Thread: Good time to do HCG???
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/good-time-to-do-hcg.10811/>
**Subforum:** HCG Protocol
**Posts:** 200
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### nicld — 2014-05-12T17:39:11+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/good-time-to-do-hcg.10811/post-126456>

When is a good time to do HCG?

Quick background. I did 3 rounds starting in July 2012 and ending in March of 2013 and lost about 45 pounds. It stayed off for about 1 month then BOOM, rapidly put it back on. I did not over eat or eat tons of carbs. I used the hunger scale and was very away of my eating and proved that weight gain is not all about food. I did not find Jack until June when the gain was too much. I probably did not do some of the best things right off. Started IF right away with BPC, sleep was still crap and had SIBO. Started hacking the labs in July 2013 and have learned so much.

Fast forward to now. My sleep is vastly improved, have done CT like a mad woman (and loving it), my gut is getting so much better (regular in the first time that I can remember), drinking tons of RO water, reducing EMF (I know I could do better though), been eating more seafood and started eating more raw and have a Magnetico coming on Wednesday. My labs are better than a year ago but still need way more improvements (bun/Creatine still too high, thyroid still a bit low, sex hormones still low, cortisol and salivary melatonin were good last time I checked and need to checked this summer).

I am working with Dr. Tim on getting rid of some microplasma pneumoniae that seems to be the cause for my inflammation on my labs and why my sex steroid hormones and thyroid is still not where it should be. Also dealing with some fat malabsorption issues too. He said that weight loss cannot happen with this still going on.

The only thing that has not got better is the weight. I am trying to concentrate on the positives that have happened but it is hard when you are not the happiest with what is looking back at you in the mirror. I am in a much better state internally now I would like the external to start changing.

I have about 7 days to figure out if now would be a good time since I have something going on in the middle of July that I need to be in P4 or at least the last stages of P3 for. I know it works to get it off but I don’t want to do a round only to have it pile back on again.

Is it worth trying while working on the microplasma issue?

### cantweight — 2014-05-12T17:59:36+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/good-time-to-do-hcg.10811/post-126459>

I have spent the morning reading about desbio's Ha2cg....
Was going to order the real stuff last night and chickened out. I had the same experience as you...a lot came off and a lot came back on, fast. I went back and read what tiny bit of notes I took and there was the same sentiment of hitting a wall day 21. I am wondering if smaller more frequent rounds might be my answer. I am also assuming one of my problems lies in being a poor methylator/detoxifier and too much fat loss was mucking me up big time. So i am going to give ha2cg a go with the detoxifiers they recommend....I dont put a lot of stock in homeopathy, not really sure why...has a hocus pocus quality to me, BUT....my func med doc from my lyme days that got me to an incredibly healthy state in some of the worst living conditions swore by them and I took all kinds of drops and crap. I got rid of H.pylori with these drops w/o any other abx. I also took these drops for all kinds of detox and couldnt say if they worked or not because i was using so many things....but they certainly didnt hurt.

As for your mycoplasma....are you symptomatic? Maybe ask Tim what he thinks of this product.... [http://www.pureformulas.com/mycoplasma-10-vial-kits-by-deseret-biologicals.html#sthash.Q9rF9vOI.dpbs](http://www.pureformulas.com/mycoplasma-10-vial-kits-by-deseret-biologicals.html#sthash.Q9rF9vOI.dpbs)

**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/good-time-to-do-hcg.10811/post-126466>

The only symptoms I have are poor labs despite taking supplements for what is low and the fact that the FAT won't leave. I was hoping all of the deep deep CT would have helped but it has not budged.
He is having me get this stuff
[http://www.raintreeformulas.com/myco-capsules](http://www.raintreeformulas.com/myco-capsules) when it comes back in stock. I am also to take lauricidne for 3-6 months.

I feel I am in a much better place than 2 years ago, just don't know if it is good enough. I have a bottle of pellets that someone else I know used and they worked great for her. My previous rounds were injections.

### cantweight — 2014-05-12T18:25:47+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/good-time-to-do-hcg.10811/post-126469>

To me that says your redox is good enough that the mycoplasma is not causing you trouble. The only thing I would be careful about is detoxing throughout fat loss. I think this time of year is great since you can stabilize prior to fall. When sunshine and grass are plentiful to support.
