### sooperb — 2014-05-13T20:41:10+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/earthing-grounding-why-do-i-feel-worse.10813/post-126715>

Have you self-diagnosed your ailments or have you had tests which have shown them? If you're guessing then maybe you need a firm diagnosis as trying to treat what you think is wrong with you as opposed to what you know is wrong with you is not the best way forward. I can't think that Earthing would make you feel worse, it can be a bit overwhelming if you throw yourself in at the deep end, rather like overeating after fasting for weeks. When I started with the sheet I found it difficult, couldn't sleep and became very unsettled to the point where I removed it. Only when I restarted by using it gradually for an hour in the evening and gradually working up to putting it back on the bed was I able to adapt to it. Now it's on the bed all the time without a problem.

### sooperb — 2014-05-14T00:05:34+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/earthing-grounding-why-do-i-feel-worse.10813/post-126749>

Ha, enlightened GPs in the UK? Needle and haystack spring to mind. I think the best you can hope for is to try your best to work out what's wrong and then convince your GP to test for it, not an easy task when they are all so tied to the mothership that is the BMA! You only have to look at the advice diabetics are given and how statins are handed out like sweets if your cholesterol rises even a small amount to realise that most of them prefer not to be innovative or curious but to stick to the old and familiar. SeanB4 has had some success in persuading his GP to be a little more flexible, he has a journal that might be a useful read for you in the journal section.

There is the private testing option but it isn't cheap and of course you need an idea of what to test for. It's pretty awful never feeling well and not knowing the answers, I have some symptoms that despite some investigation have not resulted in any sort of diagnosis or help via standard medical process and I've been closed by the specialist so I'm no further forward. It's a slow process tracking down what causes what and then what you can do to try to make it right. I think you have to take care what you action you take promoted by websites you read, they all offer solutions but one size seldom fits all and you have to be careful that in trying to make yourself right, you don't make yourself worse .

### Jack Kruse — 2014-05-16T15:36:13+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/earthing-grounding-why-do-i-feel-worse.10813/post-127212>

LubylouUK
let me be clear: It is not about the offending agent or the organ affect: The loss of electrons cause a loss of organization in those cells and it is why you fell like shit. That is why they don't work. So lets consider an analogy to make this point clear: mold intoxication. Is it the mold? No it is not. Mold is ubiquitous on Earth everywhere. We are designed to deal with it effectively using our cell mediated immunity. Is it the poor functioning of cell mediated immunity? Yes, it is. If you read the last few blogs you will see the reason an organ system (immune system) goes south is because there is not enough electrons present within the cell membranes or the mitochondria of these cells or where T cells and B cells mature. That is the thymus and bone marrow. The goal is not to avoid things naturally found in your environment, but to fix the structure of the organ systems involved in cell mediated immunity to get properly functioning cells to deal with the mold as they are designed by replacing the electrons lost. The loss of electrons is what causes these cell not to perform because it directly alters the organization of how the tissues are constructed. Their masses increase and function goes down. I also totally disagree with you because you are rural EMF is not your issue. Often times living rurally can be worse because your devices have to constantly search out an antenna to connect and therefore your exposure time is worse. Stop guessing and test.........I bet you are shocked at what you learn.

**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/e-mc2.10814/post-126470>

E=mc2 = c2m=E

Now consider this analogy

The photons absorbed by green plants split water molecules and reduce carbon dioxide, resulting in the formation of carbohydrates and oxygen. In respiration, the converse takes place: carbohydrates are oxidized to make a stream of electrons to restore carbon dioxide and water

Photons and electrons.......used in reciprocal processes.

Then we have the photoelectric effect.

Life is organized around the thermodynamic givens present on planet Earth.

### NeilBB — 2014-05-12T20:37:16+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/e-mc2.10814/post-126488>

I confess that I have never fully understood what you meant by "reversing Einstein's equation." I have been waiting for that spiral theory of knowledge that I spoke of to kick in. This is what I would say right now:

Everyone thinks of that equation as the liberation of energy from mass, like in a nuclear bomb. Feynman pointed out the enormous energy released in a "nuclear" explosion is actually electromagnetic energy that is liberated when atomic nuclei are disrupted, not nuclear, that is actually a misnomer.

So, to me, reversing Einstein's equation implies that the flow of _electromagnetic_ energy would be _harnessed_ to organize matter. The reverse of a nuclear explosion. Like in a star?

The more organized something is, the less random motion, the less brownian motion, the less entropy. Organization of matter is the opposite of an explosion. Life is the most sophisticated example of the organization of matter.

Entropy would also seem related to thermal motion of matter particles, i. e. fermions. So nuclei can produce a lot of entropy and electrons produce some too, but a lot less because they are much less massive. But bosons (photons) are massless force particles that obey different rules. E = hv. Not E = mc2. They mediate electromagnetic radiation which truly is "free energy" in the sense that having no mass, it creates no motion of matter, no entropy, and it doesn't care about temperature either. It is like a perpetual motion machine, not subject to frictional forces in that way.

So if life figured out how to even partially harness this perpetual free energy source to power some fraction of its processes, I would think it would go a long way towards mitigating the second law of thermodynamics, depending on how efficiently it could do that. The energy extraction process would be a lot cleaner, like a solar cell versus an oil well or windmill. And the better it did it, the longer it could theoretically live and the more competitive advantage it could have over more thermodynamically limited organisms. And the more that system became dysfunctional due to environmental changes and mismatches, the more the primitive systems would have to be relied upon. De-evolution of an organism and an ecosystem because of a disruption in evolution's plan to manage energy flows and keep entropy contained.

Cycles lead to coupling and lead to energy flow. Energy is life. Life is energy.

Coupled cycles are the ultimate wisdom of nature. They go on at all levels, from the ecological to the quantum molecular through a wide range of characteristic timescales from millennia to split seconds. Thus, the transformation of light energy into chemical energy by green plants yields food for other organisms whose growth and subsequent decay provide nutrients in the soil on which green plants depend. The energy in foodstuffs is transformed into the mechanical, osmotic, electrical and biosynthetic work both within the plants themselves and in other organisms in all the trophic levels dependent on green plants. ### cantweight — 2014-05-13T17:47:51+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/tick-bite-prevention.10815/post-126671>

My 2 cents....Lyme did not almost destroy me until my body had reached such a low point that it was susceptible to destruction. I walked around with those spirochetes in my system for 17 years before they "got" me.

I believe focus on good redox and general good health will keep things from getting out of hand and also make it easy to treat if illness is contracted.

The ticks that we see and pull off are generally not the ones to worry about....its those teeny tiny poppy seed size jerks that do the most damage.

I also think living in an area where the eco system is more intact helps a lot....where there are plenty of mice and deer for the ticks to feed on.

When I lived in our old neighborhood the kids would go out and play in the cul-de-sac and come in with multiple ticks....apparently the homes built drove away the wildlife and the ticks flocked to the little patches of grass along the road because they were drawn to the co2 emitted from cars.

I think it is rosemary and lemon oil that are deterrent too.

I'm no expert, but the text I linked to earlier, says

> In other words Lyme bacteria all die at a temperature of 106 Fahrenheit for 24 hours. It's a higher temp than what kills Syphilis. Unfortunately that could kill you too. But they do have a hard time at 102F, and at 103 or 104. One of the problems with heat therapy is some tissues in your body might be a bit cooler and the bacteria there might survive. It can be dangerous therapy if it's not done in moderation. Temperature treatment for spirochetal illnesses has it's roots in the old Malaria therapy for Syphilis. The old Malaria therapy had a death rate of 1/20, and a successful complete remission rate of 50%. Some people just soak in very hot baths in addition to other therapies and swear by it.

So it seems you'd have to have a fairly serious fever before these bacteria are really troubled.

>
> How do you know you had it in your system that long? Did you get tested? I got bitten maybe 12 years ago and occasionally that exact area flares up just like it did at the time. I've never been tested though.

It is an assumption..... My mom, dad, half brother and half sister (I only say half because genetically they are very different than me) had multiple bouts of lyme disease in the few years we lived in CT. They got very sick right off the bat bullseyes and flu symptoms and joint pain/swelling...very textbook. Its damn near impossible for them all to have been infected multiple times and me never. Coupled with the fact that when I would get sick....I would get really sick with bizarre symptoms. But then get well again.

How often is that echoed in the Lyme community....I was fine then I had a car accident, a baby, divorce....and have been sick ever since. No history of tick bite. Most Lyme sufferers will say its amazing how good they feel in the summer....always thinking it was the heat killing off spirochetes. Even MD junction has the rare person that will say hey....I added some great herbs, ate like a champ, lowered my stress and I am better.

I bet about 90% of the population are walking around with Lyme antibodies.

So many questions about treatment are answered by redox potential....I went round and round with this crap....can not even begin to tell you.

I also base my assumption on the years of carrying due to all 3 of my kids having the same strains as I did Lyme, Bab, Bart.

That said I do wonder...and I can feel Jack slapping my wrist....when the body is too beaten down with illness do abx have a purpose? I feel like my N=1 says yes....kill some of the crap with meds to give me a fighting chance. My doc practiced like this...she always wanted me to be at a point I could fight with out meds...and we got there. If I had to go back and do it again Id do it differently, but not entirely.

I have stared down this beast for my self and my entire family....i dont think it is as scary as it is made out to be. People that know me and what my family has been through are shocked when they ask me what i do to deal with ticks and I say "nothing."

### prAna303 — 2014-05-14T00:37:16+0300

> Maybe people are generally lazy?

Not by their own fault...

### sooperb — 2014-05-14T00:39:04+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/tattoos.10819/post-126766>

Where would be without business? Everything we have is made by someone, without manufacturing we would have nothing, without ideas coming to fruition we wouldn't have all the things you take for granted, houses, medicine, roads, books, food and anything you care to name. Of course not all inventions have been good or safe or necessary but you can't condemn all because of some.

I can remember many train journeys in my past made without anything digital, I don't recall them being great social events, people read newspapers, books, played with their children, slept, looked out at the view. We still travel and we still do most of those things, some in a different way, e-readers replace books. I don't believe humans are any less social, just that we socialise in a different way. The way we live is constantly changing, some of the changes can be considered undesirable but one thing that isn't going to happen, we will never be able to turn back the clock. Would we want to? Who wants to go back to the times when it wasn't safe to drink the water, where travel was by horse on roads that were no more than tracks full of potholes and danger. Of course some of the good things have been lost along the way but as a race we have made many gains.

We need to stop looking over our shoulders and wishing things were the same, we need to learn how to adapt to how things are now. Touch hasn't gone away, but you can be lonely and miss human contact, people still talk to each other but not always in person, is that any the less valuable, would you prefer not to hear from someone because they want to use a phone? As for forums, they are exactly what you say they aren't Peter, they are a form of social media, where people who are like minded (or not) communicate ideas, thoughts, emotions and more.

Think about what you are saying, no digital means no computers no access to knowledge that would otherwise be denied us, no access to Jack and his ideas, and much more, is that what you really want? For all your avowed dislike of these forms of contact, you are here quite a lot, you wear clothes that someone made, you eat food that someone grew or raised and you live in a home that was made from component parts made by other people, we are all connected by this. People live in villages and towns and yes, even cities because that's how society has developed. Sure the countryside is attractive for the peace and quiet and beauty that it offers, it hasn't vanished and visiting it isn't banned. Life is about balance and the balance changes as the years pass. It is what life on a planet is and everyone who lives on it accepts that, probably without even thinking about it. It's not perfect and yes, we should strive to protect it but from a standpoint of knowledge not supposition, proof not paranoia.

### prAna303 — 2014-05-14T00:54:19+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/tattoos.10819/post-126772>

> Think about what you are saying, no digital means no computers no access to knowledge that would otherwise be denied us, no Jack and his ideas, and much more, is that what you really want?

Ok, have i said no computers? I focus on the social media side, google, facebook and other things that is going the wrong way. I have been thru the whole world of information technology, way bigger than most of you and with that i know what the main goal is AND that is not to educate us IT IS TO ENTERTAIN US.

> For all your avowed dislike of these forms of contact, you are here quite a lot

> Life is about balance and the balance changes as the years pass. It is what life on a planet is and everyone who lives on it accepts that, probably without even thinking about it.

Here i would like others to expand also. Is it about balance only? When we have wonderful tools of science should we just accept a world that looks like this or should we want to try to understand more?

Perhaps, looking at what has been done and accepting that many things gone wrong is hard but we must take some steps back and look at science from another angle...

And i said it many times, we can not go back and i do not want to, i live an new old fashioned life with quantum physics for kicks, wine for thrills and girls for passions.

### JoeBranca — 2014-05-14T00:55:11+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/tattoos.10819/post-126773>

> I don't believe humans are any less social, just that we socialise in a different way. The way we live is constantly changing, some of the changes can be considered undesirable but one thing that isn't going to happen, we will never be able to turn back the clock.

1) No one should be "anti technology". That is an incoherent notion. The best definition of technology is just art + ideas, period. We're a tech'ing species.

2) The sentiment is not to turn back the clock from everything digital. The sentiment is to reconsider the historically unprecedented acceleration by which we have collectively reshaped our lives through mobile and social media, which was only made possible through digital technology true. That doesn't necessarily implicate digital technology itself. But the difference now is, the latest advancements have to led to **most people out and about clearly devolving their innate sense of environmental awareness, situational awareness, and even empathy.** I'm speaking of an overarching trend, of course. But you get the point. It can hardly be disputed.

I was struck by this short passage from the bio of Christopher Boyce (the Falcon and the Snowman guy, released after decades in federal prison for espionage) about his first few moments out in the world again.

> "The world had changed all right, and not at all for the better. There were bombs to worry about, guns, people with horrifying intentions. He supposed it was not much different from the world he had left behind. Only now, everything felt like it was on fast-forward. He noticed people everywhere with portable telephones attached to their ears like appendages. He wondered what everyone was talking about that was so important it couldn't wait for a face-to-face conversation."

"Fast-forward"... communication that cannot wait... that really says it all to me. Communication, by its increased speed and instantaneous efficiency, has devalued. It's information exchanging without the 'soft information' of face to face conversation. There still is soft information in digital,mobile,social media. but it is an abstracted layer built into the technology, rather than what we used to expect in daily communication - what I can only describe as something like an enrichening experience of the interior space of another individual. Senses engaged. Energies transferred.

This is how i feel expanding minds are made.

### yewwei.tan — 2014-05-14T01:57:23+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/tattoos.10819/post-126783>

Hehe, I remember when I heard the "gateway to heaven, .... gateway to hell" quote, I thought, "damn, that would make a really good hand tattoo". Still, I don't have any ink on me, and don't have any desire to get any.

\---
I would be a hypocrite if I said that I didn't like digital technology. It's been responsible for all my growth as a human being. I love seeing the entire canvas of hate, anger, love, and joy play itself out in the digital sphere.

I wish all that all everyone had to do was entertain themselves (ie: the hunter gatherer days of working 2 hours, and playing for 6). But people aren't using digital tech to entertain themselves today, but rather, to distract themselves from the shitty system they reside in. I view that as a larger systemic problem, and rather than trying to change social media, or boycott specific companies and their actions, I probably want to understand the mechanisms driving said human behaviour, and perhaps figure out an alternative system in the future.

Jack and gang have to deal with a broken medical system whether they like it or not, and on my side, I can at least help create safehavens within the environment where all the concerns that prAna brought up are addressed (and they can be -- the problem is UX and marketing, not technology).

> [caroline said:](/goto/post?id=126780)
>
> I look forward to spending time with you one day ...for coffee and wine and talk ...and more wine ...and maybe some fine cognac .... and maybe even some grange! ....
>
> I keep looking for a coffee shop like yours .....

### sooperb — 2014-05-14T11:18:54+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/tattoos.10819/post-126843>

So many good quotes for music, here's a few I found from BrainyQuotes (someone else here is a fan of the site too ):

[I think music in itself is healing. It's an explosive expression of humanity. It's something we are all touched by. No matter what culture we're from, everyone loves music.](http://www.brainyquote.com/quotes/quotes/b/billyjoel133275.html)
[Billy Joel](http://www.brainyquote.com/quotes/authors/b/billy_joel.html)

[Music is a moral law. It gives soul to the universe, wings to the mind, flight to the imagination, and charm and gaiety to life and to everything.](http://www.brainyquote.com/quotes/quotes/p/plato109438.html)
[Plato](http://www.brainyquote.com/quotes/authors/p/plato.html)

[Music has charms to sooth a savage breast, to soften rocks, or bend a knotted oak.](http://www.brainyquote.com/quotes/quotes/w/williamcon161325.html)
[William Congreve](http://www.brainyquote.com/quotes/authors/w/william_congreve.html)

Would we be without it? Surely not. Yet without the creation of vinyl we would be the poorer, without radios to hear it, we would be the poorer, digital technology has spread the wings of music much further, to all corners of the globe. Technology has its place, you simply cannot decry it on the basis that it isn't good for us. The telephone, on its own, has saved many lives, would you want not to have a mobile when your life is in danger and you're on your own miles from anyway. Be careful what you wish for as they say.

### nonchalant — 2014-05-14T12:49:59+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/tattoos.10819/post-126852>

> [prAna303 said:](/goto/post?id=126845)
>
> Quite a good reason but where i live and spend most of my time, mobiles do not work because of all the firs... Up high in the mountains you are on your own, maybe in the hands of a friend but i can never put my trust into gears, simply because they are to fragile.
>
> Yes i live not like the rest of the society so therefor we will perhaps never agree on these things.
>
> For sure i have tried most of it, way deeper than most but eight years ago i choose to split path and not go on the digital trail anymore. For sure i get into conflict, but conflict is vital.

Things may have changed since the advent of mobile phones, but when I lived in the panhandle of Texas, where in some places all you could see for miles was flat ground, cotton or wheat crops, the highway, a fence, and telephone poles...people in cars would always wave to each other, while whizzing past at 60 or 70 miles/hour. Or wave to people outside in the fields. Coming from a big city, it took me a while to get used to it. But I came to realize that out there, if you have car trouble or something, you really need a stranger to come to your aid. And someone always would stop and help. Because if they didn't, you were truly stuck. I certainly benefited from helpful strangers several times. And I have stopped for others.

Back in the big city, now with small children, I was disturbed at all the "Stranger Danger!" programs my kids were being taught in school. I finally told them that to some of their friends, I was a complete and total stranger. That seemed to blow their minds for a bit, but after they considered it for a while, it served to dispel some of the fear I saw growing in them from the constant propaganda.

### sooperb — 2014-05-14T14:12:59+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/tattoos.10819/post-126865>

The kindness of strangers has always been a variable, the Good Samaritan demonstrates this well. To have to be dependent on the possible arrival of a friendly, helpful stranger isn't the safest way to live. To give up the mobile and expect kind strangers to provide assistance isn't going to work because where communities were full of families and neighbours who were connected, these days families are often miles apart and people no longer move to live in the same street as friends or relatives. If you're fortunate you may well have neighbours who although strangers are kind or you may have neighbours that you know well who wouldn't spit on you if you were on fire. The world is full of kind, helpful people, it's a characteristic shared by many but you can't rely on any of them to turn up when you need help. It's great when they do but quite honestly if I fell down a big hole I'd prefer to have a mobile handy rather than expect a total stranger to happen along sometime and a stranger who can actually assist. Of course a stranger with a mobile would be a great help

Peter there are many millions of people in this world who use cellphones, microwaves, TVs computers et al, if they were as harmful as we are led to believe surely there would be a world wide epidemic of people keeling over with EMF plague or whatever you believe it causes. There isn't though, least, it hasn't made the news thus far. There's more danger from naturally occurring Radon gas in some areas, even that doesn't cause that many problems. I can better understand that people living under or very near to power lines might have issues but using a mobile for a few minutes isn't going to fry a brain afaik but feel free to link to any unbiased research that says I'm wrong.

### sooperb — 2014-05-14T17:54:43+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/tattoos.10819/post-126901>

I looked up the WHO site and this page: [http://www.who.int/peh-emf/project/EMF\_Project/en/](http://www.who.int/peh-emf/project/EMF_Project/en/)

It seems they have been "looking" at EMF and ELF since 1996 and the best they can offer thus far is "any health impacts need to be properly assessed." They accept there are concerns, they don't appear to have come to any firm conclusions in 18 years.

I have read the report linked by Peter, all the research listed was done with rodents, rodents are not people. You cannot extrapolate the results and assume that they apply to people. What stood out most for me in the summing up was this:

"most of the studies in the list are on short-term (minutes to hours)
exposure to low-intensity RFR. Long-term exposure studies
are sparse. In addition, we do not know if all of these reported
effects occur in humans exposed to low-intensity
RFR, or whether the reported effects are health hazards.
Biological effects do not automatically mean adverse health
effects, plus many biological effects are reversible. However
it is clear that low-intensity RFR is not biologically
inert.

I'm not suggesting that there isn't any danger, just that so far there doesn't seem to be any definite proof.

### JoeBranca — 2014-05-15T02:19:35+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/tattoos.10819/post-126997>

> [Jack Kruse said:](/goto/post?id=126781)
>
> not so sure I am in agreement with Joe on this.

So few of us can turn them off, so few of us can focus their usage to reasonable utility. Why?

The advent of social media, real time communications, info exchange and all that -- is absolutely wonderful in terms of how it can advance all sorts of better, efficient, agile collaborative enterprises. I'm all for that if and when society benefits in tangible ways.

My concern from observation is the sheer amount of usage of all the mobile tech that doesn't serve a better purpose like that. I can see how it's become a created "need" for people out and about in public, probably there to offset lots of other negative consequences of dense-but-disconnected societies we are in. It sure seems like a lot is being forfeited in the process. I work amongst a student zombie population. I commute amongst commuters who are increasingly oblivious to their surroundings and the inherent risk of navigating large chunks of metal around each other.

I sense the dynamic out in public - almost anywhere - has noticeable shifted to more resemble that cold disconnected feeling in crowded big cities, where more and more folks keep their head down, mind their own business and are in their own world. Because well things can't wait ; )

### Jack Kruse — 2018-05-06T00:45:23+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/tattoos.10819/post-240596>

Today's tattoo lesson: More on how your surfaces can be affected by a lack of sunlight and methionine in your skin. People with bad urea cycles should never have red tattoos unless they know how to source the ink themselves. Why? Below is a tattoo that this person had for six years. In December of 2014, they noted some changes within the red ink in it and decided to have it looked at. A biopsy came back as a type of skin cancer. The red pigment in the tattoo called cinnabar attacked my immune system so I have to have it removed either in a series of three surgeries or one-time removal with a skin graft. This is just another perspective of how getting a tattoo can be used as a hack. The teaching point in this person was they were blue light toxic and had a broken Kreb's bicycle. This slowed the urea cycle allowed methionine to build up and destroy autophagy for years until apoptosis was destroyed because this area NEVER got any sunlight. What was the result? Cancer in the red. Cinnabar is a compound that has mercury in it. It is safe unless your urea cycle is broken by hydrogen isoforms as you age. Did you know that food with methionine can clear mercury from our body? You can also use your tattoo's as a canary in the coal mine for the efficiency of autophagy and apoptosis if you choose to keep it. **Subforum:** The New Monster Thread

### seanb4 — 2014-05-13T14:13:32+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/protein-recycling.10820/post-126619>

Hey,

Over the last couple of weeks I have dropped down to one meal, in the afternoon, per day as it allows me consume adequate amounts of water in the morning on an empty stomach. I have noticed since doing this that I am freezing cold all day up until I eat, then I get less cold for a few hours.

Am I right in thinking that, when I have just eaten, my body has enough protein, from the food, to fold with water and thus create energy/heat. However this only lasts a short amount of time because my body is poor at recycling the protein and therefore runs out of energy causing cold/hypothyroid throughout the day?

This is also why 3 meals per day is recommended on the leptin reset to give you a steady flow of protein for super conduction throughout the day. Would this also require protein to be eaten at all 3 meals? Would fat not have the same effect?

# Thread: Best Measurement for 24 Hour Electrolyte Excretion?

**Subforum:** Optimal Labs
**Posts:** 1
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### persistence — 2014-05-14T07:57:13+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/best-measurement-for-24-hour-electrolyte-excretion.10829/post-126832>

If you wanted to see how much electrolyte your body is excreting over 24 hours, what tests would you need to order? Quest Labs has a 24 hour electrolyte urine test that measures sodium, potassium, chloride, and creatinine. To this would you add a 24 hour urine capture for magnesium and calcium? What else?

What place would hair analysis have in assessing mineral balances in the body?

For potassium, how important is it to measure intracellular potassium instead of relying on the extracellular serum potassium that is used on most blood tests? I guess intracellular potassium would be the Potassium RBC test instead of Serum Potassium?

I guess that Magnesium is also normally an extracellular serum test, whereas Magnesium is primarily used intracellular? So the intracellular Magnesium test would be the Magnesium RBC?

# Thread: A look back
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/a-look-back.10831/>
**Subforum:** The Cave
**Posts:** 4
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### nicld — 2014-05-14T16:48:30+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/a-look-back.10831/post-126891>

So, I have been talking a lot about weight issues so I thought it would be good to reflect on the positive changes that have happened with the health the past year.

1\. Sleep has improved dramatically. It no longer takes me hours to fall asleep and I no longer wake at 2 am and cannot fall back asleep.
2\. Don’t have to pee 3x during the middle of the night.
3\. Movements have become so regular. Every morning between 7-8 am and no more C. A little loose but Dr. T is not concerned.
4\. Skin looks amazeballs. I do have to say that I think I do not look almost 41.
5\. My nails and hair are also fabulous. The nails are getting so long (as long as I don’t break them on something), even the toe nails.
6\. I think my eyesight is improving. I HATE wearing my glasses now.
7\. I am no longer a freeze baby.
8\. The brain fog is getting better but still not where is was 10 years ago, but getting there.
9\. Actually waking up and being hungry. I was never one to be hungry in the morning.
10\. Cycles are getting better. Still shorter but I am no longer doubled over in pain for the first day.
11\. My food choices have expanded. Who would have thought that I would be eating oysters and raw beef.
12\. My negative thoughts about my body have got better, still there a bit, but not all consuming anymore.
13\. The sex life has improves 500%. Not sure if the hubby is all for this as he says that he is no longer 20. I keep telling him that we can fix that but he just won’t listen.

I still have things to work on, the gut first, then weight. Sleep can still be better and I can always improve my EMF situations. But overall, I am not too bad.

A huge thanks to Dr. Kruse for shining the light.

### kovita — 2014-05-14T19:28:31+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/a-look-back.10831/post-126913>

Amazing, Nicld! This is a lot! Many of your points I can copy and paste to my journal, including the eye sight and night trips to the toilet! Keep up the good work! Eventually evrything in your body settles in to the right place. It is just matter of time and trust in yourself! I truly believe it!

### Inger — 2014-05-14T19:33:15+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/a-look-back.10831/post-126915>

Great stuff - i am with Kovita! Many of your improvements are mine too

### caroline — 2014-05-15T02:33:20+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/a-look-back.10831/post-127002>

I am so very proud of you Nicole .....you always give 100%......and then some..... x

# Thread: Whole Body Cryotherapy and Frozen Shoulder
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/whole-body-cryotherapy-and-frozen-shoulder.10832/>
**Subforum:** The New Monster Thread
**Posts:** 6
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### cmegan — 2014-05-14T17:08:51+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/whole-body-cryotherapy-and-frozen-shoulder.10832/post-126893>

Any updated thoughts or experiences with whole body cryotherapy? This frozen shoulder pain is driving me to new heights of impulsive behavior, and spending $100 for a cryotherapy session is just one example! There is actually some recent evidence of it being significantly beneficial for frozen shoulder...

Something just doesn't sit well with me about it though...these temps are just so low, it seems so unnatural (as opposed to cold thermogenesis which was historically just our natural adaptations to the winter). Any thoughts?

### colt — 2014-05-14T17:37:33+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/whole-body-cryotherapy-and-frozen-shoulder.10832/post-126895>

Can you post a video of your frozen shoulder? Where is the limitation or compensation?

### cmegan — 2014-05-14T19:19:15+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/whole-body-cryotherapy-and-frozen-shoulder.10832/post-126911>

> [colt said:](/goto/post?id=126895)
>
> Can you post a video of your frozen shoulder? Where is the limitation or compensation?

Oh no need for a video, it's just your run of the mill frozen shoulder! Sorry for the confusion, I'm really just wanting to know about the forum's thoughts or experiences regarding whole body cyrotherapy! Thanks!

### Jack Kruse — 2014-05-16T18:15:04+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/whole-body-cryotherapy-and-frozen-shoulder.10832/post-127230>

anything that increase electrons is a win......cold does this.

### Danco3636 — 2014-05-16T20:13:22+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/whole-body-cryotherapy-and-frozen-shoulder.10832/post-127242>

I did cryotherapy for a while.... but my experience is that the sessions are to short and an ice bath or ice packs are more effective and less expensive.... Plus no traveling time as I can do it at home and when I can best fit it in.....

When I was doing it they where a bit thrown back when I was taking a pre-cold shower and having them to max lowest tempreture and asking for more time..... I did all I could to make it colder like jumping right in the chamber without time spent in the cooler one... and moving around alot to circulate the cold more.... I still wanted more....

Then they wanted me to ride a bike to warm back up..... I just went to my car and put on an ice pack onto my back and shoulders and crank on the A/C.... Had it in a cooler waiting for me.... So you can see the hassle...

But like Dr. Jack has said..... any CT is better than none...

### cmegan — 2014-05-24T13:56:35+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/whole-body-cryotherapy-and-frozen-shoulder.10832/post-128212>

Thanks for your reply it is very helpful...my frozen shoulder has led me to a visit with a rheumatologist (also for recent aches and pains) and I was diagnosed with psoriatic arthritis. It has been really difficult doing CT when all I want to do is dive into a warm mineral spring! I woke up this morning thinking I should go to the cryo center today and try it, and then I saw your reply. With the drive and the cost I'll continue at the ocean and home. Below are some positive outcomes on WBC on rheum and psoriatic arthritis...too bad no one has measured the effects of bath CT on this...I guess measuring the effects of new technology is much more fun!

[http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/18548207](http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/18548207)

# Thread: Help me out with an experiment,please
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/help-me-out-with-an-experiment-please.10833/>
**Subforum:** The Cave
**Posts:** 20
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Lahelada — 2014-05-14T17:41:01+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/help-me-out-with-an-experiment-please.10833/post-126898>

This could be an interesting experiment that will only take 5 minutes of your time . I would like to ask you to do the experiment exactly as I tell you though
You will need :
another person of more strength than you
something that weighs around 8-10 ozs that from its dimensions you can comfortably hold in your hand
your cellphone
a quiet place your bedroom ,your bathroom where you arae away from distractions like television ,phone ,kids

!st experiment : stretch out your arm in front of you. Ask the person with you to push down your arm resisting them all the way
2 nd experiment : Repeat the experiment with the object of max 10 ozs you have selected
3\. Repeat the exercise holding your switched on cellphone
4\. Repeat the exercise with your cellphone switched off
5\. Repeat the exercise with the roles reversed.

**IMPORTANT :** **DO NOT** report your findings here or discuss them amongst yourselves in pms until 24 hours are up . This is to avoid opinion makers which exist on any forum to sway the outcome of this forum .
This experiment presumes that your arms are strong enough to hold a 10 oz object for 1-2 mins with an outstretched arm. If you feel you cannot you can still do this experiment with two others with you as the independent observer .
If you cannot contain yourself you can pm me as I have done this experiment already.
I would especially like Dr K to refrain from commenting in general ( sorry I know this is written on your dime, but your word can sway a lot of people) if you would be so kind. Afterwards I would love to hear your opinion of the validity of the experiment.
Ready. The time here is 11.45 am (GMT- 3 ) Clock is ticking.

### Lahelada — 2014-05-15T18:51:17+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/help-me-out-with-an-experiment-please.10833/post-127087>

So did anyone try this ? Think it is of no use and why? I did it and the result was that only the switched on phone made it easy to push down my arm. Does it prove what we are saying? Hocuspocus?

### fitness@home — 2014-05-16T00:29:34+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/help-me-out-with-an-experiment-please.10833/post-127133>

1: Stretch out your arm in front of you. Ask the person with you to push down your arm resisting them all the way
Me – Strong
Gwen – Strong

2: Repeat the experiment with the object of max 10 oz. you have selected
Me – Strong
Gwen - Strong

3: Repeat the exercise holding your switched on cellphone
Me – Arm shaking visibly trying to hold it up while she pushed down. Decreased strength.
Gwen – Very noticeable strength decrease

4: Repeat the exercise with your cellphone switched off
Me – Was able to push arm down some
Gwen – Was able to push arm down some

### Shijin13 — 2014-05-16T00:32:48+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/help-me-out-with-an-experiment-please.10833/post-127134>

### Josh — 2014-05-16T04:13:44+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/help-me-out-with-an-experiment-please.10833/post-127176>

Muscle testing and EAV machines, Radionics, etc, are blind men touching elephants and extrapolating....all powerful tools and there are several catches...

1.  Heisenberg and Shroedinger's Cat
2.  The redox and other magnetic and spin issues of the electrons in the testor
3.  The environment that one is testing in
4.  One needs to know what an elephant looks like and how it behaves in order to extrapolate the results with some reliability....

One can gather so much information with so many possible interpretations, that one needs to understand what one is asking and preferably be able to back it up with another tool such as a lab test. All this being said, I have found it invaluable. I had to learn 3 times from 3 different "schools" over 12 years before I got a sense that what I was doing is reliable and to what extent. Perhaps take a look at the Omura site:

[http://www.omurabdort.com/Omura/Omura.html](http://www.omurabdort.com/Omura/Omura.html)

In Orlando, I did a test I have done many times with what seem to me to be reliable results and the doctor rejected it and insisted that someone untrained and "unbiased" repeat it. Of course since no one was trained and everyone without experience except for a rare few have "bias" it was not repeatable to his satisfaction. I am used to this and like everything else, take what you like and leave the rest. The world is not quite ready for resonant quantum based diagnosis beyond MRI and PET scans at this time, so be it.....

### Jack Kruse — 2014-05-16T15:57:31+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/help-me-out-with-an-experiment-please.10833/post-127220>

It is not about the offending agent or the organ affect: The loss of electrons cause a loss of organization in those cells. That is why they dont work. So lets consider mold. Is it the mold? no. Mold is ubiquitous. Is it the poor functioning of cell mediated immunity? Yes. If you read the last few blogs you will see the reason an organ system ( immune system) goes south is because there is not enough electrons present within the cell membranes or the mitochondria of these cells or where T cells and B cells mature. That is the thymus and bone marrow. The goal is not to avoid things but to fix the structure of the organ systems involved in cell mediated immunity to get properly functioning cells to deal with the mold as they are designed by replacing the electrons lost. The loss of electrons is what causes these cell not to perform.

### Josh — 2014-05-16T17:34:55+0300

This is where people do not understand the tool well. Essentially all resonant testing shows whether something distorts the flow of and or lack of electrons in your body and interprets that as a problem. Depending upon how the inquiry is framed, conclusions are drawn. The man who trained me did not like automated computerized systems for a variety of reasons including the digital versus analog signature of the tested material, etc.. Taking your example, I would guess that the machine found that these digital signatures against your and your son's energy flow that it was sampling and running digital signatures of resonant information with to see how it changed from the baseline it started with. The baseline signals would have changed and become weaker and or less coherent. The machine/computer had been programmed to interpret that as "bad" and the practitioner believed the bias/interpretation of the person/people who built and programmed the machine. At one point, I would have accepted this as conclusive as well. However, a few years down the road, I know that without enough cofactors ranging from HCL to Manganese to Molybdenum and more with the issues related to SNP's inherited or similar issues related to poor redox, these things may test poorly and digest poorly/cause inflammatory response unless the cofactors necessary for utilization are added. Some machines and practitioners do this as well.

While seafood has most of the cofactors for utilization of its macronutrients; think protein and DHA among others, in some quantity, there will be variability from organism to organism consumed and the needs of the human will vary depending upon their COE's and N=1 physiology. It makes the resonant information useful and far from conclusive. It is an opportunity to contrast it with theory, labs, history, s/sx's and inquire further based on application of the state of the art conceptual framework (s) that a practitioner is hopefully using.....Remember Dr. Kruse's example of how he uses MRI images versus how most other doctors do. He further qualified in Orlando that the biophysicists are using them as he does, but the doctors by and large are not....

In terms of resonant testing, consider that the human body is the most sophisticated piece of technology on this planet and also the hardest to use reliably.....viewpoint....perspective....N=1

> Sometimes that research can lead to surprising breakthroughs for humans. One toxin Johnson and his team have identified among their current patients is domoic acid. Mussels filter the contaminated water through their systems, and when people eat the shellfish, the toxin can cause brain damage and memory loss.

# Thread: Saturn 2014
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/saturn-2014.10837/>
**Subforum:** The Cave
**Posts:** 2
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Jack Kruse — 2014-05-15T05:01:09+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/saturn-2014.10837/post-127045>

Over the weekend, Saturn reached [opposition](http://www.slate.com/blogs/bad_astronomy/2013/10/08/uranus_at_opposition_the_seventh_planet_at_its_best.html) in the sky. This means it’s opposite the Sun—literally, the Earth is physically between Saturn and the Sun—so it rises when the Sun sets and sets when the Sun rises. That means it’s up all night, making it easier to see … with the added bonus that it’s as close to the Earth as it’ll get all year, so it looks bigger and brighter, too.

This is the best time to see Saturn, so I highly recommend getting out and taking a look, especially if you have binoculars or a small telescope. [EarthSky has great directions on how to find it](http://earthsky.org/astronomy-essentials/give-me-five-minutes-ill-give-you-saturn). It doesn’t have to be right away; it’ll provide excellent viewing for weeks.

### caroline — 2014-05-15T08:36:34+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/saturn-2014.10837/post-127057>

I love this guy's voice ...holy heck .....

### nicld — 2014-05-15T16:41:33+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/my-10-gauss-magnetico.10841/post-127070>

I got my 10 gauss Magnetico yesterday and last night was the first night on it and it did not go too bad.

Our bed is a newer king size hybrid meaning it has some springs on the bottom and the top half is memory foam. I think the mattress is about 10-12” thick but I will double check when I get home.

I am pleased to say that I did not have any issues with it. Did not feel anything weird on it the way I did when I started putting the magnets under it from Harbor Freight. Did wake up early but that is because my back and neck have been bothering me for a while, which means a trip to my Chiropractor tonight (this has been going on for weeks). My husband did not have any real issues either. I am hoping once my neck and hips get straight my sleep will improve.

I will be curious to see if my animals sleep on it now. My one cat seemed to avoid the bed last night.

If one is remediating methylation issue with bypass supplementation, it may be somewhat safer. Still a good redox is the key to reliable detoxification.

### Josh — 2014-05-27T20:32:16+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/my-10-gauss-magnetico.10841/post-128592>

> [Clayton said:](/goto/post?id=128589)
>
> Nicld,
>
> I just re-read through EE12 yesterday..if you Ctrl F "chelation" there is a section of the blog that basically reiterates what Josh just said.

> **A decrease in entropy means two things in the quantum world:**
>
>
> 1.  **It means less chaos and more information is present in the system.** Any thing that oxidizes our cells causes us to make less oxygen from our mitochondria for respiration. This creates molecular chaos and swelling inside a cell. The cell uses these changes to adapt to stresses. The key signalers are RNS and ROS.
> 2.  **It also decreases the exclusion zone that water can generate.** When this happens, our tissues begin to slowly collect transition metals like aluminum, zinc and heavy metals in our cytosol. This is analogous to what happens in a star just before it dies as a supernova.
>
> A decrease in entropy is a quantum effect, not a biologic one, contrary to popular belief, mirroring how nature acts fundamentally, in your body and in a star. When the redox potential is lost, all matter struggles to deal with transition metals. Glutathione recycling is how these reactive transition metals are controlled in life. Sulfur-containing compounds also control the condensation of gases that form galaxies in space. The congruence of nature is astounding if you examine it.
>
> **_Chelating these transition metals is often a very bad thing to do when your cellular cytosol has simultaneous low levels of cysteine or glutathione. Freeing up a toxin when you cannot clear it makes the situation worse, not better. Very few people get told that by healthcare professionals. When chelation is done in the face of low glutathione or cysteine levels, massive levels of inflammation can occur very quickly and this accelerates aging and disease processes._**
>
> Vegans have very low levels of cysteine by design of their diet, and their tissues made of collagen show this effect first. One of the first places a human can assess their glutathione functioning is by looking in the mirror at their skin. If you have a lot of skin infections or red erythematous skin for any reason at all, you can bet your intracellular glutathione levels are not close to optimal. **Glutathione in increased doses posts a remarkable good “side effect,” such as good SKIN coloring**. The gut and lung are the next places you will see the effect.
>
> The greater the exposure to any toxins, the faster the body uses up its glutathione to detoxify other chemicals and metals in the body. This is why so many people with “gut issues” simultaneously have poor skin. [People also forget that there is a brain gut axis where cysteine and glutathione interactions become critical](http://jackkruse.com/brain-gut-9-what-really-killed-michael-jackson/). The skin and brain are also derived from neuro-ectoderm, so if your skin is suboptimal, you can bet your brain is also inflamed to some degree. Here is where the Vitamin A and D cycles are impacted because of how they are fundamentally tied to the electromagnetic spectrum of light.
>
> Without the protection of intracellular glutathione, cells die at a faster rate, making people age quicker and putting them at risk for toxin induced diseases like cancer.

### Josh — 2014-05-28T03:53:05+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/my-10-gauss-magnetico.10841/post-128640>

Consider with palpitations that if it something such as excitotoxicity secondary to not being able to handle methylxanthines, the cause may be lack of enough methylation support/bypass such as molybdenum or manganese at the time it is needed. Molybdenum especially clears the system quite quickly through the kidneys. If the palpitations are at night, I figure I may have peed out the methylation support before the body used it to handle whatever I am digesting and or detoxifying. I have tried extra doses at bedtime with some success. It still says nothing encouraging about my redox, but it helps me sleep and not suffer occasional palpitations on nights when I am more tired or stressesd.

I use to get occasional headaches from time to time and body aches. I would take some ibuprofen or NSAID to help.
That all became history when I started doing CT consistently and almost daily.
I now never get headaches and take no NSAID's.
Just my experience with doing CT constantly.

I also daily, get sun, go barefoot (earthing), sleep on a magnetico, eat Epi-Paleo, drink lots of structured RO water, protect against EMF. But I think the biggest impact has been consistency with my CT.

### Danco3636 — 2014-06-06T17:44:37+0300

My CT sessions start out at 15 min daily. If I have extra time I do a bit longer. Usually until slight shiver. I then know I made progress. I just do some sort of CT almost daily and usually first thing in the morning.
For my water intake. Larger glass (prior CT) and intake in the AM and taper off durning the day. I sleep better when not having to get up to go to the bathroom at night. I sip on it during the day and get fluid also through my food (raw oysters)... I don't like the feeling of being bloated from water.

I also sleep real good and wake up with boat loads of energy. I usually, first thing in the AM, walk outside and urinate out doors and walk around outside my home with minimal clothing and barefoot. Just a short walk around my house and get sun on my eyes and body. Sometimes climb a tree to burn off some energy.
My circadian rhythm is set pretty good. When the sun goes down I get extremely tired.

### Jack Kruse — 2014-06-10T05:41:30+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/my-10-gauss-magnetico.10841/post-130320>

Time to straighten out a big misconception. Bonalie mentions chlorella for detox? Why? it is an algae that has DHA. There is no real detox with a magnetico.
I really do not believe this at all. My bet is people get unusual reactions when they have a substantial deficit of DHA in their cells and this causes some thermodynamics fluctuations. If you continue to add DHA it goes away with time. Why? When you use a static unipolar magnetic field you are actively replacing "bad PUFA's" with the "correct PUFA" that allows for optimal toplogic control of energy, information, spin, and opto-phontonics in a cell. Moreover, most of you are blaming DHA or the pad, when in reality it is the breakdown metabolites of the bad PUFA's from lipid bilayer turnover. DHA is replacing these nasty actors.

In the context of a [nuclear magnetic resonance](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Nuclear_magnetic_resonance) experiment you are all living in today on spaceship Earth, the Rabi frequency is the nutation frequency of a sample's net nuclear magnetization vector about a radio-frequency field. This distinction is important because life is lived in a conceptual nuclear magnetic resonance experiment today because of the variant non native EMF of modern technology. I must make a distinction here between the Rabi frequency and the static magnetic unipolar field of the magnetico sleep pad. The pad offers a **_subtle control of spintronics_** in a world gone wild because it deals mostly with the Larmor frequency.

The Rabi requency in my view is the more important action because is deals with atomic transitions in a given light field. Since our brain uses an electron as conduit to get to the photon power by using mitochondrial cytochromes and our proteins to harvest this power, the Rabi frequency is the more important frequency than the Larmor frequency. Even Bonalie is clueless about this. You should not be, because the optics dictate this.......not the electronics of precession.

Our brain is designed to recapitulate a red giant thermodynamically using optophotonics. So far the magnetico is the only tool we have to deal with spintronics of an electron in a highly energized environment. Is it perfect? No, but it is the best we have today. In this way it can be thought of Newton’s laws of physics in 1890. Good enough for 99.9% of motions in the cosmos, but it could not explain Mercury’s perihelion. So the magnetico will help alot........but it is not perfect. This is where Bonalie and I differ big time in the application of his pad. The Rabi frequency is distinct from the [Larmor frequency](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Larmor_precession), which characterizes the precession of an electron via transverse nuclear magnetization about a static magnetic field.

In OSF 5, I explain in detail that DHA is designed to break down to the most anti inflammatory metabolites called resolvins and protectins. The same break down products are not found with other" bad PUFA's" that have substituted for DHA in your cells, hence their metabolites are very inflammatory........and they can cause these unusual reactions that people often blame on seafood. Rarely is it the DHA or the magnetico. The magnetico helps you replace bad PUFA's and this "detox response is good not bad thing. The longe it lasts the more bad PUFA's you original had. Here you can learn about how far away your redox really is. Most paleo people, alternative medicine, and paleo leaders have made this mistake in their writings on forums with the respect to DHA and excessive omega 6 or 9 replacments. Peskin and KAne have also tripped over this in my view.

### Jack Kruse — 2014-06-11T20:07:12+0300

There are so many misconceptions about DHA, ATP, mitochondria, ETC that what many call detox is in fact the movements to health. The alternative health movement causes those misconceptions.

Recent findings indicate that apoptosis does not require mitochondrial ATP synthesis but a functional electron transport chain (Jia et al., 1997a) and that the release of cytochrome c from the mitochondrial intermembrane space, a key step of apoptosis, is not accompanied by m∆ψ ( delta psi) changes (Kluck et al., 1997)

### Jack Kruse — 2014-06-13T01:33:22+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/my-10-gauss-magnetico.10841/post-130682>

Inhibiting mitochondrial β-oxidation selectively reduces levels of nonenzymatic oxidative PUFA's metabolites in the brain. This has been studied. Now this implies that normal mitochondrial β-oxidation increases levels of non enzymatic oxidative PUFA metabolites in the brain. This is why you sense a detox.........because bad PUFA lead to the inflammatory metabolites where DHA is reserved for use and always conserved best. When it is destroyed it is usually due to blue light UV light photons.......hence why the retina has more DHA than the brain since this is where blue light is commonly found in humans.

CITE: [http://www.nature.com/jcbfm/journal/v34/n3/full/jcbfm2013221a.html](http://www.nature.com/jcbfm/journal/v34/n3/full/jcbfm2013221a.html)

### paul hess — 2015-07-31T00:01:41+0300

Has anyone used a mattress that gets them down to 1 inch from the Magnetico, the minimum distance to avoid the positive charge and get the maximum charge?

Dr Bonolie and his staff recommend a 4 inch mattress assuming part of it bottoms out at I inch. They said the 4 inch distance creates about 13.5g in 10 g mattress at 8 inches.

I want to have a lower but firmer mattress still above the inch minimum but more of my body close to the 1 inch distance.

I am looking at these inflatable camp mattress because i have a thinner Therarest that is quite nice at only 1 inch thick total. REI makes the same kind of inflatable with foam mattresses that are 2.5 and 3-5 inches thick, while Thermarest makes ones that are 2 and 3 inches thick. An air cushion i can adjust it to get down to one inch and want to have that right amount of cushion, so i will be trying different thicknesses at the REI store. I am transitioning to firmer sleeping surfaces.

[http://www.rei.com/product/870757/rei-camp-bed-35-self-inflating-sleeping-pad](http://www.rei.com/product/870757/rei-camp-bed-35-self-inflating-sleeping-pad)

These models come in 30 in widths, which seems minimum for a permanent sleeping surface and to keep my arms and legs from going over the edge too much.

The biggest problem is that these mattresses are waterproof so get hot for indoor sleeping, in winter i open the window but summer is hard to cool off enough.

I will soon receive my 10G Magnetico.

### Jack Kruse — 2014-05-15T17:39:08+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-does-magnetism-scale-to-a-cell.10842/post-127075>

In March 2015, NASA will launch four identical spacecraft to study how magnetic fields around Earth connect and disconnect, explosively releasing energy – a process known as magnetic reconnection that can accelerate electrons to nearly the speed of light.

Have a look here:

Why is this acceleration important? The donut hole is wide open in the Southern hemisphere. This means the radiant energy can be pulsed into the upper atmosphere between Antarctica and Australia to affect all life below it because these photons can energize electron of life below. The photoelectric effect and water are linked big time on Earth while the photoelectric effect and the magnetiosphere interact in space at the planets magnetic boundary. Those energies however are transferred over the electromagnetic spectrum to life below: Life catches the electron with DHA. It is common knowledge that the ultimate source of all our energy and negative entropy is the radiation of the sun. When a photon interacts with a material particle on our globe it lifts one electron from an electron pair to a higher level. This is how the photoelectric effect works. This excited state as a rule has but a short lifetime and the electron drops back within 10–7 to 10–8 seconds to the ground state giving off its excess energy in one way or another. Life has learned to catch the electron in the excited state, uncouple it from its partner and let it drop back to the ground state through its biological machinery utilizing its excess energy for life processes'. Instead, life depends on catching an excited electron quite precisely (DHA) — by means of specific light absorbing pigments connected to DHA — and then tapping off its energy as it falls back towards the ground state. Life uses the highest grade of energy, the packet or quantum size of which is sufficient to cause the specific motion of electrons in the outer orbital of molecules in the neural cell membranes. It is on account of this that living systems can populate their high energy levels without heating up the body excessively. Energy is trapped directly at the electronic level by the electrons in DHA. It is stored not only as vibrational and electronic bond energies in DHA and the proteins DHA has selected for in neuronal cell membranes. Energy is also stored in the structure of the system, specifically in the electrons and they release that energy to water and collagen in CSF in the form of light allowing the electron to fall back to its ground state. Energy is also stored in piezoelectric collagen everywhere in the cell. This is found in energy gradients, fields and flow patterns, compartments, organelles, cells and tissues. All this in turn enables organisms to mobilize their energies coherently and hence make available the entire spectrum of stored energies for work, most efficiently and rapidly, whenever and wherever energy is required. Energy is moved from the electronic storage to any type of energy needed based upon the tissue systems design. All forms of energies however have to abide by mass equivalence because it is a universal law.

The electromagnetic spectrum starts from wavelengths of 10(−14) m at one extreme to 10(8) m at the other, spanning a range of 10(22), in terms of frequency doublings, 1022 ≈ 2(73), or 73 octaves. It is on these 73 octaves where the instructions of life are carried.

### Jack Kruse — 2014-05-15T17:40:41+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-does-magnetism-scale-to-a-cell.10842/post-127076>

The dielectric point of this junction is critical to life. The dielectric constant measures how effectively the medium shields the negative and positive charges from one another and hence reduces the force between them. Water, a major constituent of living systems, is an excellent dielectric because of its large effective dipole moment. Well the Earth has its own dipole moment as the video states. What NASA does not realize yet is how light and the magnetic dipole of the Earth scale to a cell. All via DHA and water interface.

Water molecules can surround the ions in solution and effectively neutralize charges just like the magnetosphere does to sunlight

Water has a dipole moment of 1.85 Debye. Polypeptide chains in the α-helical configuration have enormous dipole moments upwards of 500 Debye, as the individual moments of the peptide bonds are all aligned. In the double helical DNA, on the other hand, the anti-parallel arrangement of the two strands means that there is no net dipole moment, even though the single strands have their dipole moments due to the sugar-phosphate bonds in the backbone all aligned in the same direction. This is why DNA can save information magnetically. I wrote about this in OSF 2 about the magnetic moment of DNA because of its atomic arrangement and how it determines how it can act in a quantized fashion.

The other way water acts in quantized fashion with hydrophilic proteins is via hydrogen bonds. Hydrogen bonds are responsible for stabilizing the α-helical secondary structure of the polypeptide chain, as well as the β-pleated sheet structures between polypeptide chains. They also stabilize various ‘conformations’ or folded tertiary and quarternary structures of polypeptides and proteins. The hydrogen bonds between base pairs in the DNA double helix are responsible for the template mechanism, which ensures the faithful reproduction of the base sequence of the DNA molecule during replication.

This shows you how the interface of light and magnetism can scale to DNA in your cell. Macrocosm meets microcosm on a quantum scale. Most people do not see what I see.

### Jack Kruse — 2014-05-15T17:59:29+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-does-magnetism-scale-to-a-cell.10842/post-127078>

Let me give you an example of how I learned the hard way that Pollack's work scaled to the magnetic field. It is clear that electric and electromagnetic forces underlie all molecular and intermolecular interactions.

Fifty years ago discussions started about the interpretation of van der Waals’ universal molecular attraction as a result of the Coulombic interactions of the electrical components of the molecule. The average electrical field around a molecule was analyzed, which led to its characterization by dipole-, quadrupole- and higher moments. The mutual orientation effect of such molecules was recognised as a reason for attraction. However, the higher the temperature, the smaller the effect of such an orientation has to become. So the conclusion to be drawn was that at high enough temperatures molecular attraction should vanish. This obviously was in contradiction to the experimental facts, and so the picture of molecules as rigid electrical structures was completely abandoned. Instead, their structure was recognized as deformable and plasticized under the influence of an electric field: polarizability was introduced. The result was that, under the combined influence of the electrical field carried by one molecule and the polarization induced by it in its partner an additional potential energy emerges which is proportional to the square of the field and drives the molecules to each other. This still was not enough, since it made the mutual attraction of single atoms like neon or argon, around which the average field is zero, not understandable. The last difficulty was overcome by London when he recognized that the field important for the interaction should be taken as the instantaneous field.

Then when quantum theory was introduced as a new and essential background..........for molecular interaction things took off. It was at this juncture that colloid chemistry entered into the field with the recognition that the attraction between colloid particles was the result of essentially the same molecular forces as those which determine, for instance, the heat of vaporization of a liquid. Then Pollack's work on gels, crystals and water showed up..........in the early 1990's and things really look different for quantum biochemistry

Most people initially felt it had to be electrostatic forces.........but it wasn't

This attraction depends solely on two fundamental constants, Planck’s quantum of action h and the light velocity c, and illustrates emphatically how intimate the relation is between van der Waals’ universal attraction principles and the quantum fluctuations of the electromagnetic field the molecules find themselves in...........once again showing you how important the native EMF field is for life and how everything we think is biologic is quantized

### Jack Kruse — 2016-02-23T15:40:29+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-does-magnetism-scale-to-a-cell.10842/post-189639>

Around 130 BC, the Greek astronomer Hipparchus of Nicea discovered that **something** about the celestial sphere was slowly changing. What was changing?

-
    He noticed that the point where the celestial equator crossed the ecliptic slowly shifted. At that time it was in the constellation of **Aries**, the ram. It was shifting to **Pisces**, the fish, and is now entering **Aquarius**, the water carrier.

\-- What did that observation tell about the Earth's axis in space

-
    That the Earth's axis was slowly rotating around the direction perpendicular to the ecliptic, tracing a complete cone each 26000 years or so.

\-- What is the half-angle of that cone?

-
    The half-angle of that cone is 23.5 degrees. From that axis of tilt comes the seasons of the Earth................that tilt determines circadian biologic cycles for cells. From the largest things in the world comes the controlling arm for the smallest things in your cells.

This means no matter how we view it, it remains the same.
