# Thread: Aerobic and Anaerobic Respiration
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/aerobic-and-anaerobic-respiration.10942/>
**Subforum:** Biohacking 101
**Posts:** 14
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### persistence — 2014-05-28T22:55:05+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/aerobic-and-anaerobic-respiration.10942/post-128743>

Is there a decent two to three page reference that would give an overview of how the body transitions through different forms of energy use? I've read about anaerobic and aerobic respiration (glycolysis, krebs cycle, electron transport), but I have problems seeing the larger picture.

Which of these are we using when at rest?

Which of these are we using when we walk around?

Which of these are we using during a brisk 30 minute walk?

How does energy utilization change during a 30 minute jog?

If you are doing some anerobic sprinting, how does that transition to aerobic?

### Albert83BCN — 2014-05-28T23:21:17+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/aerobic-and-anaerobic-respiration.10942/post-128746>

I don't have any reference at hand but if you do a search on the net focusing on endurance running you'll find a lot of good articles that, at least, cover the basics about all that questions regarding how the metabolism changes between fuels. It's something that is quite important in endurance sports so there's a ton of info regarding that subject. I've found this book that is not a quick reference but seems pretty detailed yet more or less easy to understand (at least has colours, with me that helps )

[http://books.google.es/books?id=RWP3JQfvHGEC&lpg=PA49&dq=endurance metabolism&hl=es&pg=PA39#v=onepage&q=endurance metabolism&f=false](http://books.google.es/books?id=RWP3JQfvHGEC&lpg=PA49&dq=endurance%20metabolism&hl=es&pg=PA39#v=onepage&q=endurance%20metabolism&f=false)

Basically, at rest you use fat and also when walking, considering that you walk slowly or at least not with much effort. As you start using more oxygen, feel the effort and your heart rate goes up you start using more glucose and less fat at the same time, the point where you use more glucose than fat, which is when you get to your aerobic threshold that, if I call remember correctly is when you're running at 70% Vo2Max or so that, for me, equals 160 heart rate or so. If you go even higher intensity then you end up using almost glucose and cannot satisfy the oxygen demand and then that's where you start building up lactate (which causes that burning feeling, well in fact is not lactate but their H+ ions). The threshold seems to be at the intensity where you could run up to 1h at that even effort level so to speak.

{ "lightbox\_close": "Close", "lightbox\_next": "Next", "lightbox\_previous": "Previous", "lightbox\_error": "The requested content cannot be loaded. Please try again later.", "lightbox\_start\_slideshow": "Start slideshow", "lightbox\_stop\_slideshow": "Stop slideshow", "lightbox\_full\_screen": "Full screen", "lightbox\_thumbnails": "Thumbnails", "lightbox\_download": "Download", "lightbox\_share": "Share", "lightbox\_zoom": "Zoom", "lightbox\_new\_window": "New window", "lightbox\_toggle\_sidebar": "Toggle sidebar" }

When you do anaerobic sprinting... it depends, if it's less than 10s short then you basically use the creatine-phosphate ATP system which does not use any glucose just your already available short-term energy, but if it lasts a little bit more then you get again into the lactic glycolithic pathway, and you get aerobic once you ease until you can work again with more oxygen to use the beta-oxidation from fats.

More or less that's it with a lot of oversimplification.

### Albert83BCN — 2014-05-28T23:45:35+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/aerobic-and-anaerobic-respiration.10942/post-128749>

I've just found this site that I remember consulting every now and then when I was more into endurance running, it's quite good source for info:
[http://www.brianmac.co.uk/energy.htm](http://www.brianmac.co.uk/enduranc.htm#at)

### persistence — 2014-05-29T00:08:31+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/aerobic-and-anaerobic-respiration.10942/post-128750>

> [Albert83BCN said:](/goto/post?id=128746)
>
> I don't have any reference at hand but if you do a search on the net focusing on endurance running you'll find a lot of good articles that, at least, cover the basics about all that questions regarding how the metabolism changes between fuels. It's something that is quite important in endurance sports so there's a ton of info regarding that subject. I've found this book that is not a quick reference but seems pretty detailed yet more or less easy to understand (at least has colours, with me that helps )
>
> [http://books.google.es/books?id=RWP3JQfvHGEC&lpg=PA49&dq=endurance metabolism&hl=es&pg=PA39#v=onepage&q=endurance metabolism&f=false](http://books.google.es/books?id=RWP3JQfvHGEC&lpg=PA49&dq=endurance%20metabolism&hl=es&pg=PA39#v=onepage&q=endurance%20metabolism&f=false)

Good reference, but much more technical than I want to be for a high level of understanding....

> Basically, at rest you use fat and also when walking, considering that you walk slowly or at least not with much effort. As you start using more oxygen, feel the effort and your heart rate goes up you start using more glucose and less fat at the same time, the point where you use more glucose than fat, which is when you get to your aerobic threshold that, if I call remember correctly is when you're running at 70% Vo2Max or so that, for me, equals 160 heart rate or so. If you go even higher intensity then you end up using almost glucose and cannot satisfy the oxygen demand and then that's where you start building up lactate (which causes that burning feeling, well in fact is not lactate but their H+ ions). The threshold seems to be at the intensity where you could run up to 1h at that even effort level so to speak.
>
>
>
>
> When you do anaerobic sprinting... it depends, if it's less than 10s short then you basically use the creatine-phosphate ATP system which does not use any glucose just your already available short-term energy, but if it lasts a little bit more then you get again into the lactic glycolithic pathway, and you get aerobic once you ease until you can work again with more oxygen to use the beta-oxidation from fats.
>
>
>
>
>
> More or less that's it with a lot of oversimplification.

So how do the three stages of aerobic respiration map to this? Those are glycolysis, krebs cycle, and electron transport.

Basically what your diagram calls "Glycogen-lactic acid system" is the glycolysis stage of aerobic respiration? And what you are labeling as "aerobic respiration" is where krebs cycle begins?

Why I care about all of this: it turns out that I may have classic "chronic fatigue syndrome". The key characteristic of this disease is mitochondrial collapse in energy production after aerobic exercise. The exciting new development in diagnosing this disease is a \*TWO\* day VO2 max test. In people with this disease, on day two of the test their aerobic capacity during testing collapses by more than 10%. Google Workwell Foundation in Rincon California for details.

It's very much a research question still why the mitochondria stop producing energy, and at what stage this happens. But I very much want to start understanding the cycle.

Another exciting discovery from research is that people with CFS appear to be able to do anaerobic exercise without creating an energy collapse. So doing weight workouts with less than six reps per set makes sense. I'm going to try experimenting with sprint-swimming single laps followed by long rests to recovery.

Definitely as soon as I transition to any kind of aerobic exercise, I get a deep muscular weakness (which is probably either carbonic acid / CO2 or H+ from conversion of lactic acid to lactate). I have discovered some ways to clear the lactic acid, but it doesn't treat the mitochondrial loss of energy production.

### persistence — 2014-05-29T00:41:29+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/aerobic-and-anaerobic-respiration.10942/post-128752>

### Albert83BCN — 2014-05-29T00:42:19+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/aerobic-and-anaerobic-respiration.10942/post-128753>

Uhmm... I'm not an expert so I'll give you my understanding and maybe Barry or anyone else can chime in to clarify. The krebs cycle is always working and producing ATP for you, for that, it uses AcetylCoA mainly as a substrate. You can get AcetylCoa both via the beta-oxidation from fats or the conversion of glucose to pyruvate or other steps like the gluconeogenesis if you get it from aminoacids (last resort), etc. When you are doing very low intensity activity you can get to oxidize fats via the beta-oxidation that your mitochondria use with oxygen to get the Acetyl-CoA that is a fuel that can enter the Krebs Cycle. Once it gets in, you get some ATP and enzimes to generate NADH and FADH2 from that via some steps that take place that then fuel the Electron Transport Chain to get even more ATP. I guess Jack would tell you that this is bullshit and what counts here is electron flow and not ATP to get the energy... but I'll let that for him!

So, in this case that your mitochondria can use oxygen to fuel all your demands, we're using beta-oxidation, get AcetylCoa and fuel the Krebs Cycle that fuels the ECT and that allows you to get up to 146 or so ATP, which is quite much indeed. Not a problem with slow activity like working on the PC, walking or even running at very mild pace. That's what I understand as "aerobic respiration". But, there are too many steps on the cycle to get so much ATP by this way and this cannot always happen fast enough to burn the fat with respiration. That's when the glycogen-lactic thing gets in, the "anaerobic process".

In this case you start to get AcetylCoA from pyruvate which comes from glycolysis mainly, breakdown of glycogen to glucose, as we said once you get the AcetylCoA via one route or another you can fuel the Krebs cycle and then the ECT. Both sustrates end powering the Krebd Cycle but in this case you don't use oxygen, is anaerobic and the process is quite faster than using fat, but you also get only 36 ATP or so, and lactate as a by-product, which is good because it can be reused to get even more ATP but if it builds up faster than you can recycle it via other mechanisms then your fatigue starts to show up via de H+ ions that acidify the muscles and then you have to ease down the pace.

Not sure if it makes it any clear than before?

BTW, I'm not an expert on that (although I've had some degree of fatigue myself in the past and looked at the subject, and in fact that's why I don't engage in endurance running anymore at least at this moment...) but I suspect that Jack would tell you that it's not a disease per se but a state where your HPA is off and probably your thyroid is suffering and this cannot get enough free t3 to power the mitochondria... but again, not sure... maybe he can chime in and clarify.

Uhmm. for that weakness feeling... have you tested for RBC, iron, folate and Vit B12? any methylation issue? I also had a lot of weak leg feeling when I was training a lot, even on the rest days just by walking on the street, and turned out it correlated with a slightly low haematocrite and iron deficiency... solved it once I stopped it and gave myself some inner peace and verified these values where in range, but of course every case is different and similar issues may come from very different triggers...

Well, hope something might help!!

### Albert83BCN — 2014-05-29T01:02:29+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/aerobic-and-anaerobic-respiration.10942/post-128754>

The CFS and anaerobic exercise capability makes sense to me if you're doing short term high intensity efforts with ample recovery times as you mainly use the ATP-CP system (alactic) because it relies on your immediate pool which is filled with aminoacids from the diet and creatine phosphorilation, its ready to go at anytime as long as you only do short bursts, but once you have to get the 'burning furnaces' to get started is when the loss of efficiency might be noticeable the most, that's just my guess I might be missing something.

In any case, the leptin reset protocol is pretty clear that until someone is not leptin sensitive (2011 vision of the mitochondrial efficiency perspective, now in 2014 seems to be an electron loss issue due to poor redox potential, but all gets down to the same principles) is better to focus on getting that first solvend and then restarting any time of exercise. I think only walking is advised as a form of exercise.

### persistence — 2014-05-29T01:08:11+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/aerobic-and-anaerobic-respiration.10942/post-128755>

> [Albert83BCN said:](/goto/post?id=128753)
>
> BTW, I'm not an expert on that (although I've had some degree of fatigue myself in the past and looked at the subject, and in fact that's why I don't engage in endurance running anymore at least at this moment...) but I suspect that Jack would tell you that it's not a disease per se but a state where your HPA is off and probably your thyroid is suffering and this cannot get enough free t3 to power the mitochondria... but again, not sure... maybe he can chime in and clarify.
>

I also had a lot of weak leg feeling when I was training a lot, even on the rest days just by walking on the street, and turned out it correlated with a slightly low haematocrite and iron deficiency... solved it once I stopped it and gave myself some inner peace and verified these values where in range, but of course every case is different and similar issues may come from very different triggers...
>
> Well, hope something might help!!

My labs so far are good. There may be a methylation problem because my homocysteine is 14 and my other inflammatory markers are near zero. I will get genetic testing.

The feeling here is not a weak leg feeling. It is a catastrophic collapse in energy. At its worst, I would go out for an hour of sprinting and then lay awake that night drinking 1/2 litre of water every hour, desperately dehydrated, breathing heavily and abnormally (compensatory respiratory alkalosis to metabolic acidosis), and then wake feeling like the muscle had no ability to generate any energy for even simple movement.

I went from 14 hour workdays to three hour workdays, quickly. It's not a fine-tuning thing. It's massive and overwhelming.

### persistence — 2014-05-29T01:11:40+0300

### Albert83BCN — 2014-05-29T01:24:27+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/aerobic-and-anaerobic-respiration.10942/post-128757>

Yep, reverse leptin resistance is the goal so you get leptin sensitive. Did I say anything wrong? I'm writing a little bit fast and my non-native english starts to show up hehe. Uhmm... I guess that Jack would say that if your mitochondria is off then you are leptin resistant to some degree... but not sure, there might be more deep, particular issues like Jeremy's blogpost where the root cause is more deep than just the hypothalamus not regulating the fuel for the ferrari as a result chain of stressful events... As you've guessed testing leptin is not easy because the receptor affinity seems to have the role here not just the leptin levels, similar to what happens with insulin and insulin resistance.

# Thread: Calreticulin
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/calreticulin.10944/>
**Subforum:** Redox Rx
**Posts:** 48
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Josh — 2014-05-29T07:28:51+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/calreticulin.10944/post-128782>

[Biochem Biophys Res Commun.](http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/23791743#) 2013 Aug 9;437(4):497-501. doi: 10.1016/j.bbrc.2013.06.023. Epub 2013 Jun 17.
**Calreticulin negatively regulates the surface expression of Cav1.3 L-type calcium channel.**
[Karnabi E](http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed?term=Karnabi%20E%5BAuthor%5D&cauthor=true&cauthor_uid=23791743)1, [Qu Y](http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed?term=Qu%20Y%5BAuthor%5D&cauthor=true&cauthor_uid=23791743), [Yue Y](http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed?term=Yue%20Y%5BAuthor%5D&cauthor=true&cauthor_uid=23791743), [Boutjdir M](http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed?term=Boutjdir%20M%5BAuthor%5D&cauthor=true&cauthor_uid=23791743).
**[Author information](http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/23791743#)**

**Abstract**
**BACKGROUND:**
The neuroendocrine Cav1.3 L-type Ca channels have been recently found in the Human fetal heart and shown to play a vital role in Ca entry from the sarcolemma into the cell and in Ca homeostasis. Calreticulin, a Ca binding endoplasmic reticulum (ER) resident protein, has been recently shown to translocate to the cell surface where its role and function are just emerging. Here, we demonstrated a novel mechanism of Cav1.3 and calreticulin interaction resulting in downregulation of Cav1.3 channel densities in native Human fetal cardiac cells and Human Embryonic Kidney cell lines (tsA201).

**METHODS AND RESULTS:**
Cell surface and cytoplasmic staining of calreticulin was demonstrated first in cultured human fetal cardiomyocytes (HFC), gestational age 18-24 weeks, using confocal microscopy thereby establishing that calreticulin is present at the cell surface in HFC. Co-immunoprecipitation from HFC using anti-Cav1.3 Ca channel antibody, and probing with anti-calreticulin antibody revealed a 46 kDa band corresponding to calreticulin suggesting that Cav1.3 Ca channel and calreticulin co-assemble in a macromolecular complex. Co-expression of Cav1.3 and calreticulin in tsA201 cells resulted in a decrease in surface expression of Cav1.3 Ca channels. These findings were consistent with the electrophysiological studies showing that co-transfection of Cav1.3 Ca channel and calreticulin resulted in 55% reduction of Cav1.3 Ca current densities recorded from tsA201 cells.

**CONCLUSIONS:**
The results show the first evidence that calreticulin: (1) is localized outside the ER on the cell surface of HFC; (2) coimmunoprecipitates with Cav1.3 L-type Ca channel; (3) negatively regulates Cav1.3 surface expression thus resulting in decreased Cav1.3 Ca current densities. The data demonstrate a novel mechanism of modulation of Cav1.3 Ca channel by calreticulin, which may be involved in pathological settings such as autoimmune associated congenital heart block where Cav1.3 Ca channels are downregulated.

Published by Elsevier Inc.

[http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/23791743](http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/23791743)

### Josh — 2014-05-29T07:32:41+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/calreticulin.10944/post-128784>

So I started rereading CPC 8 had the thought that maybe the hookworms modulate Calcium Channels popped in my head. It makes sense because it is a key function for them to defend against antihelminthic agents and such. So I found a suggestion that they like most organisms make Calreticulin:

> Calreticulin
> The calcium-binding protein calreticulin, ubiquitously expressed
> in the cells of higher organisms, has been proposed
> to have numerous functions, including molecular chaperoning
> (98), calcium signaling (90), and integrin-mediated signaling
> and cell adhesion (28). Probably found in the secretions of
> N. americanus, calreticulin was recently shown to be the major
> allergen recognized by IgE from patients infected with the
> parasite (108). Its role as an extracellular secreted product of
> nematodes remains speculative, but some intriguing immunomodulatory
> properties can be extrapolated from studies with
> mammalian calreticulin. First, it can bind to C1q of the complement
> system (39), and, second, it has lectin-like properties
> (67), which might be exploited by secreted parasite homologs
> to bind to and cross-link cell surface glycoproteins involved in
> the regulation of IgE production (108). Given the lectin-like
> properties of calreticulin, it might also be involved in skewing
> the immune response towards the Th2 phenotype (see “C-type
> lectins” above).
> Since investigations in the field of hookworm secretory molecules
> are still in their infancy, numerous intriguing findings
> are expected in the near future.

[http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC89000/](http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC89000/)

So I wonder whether extra endogenous Calreticulin form the microbiome can modulate calcium efflux and help us out with some of the EMF effects?

### Josh — 2014-05-29T07:42:26+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/calreticulin.10944/post-128787>

So could the immune modulation effect from hookworm infection be related to their ability to modulate Calcium efflux and signalling through effecting calcium channel function and intracellular Ca2+ levels? Calreticulin seems to be important in Glucocorticoid function.

> **ABSTRACT**
> We have characterized a pathway for nuclear export of the glucocorticoid receptor (GR) in mammalian cells. This pathway involves the Ca2+ -binding protein calreticulin (CRT), which directly contacts the DNA binding domain (DBD) of GR and facilitates its delivery from the nucleus to the cytoplasm. In the present study, we investigated the role of Ca2+ in CRT-dependent export of GR. We found that removal of Ca2+from CRT inhibits its capacity to stimulate the nuclear export of GR in digitonin-permeabilized cells and that the inhibition is due to the failure of Ca2+-free CRT to bind the DBD. These effects are reversible, since DBD binding and nuclear export can be restored by Ca2+ addition. Depletion of intracellular Ca2+inhibits GR export in intact cells under conditions that do not inhibit other nuclear transport pathways, suggesting that there is a Ca2+ requirement for GR export in vivo. We also found that the Ran GTPase is not required for GR export. These data show that the nuclear export pathway used by steroid hormone receptors such as GR is distinct from the Crm1 pathway. We suggest that signaling events that increase Ca2+ could positively regulate CRT and inhibit GR function through nuclear export.

[http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC133999/](http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC133999/)

### Josh — 2014-05-29T07:57:37+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/calreticulin.10944/post-128790>

Interesting stuff, regardless of what inspires one to take a look...lo and behold there is actually a **_"Hookworm Conundrum"_**....

> **Abstract**
> Hookworm parasites possess effective defence mechanisms against host innate and adaptive immune responses such as complement activation, eosinophilia and the respiratory burst in granulocytes. However there does appear to be a strong relationship between helminthic infection and the allergic phenotype in infected individuals. One candidate parasite antigen implicated in each of the above phenomena is calreticulin. It was originally identified by screening parasite cDNA expression libraries with IgE antibody, and once cloned to homogeneity was shown to modulate complement activation and bind to the signalling domains of integrins involved in haemostasis and inflammation. Hence the term the hookworm calreticulin conundrum. In hookworms, calreticulin is acting as a potential pro-inflammatory allergen, yet has the ability to moderate inflammation. However, to fulfil these functions, calreticulin should be secreted by the parasite, in a manner already described for fleas and ticks.

[http://www.landesbioscience.com/iu/Eggleton\_9781587064746.pdf](http://www.landesbioscience.com/iu/Eggleton_9781587064746.pdf)

[http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/gene/811#interactions](http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/gene/811#interactions)

Superoxide chemistry can also account for the puzzling results seen when more nutrients were added to the soil in the LR experiment; because life multiplies, the amount of gas should have increased when a second or third batch of nutrients was added, but if the effect was due to a chemical being consumed in the first reaction, no new gas would be expected. Lastly, many superoxides are relatively unstable and are destroyed at elevated temperatures, also accounting for the "sterilization" seen in the LR experiment."[\[7\]](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Viking_lander_biological_experiments#cite_note-PlaxcoGross2011_2-7)

### Jack Kruse — 2014-10-23T18:11:17+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/calreticulin.10944/post-144858>

> [Inger said:](/goto/post?id=144824)
>
> I use mud/clay mixed with coconut oil on my white spots and nose if I am too much in the sun  Works really well for a sunblocker... will look funny tho but that just adds to the fun things in life

Do you know why? CO has sulfur in it. **Coconut does contain natural sulfur.** It is naturally occurring and we need sulfur in our diet. When coconut is sprayed with a sulfur containing preservative to keep it nice and white, it usually had 200 ppm of sulfur on it. The naturally occurring sulfur content of raw, unadulterated coconut flakes is up to 10 ppm. Because the flour is made by removing the oil from the flakes, this concentrates the sulfated proteins. Sulfur content in coconut flour can be as high as 30 ppm because of that process. Coconut Flour does contain sulfur, but it is at concentration between 10 and 30 ppm and completely natural.

# Thread: The Emotional / Spiritual Roots to Disease
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-emotional-spiritual-roots-to-disease.10948/>
**Subforum:** The Cave
**Posts:** 95
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### PaulG — 2014-05-29T16:40:53+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-emotional-spiritual-roots-to-disease.10948/post-128833>

I have recently been talking to many seniors about their health ailments and a large percentage say that x disease started at a time of intense emotional upheaval/trauma/stress, such as a loved one passing away or a family member getting sick or strife/bitterness/jealosy/anger etc. For example, in the last two days I talked to two women whose husbands tragically died of heart attacks and who went from normal 7am awakening to 3am awakening and which continued years after the event. In my own case I developed a condition around the time of extreme stress at work and I feel that somehow the events around that time have been locked in and my search for 'physical' keys have not so far opened/released the lock.

We overplayed the food/diet card and its importance in relation to obesity and disease but perhaps we are overplaying the electron card. We do not talk much here about the negative emotional / spiritual roots of disease but we all intuitivetely and experientially recognize its importance but it seems to be a taboo area. There is a lot of seemingly woo woo stuff on emotional release therapy, emotional healing etc but maybe we think it is woo woo because we know so little about it.

Of course it is well known that stress causes a host of physiological responses. See [here](http://pni.osumc.edu/KG%20Publications%20\(pdf\)/195.pdf)

> Chronic low grade inflammation is a core pathophysiological process that may be involved in many age-related diseases. Elevations in circulating markers of inflammation have been associated with frailty and disability, osteoporosis, certain cancers, cardiovascular disorders, type II diabetes, rheumatoid arthritis, and Alzheimer’s disease. Importantly, stress and negative emotions appear to promote the production of inflammatory mediators, providing a physiological mechanism by which negative psychological states may impact health.This chapter reviews the basic physiological processes of inflammation, the relationships among stress, negative emotions, social relationships, and inflammation, and the behavioral and physiological pathways linking stress, emotions,and inflammation.

> Caregivers must deal daily with the changes in cog- nitive functioning (e.g., memory loss, confusion), behaviors (e.g., agitation, aggression), and personal- ity (e.g. apathy, inappropriate emotion) of their loved one. Caregivers have an increased risk for anx- iety and depressive disorders, a greater frequency of infectious illnesses, poorer responses to vaccines, delayed wound healing, and even an increased risk of death, compared to noncaregiving controls (Gouin, Hantsoo, & Kiecolt-Glaser, 2008).

### nonchalant — 2014-05-29T18:47:23+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-emotional-spiritual-roots-to-disease.10948/post-128853>

Clayton, I have no doubt that our emotions and stress have huge impacts on our health. But underlying those emotions and stress is our health. Our state of health is the real root cause.

As I get more healthy, I find my emotions are changing. More stable, less controlling of my actions and thoughts. I feel plenty of emotion, looking at the sunrise, getting into the water, etc; but I seldom ask myself "Why did I go and do that?" I don't end up regretting things I've said in haste anymore.

Losing a loved one is devastating. I know. But I don't think we should end up sacrificing our lives or health, unintentionally or not. No one would want that, including the dearly departed. 'Trapped emotions' sounds like it might be like a tune that is stuck in your head (and Jack recently discussed that phenomenon.) I should know, I have some trapped emotions of my own. I'm interested in that book, thanks for the link.

### fitness@home — 2014-05-29T18:48:55+0300

It made me ill to the point of IBS kicking in. I went to the doctor with a list of "symptoms" and she ran a bunch of labs in order to rule things out. Felt like total sh\*t at the time.

That was the same time period that my labs first showed elevated antibodies the Anti-Sceleroderma 70 and ANA-d. I am in agreement that stressful life events can have negative effects on our health.

I also know it is how we handle our stress and I've worked on that. EFT was just a part of what I did to resolve that stress.

p.s. Thanks for the link

### sooperb — 2014-05-30T00:13:57+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-emotional-spiritual-roots-to-disease.10948/post-128873>

I think emotional health is just as important as physical health, each impacts on the other. I noticed with my daughter when her mental health was in crisis it would trigger her asthma, when her asthma was really bad her mental health suffered. I don't think you can dismiss emotional health as being of lesser importance. Comfort eating caused by emotional issues will lead to obesity. The cure isn't dieting, it's tackling the cause of the emotional trigger of the comfort eating. There are lots of similar disorders that are more rooted in emotional causes than physical.

### NeilBB — 2014-05-30T01:04:34+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-emotional-spiritual-roots-to-disease.10948/post-128878>

I would say that it is not an either/or thing, electrons or emotions. Rather, the experiencing of negative emotions is by definition a physical, neurological, autonomic, and endocrine process that is stressful and involves a loss of electrons.

The healthier your mind and body are (i.e., the more energy efficient, the more electron dense you are) prior to receiving some external stressor, the better chance you will have of being able to withstand it without it completely destroying you. The more electrons you add to your system, the better equipped you will be to adapt to the situation, no matter how bad it is.

This is not to diminish the importance of emotions, feelings, or circumstances, but just to state a relationship. Adding electrons to the system will improve the stress response and thereby improve emotions. Human beings will always be subject to massive stressors. We live, we die. Loved ones will live and die. People hurt people and kill people. Injustice has always been present. Health involves the ability to manage stress. Stressors can't be eliminated. Energy medicine and psychological techniques work if and to the extent that they improve the system of energy efficiency. I agree with Josh. There is really no dichotomy here.

> [PaulG said:](/goto/post?id=128910)
>
> true, if you understand the world in mechanstic material reductive sense not true if you believe we are mind, body and spirit beings.

Whether you are a mechanistic reductionist or a Cartesian dualist, you still have to acknowledge that the two "domains" intersect in humans and influence each other. Emotions do have a number of easily measureable physical biological expressions. Also, note that patients emotional responses can be drastically altered after strokes or other brain damage. This is commonplace in my work. Healthy emotional expression definitely requires a functional brain in my opinion.

Furthermore, a scientific approach to "spirit" (like Rupert Sheldrake with his idea of morphogenic fields) defines "spirit" in terms of energy and according to physics, matter and energy are interrelated and interconvertable. Sheldrake is vehemently opposed to materialism, in fact. He would likely argue that the mind-body problem is an artifact of the prevailing overly "materialist" scientific bias.

Most of the things Jack is talking about involve matter-energy interactions. That's why the mass equivalence equation keeps coming up. Electrons are key particles that mediate this matter energy interface. I think the answer to the mind-body problem likely lies in the physics of mass-energy relationships. But this view is not really "materialism," as such. More accurately, it is quantum physics, which acknowledges the primacy of non-material energy.

### PaulG — 2014-05-30T15:03:59+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-emotional-spiritual-roots-to-disease.10948/post-128914>

This is where I am coming from..

Jack has been pealing away layers of the onion to reveal deeper and more fundamental truths more closely aligned to natures laws.

One of my favourite books is [A Voyage to Arcturus](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/A_Voyage_to_Arcturus) by David Lyndsay and a good summary is [here](http://www.violetapple.org.uk/vta/philorvision.php). In a number of allegorical fantastic adventures the hero Maskull pursues a world view only to discover at the end that it is an illusion as depicted by the hideous laughing Crystalman grin. Simlarly in [gnostic](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Gnosticism) writings Humans are generally ignorant of the divine spark resident within them. This ignorance is fostered in human nature by the influence of the false creator and his Archons, who together are intent upon keeping men and women ignorant of their true nature and destiny. Anything that causes us to remain attached to earthly things serves to keep us in enslavement to these lower cosmic rulers.
The [Kabbalah](http://home.earthlink.net/~gnosisla/April5.html) and [Gnosticism](http://gnosis.org/gnintro.htm) have many parallels.

I am concerned that in peeling away the onion we do not completely throw away the pieces that make the onion. Jack gets frustrated when people talk about methylation as he has a deeper understanding of its role and significance and its relative importance. From a certain perspective I resolved excess estrogen levels not from gaining electrons but from discovering I had a SNP expression that caused a detox blockage and supplementing with DIM fixed it, although it could be argued that at the end of the day the net result is better redox. My point is that my level of understanding is not at Jacks level and when you try to filter/interpret from a lower level to a higher level you get misunderstanding (witness the recent epic circadean biohackers [thread](https://www.facebook.com/groups/circadianbiohackers/647204542022882/?comment_id=647564521986884&notif_t=like) between Jack and Bill Syrjala). Jacks vision and work is revolutionary and subversive in a way as it potentially makes redundant much of the bullshit thats currently gets peddled in the name of health and wellness. Do we need to see through the illusions! yes, do we neecd to be on Jacks level, no (we can enjoy chinese food without speaking chinese) but I guess we have a responsibility to help and educate others if we want to change the world.

### NeilBB — 2014-05-30T16:34:39+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-emotional-spiritual-roots-to-disease.10948/post-128929>

> [Lahelada said:](/goto/post?id=128920)
>
> This fits nicely here. Are emotions thoughts? s the division of the two an error? My gut feeling (!) is yes.
>
> [http://forum.jackkruse.com/index.php?threads/are-thoughts-endocrine-secretions.10752/](http://forum.jackkruse.com/index.php?threads/are-thoughts-endocrine-secretions.10752/)
> .

I would say that emotions are a "kind of thought," but also always involve an evaluative component (subconscious value judgment), and a biophysical manifestation (neuroendocrine response).

And so if emotions and conscious thoughts are constantly in disagreement, it does suggest internal conflict in thinking.

But shorter-term appropriate emotional responses to a specific event would not however suggest internal conflict, merely a negative (or positive) assessment of the event in question.

### JackFrake — 2014-05-30T21:28:52+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-emotional-spiritual-roots-to-disease.10948/post-128952>

Here's a nice illustration of emotions leading to action. In this case, one teen's action triggering a long-range coordination of cycles of epi-cycles, and increasing the ecosystem's probability for life.

I'd say he returned a hefty electron profit from that 'thought.'

[http://www.thebetterindia.com/10904/jadav-molai-payeng-forest-man-india/](http://www.thebetterindia.com/10904/jadav-molai-payeng-forest-man-india/)

He was 16 when the flood hit Assam, and Payeng observed that the flow of migratory birds was gradually declining to the forest areas and wetlands near his home and snakes were disappearing in large numbers. This disturbed him.

_“I asked my elders, what would they do if all of us die one day, like these snakes. They just laughed and smirked but I knew I had to make the planet greener,”_ he says.

His village elders told him that with decline in forest cover and deforestation, animals lost their homes. The solution was to build new homes or forests for the animals, they said. ([Source](http://www.aljazeera.com/indepth/features/2014/01/indias-forest-man-201411762848958203.html))

He alerted the forest department but they asked him to plant trees himself (which he actually did). He located a riverine island, on the banks of River Brahmaputra, and began to plant the saplings. Payeng visited the island and planted a few saplings **every day for three decades.**

>
> The healthier your mind and body are (i.e., the more energy efficient, the more electron dense you are) prior to receiving some external stressor, the better chance you will have of being able to withstand it without it completely destroying you. The more electrons you add to your system, the better equipped you will be to adapt to the situation, no matter how bad it is.
>
> This is not to diminish the importance of emotions, feelings, or circumstances, but just to state a relationship. Adding electrons to the system will improve the stress response and thereby improve emotions. Human beings will always be subject to massive stressors. We live, we die. Loved ones will live and die. ### NeilBB — 2014-06-05T02:01:54+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-emotional-spiritual-roots-to-disease.10948/post-129696>

> [Josh (Paleo Osteo) said:](/goto/post?id=129377)
>
> Still talking about seperation in health!?
> We are whole, that is all there is to it.

We are whole, yes. But human consciousness is fragmentary and we basically perceive isolated chunks of data at any one time. Hence the need for epistemology. Hence the need for classifications and terminology and concepts at all. Our mental organization and conception of "the whole" varies greatly from person to person and from time to time in the same person.

We have to recreate a mental representation of the whole by perceiving, studying, deconstructing, and re-integrating the fragments. This takes several different forms in philosophy: analysis vs synthesis, differentiation vs integration, deduction vs induction. (There are neuroscientific and quantum aspects to this process as well, and they are very complex.) But ultimately, it's all describing the same thing. "The whole." But we do have to analyze it, i.e. deconstruct it to see it. That's the human epistemological process.

Today's technical and scientific world is completely stuck in the analysis and deconstruction side of the equation. It has no clue how to synthesize and integrate anything into a whole. That's just where society is today unfortunately. It is only facile in one half of the equation. But you need both damn halves to understand anything! (I just looked at some flyers for upcoming neuroscience conferences at my center. The mind-numbing molecular details that PhD researchers are discussing is astounding, but nobody has a clue of how any of the bits fit together--that would be hard multidisciplinary work that would violate the protocol of our "division of mental labor" society and is simply not being done.)

Anyhow, I get where Paul is coming from here... He just needs to deconstruct this stuff further before he re-integrates it back together in my opinion. He's just in a different part of the process as it pertains to this particular issue right now.

But ultimately I would say this to answer the original question. "Electrons" are important and "spiritual matters" are important. They are not separate in reality for a human. Considering them apart from one another is just a mental construct. An illusion. In reality, for a human, they simply are two sides of the same coin. When you take steps to improve either, you necessarily improve BOTH!

It gets less fragmented the deeper you go....

> Closeness is not liked. You allow people closeness only very conditionally – she is your wife, then
> you allow her to sleep in your bed, to be with you in the night. But still you keep an invisible wall
> between you and the wife. The wall is invisible but it is there. If you want to see it, observe it, you will
> find it – a very transparent wall, a glass wall, but it is there. You remain private to yourself, your wife
> remains private to herself. Your privacies never meet. You have your secrets, she has her secrets.
> You are not really open and available to each other.
> Even in love you don’t allow the other to really come into you, you don’t allow the other to penetrate
> you. And remember, if you allow the other to penetrate you there is great bliss. When two lovers’
> bodies penetrate each other there is a physical orgasm, when two minds penetrate each other there
> is a psychological orgasm, and when two spirits penetrate each other there is a spiritual orgasm.
> You may not even have heard about the other two. Even the first is a rarity. Very few people attain
> to the real physical orgasm, they have forgotten about it. They think that ejaculation is orgasm. So
> many men believe that they have orgasm and because women don’t ejaculate, not at least visibly,
> eighty per cent of women think that they don’t have any orgasm. But ejaculation is not orgasm. It is
> a very local release, a sexual release – it is not orgasm. A release is a negative phenomenon – you
> simply lose energy – and orgasm is a totally different thing. It is a dance of energy, not a release. It
> is an ecstatic state of energy. The energy becomes a flow. And it is all over the body; it is not sexual,
> it is physical. Each cell and each fibre of your body throbs with new joy. It is rejuvenated. and great
> peace follows it.
> But people don’t even know physical orgasm, so what to say about, how to talk about the
> psychological orgasm? When you allow somebody to come very close to you – a friend, a beloved,
> a son, a father, a Master, it does not matter what kind of relationship – when you allow somebody
> so close that your minds start overlapping, penetrating, then there is something so beyond the
> physical orgasm that it is a jump. The physical orgasm was beautiful, but nothing compared to the
> psychological orgasm. Once you have known the psychological orgasm, physical orgasm by and by
> loses all attraction. It is a very poor substitute.
> But even psychological orgasm is nothing compared to the spiritual orgasm, when two spirits –
> by ’spirits’ I mean two emptinesses, two zeros – overlap. Remember, two bodies can only touch;
> they cannot overlap because they are physical. How can two bodies be in the same space? It is
> impossible. So at the most you can have a close touch. Two bodies can only touch; even in sexual
> love two bodies only touch. The penetration is very superficial, it is not more than a touch – because
> two physical objects cannot exist in the same place. If I am sitting here in this chair then nobody
> else can sit in the same place. If a stone is lying in a certain place, you cannot put another thing in
> the same place. The space is occupied.
> Physical objects occupy space, so two physical objects can only touch – that is the misery of love.
> If you know only physical love you will always be miserable because you will only be touching, and
> the deep desire is to become one. And two physical objects cannot become one. It is not possible.
> A better communion happens with two psyches. They can come closet. But even then two thoughts
> cannot occupy the same space because thoughts are subtle things. They can touch far better, they
> can get intertwined far better than two things... things are very solid, thoughts are very liquid. When
> two lovers’ bodies meet it is like two stones coming together; when two psyches meet, it is like water
> and oil coming together. Yes, it is a better meeting but still there is a subtle division.
> Two thoughts cannot occupy the same space. When you are thinking one thought you cannot think
> another thought at the same time – the first thought will have to go, only then will you be able to pay
> attention to another thought. Only one thought can be in your mind at one time and in one space.
> So even friendship, psychological friendship, misses something, lacks something. It is better than
> the first but nothing compared with the third.
> Spiritual penetration is the only possibility of really being one with someone – because spirit means
> emptiness. Two emptinesses can be together. And why two? **_ALL the emptiness of the world can
> be together in one space. They can occupy the same space simultaneously, at the same time, there
> is no problem – because they are neither concrete like objects nor liquid like water, thoughts. They
> are simply empty of themselves. You can bring as many emptinesses together as possible._**
>
> Sufis: The People of the Path, Vol 1 272 Osho
> CHAPTER 16. A LOTUS ON THE LAKE OF EMPTINESS

### PaulG — 2014-06-05T15:33:49+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-emotional-spiritual-roots-to-disease.10948/post-129768>

So, my maybe not so straight-forward intention and intuition is to raise awareness that in this life there is huge complexity and we have barely scratched the surface of what it is to be whole, healthy and spritual beings.

I have been on this journey from the beginning and have read or listen to probably 80% of what everything JK has written or spoken (quite a huge undertaking btw) and this forum and I realise that the fate of humankind rests on putting this stuff into practice. so yes I do 'get it' very much so although if my brain and redox were in better shape I would no doubt make better connections.

I understand how important this 'new science' is and that when we adopt these measures and understand the implications behind being energy efficient, returning entropy to the environment, living in harmony with nature etc then all manner of recoveries and rehabilitation and disease reversal is possible.

What I don't agree with is that it is a panacea for all things and I certainly don't expect Dr. Kruse to provide the answer to what is the meaning of life and how to resolve deep seated emotional and traumatic causes to disease. Conversley, having the attitude that we must just collect electrons is an over-simplification and we must all work on various 'centres' - physical, emotional, spiritual and if you take a reductive view to any of these then true health, longevity and evolution is not possible.

Shout out to Josh for all his effort and wisdom and sheer ability to keep up with much of this - you are an inspiration and I ponder your words deeply.

> NeilBb said:
>
> Today's technical and scientific world is completely stuck in the analysis and deconstruction side of the equation. It has no clue how to synthesize and integrate anything into a whole\]

...kind of explains my brain stuck in left brain thinking, logic and rationality due to career in IT when there is a repressed artist/poet fighting for oxygen..

Looking forward to OSF5.

> [Josh (Paleo Osteo) said:](/goto/post?id=129726)

> it is a western belief only, in my opinion, that this is the only way we can understand whole. it is a sum of the way things have been done up until now in the west. one has to question whether it is the only way to understand it. this is where dissociative drug phenomena interest me (not insomuch as taking them, but the quantum phenomenon of "altered reality"

I think the Eastern and Western paths are clearly very different. "Shortcuts" like hallicinogenics may get you there faster, but is there a trade off? Whether or not the destination is the same is pretty hard to say. They say that "the point of the journey is not to arrive" anyway.

You can take a trip to the mountains by interstate or by back roads. You can travel by bicycle, motorcycle, car, bus, or plane. You may get to same place but you will learn different things along way and have a much different experience of the "same trip."

It seems to me that the point of living is much more a _process_ than a _state_, even if you call that state "enlightenment."

So to me if "enlightenment" means anything at all it must refer to an active process rather than a target state of being. Whether you define that process in thermodynamic terms, or QED theory, or biochemical reactions, or in subjective sensory experiences.

And so this master guru in the news who is so deep in mediation that people can't tell if he's dead or alive...

Even if this is legit, I'm not sure that his achievement is such a great thing. If he is alive, he's basically been able to achieve biological metastability one rung up from equilibrium (death). But where's the fun in that? If that's enlightenment, it's probably not for me... Just sayin...

Relating back to what I said earlier. "Comfort" seeks a state (noun) as a goal. "Discomfort" embraces change (process/verb) as the goal and norm.

The only "static state" for a living thing occurs after it dies. So seeking comfort is synonymous with seeking death...

### NeilBB — 2014-06-05T19:45:24+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-emotional-spiritual-roots-to-disease.10948/post-129825>

It is my belief that the Eastern way is beautiful, and may have even been fine in the past for everyone to follow all the time. It was a slow go but mostly respectful of and in accordance with nature. And surely that approach still works for individuals today to varying degrees.

But now we live in a Western world, thoroughly altered by modern technology, in the East and the West and whether we like it or not. Pandora's box has been opened, but as a society we only have a half-ass understanding of how it all works.

Western science got us into this and only Western science can get us out of it now. Everybody meditating in Tibet for the next few centuries ain't gonna cut it because of the wheels of technology already in motion and the path of destruction they are leaving behind. That's just reality.

The only way out now is through. That means a new and improved Western science. And quantum biological engineering. That has to be the backbone of a new improved world. It's too late for individuals to just permanently retreat into quiet disconnected spirituality and consider that noble anymore. I would argue that to do so today is the opposite of respecting nature. Nature is already under assault and screaming for help. The Eastern traditions have no answer for that. How could they? No. Only a new enlightened Western engineering can save nature from the old unenlightened careless Western abuses.

Sure. I do still think there is a lot of benefit to mediation and studying Buddhism and employing such practices. They are important tools for quieting the individual mind. But society's sinking ship is not gonna be saved without some badass applications of Western science and engineering.

So I am glad that Jack's son is studying engineering and not Eastern philosophy. And I would hope that more caring kids will do the same...

### Jack Kruse — 2014-06-05T19:48:09+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-emotional-spiritual-roots-to-disease.10948/post-129828>

Life uses proteins coded or uncoded for by nucleic acids actually are what direct the arrow of time in biology. In physics time is invariant in all its equations therefore time is an illusion of nature. Time is created because of things created to be metastable of dissipative in nature. Such systems, rely totally upon nonlinear protein/quanta interactions, that can form their own mass, spatial, and temporal structures. These aspects of proteins are synonyms for the "dissipative structure" of a cell. These cells can exist as long as the system is continuously held far from equilibrium due to a continual flow of energy or matter through the system. Autophagy keeps us in this state. This means the organization of the cell is far more important than the things that feed our metabolism. Anything having these qualities has to be kept far from equilibrium to maintain a healthy life. Only at death is equilibrium obtained. I view the arrow of time and the inability of reforming the shell of a cracked egg as playing a constructive role in nature. The arrow of time is essential to the existence of biological systems, because they contain highly organized irreversible structures. In other words, the cells organization is the critical part of life, not the fuel we put into it. Nature builds enormous complexity via 6 leptons & quarks by evolving into a chemical evolution via the construction of atoms. It is the arrangement of these atoms that dictate the life we live.

I began this in OSF 4 and I finish the thought in OSF 5

### Jack Kruse — 2014-06-05T19:51:33+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-emotional-spiritual-roots-to-disease.10948/post-129830>

Obesity will never be understood until they understand it is not about food calories. It is about the particles that stuff is made from. The smallest particles, the subatomic ones. For example, in 1846 astronomers found a new planet, Neptune, because the mathematics of Newton's laws, when applied to the orbit of Uranus, said some massive body had to be there. They eventually found it, using the best telescopes available to peer into the sky. It took innovating a better teloscope to show us it was true.
Obesity is intimately tied to mass.
The search for the Higgs boson, which current theories say gives matter its mass, is being conducted in a similar way. Why is understanding the homology important?
To find the Higgs, it took the construction of particle accelerators able to reach energies measured in trillions of electronvolts – enough to push a proton to speeds a few meters per second slower than light. Just as it would take the invention of the telescope to seek Neptune, it wasn't until the Large Hadron Collider was built that anyone could see evidence of the Higgs.

The Higgs field give mass to electrons and protons and these are the particle our mitochondria deal with........when biology realizes obesity it tied to these relationships then and only then will they understand obesity is cause by a loss of energy not an excess of energy.
People in biology already know right before a mitochondria dies......it swells just like an obese person does.........all the sign posts are there. It is why fat men have low energy and low testosterone. Its why women have upside down PG/E2 ratio's.
It is also why mitochondria swell when they lose energy too......They are not gaining energy. Input to mitochondria is ETC. Output signaling is tied to protons.

The Higgs search was important to understanding the world around us. In fact it holds the key to why we are all getting fatter worldwide. It is even more than finding a new planet, it helps us answer questions that seem intuitive: why does anything have mass at all? What do we mean by "mass?"

If you don't have a clue what or how mass is given and distributed how in the hell do you know anything about the cause of obesity? Short answer is biology doesn't..........That is why I wrote the EMF 2 blog. The answer is in quantum physics.

### Jack Kruse — 2014-06-05T19:54:45+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-emotional-spiritual-roots-to-disease.10948/post-129834>

According to the exclusion principle of Wolfgang Pauli, no two particles occupying a minute region of space and possessing halfintegral spins can have the same quantum number-the same values of momentum, charge and spin. The Pauli exclusion principle accounts elegantly for the configurations of electrons that determine an element's place in the periodic table. Electrons also determine the chemistry of everything on this planet..........and people that is why life is all about electrons whether you accept it or not. And only one lipid has been found to collect and organize electrons for 600 million years in evolutionary history.

### Josh — 2014-06-05T20:48:59+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-emotional-spiritual-roots-to-disease.10948/post-129848>

Just to give Qualia some definition:

> There are many definitions of qualia, which have changed over time. One of the simpler, broader definitions is: "The 'what it is like' character of mental states. The way it feels to have mental states such as pain, seeing red, smelling a rose, etc."[\[3\]](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Qualia#cite_note-3)
>
> [Clarence Irving Lewis](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Clarence_Irving_Lewis), in his book _[Mind and the World Order](http://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Mind_and_the_World_Order&action=edit&redlink=1)_ (1929), was the first to use the term "qualia" in its generally agreed upon modern sense.
>
> There are recognizable qualitative characters of the given, which may be repeated in different experiences, and are thus a sort of universals; I call these "qualia." But although such qualia are universals, in the sense of being recognized from one to another experience, they must be distinguished from the properties of objects. Confusion of these two is characteristic of many historical conceptions, as well as of current essence-theories. The quale is directly intuited, given, and is not the subject of any possible error because it is purely subjective.
>
> [Frank Jackson](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Frank_Cameron_Jackson) (1982) later defined qualia as "...certain features of the bodily sensations especially, but also of certain perceptual experiences, which no amount of purely physical information includes" (p. 273).
>
> [Daniel Dennett](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Daniel_Dennett) identifies four properties that are commonly ascribed to qualia. According to these, qualia are:
>
>
> 1.  _[ineffable](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ineffable)_; that is, they cannot be communicated, or apprehended by any other means than direct experience.
> 2.  _intrinsic_; that is, they are non-relational properties, which do not change depending on the experience's relation to other things.
> 3.  _private_; that is, all interpersonal comparisons of qualia are systematically impossible.
> 4.  _directly or immediately apprehensible in [consciousness](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Consciousness)_; that is, to experience a quale is to know one experiences a quale, and to know all there is to know about that quale.

[http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Qualia](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Qualia)

I have never been much for philosophy...I have always looked for experience first and then tried to find a way to explain it to others...a more or less thankless endeavor which is more a device for me than a blessing for "others"...I have been called "entertaining" and "interesting" in good moments and less flattering things at other times....Dr. Kruse is the first person who gave me a reasonable model and took the time to explain it...a labor of love because it has taken him 10 years to put the material together and put it out...of course he experimented on himself first which is the ethical thing to do...

**_"You can't tell people their brains are broken until you can tell them why"

This is the problem with all types of medicine, no one can really explain to themselves or their patients what is broken, what the mechanism is, and how to manage that at the foundational axiomatic level. The Eastern and alternative systems have lost something in translation and context while allopathy is becoming a dinosaur in its resistance to the information we are confronting here...._**

ADH deficit is a lot like peripheral neuropathy development in nerves. ADH are central neurons but all neurons have the same general features........cell membranes that need DHA iodine and water and microtubules.

Myelination is a ketosis seafood story: Read this post: [http://jackkruse.com/energy-epigenetics-1-the-inf...](http://jackkruse.com/energy-epigenetics-1-the-infant-brain-is-unique/) The other key for all neuropathy is tied to the altered functioning of the microtubules. Robert O. Becker associated the mammalian life system with DC currents in his work to show that all mammals use carbon based semiconduction to generate energy for uses in bone, regeneration, and in wakefulness. In ADH case, it is also needed to make this posterior pituitary hormone and shuttle it down from the stalk to the pituitary via microtubules. This occurs right below the myelin layer. So any kind of central or peripheral neuropathy destroys this semiconducting ability and directly affects the ability for microtubules to work. Today, I believe those with these neuropathy have lost their effective semiconductor property in peripheral nerves but it begins in the brain be due to the fact that the transfer of charge carriers from DHA to water occurs in microtubules. A lack of ADH or AF is a story of bad semiconduction and electron steal syndrome. Remember, water is naturally confined by microtubules in the brain and peripheral nerves. The brain act by first accelerating electrons in the pi electron electric fields in DHA to shuttle them down the cell membranes of peripheral nerves. **This tells you all forms of neuropathy begins in the brain with a lack of DHA in the cell membrane to generate the DC current.** _Paleo meat is not going to help this at all because it has no DHA in it and even less iodine. Moreover, eating meat to excess will dehydrate you too_. This is why paleo falls way to short in disease reversals in my opinion.
This becomes analogous to the transfer of electrons between conduction bands in a semiconductor junction called the Josephson junction. I've written about these junctions in the blog. Learn about them. The semiconductor property of DHA, iodine, and water would be a direct signature of the realization of how metabolic energy quanta is transformed to a kinetic energy as water moves down a microtubule. Here you can see why EE 1 blog is critical to your question's answer. PrP is a critical protein in the cell membranes in neurons in the brain and peripheral nerves to allow the transfer of electron's energies in this process. PrP proteins are covered here in this blog:[http://jackkruse.com/organizational-structural-fa...](http://jackkruse.com/organizational-structural-failure-4-human-prions/)

The ability to change the power or energies of small proteins is the critical force that has allowed us to build our complex human nervous system. This protein can open the door to heaven or hell based upon the redox potential. This very same protein can lead to neuropathy when the redox potential in low in the cell membrane or our mitochondria. If you have neuropathy, your redox potential is low by definition. Today, it is the loss of power that is robbing of us of nerve function today. A power loss that causes the prion protein to shift from an alpha helix to beta sheet due to electron/photon loss in DHA in cell membranes of nerves. In physics, energy is power and power comes in many different forms. **The quantum mechanism provides proteins with small quanta of energy that can lead to massive alterations in function.** This is why your nerves are not working. Replacing DHA, iodine and water is critical to neuropathy or posterior pituitary hormone release.

This is where evolution gets its power to change and adapt proteins for good or bad or to open the gates to heaven or hell if you like. Proteins are the basic unit of what the matter is, and what we are fundamentally. This is where evolution starts and ends within each one of us. In order to see the true effects of evolution, we need to stop looking at morphologic changes in things we study and start looking at proteins in 3-D molecular simulations of how atoms interact as energies are changed by electrons or photons.

> A Single Photon Emission Computed Tomography (SPECT) scan is a type of nuclear imaging test that shows how blood flows to tissues and organs.

> The neurons however on the dynamic PET/SPECT scans show major hypo-metabolism and
> hypo-perfusions in the frontal and temporal lobes. Autoregulation is clearly uncoupled in AD and it is
> 100% tied to a loss of energy. Remember the only way we have to uncouple CBF from CRMO2 and keep
> the patient alive is anesthetic drugs. This begs the question…when we put a person with AD under a
> general anesthetic do they react differently since their neurons cannot control their own perfusion do to
> the formation of neurofibrillary tangles? Anesthesia literature says this is a true statement. So then why is
> it that a ketogenic diet, high in MCT oils, very helpful in improving cognitive function in AD? Here is where
> it gets real interesting.

Do Food Electrons Impart a Quantum Effect

> 4\. SK3: SK3 is a small-conductance calcium-activated potassium channel partly responsible for the
> calcium-dependent after hyperpolarisation current. KCa2.3 channels play a major role in human
> physiology, particularly in smooth muscle relaxation. The expression level of KCa2.3 channels in the
> endothelium influences arterial tone by setting arterial smooth muscle membrane potential. It appears this
> SNP allows for arterial constriction to be maintained for extended period of times in the frontal lobes and
> in the reward tracts of the brain where COMT is a big player. SPECT scans have shown hypo perfusion
> in these regions and may help explain why these patients have persistent alterations in the body images
> and perception. The expression level of KCNN3 is dependent on hormonal regulation, particularly by the
> sex hormone estrogen. Estrogen not only enhances transcription of the KCNN3 gene, but also affects the
> activity of KCa2.3 channels on the cell membrane. Since this disease shows up at puberty, it is no
> surprise that it is linked to estrogen production. Most females with anorexia have a markedly abnormal LH
> surge and they do not ovulate. This is tied to very high IL-6 levels and leptin resistance even in the face of
> low serum leptin levels. It should be obvious why these patients all suffer from sex steroid

CPC2

### biohacked — 2014-06-25T13:12:24+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-am-i-supposed-to-rehydrate-if-my-adh-is-flatlined.10951/post-132189>

@[Aerose91](https://forum.jackkruse.com/members/10465/) low ADH (or non-existant) is a biomarker for CIRS (chronic inflammatory response syndrome).

Since you have also been diagnosed with encephalitis and M.E. you are very likely to be a CIRS candidate as these are typical misdiagnoses.

Neither encephalitis or M.E. are typically specific diagnoses (unless you had a spine tap proving encephalitis) - just categories that people often get put into when the cause is unknown and there is a rough grouping of symptoms and differentials.

I was previously diagnosed with both of these also, and discovered I fitted the labs across the board for CIRS, got on Shoemaker's protocol and am doing a lot better, so am talking from experience.

I strongly encourage you to look into the work of Ritchie Shoemaker on CIRS, and biotoxin related illness. In addition, you can get a quick screening test done for free via Visual Contrast Sensitivity ([vcstest.com](http://vcstest.com)) which will give you a positive or negative based on levels of capillary hypoperfusion detected in optic nerve (impact = inability to see contrast).

Ritchie Shoemaker appeared on Bulletproof Radio/ Podcast recently and talks about the VCS as a screening mechanism around the 1 hour mark ->

@[biohacked](https://forum.jackkruse.com/members/10633/)

That's all really great info, thank you. I can say with confirmation tho that my "ME" was triggered by encephalitis. It hit me immediately and I was close.to going into ca coma, my personality completely changed and I was in full psychosis. Had no idea where I was or who I was etc... It was pretty bad. Things lightened up slightly after about 2 weeks (which they deemed the end of the acute infection) but since then I have started to go downhill drastically- both mentally and physically all due to energy expenditure. I eventually did have encephalitis confirmed through a spinal tap where they found white blood ccells but could never identify the specific pathogen.

You said you were misdiagnosed with ME as well? I've.l never heard of CIRS but Dr Kruse has described my lack of ADH for the same reason. What I don't have an answer to tho is what has caused such chronic and severe inflammation? Isn't that pretty much ME in a nutshell?

I have done DR Shoemakers genetic test for the HLA gene defect and I do have it but have dine the VCS Eye test twice and have passed both times. I've also had one positive test for Lyme come back positive so that's getting investigated further.

It sucks because all energy expenditure is my enemy so I do as little as possible while trying to follow Dr K's and my doctor's protocols but I continue to get worse.

### Jack Kruse — 2014-07-01T20:21:09+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-am-i-supposed-to-rehydrate-if-my-adh-is-flatlined.10951/post-132904>

What caused it is simple.......more protons and less electrons in your CSF. This bathes the posterior pituitary where there is no BBB. Simple. And I talked about Shoemaker a long time ago in the Lyme meet leptin blog.........

### Jack Kruse — 2014-07-01T20:26:34+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-am-i-supposed-to-rehydrate-if-my-adh-is-flatlined.10951/post-132905>

pH is a measure of proton presence. Protons are acids. Positively charged (+) protons gobble up the negative (-) electrons in our body to turn oxygen into water in our mitochondria. Failed oxygen delivery to tissues is the beginning of a spiral of descent into neolithic disease. As the photoelectric charge becomes reduced, less and less oxygen penetrates cells, creating bad news for you, and good news for your doctor! Low PE charge is often tied to a big time lack of DHA in cell membranes.

Some improvements here and there, but nothing that really got me back to that wonderful stage that I'd built myself up to.

Then my research brought me here, as it did you. This is the place. We have at our disposal a diagnostician that is simply the best in the world. Doc just cut to the chase with the above posts. No mystery for me anymore, just continuing to apply what I've learned here.

Der Head

# Thread: Sleep and Obesity Study
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/sleep-and-obesity-study.10956/>
**Subforum:** The Cave
**Posts:** 8
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Josh — 2014-05-30T14:37:35+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/sleep-and-obesity-study.10956/post-128912>

[http://www.bbc.com/news/health-27617615](http://www.bbc.com/news/health-27617615)

> Sleeping in a room with too much light has been linked to an increased risk of piling on the pounds, a study shows.
>
> A team at the Institute of Cancer Research in London found women had larger waistlines if their bedroom was "light enough to see across" at night.
>
> However, they caution there is not enough evidence to advise people to buy thicker curtains or turn off lights.
>
> The study of 113,000 women was [published in the American Journal of Epidemiology](http://aje.oxfordjournals.org/content/early/2014/05/29/aje.kwu117.short?rss=1).
>
> The women were asked to rate the amount of light in their bedrooms at night as:
>
>
> -   Light enough to read
> -   Light enough to see across the room, but not read
> -   Light enough to see your hand in front of you, but not across the room
> -   Too dark to see your hand or you wear a mask
>
> Their answers were compared to several measures of obesity. Body Mass Index, waist-to-hip ratio and waist circumference were all higher in women with lighter rooms.

[http://aje.oxfordjournals.org/content/early/2014/05/29/aje.kwu117.short?rss=1](http://aje.oxfordjournals.org/content/early/2014/05/29/aje.kwu117.short?rss=1)

> **Abstract**
> There has been a worldwide epidemic of obesity in recent decades. In animal studies, there is convincing evidence that light exposure causes weight gain, even when calorie intake and physical activity are held constant. Disruption of sleep and circadian rhythms by exposure to light at night (LAN) might be one mechanism contributing to the rise in obesity, but it has not been well-investigated in humans. Using multinomial logistic regression, we examined the association between exposure to LAN and obesity in questionnaire data from over 100,000 women in the Breakthrough Generations Study, a cohort study of women aged 16 years or older who were living in the United Kingdom and recruited during 2003–2012. The odds of obesity, measured using body mass index, waist:hip ratio, waist:height ratio, and waist circumference, increased with increasing levels of LAN exposure (_P_ < 0.001), even after adjustment for potential confounders such as sleep duration, alcohol intake, physical activity, and current smoking. We found a significant association between LAN exposure and obesity which was not explained by potential confounders we could measure. While the possibility of residual confounding cannot be excluded, the pattern is intriguing, accords with the results of animal experiments, and warrants further investigation.

### Josh — 2014-05-31T04:30:05+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/sleep-and-obesity-study.10956/post-128994>

Melatonin Research

> **Abstract**
> Melatonin is an old and ubiquitous molecule in nature showing multiple mechanisms of action and functions in practically every living organism. In mammals, pineal melatonin functions as a hormone and a chronobiotic, playing a major role in the regulation of the circadian temporal internal order. The anti-obesogen and the weight-reducing effects of melatonin depend on several mechanisms and actions. Experimental evidence demonstrates that melatonin is necessary for the proper synthesis, secretion, and action of insulin. Melatonin acts by regulating GLUT4 expression and/or triggering, via its G-protein-coupled membrane receptors, the phosphorylation of the insulin receptor and its intracellular substrates mobilizing the insulin-signaling pathway. Melatonin is a powerful chronobiotic being responsible, in part, by the daily distribution of metabolic processes so that the activity/feeding phase of the day is associated with high insulin sensitivity, and the rest/fasting is synchronized to the insulin-resistant metabolic phase of the day. Furthermore, melatonin is responsible for the establishment of an adequate energy balance mainly by regulating energy flow to and from the stores and directly regulating the energy expenditure through the activation of brown adipose tissue and participating in the browning process of white adipose tissue. The reduction in melatonin production, as during aging, shift-work or illuminated environments during the night, induces insulin resistance, glucose intolerance, sleep disturbance, and metabolic circadian disorganization characterizing a state of chronodisruption leading to obesity. The available evidence supports the suggestion that melatonin replacement therapy might contribute to restore a more healthy state of the organism.

[http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/m/pubmed/24654916/](http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/m/pubmed/24654916/)

### Hope — 2014-05-31T23:23:42+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/sleep-and-obesity-study.10956/post-129078>

For my n=1, my aMSH is super low, one more reason I don't make much melatonin among others. But I too sleep way better following the circadian stuff.......despite a tanked aMSH (which is slowly rising yaye).

### fergal — 2014-05-30T18:12:37+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/anti-inflammatories-ppis-digestion.10957/post-128936>

Greetings all

Here's hoping someone can give me a nudge in the right direction.

In a nutshell, I put my back out 2 months ago after a small car accident. Was put on Difene & PPIs for a couple of weeks. The back pain improved but my digestion took a real hit. It's like my motility has slowed down. I used to be a 3/4 on the Bristol chart but I've been a 1 for the last 5 weeks. I've been off the PPIs & anti-inflammatories for at least 5 weeks now but no improvement. I don't ever really feel hungry and feel intermittently nauseous - had an upper GI endoscope done and got the all clear. I find that dense foods (high protein, high fat) can make me feel nauseous except in very small amounts. I do feel some discomfort just below my ribs on the right which could be gall-bladder but I don't know how this relates to the Difene & PPIs or what I should be doing about it.

Any suggestions/insights at what I should be doing would be really appreciated.

Best wishes

Fergal
